original_script: अथ प्रथमोऽध्यायः - अर्जुनविषादयोगः
transliteration: Arjuna Viṣhāda Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Arjuna—the warrior; Viṣhāda—dharmic crisis; Yogaḥ—the path of synthesis.
translation_english: Chapter 1 Summary: The Yoga of Arjuna's Dharmic Crisis
deep_dive_text: Historically, Chapter 1 establishes the 'Dharmakshetra' not as a physical place, but as a legal and ethical jurisdiction. The crisis of Arjuna is documented not as a personal breakdown, but as the collapse of 'Kshatriya-dharma' when confronted by 'Kula-dharma' (familial duty). This tension reflects the social transition of the period.

original_script:
धृतराष्ट्र उवाच

धर्मक्षेत्रे कुरुक्षेत्रे समवेता युयुत्सवः।

मामकाः पाण्डवाश्चैव किमकुर्वत सञ्जय।।1.1।।
transliteration:
dhṛitarāśhtra uvācha
dharma-kṣhetre kuru-kṣhetre samavetā yuyutsavaḥ
māmakāḥ pāṇḍavāśhchaiva kimakurvata sañjaya
word_meanings: dhṛitarāśhtraḥ uvācha—Dhritarashtra said; dharma-kṣhetre—the land of dharma; kuru-kṣhetre—at Kurukshetra; samavetāḥ—having gathered; yuyutsavaḥ—desiring to fight; māmakāḥ—my sons; pāṇḍavāḥ—the sons of Pandu; cha—and; eva—certainly; kim—what; akurvata—did they do; sañjaya—Sanjay
translation_english: Dhritarashtra said: O Sanjaya, after assembling at Kurukshetra, eager for battle, what did my sons and the sons of Pandu do?
deep_dive_text: The Gita begins with a question, not an answer. Dhritarashtra's words reveal anxiety, attachment, and moral uncertainty before action even starts.

original_script:
सञ्जय उवाच

दृष्ट्वा तु पाण्डवानीकं व्यूढं दुर्योधनस्तदा।

आचार्यमुपसङ्गम्य राजा वचनमब्रवीत्।।1.2।।
transliteration:
sañjaya uvācha
dṛiṣhṭvā tu pāṇḍavānīkaṁ vyūḍhaṁ duryodhanastadā
āchāryamupasaṅgamya rājā vachanamabravīt
word_meanings: sanjayaḥ uvācha—Sanjay said; dṛiṣhṭvā—on observing; tu—but; pāṇḍava-anīkam—the Pandava army; vyūḍham—standing in a military formation; duryodhanaḥ—King Duryodhan; tadā—then; āchāryam—teacher; upasaṅgamya—approached; rājā—the king; vachanam—words; abravīt—spoke
translation_english: Sanjaya said: Seeing the Pandava army arranged for war, King Duryodhana approached teacher Drona and spoke these words.
deep_dive_text: Duryodhana's first move is one of calculated statecraft. By approaching his teacher Drona, he attempts to bridge the gap between political authority and military command. A historian would note the subtle insecurity beneath his speech; he seeks to consolidate his psychological advantage even while commanding the larger army.

original_script:
पश्यैतां पाण्डुपुत्राणामाचार्य महतीं चमूम्।

व्यूढां द्रुपदपुत्रेण तव शिष्येण धीमता।।1.3।।
transliteration:
paśhyaitāṁ pāṇḍu-putrāṇām āchārya mahatīṁ chamūm
vyūḍhāṁ drupada-putreṇa tava śhiṣhyeṇa dhīmatā
word_meanings: paśhya—behold; etām—this; pāṇḍu-putrāṇām—of the sons of Pandu; āchārya—respected teacher; mahatīm—mighty; chamūm—army; vyūḍhām—arrayed in a military formation; drupada-putreṇa—son of Drupad, Dhrishtadyumna; tava—by your; śhiṣhyeṇa—disciple; dhī-matā—intelligent
translation_english: O teacher, behold this mighty army of the sons of Pandu, expertly arranged by your intelligent disciple, the son of Drupada.
deep_dive_text: Duryodhana uses 'intelligent irony' by pointing out that Dhrishtadyumna—the general of the Pandava army—was actually Drona's own student. This highlights the complex, interconnected web of relationships that made the Kurukshetra war a tragedy of civilizational proportions, where personal affection and political duty were in direct conflict.

original_script:
अत्र शूरा महेष्वासा भीमार्जुनसमा युधि।

युयुधानो विराटश्च द्रुपदश्च महारथः।।1.4।।
transliteration:
atra śhūrā maheṣhvāsā bhīmārjuna-samā yudhi
yuyudhāno virāṭaśhcha drupadaśhcha mahā-rathaḥ.
word_meanings: atra—here; śhūrāḥ—powerful warriors; mahā-iṣhu-āsāḥ—great bowmen; bhīma-arjuna-samāḥ—equal to Bheem and Arjun; yudhi—in military prowess; yuyudhānaḥ—Yuyudhan; virāṭaḥ—Virat; cha—and; drupadaḥ—Drupad; cha—also; mahā-rathaḥ—a warrior who could single-handedly match the strength of ten thousand ordinary warriors;
translation_english: In this army stand many heroic archers, equal in battle to Bhima and Arjuna.
deep_dive_text: This verse establishes the seriousness of the Pandava side. Underestimating the opponent is replaced by sober recognition of real strength.

original_script:
धृष्टकेतुश्चेकितानः काशिराजश्च वीर्यवान्।

पुरुजित्कुन्तिभोजश्च शैब्यश्च नरपुङ्गवः।।1.5।।
transliteration:
dhṛiṣhṭaketuśhchekitānaḥ kāśhirājaśhcha vīryavān
purujit kuntibhojaśhcha śhaibyaśhcha nara-puṅgavaḥ
word_meanings: dhṛiṣhṭaketuḥ—Dhrishtaketu; chekitānaḥ—Chekitan; kāśhirājaḥ—Kashiraj; cha—and; vīrya-vān—heroic; purujit—Purujit; kuntibhojaḥ—Kuntibhoj; cha—and; śhaibyaḥ—Shaibya; cha—and; nara-puṅgavaḥ—best of men
translation_english: There are also great warriors such as Dhrishtaketu, Chekitana, the valiant king of Kashi, Purujit, Kuntibhoja, and Shaibya.
deep_dive_text: The roll call underscores that dharma-war is not a personal duel but a civilizational conflict involving many noble houses.

original_script:
युधामन्युश्च विक्रान्त उत्तमौजाश्च वीर्यवान्।

सौभद्रो द्रौपदेयाश्च सर्व एव महारथाः।।1.6।।
transliteration:
yudhāmanyuśhcha vikrānta uttamaujāśhcha vīryavān
saubhadro draupadeyāśhcha sarva eva mahā-rathāḥ
word_meanings: yudhāmanyuḥ—Yudhamanyu; vikrāntaḥ—courageous; uttamaujāḥ—Uttamauja; vīrya-vān—gallant; saubhadraḥ—the son of Subhadra; draupadeyāḥ—the sons of Draupadi; sarve—all; eva—indeed; mahā-rathāḥ—mighty chariot-warriors
translation_english: There are Yudhamanyu, Uttamauja, the son of Subhadra, and the sons of Draupadi, all powerful chariot-warriors.
deep_dive_text: The mention of younger warriors signals continuity across generations. Duty here is inherited, not invented at the last moment.

original_script:
अस्माकं तु विशिष्टा ये तान्निबोध द्विजोत्तम।

नायका मम सैन्यस्य संज्ञार्थं तान्ब्रवीमि ते।।1.7।।
transliteration:
asmākaṁ tu viśhiṣhṭā ye tānnibodha dwijottama
nāyakā mama sainyasya sanjñārthaṁ tānbravīmi te
word_meanings: asmākam—ours; tu—but; viśhiṣhṭāḥ—special; ye—who; tān—them; nibodha—be informed; dwija-uttama—best of Brahmins; nāyakāḥ—principal generals; mama—our; sainyasya—of army; sanjñā-artham—for information; tān—them; bravīmi—I recount; te—unto you
translation_english: Now, O best of brahmanas, know also the distinguished leaders of our own army.
deep_dive_text: Duryodhana shifts from praise to command. In crisis, leaders try to reclaim control by naming structure and hierarchy.

original_script:
भवान्भीष्मश्च कर्णश्च कृपश्च समितिञ्जयः।

अश्वत्थामा विकर्णश्च सौमदत्तिस्तथैव च।।1.8।।
transliteration:
bhavānbhīṣhmaśhcha karṇaśhcha kṛipaśhcha samitiñjayaḥ
aśhvatthāmā vikarṇaśhcha saumadattis tathaiva cha
word_meanings: bhavān—yourself; bhīṣhmaḥ—Bheeshma; cha—and; karṇaḥ—Karna; cha—and; kṛipaḥ—Kripa; cha—and; samitim-jayaḥ—victorious in battle; aśhvatthāmā—Ashvatthama; vikarṇaḥ—Vikarna; cha—and; saumadattiḥ—Bhurishrava; tathā—thus; eva—even; cha—also
translation_english: You, Bhishma, Karna, Kripa, Ashvatthama, Vikarna, and the son of Somadatta are all present.
deep_dive_text: By naming top veterans, Duryodhana seeks psychological stabilization. Speaking names can be a way of summoning confidence.

original_script:
अन्ये च बहवः शूरा मदर्थे त्यक्तजीविताः।

नानाशस्त्रप्रहरणाः सर्वे युद्धविशारदाः।।1.9।।
transliteration:
anye cha bahavaḥ śhūrā madarthe tyaktajīvitāḥ
nānā-śhastra-praharaṇāḥ sarve yuddha-viśhāradāḥ
word_meanings: anye—others; cha—also; bahavaḥ—many; śhūrāḥ—heroic warriors; mat-arthe—for my sake; tyakta-jīvitāḥ—prepared to lay down their lives; nānā-śhastra-praharaṇāḥ—equipped with various kinds of weapons; sarve—all; yuddha-viśhāradāḥ—skilled in the art of warfare
translation_english: Many other brave heroes are here as well, ready to lay down their lives for my sake, armed with many weapons and skilled in war.
deep_dive_text: The verse reflects war's total demand: skill, commitment, and willingness to sacrifice life itself. Ideology is now embodied in human risk.

original_script:
अपर्याप्तं तदस्माकं बलं भीष्माभिरक्षितम्।

पर्याप्तं त्विदमेतेषां बलं भीमाभिरक्षितम्।।1.10।।
transliteration:
aparyāptaṁ tadasmākaṁ balaṁ bhīṣhmābhirakṣhitam
paryāptaṁ tvidameteṣhāṁ balaṁ bhīmābhirakṣhitam
word_meanings: aparyāptam—limitless (or insufficient in fear); tat—that; asmākam—ours; balam—strength; bhīṣhma—by Grandsire Bheeshma; abhirakṣhitam—safely marshalled; paryāptam—limited (or sufficient in fear); tu—but; idam—this; eteṣhām—their; balam—strength; bhīma—Bheem; abhirakṣhitam—carefully marshalled
translation_english: Our strength, protected by Bhishma, seems limitless; their strength, protected by Bhima, seems limited.
deep_dive_text: This is strategic rhetoric, not neutral analysis. Duryodhana tries to elevate morale by projecting invincibility. Scholars note the ambiguity of 'aparyāptam', which can mean both unlimited (overconfidence) and insufficient (fear).

original_script:
अयनेषु च सर्वेषु यथाभागमवस्थिताः।

भीष्ममेवाभिरक्षन्तु भवन्तः सर्व एव हि।।1.11।।
transliteration:
ayaneṣhu cha sarveṣhu yathā-bhāgamavasthitāḥ
bhīṣhmamevābhirakṣhantu bhavantaḥ sarva eva hi
word_meanings: ayaneṣhu—at the strategic points; cha—also; sarveṣhu—all; yathā-bhāgam—in respective position; avasthitāḥ—situated; bhīṣhmam—to Grandsire Bheeshma; eva—only; abhirakṣhantu—defend; bhavantaḥ—you; sarve—all; eva hi—even as
translation_english: Therefore, stationed at your strategic points, all of you must support and protect Bhishma.
deep_dive_text: Bhishma becomes the symbolic center of Kaurava confidence. Protecting symbols is often as important as protecting territory.

original_script:
तस्य संजनयन्हर्षं कुरुवृद्धः पितामहः।

सिंहनादं विनद्योच्चैः शङ्खं दध्मौ प्रतापवान्।।1.12।।
transliteration:
tasya sañjanayan harṣhaṁ kuru-vṛiddhaḥ pitāmahaḥ
siṁha-nādaṁ vinadyochchaiḥ śhaṅkhaṁ dadhmau pratāpavān
word_meanings: tasya—his; sañjanayan—causing; harṣham—joy; kuru-vṛiddhaḥ—the grand old man of the Kuru dynasty (Bheeshma); pitāmahaḥ—Grandsire; sinha-nādam—lion’s roar; vinadya—sounding; uchchaiḥ—very loudly; śhaṅkham—conch shell; dadhmau—blew; pratāpa-vān—the glorious
translation_english: Then Bhishma, the grandsire of the Kurus, roared like a lion and blew his conch loudly, delighting Duryodhana.
deep_dive_text: Bhishma's conch is a signal of command and emotional ignition. Sound marks the transition from preparation to irreversible action.

original_script:
ततः शङ्खाश्च भेर्यश्च पणवानकगोमुखाः।

सहसैवाभ्यहन्यन्त स शब्दस्तुमुलोऽभवत्।।1.13।।
transliteration:
tataḥ śhaṅkhāśhcha bheryaśhcha paṇavānaka-gomukhāḥ
sahasaivābhyahanyanta sa śhabdastumulo ’bhavat
word_meanings: tataḥ—thereafter; śhaṅkhāḥ—conches; cha—and; bheryaḥ—bugles; cha—and; paṇava-ānaka—drums and kettledrums; go-mukhāḥ—trumpets; sahasā—suddenly; eva—indeed; abhyahanyanta—blared forth; saḥ—that; śhabdaḥ—sound; tumulaḥ—overwhelming; abhavat—was
translation_english: Immediately conches, drums, kettledrums, trumpets, and horns sounded together with a tremendous uproar.
deep_dive_text: The collective roar creates shared momentum. Public noise can drown private doubt.

original_script:
ततः श्वेतैर्हयैर्युक्ते महति स्यन्दने स्थितौ।

माधवः पाण्डवश्चैव दिव्यौ शङ्खौ प्रदध्मतुः।।1.14।।
transliteration:
tataḥ śhvetairhayairyukte mahati syandane sthitau
mādhavaḥ pāṇḍavaśhchaiva divyau śhaṅkhau pradadhmatuḥ
word_meanings: tataḥ—then; śhvetaiḥ—by white; hayaiḥ—horses; yukte—yoked; mahati—glorious; syandane—chariot; sthitau—seated; mādhavaḥ—Shree Krishna; pāṇḍavaḥ—Arjun; cha—and; eva—also; divyau—Divine; śhaṅkhau—conch shells; pradadhmatuḥ—blew
translation_english: Then Krishna and Arjuna, seated in their splendid chariot drawn by white horses, blew their divine conches.
deep_dive_text: Krishna and Arjuna's conches introduce sacred intentionality. Their response is not louder panic but poised presence.

original_script:
पाञ्चजन्यं हृषीकेशो देवदत्तं धनंजयः।

पौण्ड्रं दध्मौ महाशङ्खं भीमकर्मा वृकोदरः।।1.15।।
transliteration:
pāñchajanyaṁ hṛiṣhīkeśho devadattaṁ dhanañjayaḥ
pauṇḍraṁ dadhmau mahā-śhaṅkhaṁ bhīma-karmā vṛikodaraḥ
word_meanings: pāñchajanyam—the conch named Panchajanya; hṛiṣhīka-īśhaḥ—Shree Krishna; devadattam—the conch named Devadutta; dhanam-jayaḥ—Arjun; pauṇḍram—the conch named Paundra; dadhmau—blew; mahā-śhaṅkham—mighty conch; bhīma-karmā—Bheem; vṛika-udaraḥ—the voracious eater
translation_english: Krishna blew Panchajanya, Arjuna blew Devadatta, and mighty Bhima blew his great conch Paundra.
deep_dive_text: Each conch has identity, suggesting that dharma honors individuality within disciplined action.

original_script:
अनन्तविजयं राजा कुन्तीपुत्रो युधिष्ठिरः।

नकुलः सहदेवश्च सुघोषमणिपुष्पकौ।।1.16।।
transliteration:
anantavijayaṁ rājā kuntī-putro yudhiṣhṭhiraḥ
nakulaḥ sahadevaśhcha sughoṣha-maṇipuṣhpakau
word_meanings: ananta-vijayam—Anantavijay; rājā—king; kuntī-putraḥ—son of Kunti; yudhiṣhṭhiraḥ—Yudhishthir; nakulaḥ—Nakul; sahadevaḥ—Sahadev; cha—and; sughoṣha-maṇipuṣhpakau—the conches Sughosh and Manipushpak
translation_english: King Yudhishthira blew Anantavijaya, while Nakula and Sahadeva blew Sughosha and Manipushpaka.
deep_dive_text: The participation of Yudhishthira, Nakula, and Sahadeva shows alignment across temperament and role. Unity is now audible.

original_script:
काश्यश्च परमेष्वासः शिखण्डी च महारथः।

धृष्टद्युम्नो विराटश्च सात्यकिश्चापराजितः।।1.17।।
transliteration:
kāśhyaśhcha parameṣhvāsaḥ śhikhaṇḍī cha mahā-rathaḥ
dhṛiṣhṭadyumno virāṭaśhcha sātyakiśh chāparājitaḥ
word_meanings: kāśhyaḥ—King of Kashi; parama-iṣhu-āsaḥ—excellent archer; śhikhaṇḍī—Shikhandi; mahā-rathaḥ—mighty warrior; dhṛiṣhṭadyumnaḥ—Dhrishtadyumna; virāṭaḥ—Virat; sātyakiḥ—Satyaki; aparājitaḥ—invincible
translation_english: The king of Kashi, the great archer, Shikhandi, Dhrishtadyumna, Virata, and undefeated Satyaki also blew their conches.
deep_dive_text: This verse broadens the moral coalition. Dharma is not a lone hero narrative but coordinated righteousness.

original_script:
द्रुपदो द्रौपदेयाश्च सर्वशः पृथिवीपते।

सौभद्रश्च महाबाहुः शङ्खान्दध्मुः पृथक्पृथक्।।1.18।।
transliteration:
drupado draupadeyāśhcha sarvaśhaḥ pṛithivī-pate
saubhadraśhcha mahā-bāhuḥ śhaṅkhāndadhmuḥ pṛithak pṛithak
word_meanings: drupadaḥ—Drupad; draupadeyāḥ—sons of Draupadi; pṛithivī-pate—Ruler of the earth; saubhadraḥ—Abhimanyu; mahā-bāhuḥ—mighty-armed; śhaṅkhān—conch shells; pṛithak pṛithak—individually
translation_english: Drupada, the sons of Draupadi, and the strong-armed son of Subhadra all blew their conches from every side.
deep_dive_text: Even allies from different families and generations sound one purpose. Shared conviction turns diversity into strength.

original_script:
स घोषो धार्तराष्ट्राणां हृदयानि व्यदारयत्।

नभश्च पृथिवीं चैव तुमुलो व्यनुनादयन्।।1.19।।
transliteration:
sa ghoṣho dhārtarāṣhṭrāṇāṁ hṛidayāni vyadārayat
nabhaśhcha pṛithivīṁ chaiva tumulo nunādayan
word_meanings: saḥ—that; ghoṣhaḥ—sound; dhārtarāṣhṭrāṇām—of Dhritarashtra’s sons; hṛidayāni—hearts; vyadārayat—shattered; nabhaḥ—the sky; pṛithivīm—the earth; tumulaḥ—terrific sound; abhyanunādayan—thundering
translation_english: That thunderous sound shook earth and sky and pierced the hearts of Dhritarashtra's sons.
deep_dive_text: Psychological warfare begins before physical warfare. Sound penetrates where weapons have not yet reached.

original_script:
अथ व्यवस्थितान् दृष्ट्वा धार्तराष्ट्रान्कपिध्वजः।

प्रवृत्ते शस्त्रसंपाते धनुरुद्यम्य पाण्डवः।।1.20।।
transliteration:
atha vyavasthitān dṛiṣhṭvā dhārtarāṣhṭrān kapi-dhwajaḥ
pravṛitte śhastra-sampāte dhanurudyamya pāṇḍavaḥ
word_meanings: atha—thereupon; vyavasthitān—arrayed; dṛiṣhṭvā—seeing; dhārtarāṣhṭrān—Dhritarashtra’s sons; kapi-dwajaḥ—the Monkey Bannered; pravṛitte—about to commence; śhastra-sampāte—to use weapons; dhanuḥ—bow; udyamya—taking up; pāṇḍavaḥ—Arjun
translation_english: At that moment, Arjuna, whose banner bore Hanuman, lifted his bow and prepared to speak to Krishna.
deep_dive_text: Arjuna's readiness is visible, but inner conflict is approaching. Outer courage and inner clarity are not always synchronized.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच

हृषीकेशं तदा वाक्यमिदमाह महीपते।

सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये रथं स्थापय मेऽच्युत।।1.21।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
senayor ubhayor madhye rathaṁ sthāpaya me ’chyuta
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjun said; senayoḥ—armies; ubhayoḥ—both; madhye—in the middle; ratham—chariot; sthāpaya—place; me—my; achyuta—Shree Krishna, the infallible One
translation_english: Arjuna said: O Achyuta, place my chariot between the two armies.
deep_dive_text: Arjuna's request is deliberate: before fighting, he wants to see clearly. Ethical action begins with direct perception.

original_script:
यावदेतान्निरीक्षेऽहं योद्धुकामानवस्थितान्।

कैर्मया सह योद्धव्यमस्मिन्रणसमुद्यमे।।1.22।।
transliteration:
yāvadetān nirīkṣhe ’haṁ yoddhu-kāmān avasthitān
kairmayā saha yoddhavyam asmin raṇa-samudyame
word_meanings: yāvat—as many as; etān—these; nirīkṣhe—look; aham—I; yoddhu-kāmān—for battle; avasthitān—arrayed; kaiḥ—with whom; mayā—by me; saha—together; yoddhavyam—must fight; asmin—in this; raṇa-samudyame—great combat
translation_english: Let me see those who stand here eager to fight and with whom I must battle in this great war.
deep_dive_text: He asks not for advantage but understanding. Wisdom requires knowing exactly what one is stepping into.

original_script:
योत्स्यमानानवेक्षेऽहं य एतेऽत्र समागताः।

धार्तराष्ट्रस्य दुर्बुद्धेर्युद्धे प्रियचिकीर्षवः।।1.23।।
transliteration:
yotsyamānān avekṣhe ’haṁ ya ete ’tra samāgatāḥ
dhārtarāṣhṭrasya durbuddher yuddhe priya-chikīrṣhavaḥ
word_meanings: yotsyamānān—those who come to fight; avekṣhe aham—I desire to see; ye—who; ete—those; atra—here; samāgatāḥ—assembled; dhārtarāṣhṭrasya—of Dhritarashtra’s son; durbuddheḥ—evil-minded; yuddhe—in the fight; priya-chikīrṣhavaḥ—wishing to please
translation_english: I wish to observe those gathered here to please the evil-minded son of Dhritarashtra.
deep_dive_text: This verse exposes motive analysis: Arjuna wants to understand who is driven by loyalty, fear, or ambition.

original_script:
संजय उवाच

एवमुक्तो हृषीकेशो गुडाकेशेन भारत।

सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये स्थापयित्वा रथोत्तमम्।।1.24।।
transliteration:
sañjaya uvācha
evam ukto hṛiṣhīkeśho guḍākeśhena bhārata
senayor ubhayor madhye sthāpayitvā rathottamam
word_meanings: sañjayaḥ uvācha—Sanjay said; evam—thus; uktaḥ—addressed; hṛiṣhīkeśhaḥ—Shree Krishna; guḍākeśhena—by Arjun; bhārata—descendant of Bharat; senayoḥ—armies; ubhayoḥ—the two; madhye—between; sthāpayitvā—having drawn; ratha-uttamam—magnificent chariot
translation_english: Sanjaya said: Addressed by Arjuna, Krishna drove the excellent chariot to the space between both armies.
deep_dive_text: Krishna complies without argument, honoring Arjuna's process. True guidance often begins by creating the right vantage point.

original_script:
भीष्मद्रोणप्रमुखतः सर्वेषां च महीक्षिताम्।

उवाच पार्थ पश्यैतान्समवेतान्कुरूनिति।।1.25।।
transliteration:
bhīṣhma-droṇa-pramukhataḥ sarveṣhāṁ cha mahī-kṣhitām
uvācha pārtha paśhyaitān samavetān kurūn iti
word_meanings: bhīṣhma—Grandsire Bheeshma; droṇa—Dronacharya; pramukhataḥ—in the presence; sarveṣhām—all; cha—and; mahī-kṣhitām—other kings; uvācha—said; pārtha—Arjun; paśhya—behold; etān—these; samavetān—gathered; kurūn—descendants of Kuru; iti—thus
translation_english: Before Bhishma, Drona, and all the kings, Krishna said: O Partha, behold these Kurus assembled for battle.
deep_dive_text: Krishna's word 'behold' is diagnostic and spiritual. Seeing fully is the first medicine for confusion.

original_script:
तत्रापश्यत्स्थितान्पार्थः पितॄनथ पितामहान्।

आचार्यान्मातुलान्भ्रातॄन्पुत्रान्पौत्रान्सखींस्तथा।।1.26।।
transliteration:
tatrāpaśhyat sthitān pārthaḥ pitṝīn atha pitāmahān
āchāryān mātulān bhrātṝīn putrān pautrān sakhīṁs tathā
word_meanings: tatra—there; apaśhyat—saw; sthitān—stationed; pārthaḥ—Arjun; pitṝīn—fathers; atha—thereafter; pitāmahān—grandfathers; āchāryān—teachers; mātulān—maternal uncles; bhrātṝīn—brothers; putrān—sons; pautrān—grandsons; sakhīn—friends; tathā—also
translation_english: There Arjuna saw fathers, grandfathers, teachers, uncles, brothers, sons, grandsons, and friends on both sides.
deep_dive_text: Arjuna's battlefield transforms into a family map. Abstract duty collapses into personal memory.

original_script:
श्वशुरान्सुहृदश्चैव सेनयोरुभयोरपि।

तान्समीक्ष्य स कौन्तेयः सर्वान्बन्धूनवस्थितान्।।1.27।।
transliteration:
śhvaśhurān suhṛidaśh chaiva senayor ubhayor api
tān samīkṣhya sa kaunteyaḥ sarvān bandhūn avasthitān
word_meanings: śhvaśhurān—fathers-in-law; suhṛidaḥ—well-wishers; cha—and; eva—indeed; senayoḥ—armies; ubhayoḥ—in both; api—also; tān—these; samīkṣhya—on seeing; saḥ—he; kaunteyaḥ—Arjun; sarvān—all; bandhūn—relatives; avasthitān—present
translation_english: Seeing all his relatives arrayed there, Arjuna was overcome with deep compassion and sorrow.
deep_dive_text: Compassion here is real, but it is mixed with paralysis. The Gita will teach how to purify compassion into wise action.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच

कृपया परयाऽऽविष्टो विषीदन्निदमब्रवीत्।

दृष्ट्वेमं स्वजनं कृष्ण युयुत्सुं समुपस्थितम्।।1.28।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
kṛipayā parayāviṣhṭo viṣhīdann idam abravīt
dṛiṣhṭvemaṁ sva-janaṁ kṛiṣhṇa yuyutsuṁ samupasthitam
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjun said; kṛipayā—by compassion; parayā—great; āviṣhṭaḥ—overwhelmed; viṣhīdan—grieving; idam—this; abravīt—spoke; dṛiṣhṭvā—on seeing; imam—these; sva-janam—kinsmen; kṛiṣhṇa—Krishna; yuyutsum—eager to fight; samupasthitam—present
translation_english: Arjuna said: O Krishna, seeing my own people eager to fight, my limbs fail and my mouth dries up.
deep_dive_text: The body registers what the intellect cannot yet process. Spiritual crisis often appears first as physical disturbance.

original_script:
सीदन्ति मम गात्राणि मुखं च परिशुष्यति।

वेपथुश्च शरीरे मे रोमहर्षश्च जायते।।1.29।।
transliteration:
sīdanti mama gātrāṇi mukhaṁ cha pariśhuṣhyati
vepathuśh cha śharīre me roma-harṣhaśh cha jāyate
word_meanings:
sīdanti—quivering; mama—my; gātrāṇi—limbs; mukham—mouth; cha—and; pariśhuṣhyati—is drying up
vepathuḥ—shuddering; cha—and; śharīre—on the body; me—my; roma-harṣhaḥ—standing of bodily hair on end; cha—also; jāyate—is happening
translation_english: My body trembles, my hair stands on end, and my skin burns.
deep_dive_text: Somatic symptoms intensify: trembling, burning, shock. The verse captures authentic human vulnerability.

original_script:
गाण्डीवं स्रंसते हस्तात्त्वक्चैव परिदह्यते।

न च शक्नोम्यवस्थातुं भ्रमतीव च मे मनः।।1.30।।
transliteration:
gāṇḍīvaṁ sraṁsate hastāt tvak chaiva paridahyate
na cha śhaknomy avasthātuṁ bhramatīva cha me manaḥ
word_meanings: gāṇḍīvam—Arjun’s bow; sraṁsate—is slipping; hastāt—from (my) hand; tvak—skin; cha—and; eva—indeed; paridahyate—is burning all over; na—not; cha—and; śhaknomi—am able; avasthātum—remain steady; bhramati iva—whirling like; cha—and; me—my; manaḥ—mind
translation_english: My bow Gandiva slips from my hand, my mind reels, and I can no longer stand steady.
deep_dive_text: Loss of grip on Gandiva symbolizes loss of role-confidence. Identity crisis often follows moral conflict.

original_script:
निमित्तानि च पश्यामि विपरीतानि केशव।

न च श्रेयोऽनुपश्यामि हत्वा स्वजनमाहवे।।1.31।।
transliteration:
nimittāni cha paśhyāmi viparītāni keśhava
na cha śhreyo ’nupaśhyāmi hatvā sva-janam āhave
word_meanings: nimittāni—omens; cha—and; paśhyāmi—I see; viparītāni—misfortune; keśhava—Shree Krishna; na—not; cha—also; śhreyaḥ—good; anupaśhyāmi—I foresee; hatvā—from killing; sva-janam—kinsmen; āhave—in battle
translation_english: I see inauspicious omens, O Keshava; I see no good in killing my own people in battle.
deep_dive_text: Arjuna's moral instinct rejects violence against kin, but he has not yet integrated duty and compassion. This tension drives the dialogue.

original_script:
न काङ्क्षे विजयं कृष्ण न च राज्यं सुखानि च।

किं नो राज्येन गोविन्द किं भोगैर्जीवितेन वा।।1.32।।
transliteration:
na kāṅkṣhe vijayaṁ kṛiṣhṇa na cha rājyaṁ sukhāni cha
kiṁ no rājyena govinda kiṁ bhogair jīvitena vā
word_meanings: na—nor; kāṅkṣhe—do I desire; vijayam—victory; kṛiṣhṇa—Krishna; na—nor; cha—as well; rājyam—kingdom; sukhāni—happiness; cha—also; kim—what; naḥ—to us; rājyena—by kingdom; govinda—Krishna; kim—what?; bhogaiḥ—pleasures; jīvitena—life; vā—or
translation_english: I do not desire victory, kingdom, or pleasures, O Krishna.
deep_dive_text: Pleasure and power lose meaning when conscience is unsettled. Desire collapses in the face of ethical pain.

original_script:
येषामर्थे काङ्क्षितं नो राज्यं भोगाः सुखानि च।

त इमेऽवस्थिता युद्धे प्राणांस्त्यक्त्वा धनानि च।।1.33।।
transliteration:
yeṣhām arthe kāṅkṣhitaṁ no rājyaṁ bhogāḥ sukhāni cha
ta ime ’vasthitā yuddhe prāṇāṁs tyaktvā dhanāni cha
word_meanings: yeṣhām—for whose; arthe—sake; kāṅkṣhitam—coveted for; naḥ—by us; rājyam—kingdom; bhogāḥ—pleasures; sukhāni—happiness; cha—also; te—they; ime—these; avasthitāḥ—situated; yuddhe—for battle; prāṇān—lives; tyaktvā—giving up; dhanāni—wealth; cha—also
translation_english: What use is kingdom or enjoyment to us when those for whom we desire them stand here ready to die?
deep_dive_text: Arjuna questions the very purpose of achievement. Ends cannot justify means when the means destroy what gives the end value.

original_script:
आचार्याः पितरः पुत्रास्तथैव च पितामहाः।

मातुलाः श्चशुराः पौत्राः श्यालाः सम्बन्धिनस्तथा।।1.34।।
transliteration:
āchāryāḥ pitaraḥ putrās tathaiva cha pitāmahāḥ
mātulāḥ śhvaśhurāḥ pautrāḥ śhyālāḥ sambandhinas tathā
word_meanings: āchāryāḥ—teachers; pitaraḥ—fathers; putrāḥ—sons; tathā—as well; eva—indeed; cha—also; pitāmahāḥ—grandfathers; mātulāḥ—maternal uncles; śhvaśhurāḥ—fathers-in-law; pautrāḥ—grandsons; śhyālāḥ—brothers-in-law; sambandhinaḥ—kinsmen
translation_english: Teachers, fathers, sons, grandfathers, uncles, in-laws, grandsons, and many relatives stand here in battle.
deep_dive_text: By naming relationships, he personalizes the cost of war. Dharma cannot be discussed without acknowledging relational consequence.

original_script:
एतान्न हन्तुमिच्छामि घ्नतोऽपि मधुसूदन।

अपि त्रैलोक्यराज्यस्य हेतोः किं नु महीकृते।।1.35।।
transliteration:
etān na hantum ichchhāmi ghnato ’pi madhusūdana
api trailokya-rājyasya hetoḥ kiṁ nu mahī-kṛite
word_meanings: etān—these; na—not; hantum—to slay; ichchhāmi—I wish; ghnataḥ—killed; api—even though; madhusūdana—Shree Krishna; api—even though; trai-lokya-rājyasya—dominion over three worlds; hetoḥ—for sake of; mahī-kṛite—for the earth
translation_english: Even for sovereignty over the three worlds, I do not wish to kill them, what to speak of ruling this earth.
deep_dive_text: Arjuna rejects utilitarian victory. Moral boundaries remain even under maximum possible reward.

original_script:
निहत्य धार्तराष्ट्रान्नः का प्रीतिः स्याज्जनार्दन।

पापमेवाश्रयेदस्मान्हत्वैतानाततायिनः।।1.36।।
transliteration:
nihatya dhārtarāṣhṭrān naḥ kā prītiḥ syāj janārdana
pāpam evāśhrayed asmān hatvaitān ātatāyinaḥ
word_meanings: nihatya—by killing; dhārtarāṣhṭrān—sons of Dhritarashtra; naḥ—our; kā—what; prītiḥ—pleasure; syāt—will there be; janārdana—Shree Krishna; pāpam—vices; eva—certainly; āśhrayet—must come upon; asmān—us; hatvā—by killing; etān—all these; ātatāyinaḥ—aggressors
translation_english: By killing Dhritarashtra's sons, sin alone will come upon us, even though they are aggressors.
deep_dive_text: He fears inner corruption more than external loss. The verse reframes success as purity of action, not mere outcome.

original_script:
तस्मान्नार्हा वयं हन्तुं धार्तराष्ट्रान्स्वबान्धवान्।

स्वजनं हि कथं हत्वा सुखिनः स्याम माधव।।1.37।।
transliteration:
tasmān nārhā vayaṁ hantuṁ dhārtarāṣhṭrān sa-bāndhavān 
sva-janaṁ hi kathaṁ hatvā sukhinaḥ syāma mādhava
word_meanings: tasmāt—hence; na—never; arhāḥ—behoove; vayam—we; hantum—to kill; dhārtarāṣhṭrān—the sons of Dhritarashtra; sva-bāndhavān—along with friends; sva-janam—kinsmen; hi—certainly; katham—how; hatvā—by killing; sukhinaḥ—happy; syāma—will we become; mādhava—Shree Krishna
translation_english: Therefore we should not kill our own kinsmen; how can we be happy after slaying our own people?
deep_dive_text: This is a direct ethics question: can happiness be built on betrayal of one's own? Arjuna's answer is no.

original_script:
यद्यप्येते न पश्यन्ति लोभोपहतचेतसः।

कुलक्षयकृतं दोषं मित्रद्रोहे च पातकम्।।1.38।।
transliteration:
yady apy ete na paśhyanti lobhopahata-chetasaḥ
kula-kṣhaya-kṛitaṁ doṣhaṁ mitra-drohe cha pātakam
word_meanings: yadi api—even though; ete—they; na—not; paśhyanti—see; lobha—greed; upahata—overpowered; chetasaḥ—thoughts; kula-kṣhaya-kṛitam—in annihilating relatives; doṣham—fault; mitra-drohe—to wreak treachery upon friends; cha—and; pātakam—sin
translation_english: Though their minds are overpowered by greed and they see no wrong in destroying families, we should know better.
deep_dive_text: Arjuna distinguishes between their blindness and his responsibility. Dharma does not imitate adharma, even when provoked.

original_script:
कथं न ज्ञेयमस्माभिः पापादस्मान्निवर्तितुम्।

कुलक्षयकृतं दोषं प्रपश्यद्भिर्जनार्दन।।1.39।।
transliteration:
kathaṁ na jñeyam asmābhiḥ pāpād asmān nivartitum
kula-kṣhaya-kṛitaṁ doṣhaṁ prapaśhyadbhir janārdana
word_meanings: katham—why; na—not; jñeyam—should be known; asmābhiḥ—we; pāpāt—from sin; asmāt—these; nivartitum—to turn away; kula-kṣhaya—killing kindered; kṛitam—done; doṣham—crime; prapaśhyadbhiḥ—who can see; janārdana—Shree Krishna
translation_english: O Janardana, since we clearly see the evil in family destruction, why should we not turn away from this sin?
deep_dive_text: Knowledge creates duty. Seeing clearly and still acting wrongly becomes a deeper fall.

original_script:
कुलक्षये प्रणश्यन्ति कुलधर्माः सनातनाः।

धर्मे नष्टे कुलं कृत्स्नमधर्मोऽभिभवत्युत।।1.40।।
transliteration:
kula-kṣhaye praṇaśhyanti kula-dharmāḥ sanātanāḥ
dharme naṣhṭe kulaṁ kṛitsnam adharmo ’bhibhavaty uta
word_meanings: kula-kṣhaye—destruction of a dynasty; praṇaśhyanti—are vanquished; kula-dharmāḥ—family traditions; sanātanāḥ—eternal; dharme—religion; naṣhṭe—is destroyed; kulam—family; kṛitsnam—the whole; adharmaḥ—irreligion; abhibhavati—overcome
translation_english: With the destruction of a family, its eternal traditions perish; when dharma is lost, adharma prevails.
deep_dive_text: Family dharma is presented as social infrastructure. When it collapses, disorder is not immediate chaos but gradual decay.

original_script:
अधर्माभिभवात्कृष्ण प्रदुष्यन्ति कुलस्त्रियः।

स्त्रीषु दुष्टासु वार्ष्णेय जायते वर्णसङ्करः।।1.41।।
transliteration:
adharmābhibhavāt kṛiṣhṇa praduṣhyanti kula-striyaḥ
strīṣhu duṣhṭāsu vārṣhṇeya jāyate varṇa-saṅkaraḥ
word_meanings: adharma—deviation from cosmic order; abhibhavāt—preponderance; kṛiṣhṇa—Shree Krishna; praduṣhyanti—become unmoored from duty; kula-striyaḥ—women of the lineage; strīṣhu—of women; duṣhṭāsu—become unmoored; vārṣhṇeya—descendant of Vrishni; jāyate—are born; varṇa-saṅkaraḥ—breakdown of social structures/Varnashrama
translation_english: When deviation from the cosmic order prevails, the lineage is corrupted, and the breakdown of the foundational social structure (Varnashrama) arises.
deep_dive_text: Arjuna expresses a historical concern regarding generational stability. In the Iron Age context, 'varṇa-saṅkaraḥ' refers specifically to the collapse of the social and ethical inheritance system that maintained civilizational continuity.

original_script:
सङ्करो नरकायैव कुलघ्नानां कुलस्य च।

पतन्ति पितरो ह्येषां लुप्तपिण्डोदकक्रियाः।।1.42।।
transliteration:
saṅkaro narakāyaiva kula-ghnānāṁ kulasya cha
patanti pitaro hy eṣhāṁ lupta-piṇḍodaka-kriyāḥ
word_meanings: saṅkaraḥ—unwanted children; narakāya—hellish; eva—indeed; kula-ghnānām—for family destroyers; kulasya—of the family; cha—also; patanti—fall; pitaraḥ—ancestors; hi—verily; eṣhām—their; lupta—deprived; piṇḍodaka-kriyāḥ—sacrificial offerings
translation_english: Such disorder leads both the destroyers of the family and the family itself toward downfall, and the ancestors are deprived of offerings.
deep_dive_text: The verse links personal sin to ancestral disruption. In Vedic thought, duty is trans-generational, not individualistic.

original_script:
दोषैरेतैः कुलघ्नानां वर्णसङ्करकारकैः।

उत्साद्यन्ते जातिधर्माः कुलधर्माश्च शाश्वताः।।1.43।।
transliteration:
doṣhair etaiḥ kula-ghnānāṁ varṇa-saṅkara-kārakaiḥ
utsādyante jāti-dharmāḥ kula-dharmāśh cha śhāśhvatāḥ
word_meanings: doṣhaiḥ—through evil deeds; etaiḥ—these; kula-ghnānām—of those who destroy family; varṇa-saṅkara—unwanted progeny; kārakaiḥ—causing; utsādyante—are ruined; jāti-dharmāḥ—social welfare; kula-dharmāḥ—family traditions; śhāśhvatāḥ—eternal
translation_english: Through these faults, eternal family duties and social order are destroyed.
deep_dive_text: Adharma spreads structurally when institutions fail. Arjuna recognizes that private violence has public consequences.

original_script:
उत्सन्नकुलधर्माणां मनुष्याणां जनार्दन।

नरकेऽनियतं वासो भवतीत्यनुशुश्रुम।।1.44।।
transliteration:
utsanna-kula-dharmāṇāṁ manuṣhyāṇāṁ janārdana
narake ‘niyataṁ vāso bhavatītyanuśhuśhruma
word_meanings: utsanna—destroyed; kula-dharmāṇām—whose traditions; manuṣhyāṇām—of such human beings; janārdana—Shree Krishna; narake—in hell; aniyatam—indefinite; vāsaḥ—dwell; bhavati—is; iti—thus; anuśhuśhruma—I have heard
translation_english: We have heard that those whose family traditions are ruined dwell in hell for a long time.
deep_dive_text: Tradition here is not blind ritual but civilizational memory. Destroying it produces long-term spiritual homelessness.

original_script:
अहो बत महत्पापं कर्तुं व्यवसिता वयम्।

यद्राज्यसुखलोभेन हन्तुं स्वजनमुद्यताः।।1.45।।
transliteration:
aho bata mahat pāpaṁ kartuṁ vyavasitā vayam
yad rājya-sukha-lobhena hantuṁ sva-janam udyatāḥ
word_meanings: aho—alas; bata—how; mahat—great; pāpam—sins; kartum—to perform; vyavasitāḥ—decided; vayam—we; rājya-sukha-lobhena—desire for pleasure; hantum—to kill; sva-janam—kinsmen; udyatāḥ—intending
translation_english: Alas, we are preparing to commit great sin, driven by desire for royal pleasure and power.
deep_dive_text: Arjuna's lament shows conscience awakening under pressure. This painful honesty opens the door to transformation.

original_script:
यदि मामप्रतीकारमशस्त्रं शस्त्रपाणयः।

धार्तराष्ट्रा रणे हन्युस्तन्मे क्षेमतरं भवेत्।।1.46।।
transliteration:
yadi mām apratīkāram aśhastraṁ śhastra-pāṇayaḥ
dhārtarāṣhṭrā raṇe hanyus tan me kṣhemataraṁ bhavet
word_meanings: yadi—if; mām—me; apratīkāram—unresisting; aśhastram—unarmed; śhastra-pāṇayaḥ—those with weapons; dhārtarāṣhṭrāḥ—sons of Dhritarashtra; raṇe—battlefield; hanyuḥ—kill; kṣhema-taram—better; bhavet—would be
translation_english: It would be better for me if the sons of Dhritarashtra kill me unresisting and unarmed on the battlefield.
deep_dive_text: He prefers personal loss over ethically compromised action. Refusal to retaliate can be moral strength, not weakness.

original_script:
सञ्जय उवाच

एवमुक्त्वाऽर्जुनः संख्ये रथोपस्थ उपाविशत्।

विसृज्य सशरं चापं शोकसंविग्नमानसः।।1.47।।
transliteration:
sañjaya uvācha
evam uktvārjunaḥ saṅkhye rathopastha upāviśhat
visṛijya sa-śharaṁ chāpaṁ śhoka-saṁvigna-mānasaḥ
word_meanings: sañjayaḥ uvācha—Sanjay said; evam uktvā—speaking thus; arjunaḥ—Arjun; saṅkhye—battlefield; ratha upasthe—on the chariot; upāviśhat—sat; visṛijya—casting aside; sa-śharam—arrows; chāpam—the bow; śhoka—with grief; saṁvigna—distressed; mānasaḥ—mind
translation_english: Sanjaya said: Speaking thus, Arjuna cast aside his bow and arrows and sat down in the chariot, his mind overwhelmed by grief.
deep_dive_text: Action halts; despair peaks. This collapse is the necessary threshold before true wisdom enters.

original_script: अर्जुनविषादयोगः
transliteration: Arjuna Viṣhāda Yogaḥ
translation_english: Chapter 1: The Crisis of Identity
deep_dive_text: The narrative begins with a profound crisis of conscience. On the battlefield of Kurukshetra, the preeminent warrior Arjuna is paralyzed by the moral weight of fighting his own kin. This chapter represents the 'dark night of the soul'—the state of absolute vulnerability (śaraṇāgati) that precedes the transmission of higher wisdom. Historically, it establishes the conflict as one of 'Dharma' (righteousness) versus 'Adharma' (moral decay).

original_script: अथ द्वितीयोऽध्यायः - साङ्ख्ययोगः
transliteration: Sāṅkhya Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Sāṅkhya—analytical deduction; Yogaḥ—the path of synthesis.
translation_english: Chapter 2 Summary: The Yoga of Analytical Knowledge
deep_dive_text: This chapter serves as the intellectual synthesis of the Samkhya (analytical) and Yoga (practical) traditions. Krishna introduces the indestructible nature of the 'Atman' (Self) versus the 'Deha' (Body), providing a metaphysical framework for action. It establishes 'Samatvam' (equanimity) as the benchmark for wisdom, transitioning Arjuna from a state of paralysis to a state of detached, dharmic engagement.

original_script:
सञ्जय उवाच

तं तथा कृपयाऽविष्टमश्रुपूर्णाकुलेक्षणम्।

विषीदन्तमिदं वाक्यमुवाच मधुसूदनः।।2.1।।
transliteration:
sañjaya uvācha
taṁ tathā kṛipayāviṣhṭamaśhru pūrṇākulekṣhaṇam
viṣhīdantamidaṁ vākyam uvācha madhusūdanaḥ
word_meanings: sañjayaḥ uvācha—Sanjay said; tam—to him; tathā—thus; kṛipayā—with pity; āviṣhṭam—overwhelmed; aśhru-pūrṇa—full of tears; ākula—distressed; īkṣhaṇam—eyes; viṣhīdantam—grief-stricken; idam—these; vākyam—words; uvācha—said; madhusūdanaḥ—Shree Krishna
translation_english: Sanjaya said: To Arjuna, overwhelmed by pity and sorrow, with tearful eyes and dejected heart, Krishna spoke.
deep_dive_text: Sanjaya paints a vivid picture of Arjuna’s state—his eyes are blurred with tears. Krishna is referred to here as 'Madhusudana' (the slayer of the demon Madhu). This is a subtle hint that Krishna is about to slay the 'demon' of doubt and delusion inside Arjuna’s mind.

original_script:
श्री भगवानुवाच

कुतस्त्वा कश्मलमिदं विषमे समुपस्थितम्।

अनार्यजुष्टमस्वर्ग्यमकीर्तिकरमर्जुन।।2.2।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
kutastvā kaśhmalamidaṁ viṣhame samupasthitam
anārya-juṣhṭamaswargyam akīrti-karam arjuna
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; kutaḥ—wherefrom; tvā—to you; kaśhmalam—delusion; idam—this; viṣhame—hour of peril; samupasthitam—overcome; anārya—crude person; juṣhṭam—practiced; aswargyam—not leading to higher abodes; akīrti-karam—leading to disgrace; arjuna—Arjun
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: From where has this weakness arisen in this critical hour? It is unworthy, dishonorable, and does not lead to higher life.
deep_dive_text: Krishna’s initial response is a sharp challenge to Arjuna's warrior identity. He uses the term 'Kaśhmalam' (delusion/impurity) to suggest that Arjuna’s grief is not high-minded compassion, but a 'hṛidaya-daurbalyam' (weakness of heart) that is 'anārya-juṣhṭam' (unworthy of a noble person). This sets the stage for a lecture on the distinction between the temporary body and the eternal Self.

original_script:
क्लैब्यं मा स्म गमः पार्थ नैतत्त्वय्युपपद्यते।

क्षुद्रं हृदयदौर्बल्यं त्यक्त्वोत्तिष्ठ परन्तप।।2.3।।
transliteration:
klaibyaṁ mā sma gamaḥ pārtha naitat tvayyupapadyate
kṣhudraṁ hṛidaya-daurbalyaṁ tyaktvottiṣhṭha parantapa
word_meanings: klaibyam—unmanliness; mā sma—do not; gamaḥ—yield to; pārtha—Arjun; na—not; etat—this; tvayi—to you; upapadyate—befitting; kṣhudram—petty; hṛidaya—heart; daurbalyam—weakness; tyaktvā—giving up; uttiṣhṭha—arise; param-tapa—conqueror of enemies
translation_english: Do not yield to this unmanliness, O Partha. Cast off this petty weakness of heart and arise, O scorcher of foes.
deep_dive_text: This is one of the most powerful 'motivational' verses in the Gita. Krishna calls Arjuna's state 'Klaibyam' (impotence or unmanliness). He is challenging Arjuna’s identity as a warrior. The command 'Uttishtha' (Arise!) is a call to action—not just physically standing up, but rising above his mental confusion.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच

कथं भीष्ममहं संख्ये द्रोणच मधुसूदन।

इषुभिः प्रतियोत्स्यामि पूजार्हावरिसूदन।।2.4।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
kathaṁ bhīṣhmam ahaṁ saṅkhye droṇaṁ cha madhusūdana
iṣhubhiḥ pratīyotsyāmi pūjārhāv arisūdana
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjun said; katham—how; bhīṣhmam—Bhishma; aham—I; saṅkhye—in battle; droṇam—Drona; cha—and; madhusūdana—Krishna; iṣhubhiḥ—with arrows; pratiyotsyāmi—shall fight; pūjā-arhau—worthy of worship; ari-sūdana—slayer of enemies
translation_english: Arjuna said: O Madhusudana, how can I fight with arrows in battle against men like Bhishma and Drona, who are worthy of my worship?
deep_dive_text: Arjuna pushes back against Krishna's rebuke. He points out a genuine moral dilemma: his opponents aren't just 'enemies,' they are his elders and gurus. To shoot arrows at someone whose feet you should be touching is a psychological barrier Arjuna cannot yet cross.

original_script:
गुरूनहत्वा हि महानुभावान्
श्रेयो भोक्तुं भैक्ष्यमपीह लोके।
हत्वार्थकामांस्तु गुरूनिहैव
भुञ्जीय भोगान्रुधिरप्रदिग्धान्।।2.5।।
transliteration:
gurūn ahatvā hi mahānubhāvān
śhreyo bhoktuṁ bhaikṣhyam apīha loke
hatvārtha-kāmāṁs tu gurūn ihaiva
bhuñjīya bhogān rudhira-pradigdhān
word_meanings: gurūn—teachers; ahatvā—not killing; hi—certainly; mahā-anubhāvān—noble; śhreyaḥ—better; bhoktum—to enjoy; bhaikṣhyam—begging; api—even; iha loke—in this world; hatvā—killing; artha—gain; kāmān—desiring; tu—but; gurūn—teachers; iha—in this world; eva—certainly; bhuñjīya—would enjoy; bhogān—wealth and pleasures; rudhira—with blood; pradigdhān—tainted
translation_english: It would be better to live in this world by begging than to enjoy life by killing these noble teachers. If I kill them, any wealth or pleasure I enjoy will be tainted with their blood.
deep_dive_text: Arjuna is choosing poverty over what he perceives as a 'bloody' success. This shows that he is driven by a high sense of morality, but Krishna will eventually show him that 'inaction' can sometimes be more harmful than 'action'.

original_script:
न चैतद्विद्मः कतरन्नो गरीयो
यद्वा जयेम यदि वा नो जयेयुः।
यानेव हत्वा न जिजीविषाम-
स्तेऽवस्थिताः प्रमुखे धार्तराष्ट्राः।।2.6।।
transliteration:
na chaitad vidmaḥ kataran no garīyo
yad vā jayema yadi vā no jayeyuḥ
yān eva hatvā na jijīviṣhāmas
te ’vasthitāḥ pramukhe dhārtarāṣhṭrāḥ
word_meanings: na—not; cha—also; etat—this; vidmaḥ—we know; katarat—which; naḥ—for us; garīyaḥ—better; yat vā—whether; jayema—we might conquer; yadi vā—or if; naḥ—us; jayeyuḥ—they might conquer; yān—whom; eva—certainly; hatvā—by killing; na—not; jijīviṣhāmaḥ—we desire to live; te—they; avasthitāḥ—standing; pramukhe—before us; dhārtarāṣhṭrāḥ—the sons of Dhritarashtra
translation_english: I do not even know which is better—conquering them or being conquered by them. The very people we do not wish to live without are standing before us ready for war.
deep_dive_text: This is a moment of total uncertainty. Arjuna is questioning the value of victory itself. If the people you love are dead, who are you winning for? He is trapped in a logical loop where every outcome seems like a loss.

original_script:
कार्पण्यदोषोपहतस्वभावः
पृच्छामि त्वां धर्मसम्मूढचेताः।
यच्छ्रेयः स्यान्निश्चितं ब्रूहि तन्मे
शिष्यस्तेऽहं शाधि मां त्वां प्रपन्नम्।।2.7।।
transliteration:
kārpaṇya-doṣhopahata-svabhāvaḥ
pṛichchhāmi tvāṁ dharma-sammūḍha-chetāḥ
yach chhreyaḥ syān niśhchitaṁ brūhi tan me
śhiṣhyas te ’haṁ śhādhi māṁ tvāṁ prapannam
word_meanings: kārpaṇya—cowardice; doṣha—flaw; upahata—overpowered; sva-bhāvaḥ—nature; pṛichchhāmi—I ask; tvām—you; dharma—duty; sammūḍha—confused; chetāḥ—mind; yat—what; śhreyaḥ—good; syāt—may be; niśhchitam—decisively; brūhi—tell; tat—that; me—to me; śhiṣhyaḥ—disciple; te—your; aham—I am; śhādhi—instruct; mām—me; tvām—to you; prapannam—surrendered
translation_english: I am weighed down by a sense of helpless cowardice; my mind is confused about my duty. I beg you to tell me clearly what is best for me. I am now your disciple; please instruct me, for I have surrendered to you.
deep_dive_text: This is the 'Pivot Point' of the Gita. Arjuna stops arguing and starts listening. He realizes his own intellect has failed him and asks Krishna to be his Guru. True wisdom can only enter when the ego admits it doesn't have the answers.

original_script:
न हि प्रपश्यामि ममापनुद्या-
द्यच्छोकमुच्छोषणमिन्द्रियाणाम्।
अवाप्य भूमावसपत्नमृद्धं
राज्यं सुराणामपि चाधिपत्यम्।।2.8।।
transliteration:
na hi prapaśhyāmi mamāpanudyād
yach chhokam uchchhoṣhaṇam indriyāṇām
avāpyā bhūmāvasapatnam ṛiddhaṁ
rājyaṁ surāṇām api chādhipatyam
word_meanings: na—not; hi—certainly; prapaśhyāmi—I see; mama—my; apanudyāt—can drive away; yat—which; śhokam—sorrow; uchchhoṣhaṇam—drying up; indriyāṇām—of the senses; avāpya—attaining; bhūmau—on earth; asapatnam—unrivaled; ṛiddham—prosperous; rājyam—kingdom; surāṇām—of the celestial gods; api—even; cha—also; adhipatyam—sovereignty
translation_english: I can find no means to drive away this grief which is drying up my senses. I would not be able to dispel it even if I won a prosperous, unrivaled kingdom on earth or sovereignty like that of the gods.
deep_dive_text: Arjuna admits that material success cannot heal a spiritual wound. He realizes that even becoming the king of the world or a god wouldn't provide inner peace. This is a crucial realization for any seeker: external achievements are no substitute for internal clarity.

original_script:
सञ्जय उवाच
एवमुक्त्वा हृषीकेशं गुडाकेशः परन्तप।
न योत्स्य इति गोविन्दमुक्त्वा तूष्णीं बभूव ह।।2.9।।
transliteration:
sañjaya uvācha
evam uktvā hṛiṣhīkeśhaṁ guḍākeśhaḥ parantapa
na yotsya iti govindam uktvā tūṣhṇīṁ babhūva ha
word_meanings: sañjayaḥ uvācha—Sanjay said; evam—thus; uktvā—having spoken; hṛiṣhīkeśham—to Krishna; guḍākeśhaḥ—Arjuna, the conqueror of sleep; parantapa—the scorcher of enemies; na yotsye—I shall not fight; iti—thus; govindam—to Krishna; uktvā—having said; tūṣhṇīm—silent; babhūva—became
translation_english: Sanjaya said: Having spoken thus, Arjuna, the chastiser of enemies, told Krishna, 'I shall not fight,' and became silent.
deep_dive_text: Arjuna's final act of defiance before surrendering. He draws a hard line, but his silence afterward shows he is exhausted and ready to be led. For the user, this represents the 'point of total surrender'.

original_script:
तमुवाच हृषीकेशः प्रहसन्निव भारत।
सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये विषीदन्तमिदं वचः।।2.10।।
transliteration:
tam uvācha hṛiṣhīkeśhaḥ prahasann iva bhārata
senayor ubhayor madhye viṣhīdantam idaṁ vachaḥ
word_meanings: tam—to him; uvācha—said; hṛiṣhīkeśhaḥ—Krishna; prahasan—smiling; iva—as if; bhārata—descendant of Bharat; senayoḥ—of the armies; ubhayoḥ—of both; madhye—in the midst; viṣhīdantam—to the grief-stricken one; idam—this; vachaḥ—words
translation_english: O Dhritarashtra, thereafter, in the midst of both armies, Krishna, smiling as it were, spoke these words to the grief-stricken Arjuna.
deep_dive_text: Krishna's smile is significant. He isn't mocking Arjuna; he is smiling because he sees the 'play' of delusion and knows he is about to dispel it. His smile contrasts with Arjuna's tears, representing the difference between a master of the world and a victim of circumstances.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
अशोच्यानन्वशोचस्त्वं प्रज्ञावादांश्च भाषसे।
गतासूनगतासूंश्च नानुशोचन्ति पण्डिताः।।2.11।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
aśhochyān anvaśhochas tvaṁ prajñā-vādāṁśh cha bhāṣhase
gatāsūn agatāsūṁśh cha nānuśhochanti paṇḍitāḥ
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; aśhochyān—not worthy of grief; anvaśhochaḥ—you are mourning; tvam—you; prajñā-vādān—learned words; cha—and; bhāṣhase—speaking; gata—gone; asūn—life; agata—not gone; asūn—life; cha—and; na—never; anuśhochanti—lament; paṇḍitāḥ—the wise
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: While speaking learned words, you are mourning for what is not worthy of grief. The wise lament neither for the living nor for the dead.
deep_dive_text: Krishna begins his lecture by exposing Arjuna's hypocrisy. Arjuna was using 'high-level' moral arguments to justify a decision based on fear. Krishna points out that if Arjuna truly understood life and death, he wouldn't be grieving in the first place.

original_script:
न त्वेवाहं जातु नासं न त्वं नेमे जनाधिपाः।
न चैव न भविष्यामः सर्वे वयमतः परम्।।2.12।।
transliteration:
na tvevāhaṁ jātu nāsaṁ na tvaṁ neme janādhipāḥ
na chaiva na bhaviṣhyāmaḥ sarve vayam ataḥ param
word_meanings: na—never; tu—but; eva—certainly; aham—I; jātu—at any time; na—not; āsam—existed; na—not; tvam—you; na—not; ime—these; jana-adhipāḥ—kings; na—not; cha—also; eva—certainly; na—not; bhaviṣhyāmaḥ—shall exist; sarve—all; vayam—we; ataḥ param—hereafter
translation_english: Never was there a time when I did not exist, nor you, nor all these kings; nor in the future shall any of us cease to be.
deep_dive_text: This is the introduction to Reincarnation. Krishna is explaining that the 'Self' is eternal. We are like actors changing costumes; the actors remain even when the play ends. This shifts Arjuna's focus from the temporary body to the permanent Soul.

original_script:
देहिनोऽस्मिन्यथा देहे कौमारं यौवनं जरा।
तथा देहान्तरप्राप्तिर्धीरस्तत्र न मुह्यति।।2.13।।
transliteration:
dehino ’smin yathā dehe kaumāraṁ yauvanaṁ jarā
tathā dehāntara-prāptir dhīras tatra na muhyati
word_meanings: dehinaḥ—of the embodied soul; asmin—in this; yathā—as; dehe—body; kaumāram—childhood; yauvanam—youth; jarā—old age; tathā—similarly; deha-antara—another body; prāptiḥ—achievement; dhīraḥ—the wise; tatra—thereupon; na—never; muhyati—is deluded
translation_english: Just as the embodied soul passes from childhood to youth and then to old age, so does it pass into another body at death. The wise are not deluded by this.
deep_dive_text: Krishna uses a simple analogy: we don't cry when we stop being children and become adults, even though our 'child-body' has technically died. Death is just another such transition for the soul.

original_script:
मात्रास्पर्शास्तु कौन्तेय शीतोष्णसुखदुःखदाः।
आगमापायिनोऽनित्यास्तांस्तितिक्षस्व भारत।।2.14।।
transliteration:
mātrā-sparśhās tu kaunteya śhītoṣhṇa-sukha-duḥkha-dāḥ
āgamāpāyino ’nityās tāṁs titikṣhasva bhārata
word_meanings: mātrā-sparśhāḥ—contact of the senses with their objects; tu—indeed; kaunteya—son of Kunti (Arjun); śhīta—cold; uṣhṇa—heat; sukha—happiness; duḥkha—distress; dāḥ—givers; āgama—appearing; apāyinaḥ—disappearing; anityāḥ—non-permanent; tān—them; titikṣhasva—endeavor to tolerate; bhārata—descendant of Bharat
translation_english: O son of Kunti, the contact between the senses and their objects gives rise to feelings of heat and cold, pleasure and pain. These are temporary and fleeting; learn to tolerate them, O descendant of Bharat.
deep_dive_text: Krishna introduces the concept of emotional resilience. Just as we don't stop living because it's winter or summer, we shouldn't let transient feelings of joy or sorrow stop us from doing our duty. He advises 'Titiksha'—the power to endure without being disturbed.

original_script:
यं हि न व्यथयन्त्येते पुरुषं पुरुषर्षभ।
समदुःखसुखं धीरं सोऽमृतत्वाय कल्पते।।2.15।।
transliteration:
yaṁ hi na vyathayantyete puruṣhaṁ puruṣharṣhabha
sama-duḥkha-sukhaṁ dhīraṁ so ’mṛitatvāya kalpate
word_meanings: yam—whom; hi—verily; na—not; vyathayanti—disturb; ete—these; puruṣham—a person; puruṣha-ṛiṣhabha—the best of men; sama—equal; duḥkha—distress; sukham—happiness; dhīram—the wise; saḥ—he; amṛitatvāya—for immortality; kalpate—becomes eligible
translation_english: O best of men, that person who is not disturbed by these, and who remains steady in both pain and pleasure, becomes eligible for immortality.
deep_dive_text: This is the definition of a 'Dhira' (a wise person). Immortality isn't about the body living forever, but about the consciousness rising above the binary of 'good' and 'bad' moments to reach a state of permanent peace.

original_script:
नासतो विद्यते भावो नाभावो विद्यते सतः।
उभयोरपि दृष्टोऽन्तस्त्वनयोस्तत्त्वदर्शिभिः।।2.16।।
transliteration:
nāsato vidyate bhāvo nābhāvo vidyate sataḥ
ubhayor api dṛiṣhṭo ’ntas tv anayos tattva-darśhibhiḥ
word_meanings: na—never; asataḥ—of the unreal; vidyate—is; bhāvaḥ—endurance; na—never; abhāvaḥ—changing; vidyate—is; sataḥ—of the real; ubhayoḥ—of both; api—also; dṛiṣhṭaḥ—observed; antaḥ—conclusion; tu—but; anayoḥ—of them; tattva-darśhibhiḥ—by the seers of the truth
translation_english: The unreal has no permanent existence, and the real never ceases to be. The seers of the truth have concluded this by studying the nature of both.
deep_dive_text: This is a foundational verse of Vedantic logic. 'Sat' (The Real/Soul) is that which never changes; 'Asat' (The Unreal/Body/Emotions) is that which is in constant flux. Krishna tells Arjuna that his grief is based on 'Asat'—the temporary forms of his relatives.

original_script:
अविनाशि तु तद्विद्धि येन सर्वमिदं ततम्।
विनाशमव्ययस्यास्य न कश्चित्कर्तुमर्हति।।2.17।।
transliteration:
avināśhi tu tadviddhi yena sarvam idaṁ tatam
vināśham avyayasyāsya na kaśhchit kartum arhati
word_meanings: avināśhi—imperishable; tu—but; tat—that; viddhi—know; yena—by whom; sarvam—all; idam—this; tatam—pervaded; vināśham—destruction; avyayasya—of the imperishable; asya—of it; na kaśhchit—no one; kartum—to do; arhati—is able
translation_english: Know that the consciousness which pervades the entire body is indestructible. No one can destroy the imperishable soul.
deep_dive_text: Krishna is explaining 'energy' through a spiritual lens. Just as space cannot be cut or burned, the soul (the observer) exists behind the physical matter and is untouched by it.

original_script:
अन्तवन्त इमे देहा नित्यस्योक्ताः शरीरिणः।
अनाशिनोऽप्रमेयस्य तस्माद्युध्यस्व भारत।।2.18।।
transliteration:
antavanta ime dehā nityasyoktāḥ śharīriṇaḥ
anāśhino ’prameyasya tasmād yudhyasva bhārata
word_meanings: antavantaḥ—perishable; ime—all these; dehāḥ—bodies; nityasya—of the eternal; uktāḥ—are said; śharīriṇaḥ—of the embodied soul; anāśhinaḥ—of the indestructible; aprameyasya—of the immeasurable; tasmāt—therefore; yudhyasva—fight; bhārata—descendant of Bharat
translation_english: Only the material body is perishable; the soul within is eternal, indestructible, and immeasurable. Therefore, fight, O descendant of Bharat.
deep_dive_text: Krishna links high philosophy to immediate action. Since the 'real' person cannot be killed, Arjuna should not hesitate to fulfill his duty as a warrior. It's a call to act without being paralyzed by the physical outcome.

original_script:
य एनं वेत्ति हन्तारं यश्चैनं मन्यते हतम्।
उभौ तौ न विजानीतो नायं हन्ति न हन्यते।।2.19।।
transliteration:
ya enaṁ vetti hantāraṁ yaśhchainaṁ manyate hatam
ubhau tau na vijānīto nāyaṁ hanti na hanyate
word_meanings: yaḥ—who; enam—this; vetti—thinks; hantāram—the killer; yaḥ—who; cha—and; enam—this; manyate—thinks; hatam—killed; ubhau—both; tau—they; na—not; vijānītaḥ—know; na—never; ayam—this; hanti—kills; na—nor; hanyate—is killed
translation_english: Neither he who thinks the soul a killer, nor he who thinks it killed, knows the truth; for the soul neither kills nor is killed.
deep_dive_text: This addresses Arjuna's fear of 'killing'. From a cosmic perspective, no one truly dies because the essence of life is unchangeable. Krishna is trying to separate the 'act' on the battlefield from the 'identity' of the beings involved.

original_script:
न जायते म्रियते वा कदाचि-
न्नायं भूत्वा भविता वा न भूयः।
अजो नित्यः शाश्वतोऽयं पुराणो
न हन्यते हन्यमाने शरीरे।।2.20।।
transliteration:
na jāyate mriyate vā kadāchin
nāyaṁ bhūtvā bhavitā vā na bhūyaḥ
ajo nityaḥ śhāśhvato ’yaṁ purāṇo
na hanyate hanyamāne śharīre
word_meanings: na—never; jāyate—is born; mriyate—dies; vā—or; kadāchit—at any time; na—not; ayam—this; bhūtvā—having come into being; bhavitā—will come into being; vā—or; na—not; bhūyaḥ—again; ajaḥ—unborn; nityaḥ—eternal; śhāśhvataḥ—everlasting; ayam—this; purāṇaḥ—primeval; na—not; hanyate—is killed; hanyamāne—when killed; śharīre—the body
translation_english: The soul is neither born, nor does it ever die; nor having once existed, does it ever cease to be. The soul is unborn, eternal, ever-existing, and primeval. It is not slain when the body is slain.
deep_dive_text: This is the 'Anthem of the Soul'. It provides absolute security against the fear of death. For your app, this verse represents the peak of spiritual confidence.

original_script:
वेदाविनाशिनं नित्यं य एनमजमव्ययम्।
कथं स पुरुषः पार्थ कं घातयति हन्ति कम्।।2.21।।
transliteration:
vedāvināśhinaṁ nityaṁ ya enam ajam avyayam
kathaṁ sa puruṣhaḥ pārtha kaṁ ghātayati hanti kam
word_meanings: veda—knows; avināśhinam—indestructible; nityam—always; yaḥ—who; enam—this (soul); ajam—unborn; avyayam—immutable; katham—how; saḥ—that; puruṣhaḥ—person; pārtha—Arjun; kam—whom; ghātayati—causes to be killed; hanti—kills; kam—whom
translation_english: O Partha, how can a person who knows that the soul is indestructible, eternal, unborn, and immutable, kill anyone or cause anyone to be killed?
deep_dive_text: Krishna is reinforcing the concept of 'non-doership'. If the soul is truly eternal, then the physical act of war is merely a transition of forms, not an end of existence. He is removing the psychological burden of 'murder' from Arjuna by shifting his vision to the spiritual reality.

original_script:
वासांसि जीर्णानि यथा विहाय
नवानि गृह्णाति नरोऽपराणि।
तथा शरीराणि विहाय जीर्णा-
न्यन्यानि संयाति नवानि देही।।2.22।।
transliteration:
vāsāṁsi jīrṇāni yathā vihāya
navāni gṛihṇāti naro ’parāṇi
tathā śharīrāni vihāya jīrṇān
yanyāni saṁyāti navāni dehī
word_meanings: vāsāṁsi—garments; jīrṇāni—worn out; yathā—as; vihāya—giving up; navāni—new; gṛihṇāti—takes up; naraḥ—a man; aparāṇi—other; tathā—similarly; śharīrāni—bodies; vihāya—giving up; jīrṇāni—worn out; anyāni—other; saṁyāti—enters; navāni—new; dehī—the embodied soul
translation_english: As a person puts on new garments, giving up old ones, the soul similarly accepts new material bodies, giving up the old and useless ones.
deep_dive_text: One of the most famous analogies in world literature. It removes the 'sting' of death by comparing it to something as mundane as changing clothes. It frames life as a series of experiences rather than a one-off event.

original_script:
नैनं छिन्दन्ति शस्त्राणि नैनं दहति पावकः।
न चैनं क्लेदयन्त्यापो न शोषयति मारुतः।।2.23।।
transliteration:
nainaṁ chhindanti śhastrāṇi nainaṁ dahati pāvakaḥ
na chainaṁ kledayantyāpo na śhoṣhayati mārutaḥ
word_meanings: na—never; enam—this (soul); chhindanti—shred/cut; śhastrāṇi—weapons; na—never; enam—this; dahati—burns; pāvakaḥ—fire; na—never; cha—also; enam—this; kledayanti—wet; āpaḥ—water; na—never; śhoṣhayati—withers/dries; mārutaḥ—wind
translation_english: Weapons cannot shred the soul, nor can fire burn it. Water cannot wet it, nor can the wind dry it.
deep_dive_text: Krishna lists the four elements (Earth/Weapons, Fire, Water, Air) to show that the soul is beyond the laws of physics. It is intangible and invulnerable.

original_script:
अच्छेद्योऽयमदाह्योऽयमकलेद्योऽशोष्य एव च।
नित्यः सर्वगतः स्थाणुरचलोऽयं सनातनः।।2.24।।
transliteration:
achchhedyo ’yam adāhyo ’yam akledyo ’śhoṣhya eva cha
nityaḥ sarva-gataḥ sthāṇur achalo ’yaṁ sanātanaḥ
word_meanings: achchhedyaḥ—unbreakable; ayam—this (soul); adāhyaḥ—unburnable; ayam—this; akledyaḥ—insoluble; aśhoṣhyaḥ—cannot be dried; eva—certainly; cha—and; nityaḥ—everlasting; sarva-gataḥ—all-pervading; sthāṇuḥ—unchangeable; achalaḥ—immovable; ayam—this; sanātanaḥ—eternally the same
translation_english: This soul is unbreakable and incombustible; it can neither be dampened nor dried. It is everlasting, all-pervading, unchangeable, immovable, and eternally the same.
deep_dive_text: Krishna repeats the invulnerability of the soul to emphasize it. He uses five different adjectives to ensure Arjuna understands that no physical force in the universe can touch the true 'Self'. For your app, this verse is like a 'Shield'—it represents absolute spiritual safety.

original_script:
अव्यक्तोऽयमचिन्त्योऽयमविकार्योऽयमुच्यते।
तस्मादेवं विदित्वैनं नानुशोचितुमर्हसि।।2.25।।
transliteration:
avyakto ’yam achintyo ’yam avikāryo ’yam uchyate
tasmād evaṁ viditvainaṁ nānuśhochitum arhasi
word_meanings: avyaktaḥ—invisible; ayam—this (soul); achintyaḥ—inconceivable; ayam—this; avikāryaḥ—unchangeable; ayam—this; uchyate—is said; tasmāt—therefore; evam—thus; viditvā—knowing; enam—this; na—not; anuśhochitum—to grieve; arhasi—you deserve
translation_english: The soul is said to be invisible, inconceivable, and unchangeable. Knowing this, you should not grieve for the body.
deep_dive_text: Because the soul is 'Achintya' (beyond the mind/inconceivable), it cannot be understood through logic alone; it must be realized through experience. Krishna tells Arjuna that his grief is a result of trying to measure the infinite soul with a finite, confused mind.

original_script:
अथ चैनं नित्यजातं नित्यं वा मन्यसे मृतम्।
तथापि त्वं महाबाहो नैवं शोचितुमर्हसि।।2.26।।
transliteration:
atha chainaṁ nitya-jātaṁ nityaṁ vā manyase mṛitam
tathāpi tvaṁ mahā-bāho naivaṁ śhochitum arhasi
word_meanings: atha—if; cha—and; enam—this (soul); nitya-jātam—constantly born; nitya—constantly; vā—or; manyase—you think; mṛitam—dead; tathā api—even then; tvam—you; mahā-bāho—mighty-armed one; na—not; evam—thus; śhochitum—to grieve; arhasi—deserve
translation_english: Even if you think that the soul is constantly born and constantly dies, you still have no reason to grieve, O mighty-armed one.
deep_dive_text: Krishna is using a 'worst-case scenario' logic here. He says, even if you don't believe in the eternal soul and think life is just a random biological event that starts and ends, grief is still illogical because you cannot stop the natural cycle of birth and death.

original_script:
जातस्य हि ध्रुवो मृत्युर्ध्रुवं जन्म मृतस्य च।
तस्मादपरिहार्येऽर्थे न त्वं शोचितुमर्हसि।।2.27।।
transliteration:
jātasya hi dhruvo mṛityur dhruvaṁ janma mṛitasya cha
tasmād aparihārye ’rthe na tvaṁ śhochitum arhasi
word_meanings: jātasya—for one who is born; hi—certainly; dhruvaḥ—fixed; mṛityuḥ—death; dhruvam—fixed; janma—birth; mṛitasya—for the dead; cha—and; tasmāt—therefore; aparihārye—unavoidable; arthe—in the matter; na—not; tvam—you; śhochitum—to grieve; arhasi—deserve
translation_english: Death is certain for one who has been born, and birth is certain for one who has died. Therefore, you should not lament over the inevitable.
deep_dive_text: This verse is a sobering reminder of the law of the universe. Krishna encourages Arjuna to accept reality as it is. Resistance to the inevitable is the primary cause of human suffering.

original_script:
अव्यक्तादीनि भूतानि व्यक्तमध्यानि भारत।
अव्यक्तनिधनान्येव तत्र का परिदेवना।।2.28।।
transliteration:
avyaktādīni bhūtāni vyakta-madhyāni bhārata
avyakta-nidhanānyeva tatra kā paridevanā
word_meanings: avyakta-ādīni—unmanifest in the beginning; bhūtāni—beings; vyakta—manifest; madhyāni—in the middle; bhārata—descendant of Bharat; avyakta—unmanifest; nidhanāni—at death; eva—certainly; tatra—there; kā—what; paridevanā—grief
translation_english: All beings are unmanifest before birth, manifest in the middle, and unmanifest again after death. So why should one grieve?
deep_dive_text: Krishna frames life as a brief flash of light between two darknesses. We come from the unknown and return to the unknown. Since the 'manifest' part is so temporary, getting overly attached to it is seen as a lack of perspective.

original_script:
आश्चर्यवत्पश्यति कश्चिदेन-
माश्चर्यवद्वदति तथैव चान्यः।
आश्चर्यवच्चैनमन्यः शृणोति
श्रुत्वाप्येनं वेद न चैव कश्चित्।।2.29।।
transliteration:
āśhcharya-vat paśhyati kaśhchid enam
āśhcharya-vat vadati tathaiva chānyaḥ
āśhcharya-vach chainam anyaḥ śhṛiṇoti
śhrutvāpy enaṁ veda na chaiva kaśhchit
word_meanings: āśhcharya-vat—as amazing; paśhyati—sees; kaśhchit—someone; enam—this (soul); āśhcharya-vat—as amazing; vadati—speaks of; tathā—thus; eva—certainly; cha—also; anyaḥ—another; āśhcharya-vat—as amazing; cha—also; enam—this; anyaḥ—another; śhṛiṇoti—hears; śhrutvā—having heard; api—even; enam—this; veda—knows; na—not; cha—and; eva—certainly; kaśhchit—someone
translation_english: Some see the soul as amazing, some speak of it as amazing, and some hear of it as amazing. But even after hearing about it, hardly anyone knows it as it is.
deep_dive_text: This verse acknowledges the sheer 'mystery' of consciousness. Krishna is telling Arjuna that it's okay to find these concepts difficult. Even the most learned scholars are often left in awe of the soul's nature, yet few truly 'know' it by direct experience.

original_script:
देही नित्यमवध्योऽयं देहे सर्वस्य भारत।
तस्मात्सर्वाणि भूतानि न त्वं शोचितुमर्हसि।।2.30।।
transliteration:
dehī nityam avadhyo ’yaṁ dehe sarvasya bhārata
tasmāt sarvāṇi bhūtāni na tvaṁ śhochitum arhasi
word_meanings: dehī—the dweller in the body; nityam—always; avadhyaḥ—indestructible; ayam—this; dehe—in the body; sarvasya—of everyone; bhārata—descendant of Bharat; tasmāt—therefore; sarvāṇi—all; bhūtāni—living beings; na—not; tvam—you; śhochitum—to grieve; arhasi—deserve
translation_english: The soul that dwells within the body can never be slain. Therefore, you should not mourn for any living being.
deep_dive_text: Krishna concludes the metaphysical section with a final reassurance. He universalizes the teaching—this isn't just about Arjuna or his family, but the nature of *all* beings.

original_script:
स्वधर्ममपि चावेक्ष्य न विकम्पितुमर्हसि।
धर्म्याद्धि युद्धाच्छ्रेयोऽन्यत्क्षत्रियस्य न विद्यते।।2.31।।
transliteration:
svadharmam api chāvekṣhya na vikampitum arhasi
dharmyād dhi yuddhāch chhreyo ’nyat kṣhatriyasya na vidyate
word_meanings: sva-dharmam—one's innate duty; api—also; cha—and; avekṣhya—considering; na—not; vikampitum—to waver; arhasi—you should; dharmyāt—for cosmic righteousness; hi—indeed; yuddhāt—than engaging in battle; śhreyaḥ—better; anyat—other; kṣhatriyasya—for a warrior; na—not; vidyate—exists
translation_english: Considering your specific duty as a warrior, you should not waver. Indeed, for a Kshatriya, there is no higher path than a battle fought for the restoration of cosmic order.
deep_dive_text: Krishna anchors 'Svadharma' here specifically to the warrior class's duty to protect society. This is a historical appeal to social obligation over personal preference.

original_script:
यदृच्छया चोपपन्नं स्वर्गद्वारमपावृतम्।
सुखिनः क्षत्रियाः पार्थ लभन्ते युद्धमीदृशम्।।2.32।।
transliteration:
yadṛichchhayā chopapannaṁ swarga-dwāram apāvṛitam
sukhinaḥ kṣhatriyāḥ pārtha labhante yuddham īdṛiśham
word_meanings: yadṛichchhayā—by its own accord; cha—and; upapannam—arrived; swarga—heaven; dwāram—door; apāvṛitam—wide open; sukhinaḥ—happy; kṣhatriyāḥ—warriors; pārtha—Arjun; labhante—gain; yuddham—war; īdṛiśham—such
translation_english: O Partha, happy are the warriors to whom such an opportunity to fight comes unsought, opening for them the doors of heaven.
deep_dive_text: Krishna reframes the terrifying war as a 'gift'. He tells Arjuna that most people wait their whole lives for a chance to prove their character and serve a higher cause. This war has come 'unsought,' meaning it is a divine appointment that Arjuna should welcome, not flee.

original_script:
अथ चेत्त्वमिमं धर्म्यं सङ्ग्रामं न करिष्यसि।
ततः स्वधर्मं कीर्तिं च हित्वा पापमवाप्स्यसि।।2.33।।
transliteration:
atha chet tvam imaṁ dharmyaṁ saṅgrāmaṁ na kariṣhyasi
tataḥ sva-dharmaṁ kīrtiṁ cha hitvā pāpam avāpsyasi
word_meanings: atha—if; chet—however; tvam—you; imam—this; dharmyam—righteous; saṅgrāmam—war; na—not; kariṣhyasi—will perform; tataḥ—then; sva-dharmam—own duty; kīrtim—reputation; cha—and; hitvā—losing; pāpam—sin; avāpsyasi—will incur
translation_english: If, however, you refuse to fight this righteous war, you will abandon your duty and your reputation, and you will certainly incur sin.
deep_dive_text: This is a strong warning. Krishna is explaining that 'sin' ($Papa$) isn't just about doing bad things; it is also about failing to do the right thing when it is required of you. By walking away, Arjuna would be allowing evil to win.

original_script:
अकीर्तिं चापि भूतानि कथयिष्यन्ति तेऽव्ययाम्।
सम्भावितस्य चाकीर्तिर्मरणादतिरिच्यते।।2.34।।
transliteration:
akīrtiṁ chāpi bhūtāni kathayiṣhyanti te ’vyayām
sambhāvitasya chākīrtir maraṇād atirichyate
word_meanings: akīrtim—infamy; cha—also; api—even; bhūtāni—people; kathayiṣhyanti—will speak; te—of you; avyayām—forever; sambhāvitasya—for a respectable person; cha—and; akīrtiḥ—dishonor; maraṇāt—than death; atirichyate—is worse
translation_english: People will speak of your infamy forever. For a respectable person, dishonor is worse than death.
deep_dive_text: Krishna appeals to Arjuna’s sense of honor. In the warrior code, reputation is a life-long asset. If Arjuna runs away now, he will be remembered as a coward, and that social stigma will be more painful than any wound he could receive in battle.

original_script:
भयाद्रणादुपरतं मंस्यन्ते त्वां महारथाः।
येषां च त्वं बहुमतो भूत्वा यास्यसि लाघवम्।।2.35।।
transliteration:
bhayād raṇād uparataṁ maṁsyante tvāṁ mahā-rathāḥ
yeṣhāṁ cha tvaṁ bahu-mato bhūtvā yāsyasi lāghavam
word_meanings: bhayāt—out of fear; raṇāt—from the battlefield; uparatam—withdrawn; maṁsyante—will think; tvām—you; mahā-rathāḥ—the great generals; yeṣhām—for whom; cha—and; tvam—you; bahu-mataḥ—highly esteemed; bhūtvā—having been; yāsyasi—will go; lāghavam—to insignificance
translation_english: The great generals will think that you have withdrawn from the battlefield out of fear. Those who once held you in high esteem will now look down upon you.
deep_dive_text: Krishna exposes the 'public perception' risk. Arjuna thinks he is being 'compassionate' by not fighting, but the world—especially his enemies like Duryodhana—will interpret it as simple cowardice. Their respect will turn into mockery.

original_script:
अवाच्यवादांश्च बहून्वदिष्यन्ति तवाहिताः।
निन्दन्तस्तव सामर्थ्यं ततो दुःखतरं नु किम्।।2.36।।
transliteration:
avāchya-vādāṁśh cha bahūn vadiṣhyanti tavāhitāḥ
nindantas tava sāmarthyaṁ tato duḥkhataraṁ nu kim
word_meanings: avāchya—unspeakable; vādān—words; cha—also; bahūn—many; vadiṣhyanti—will say; tava—your; ahitāḥ—enemies; nindantaḥ—insulting; tava—your; sāmarthyam—ability; tataḥ—than that; duḥkhataram—more painful; nu—indeed; kim—what
translation_english: Your enemies will speak many unspeakable words about you and mock your skill. What could be more painful than that?
deep_dive_text: Social humiliation is a powerful motivator. Krishna is humanizing the argument by showing that spiritual decisions do not exist in a vacuum; they have social consequences that Arjuna must be prepared to face.

original_script:
हतो वा प्राप्स्यसि स्वर्गं जित्वा वा भोक्ष्यसे महीम्।
तस्मादुत्तिष्ठ कौन्तेय युद्धाय कृतनिश्चयः।।2.37।।
transliteration:
hato vā prāpsyasi swargaṁ jitvā vā bhokṣhyase mahīm
tasmād uttiṣhṭha kaunteya yuddhāya kṛita-niśhchayaḥ
word_meanings: hataḥ—killed; vā—or; prāpsyasi—you will attain; swargam—heaven; jitvā—conquering; vā—or; bhokṣhyase—you will enjoy; mahīm—earth; tasmāt—therefore; uttiṣhṭha—arise; kaunteya—son of Kunti; yuddhāya—for battle; kṛita—with determination; niśhchayaḥ—resolved
translation_english: If you are killed in battle, you will reach heaven; if you are victorious, you will enjoy the earth. Therefore, arise, O son of Kunti, with determination to fight.
deep_dive_text: Krishna presents a 'win-win' scenario for the warrior. This verse is designed to remove the fear of the 'end result'. If the cause is righteous, even failure is a form of success.

original_script:
सुखदुःखे समे कृत्वा लाभालाभौ जयाजयौ।
ततो युद्धाय युज्यस्व नैवं पापमवाप्स्यसि।।2.38।।
transliteration:
sukha-duḥkhe same kṛitvā lābhālābhau jayājayau
tato yuddhāya yujyasva naivaṁ pāpam avāpsyasi
word_meanings: sukha—happiness; duḥkhe—distress; same—equal; kṛitvā—treating; lābha-alābhau—gain and loss; jaya-ajayau—victory and defeat; tataḥ—then; yuddhāya—for battle; yujyasva—prepare; na—never; evam—in this way; pāpam—sin; avāpsyasi—you will incur
translation_english: Treat pleasure and pain, gain and loss, victory and defeat as equal. Then, engage in battle for the sake of duty alone; by doing so, you will never incur sin.
deep_dive_text: This is the core instruction of 'Equanimity'. Krishna is telling Arjuna to act not for a reward, but because it is the right thing to do. When your mind is not attached to the outcome, you are free from the stress and moral guilt of the action.

original_script:
एषा तेऽभिहिता साङ्ख्ये बुद्धिर्योगे त्विमां शृणु।
बुद्ध्या युक्तो यया पार्थ कर्मबन्धं प्रहास्यसि।।2.39।।
transliteration:
eṣhā te ’bhihitā sāṅkhye buddhir yoge tv imāṁ śhṛiṇu
buddhyā yukto yayā pārtha karma-bandhaṁ prahāsyasi
word_meanings: eṣhā—this; te—to you; abhihitā—explained; sāṅkhye—by analytical knowledge; buddhiḥ—intellect; yoge—in the practice of yoga; tu—but; imām—this; śhṛiṇu—listen; buddhyā—by intellect; yuktaḥ—endowed; yayā—by which; pārtha—Arjun; karma-bandham—shackles of karma; prahāsyasi—you shall be freed from
translation_english: So far, I have explained this to you through analytical knowledge (Sankhya). Now, listen to how it applies in the path of action (Yoga). By working with this intelligence, O Partha, you will free yourself from the shackles of Karma.
deep_dive_text: This is the 'Bridge' verse. Krishna transitions from the 'Theory' (Sankhya) to the 'Practice' (Yoga). He promises that if Arjuna applies this wisdom to his work, his actions will no longer create 'Karmic debt' that binds him to suffering.

original_script:
नेहाभिक्रमनाशोऽस्ति प्रत्यवायो न विद्यते।
स्वल्पमप्यस्य धर्मस्य त्रायते महतो भयात्।।2.40।।
transliteration:
nehābhikrama-nāśho ’sti pratyavāyo na vidyate
svalpam apy asya dharmasya trāyate mahato bhayāt
word_meanings: na—not; iha—in this; abhikrama—endeavor; nāśhaḥ—loss; asti—there is; pratyavāyaḥ—diminution; na—not; vidyate—exists; svalpam—a little; api—even; asya—of this; dharmasya—path of duty; trāyate—saves; mahataḥ—from great; bhayāt—fear
translation_english: In this path of selfless action, no effort is ever wasted, and no harm can result. Even a little progress on this path protects one from great fear.
deep_dive_text: Krishna is introducing the 'safety net' of spiritual action. In the material world, if you don't finish a task, it's a failure. But in the path of Dharma, every small step counts forever and gradually removes the deepest fears of the heart.

original_script:
व्यवसायात्मिका बुद्धिरेकेह कुरुनन्दन।
बहुशाखा ह्यनन्ताश्च बुद्धयोऽव्यवसायिनाम्।।2.41।।
transliteration:
vyavasāyātmikā buddhir ekeha kuru-nandana
bahu-śhākhā hyanantāśh cha buddhayo ’vyavasāyinām
word_meanings: vyavasāya-ātmikā—resolute; buddhiḥ—intellect; ekā—single; iha—in this world; kuru-nandana—descendant of Kuru; bahu-śhākhāḥ—many-branched; hi—indeed; anantāḥ—endless; cha—and; buddhayaḥ—intellects; avyavasāyinām—of the irresolute
translation_english: Those who are on this path are resolute in purpose, and their aim is one. O beloved child of the Kurus, the intelligence of those who are irresolute is many-branched and endless.
deep_dive_text: Krishna is teaching the power of focus. A person with a clear goal has a 'one-pointed' mind, while someone driven by endless desires is constantly distracted, their energy scattered in a thousand directions. In your app, this is the 'Focus' verse.

original_script:
यामिमां पुष्पितां वाचं प्रवदन्त्यविपश्चितः।
वेदवादरताः पार्थ नान्यदस्तीति वादिनः।।2.42।।
transliteration:
yām imāṁ puṣhpitāṁ vāchaṁ pravadanty avipaśhchitaḥ
veda-vāda-ratāḥ pārtha nānyad astīti vādinaḥ
word_meanings: yām imām—all these; puṣhpitām—flowery; vācham—words; pravadanti—speak; avipaśhchitaḥ—men of small knowledge; veda-vāda-ratāḥ—attached to the ritualistic portions of the Vedas; pārtha—Arjun; na—not; anyat—anything else; asti—there is; iti—thus; vādinaḥ—advocates
translation_english: Men of small knowledge are very much attached to the flowery words of the Vedas, which recommend various ritualistic activities for elevation to heavenly planets, resultant good birth, power, and so forth. Being desirous of sense gratification and opulent life, they say that there is nothing more than this.
deep_dive_text: Krishna warns against 'spiritual materialism'. He describes people who use religion and scripture merely as a tool to get 'stuff'—wealth, status, or a better afterlife. These 'flowery words' are attractive but shallow, keeping the soul trapped in the cycle of desire instead of leading to true liberation.

original_script:
कामात्मानः स्वर्गपरा जन्मकर्मफलप्रदाम्।
क्रियाविशेषबहुलां भोगैश्वर्यगतिं प्रति।।2.43।।
transliteration:
kāmātmānaḥ swarga-parā janma-karma-phala-pradām
kriyā-viśheṣha-bahulāṁ bhogaiśhwarya-gatiṁ prati
word_meanings: kāma-ātmānaḥ—desirous of sense gratification; swarga-parāḥ—aiming at heaven; janma-karma-phala—result of good birth and activities; pradām—yielding; kriyā-viśheṣha—pompous ceremonies; bahulām—various; bhoga—gratification; aiśhwarya—opulence; gatim—progress; prati—towards
translation_english: They are full of desires, and their highest goal is to reach the heavenly planets. They perform many pompous ceremonies to attain power and sense enjoyment, but these only result in further births and deaths.
deep_dive_text: This is a critique of the 'transactional' mindset. If your only reason for doing 'good' is to get a 'reward,' your mind remains enslaved by greed. Krishna is preparing Arjuna for a higher path where action is performed for the sake of duty, not for a cosmic paycheck.

original_script:
भोगैश्वर्यप्रसक्तानां तयापहृतचेतसाम्।
व्यवसायात्मिका बुद्धिः समाधौ न विधीयते।।2.44।।
transliteration:
bhogaiśhwarya-prasaktānāṁ tayāpahṛita-chetasām
vyavasāyātmikā buddhiḥ samādhau na vidhīyate
word_meanings: bhoga—gratification; aiśhwarya—opulence; prasaktānām—for those who are attached; tayā—by that; apahṛita-chetasām—bewildered in mind; vyavasāya-ātmikā—resolute; buddhiḥ—intellect; samādhau—in meditative absorption; na—never; vidhīyate—takes place
translation_english: In the minds of those who are too attached to sense enjoyment and material opulence, and who are bewildered by such things, the resolute determination for meditative absorption (Samadhi) does not take place.
deep_dive_text: Focus is impossible for a mind chasing pleasure. Krishna explains that 'Samadhi' (deep meditative absorption) requires a 'one-pointed' mind. If your mind is scattered across a storm of conflicting desires and external attachments, you can never reach the quiet depth of consciousness required for true spiritual wisdom.

original_script:
त्रैगुण्यविषया वेदा निस्त्रैगुण्यो भवार्जुन।
निर्द्वन्द्वो नित्यसत्त्वस्थो निर्योगक्षेम आत्मवान्।।2.45।।
transliteration:
trai-guṇya-viṣhayā vedā nistrai-guṇyo bhavārjuna
nirdvandvo nitya-sattva-stho niryoga-kṣhema ātmavān
word_meanings: trai-guṇya—the three modes of material nature; viṣhayāḥ—subject matter; vedāḥ—Vedas; nistrai-guṇyaḥ—transcendental; bhava—be; arjuna—Arjun; nirdvandvaḥ—free from dualities; nitya-sattva-sthaḥ—always established in purity; niryoga-kṣhemaḥ—free from the thought of acquisition and preservation; ātmavān—established in the Self
translation_english: The Vedas deal mainly with the three modes of material nature. Rise above these modes, O Arjuna. Be free from all dualities, settled in purity, and free from the anxiety of gain and safety, established in the Self.
deep_dive_text: This is a call to transcend 'transactional' spirituality. Krishna tells Arjuna to stop worrying about 'getting and keeping' (Yoga-Kshema) and instead focus on being centered in his true nature. It's about moving from 'doing' to 'being'.

original_script:
यावानर्थ उदपाने सर्वतः सम्प्लुतोदके।
तावान्सर्वेषु वेदेषु ब्राह्मणस्य विजानतः।।2.46।।
transliteration:
yāvān artha udapāne sarvataḥ samplutodake
tāvān sarveṣhu vedeṣhu brāhmaṇasya vijānataḥ
word_meanings: yāvān—as much; arthaḥ—utility; uda-pāne—in a well; sarvataḥ—in all respects; sampluta-udake—in a great reservoir of water; tāvān—similarly; sarveṣhu—in all; vedeṣhu—Vedas; brāhmaṇasya—of a man who knows the Brahman; vijānataḥ—who has complete knowledge
