translation_english: All the purposes served by a small well can at once be served by a great reservoir of water. Similarly, all the purposes of the Vedas are fulfilled for one who knows the Supreme Truth.
deep_dive_text: A beautiful metaphor about the hierarchy of knowledge. Why chase small, separate 'wells' (rituals) when you have access to the entire 'ocean' (Self-realization)? Krishna is encouraging Arjuna to seek the Source of all wisdom rather than getting lost in the technicalities of religious rules.

original_script:
कर्मण्येवाधिकारस्ते मा फलेषु कदाचन।
मा कर्मफलहेतुर्भूर्मा ते सङ्गोऽस्त्वकर्मणि।।2.47।।
transliteration:
karmaṇy-evādhikāras te mā phaleṣhu kadāchana
mā karma-phala-hetur bhūr mā te saṅgo ’stv akarmaṇi
word_meanings: karmaṇi—in duty; eva—only; adhikāraḥ—claim/right; te—your; mā—never; phaleṣhu—in the fruits; kadāchana—at any time; mā—never; karma-phala—results of activities; hetuḥ—cause; bhūḥ—be; mā—never; te—your; saṅgaḥ—attachment; astu—be; akarmaṇi—in inaction
translation_english: You have a mandate to perform your duty, but you have no claim to the results. Never consider yourself the cause of the fruits of your work, and never be attached to inaction.
deep_dive_text: This is a legalistic definition of 'Adhikara' (jurisdiction). Historically, it severs the link between human agency and cosmic outcomes, a core shift from early Vedic ritualism to the Gita's philosophy of detached action.

original_script:
योगस्थः कुरु कर्माणि सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा धनञ्जय।
सिद्ध्यसिद्ध्योः समो भूत्वा समत्वं योग उच्यते।।2.48।।
transliteration:
yoga-sthaḥ kuru karmāṇi saṅgaṁ tyaktvā dhanañjaya
siddhy-asiddhyoḥ samo bhūtvā samatvaṁ yoga uchyate
word_meanings: yoga-sthaḥ—established in Yoga; kuru—perform; karmāṇi—actions; saṅgam—attachment; tyaktvā—giving up; dhanañjaya—Arjun; siddhi-asiddhyoḥ—in success and failure; samaḥ—even-minded; bhūtvā—becoming; samatvam—equanimity; yogaḥ—Yoga; uchyate—is called
translation_english: Be steadfast in Yoga, O Arjuna. Perform your duty and abandon all attachment to success or failure. Such evenness of mind is called Yoga.
deep_dive_text: Krishna defines Yoga not as a physical posture, but as a state of mind: 'Samatvam' (equanimity). To be the same in victory and defeat is the ultimate sign of a Yogi. It’s about emotional stability in a chaotic world.

original_script:
दूरेण ह्यवरं कर्म बुद्धियोगाद्धनञ्जय।
बुद्धौ शरणमन्विच्छ कृपणाः फलहेतवः।।2.49।।
transliteration:
dūreṇa hy-avaraṁ karma buddhi-yogād dhanañjaya
buddhau śharaṇam anvichchha kṛipaṇāḥ phala-hetavaḥ
word_meanings: dūreṇa—by a long distance; hi—indeed; avaram—inferior; karma—action; buddhi-yogāt—than the yoga of intellect; dhanañjaya—Arjun; buddhau—in the intellect; śharaṇam—refuge; anvichchha—seek; kṛipaṇāḥ—misers; phala-hetavaḥ—those seeking fruits
translation_english: O Dhananjaya, keep all selfish work far away by the use of your intellect, and seek refuge in the evenness of mind. Those who are motivated only by the fruits of their work are misers.
deep_dive_text: Krishna calls result-seekers 'misers' because they trade their inner peace for temporary material gains. He urges Arjuna to use 'Buddhi Yoga'—higher intelligence—to stay above the small-minded desire for rewards.

original_script:
बुद्धियुक्तो जहातीह उभे सुकृतदुष्कृते।
तस्माद्योगाय युज्यस्व योगः कर्मसु कौशलम्।।2.50।।
transliteration:
buddhi-yukto jahātīha ubhe sukṛita-duṣhkṛite
tasmād yogāya yujyasva yogaḥ karmasu kauśhalam
word_meanings: buddhi-yuktaḥ—one endowed with equanimity; jahāti—gets rid of; iha—in this life; ubhe—both; sukṛita-duṣhkṛite—good and bad deeds; tasmāt—therefore; yogāya—for Yoga; yujyasva—strive; yogaḥ—Yoga; karmasu—in actions; kauśhalam—skill
translation_english: One who is joined to this equanimity of mind can get rid of both good and evil deeds even in this life. Therefore, strive for Yoga, which is the art of performing all work skillfully.
deep_dive_text: Another powerful definition: 'Yogaḥ karmasu kauśhalam' (Yoga is skill in action). A Yogi isn't someone who stops working; a Yogi is someone who works with such precision and lack of ego that the work becomes a masterpiece and a spiritual practice.

original_script:
कर्मजं बुद्धियुक्ता हि फलं त्यक्त्वा मनीषिणः।
जन्मबन्धविनिर्मुक्ताः पदं गच्छन्त्यनामयम्।।2.51।।
transliteration:
karma-jaṁ buddhi-yuktā hi phalaṁ tyaktvā manīṣhiṇaḥ
janma-bandha-vinirmuktāḥ padaṁ gachchhaty anāmayam
word_meanings: karma-jam—born of activities; buddhi-yuktāḥ—endowed with equanimity; hi—certainly; phalam—results; tyaktvā—giving up; manīṣhiṇaḥ—great sages; janma-bandha—from the shackles of birth; vinirmuktāḥ—liberated; padam—state; gachchhanti—attain; anāmayam—free from all miseries
translation_english: By thus engaging in the service of the Lord, great sages or devotees free themselves from the results of work in the material world. In this way, they become free from the cycle of birth and death and attain the state beyond all miseries.
deep_dive_text: The result of 'Karma Yoga' is freedom. When you work without ego, your actions don't leave 'Karmic residue'. You become like a lotus leaf in water—the water touches it, but never soaks it. This leads to the ultimate 'Anamayam' state (a state of health/peace free from suffering).

original_script:
यदा ते मोहकलिलं बुद्धिर्व्यतितरिष्यति।
तदा गन्तासि निर्वेदं श्रोतव्यस्य श्रुतस्य च।।2.52।।
transliteration:
yadā te moha-kalilaṁ buddhir vyatitariṣhyati
tadā gantāsi nirvedaṁ śhrotavyasya śhrutasya cha
word_meanings: yadā—when; te—your; moha—of delusion; kalilam—dense forest; buddhiḥ—intellect; vyatitariṣhyati—surpasses; tadā—at that time; gantāsi—you shall go; nirvedam—indifference; śhrotavyasya—toward all that is to be heard; śhrutasya—toward all that has been heard; cha—also
translation_english: When your intellect has cleared itself of the dense forest of delusion, you will become indifferent to all that has been heard and all that is yet to be heard.
deep_dive_text: Krishna calls confusion a 'dense forest' (Moha-kalilam). Once you step out of that forest into the light of clarity, you no longer need external 'opinions' or 'rules' to tell you what to do. You develop an intuitive 'Nirvedam' (indifference) to the noise of the world because you have found the truth within.

original_script:
श्रुतिविप्रतिपन्ना ते यदा स्थास्यति निश्चला।
समाधावचला बुद्धिस्तदा योगमवाप्स्यसि।।2.53।।
transliteration:
śhruti-vipratipannā te yadā sthāsyati niśhchalā
samādhāv-achalā buddhis tadā yogam avāpsyasi
word_meanings: śhruti—the scriptures; vipratipannā—confused by; te—your; yadā—when; sthāsyati—shall remain; niśhchalā—unmoving; samādhau—in Samadhi; achalā—unwavering; buddhiḥ—intellect; tadā—then; yogam—Yoga; avāpsyasi—you will attain
translation_english: When your intellect, no longer confused by the conflicting opinions of scriptures, remains unwavering and steady in the state of Samadhi, then you will have attained Yoga.
deep_dive_text: This verse marks the end of Arjuna's confusion. Krishna promises that once the mind stops being distracted by 'what people say' or 'conflicting theories' and settles into deep inner stillness (Samadhi), truth will be obvious.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
स्थितप्रज्ञस्य का भाषा समाधिस्थस्य केशव।
स्थितधीः किं प्रभाषेत किमासीत व्रजेत किम्।।2.54।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
sthitaprajñasya kā bhāṣhā samādhisthasya keśhava
sthitadhīḥ kiṁ prabhāṣheta kim āsīta vrajeta kim
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjun said; sthita-prajñasya—of one who is situated in fixed divine knowledge; kā—what; bhāṣhā—language; samādhi-sthasya—of one situated in Samadhi; keśhava—Krishna; sthita-dhīḥ—one with fixed intellect; kim—how; prabhāṣheta—speaks; kim—how; āsīta—sits; vrajeta—walks; kim—how
translation_english: Arjuna said: O Krishna, what are the symptoms of one whose mind is merged in Brahman? How does a person of steady wisdom speak? How does he sit, and how does he walk?
deep_dive_text: Arjuna asks for 'behavioral markers' of enlightenment. He wants to know if spiritual realization changes a person's external conduct. Krishna will answer by showing that it’s not about *what* they do, but the *consciousness* with which they do it.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
प्रजहाति यदा कामान्सर्वान्पार्थ मनोगतान्।
आत्मन्येवात्मना तुष्टः स्थितप्रज्ञस्तदोच्यते।।2.55।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
prajahāti yadā kāmān sarvān pārtha manogatān
ātmanyevātmanā tuṣhṭaḥ sthitaprajñastadochyate
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; prajahāti—discards; yadā—when; kāmān—selfish desires; sarvān—all; pārtha—Arjun; manogatān—of the mind; ātmani—in the self; eva—only; ātmanā—by the self; tuṣhṭaḥ—satisfied; sthita-prajñaḥ—situated in fixed divine knowledge; tadā—at that time; uchyate—is said
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: O Partha, when a man discards all selfish desires and cravings of the mind, and finds satisfaction in the Self by the Self alone, then he is called a person of steady wisdom.
deep_dive_text: The definition of happiness. Most people seek happiness 'outside' (in objects/people). A Sthitaprajna finds a 'fountain' of joy inside their own soul. Because they are already full, they no longer 'need' anything from the world to feel complete.

original_script:
दुःखेष्वनुद्विग्नमनाः सुखेषु विगतस्पृहः।
वीतरागभयक्रोधः स्थितधीर्मुनिरुच्यते।।2.56।।
transliteration:
duḥkheṣhv-anudvignamanāḥ sukheṣhu vigataspṛihaḥ
vītarāga-bhaya-krodhaḥ sthitadhīr munir uchyate
word_meanings: duḥkheṣhu—in the midst of adversity; anudvigna-manāḥ—whose mind is undisturbed; sukheṣhu—in pleasure; vigata-spṛihaḥ—without craving; vīta—free from; rāga—attachment; bhaya—fear; krodhaḥ—anger; sthita-dhīḥ—of steady mind; muniḥ—a sage; uchyate—is called
translation_english: One whose mind remains undisturbed amidst misery, who does not crave pleasure, and who is free from attachment, fear, and anger, is called a sage of steady wisdom.
deep_dive_text: This verse describes emotional immunity. Life will always have 'ups and downs,' but the sage is like a mountain—the weather changes, but the mountain remains unmoved. They have mastered the three great enemies: Attachment, Fear, and Anger.

original_script:
यः सर्वत्रानभिस्नेहस्तत्तत्प्राप्य शुभाशुभम्।
नाभिनन्दति न द्वेष्टि तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता।।2.57।।
transliteration:
yaḥ sarvatrānabhisnehas tat tat prāpya śhubhāśhubham
nābhinandati na dveṣhṭi tasya prajñā pratiṣhṭhitā
word_meanings: yaḥ—one who; sarvatra—everywhere; anabhisnehaḥ—without attachment; tat tat—this or that; prāpya—attaining; śhubha—good; aśhubham—evil; na—never; abhinandati—praises; na—never; dveṣhṭi—hates; tasya—his; prajñā—perfect knowledge; pratiṣhṭhitā—is fixed
translation_english: In the material world, one who is unaffected by whatever good or evil he may obtain, neither praising it nor despising it, is firmly fixed in perfect knowledge.
deep_dive_text: This is the 'Neutral Gear' of the mind. A wise person doesn't throw a party when things go well and doesn't fall into a depression when things go poorly. They see 'success' and 'failure' as two sides of the same coin, maintaining a steady, objective perspective.

original_script:
यदा संहरते चायं कूर्मोऽङ्गानीव सर्वशः।
इन्द्रियाणीन्द्रियार्थेभ्यस्तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता।।2.58।।
transliteration:
yadā saṁharate chāyaṁ kūrmo ’ṅgānīva sarvaśhaḥ
indriyāṇīndriyārthebhyas tasya prajñā pratiṣhṭhitā
word_meanings: yadā—when; saṁharate—withdraws; cha—and; ayam—this; kūrmaḥ—tortoise; aṅgāni—limbs; iva—as; sarvaśhaḥ—fully; indriyāṇi—the senses; indriya-arthebhyaḥ—from sense objects; tasya—his; prajñā—wisdom; pratiṣhṭhitā—is fixed
translation_english: One who is able to withdraw the senses from their objects, just as a tortoise withdraws its limbs into its shell, is established in steady wisdom.
deep_dive_text: The 'Tortoise' analogy for self-control. It doesn't mean the sage cannot see or hear; it means they have the 'switch' in their own hand. They interact with the world by choice, not by compulsion or addiction.

original_script:
विषया विनिवर्तन्ते निराहारस्य देहिनः।
रसवर्जं रसोऽप्यस्य परं दृष्ट्वा निवर्तते।।2.59।।
transliteration:
viṣhayā vinivartante nirāhārasya dehinaḥ
rasa-varjaṁ raso ’pyasya paraṁ dṛiṣhṭvā nivartate
word_meanings: viṣhayāḥ—objects for sense enjoyment; vinivartante—are refrained; nirāhārasya—by practicing abstinence; dehinaḥ—for the embodied soul; rasa-varjam—except the taste; rasaḥ—taste; api—also; asya—his; param—the Supreme; dṛiṣhṭvā—by experiencing; nivartate—ceases
translation_english: The embodied soul may be restricted from sense enjoyment, though the taste for sense objects remains. But, ceasing such engagements by experiencing a higher taste, he is fixed in consciousness.
deep_dive_text: The secret to self-control isn't 'willpower,' it's 'substitution'. If you just 'starve' yourself of desires, the craving (Rasa) remains hidden. But when you find a 'Higher Taste' (inner peace, spiritual joy), the old, lower desires naturally fall away like dry leaves. You don't have to quit; you just outgrow.

original_script:
यततो ह्यपि कौन्तेय पुरुषस्य विपश्चितः।
इन्द्रियाणि प्रमाथीनी हरन्ति प्रसभं मनः।।2.60।।
transliteration:
yatato hyapi kaunteya puruṣhasya vipaśhchitaḥ
indriyāṇi pramāthīni haranti prasabhaṁ manaḥ
word_meanings: yatataḥ—while endeavoring; hi—certainly; api—even; kaunteya—Arjun; puruṣhasya—of a person; vipaśhchitaḥ—learned; indriyāṇi—the senses; pramāthīni—agitated; haranti—carry away; prasabham—forcibly; manaḥ—the mind
translation_english: The senses are so strong and impetuous, O Arjuna, that they forcibly carry away the mind even of a man of discrimination who is endeavoring to control them.
deep_dive_text: A warning about overconfidence. Even if you are 'learned' and 'trying hard,' the senses are like wild horses that can hijack the mind at any moment. It acknowledges the raw difficulty of the spiritual path and the need for constant vigilance.

original_script:
तानि सर्वाणि संयम्य युक्त आसीत मत्परः।
वशे हि यस्येन्द्रियाणि तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता।।2.61।।
transliteration:
tāni sarvāṇi saṁyamya yukta āsīta mat-paraḥ
vaśhe hi yasyendriyāṇi tasya prajñā pratiṣhṭhitā
word_meanings: tāni—those (senses); sarvāṇi—all; saṁyamya—restraining; yuktaḥ—engaged in Yoga; āsīta—should sit; mat-paraḥ—with the mind in Me; vaśhe—under control; hi—certainly; yasya—whose; indriyāṇi—senses; tasya—his; prajñā—wisdom; pratiṣhṭhitā—is fixed
translation_english: One who restrains his senses, keeping them under full control, and fixes his consciousness upon Me, is known as a man of steady wisdom.
deep_dive_text: The anchor of the mind. Restraint (Samyama) alone isn't enough; you need to 'attach' your mind to something higher (Krishna/The Divine) to keep it from wandering back to the lower. True wisdom is having the senses in your pocket, not on your back.

original_script:
ध्यायतो विषयान्पुंसः सङ्गस्तेषूपजायते।
सङ्गात्सञ्जायते कामः कामात्क्रोधोऽभिजायते।।2.62।।
transliteration:
dhyāyato viṣhayān puṁsaḥ saṅgas teṣhūpajāyate
saṅgāt sañjāyate kāmaḥ kāmāt krodho ’bhijāyate
word_meanings: dhyāyataḥ—while contemplating; viṣhayān—objects of the senses; puṁsaḥ—of a person; saṅgaḥ—attachment; teṣhu—in them; upajāyate—develops; saṅgāt—from attachment; sañjāyate—arises; kāmaḥ—desire; kāmāt—from desire; krodhaḥ—anger; abhijāyate—is born
translation_english: While contemplating the objects of the senses, a person develops attachment for them, and from such attachment, desire is born; from desire, anger arises.
deep_dive_text: The psychological 'Ladder of Fall'. It starts innocently with just 'thinking' about an object. Krishna shows that our thoughts are the seeds of our actions and eventual destruction. For your app, this is a 'Warning' verse.

original_script:
क्रोधाद्भवति सम्मोहः सम्मोहात्स्मृतिविभ्रमः।
स्मृतिभ्रंशाद्बुद्धिनाशो बुद्धिनाशात्प्रणश्यति।।2.63।।
transliteration:
krodhād bhavati sammohaḥ sammohāt smṛiti-vibhramaḥ
smṛiti-bhraṁśhād buddhi-nāśho buddhi-nāśhāt praṇaśhyati
word_meanings: krodhāt—from anger; bhavati—comes; sammohaḥ—delusion; sammohāt—from delusion; smṛiti—memory; vibhramaḥ—bewilderment; smṛiti-bhraṁśhāt—from loss of memory; buddhi-nāśhaḥ—destruction of intellect; buddhi-nāśhāt—from destruction of intellect; praṇaśhyati—one perishes
translation_english: Anger leads to clouding of judgment, which results in bewilderment of memory. When memory is bewildered, the intellect is destroyed; and when the intellect is destroyed, one is ruined.
deep_dive_text: The completion of the ladder. Once you are angry, you lose your 'memory' (of your values and consequences). This leads to 'intellect death,' where a person does things they later regret forever. Krishna is explaining the mechanics of a 'downward spiral'.

original_script:
रागद्वेषवियुक्तैस्तु विषयानिन्द्रियैश्चरन्।
आत्मवश्यैर्विधेयात्मा प्रसादमधिगच्छति।।2.64।।
transliteration:
rāga-dveṣha-viyuktais tu viṣhayān indriyaiśh charan
ātma-vaśhyair vidheyātmā prasādam adhigachchhati
word_meanings: rāga—attachment; dveṣha—aversion; viyuktaiḥ—free from; tu—but; viṣhayān—objects of the senses; indriyaiḥ—with the senses; charan—acting; ātma-vaśhyaiḥ—under one's own control; vidheya-ātmā—one who has controlled his mind; prasādam—peace/mercy; adhigachchhati—attains
translation_english: But a person free from all attachment and aversion and able to control his senses through regulative principles of freedom can obtain the complete mercy of the Lord.
deep_dive_text: How to live *in* the world but not *of* it. You don't have to run away to a cave; you just have to interact with objects without 'clinging' to them (Raga) and without 'hating' them (Dvesha). This balanced state leads to 'Prasadam'—a serene, tranquil heart.

original_script:
प्रसादे सर्वदुःखानां हानिरस्योपजायते।
प्रसन्नचेतसो ह्याशु बुद्धिः पर्यवतिष्ठते।।2.65।।
transliteration:
prasāde sarva-duḥkhānāṁ hānir asyopajāyate
prasanna-chetaso hy āśhu buddhiḥ paryavatiṣhṭhate
word_meanings: prasāde—on attainment of tranquility; sarva—all; duḥkhānām—of miseries; hāniḥ—destruction; asya—his; upajāyate—takes place; prasanna-chetasaḥ—of the happy-minded; hi—certainly; āśhu—very soon; buddhiḥ—intellect; paryavatiṣhṭhate—becomes established
translation_english: For one thus satisfied [in consciousness], the three-fold miseries of material existence exist no longer; in such satisfied consciousness, one’s intelligence is soon well established.
deep_dive_text: Happiness is the soil in which wisdom grows. Most people think 'I will be happy when I am wise,' but Krishna says 'Be happy/peaceful first, and your wisdom will naturally settle'. A calm mind is the only tool that can perceive the truth correctly.

original_script:
नास्ति बुद्धिरयुक्तस्य न चायुक्तस्य भावना।
न चाभावयतः शान्तिरजशान्तस्य कुतः सुखम्।।2.66।।
transliteration:
nāsti buddhir ayuktasya na chāyuktasya bhāvanā
na chābhāvayataḥ śhāntir aśhāntasya kutaḥ sukham
word_meanings: na asti—there is no; buddhiḥ—intellect; ayuktasya—for the unsteady; na—not; cha—also; ayuktasya—for the unsteady; bhāvanā—contemplation; na—not; cha—also; abhāvayataḥ—of one who does not meditate; śhāntiḥ—peace; aśhāntasya—for the restless; kutaḥ—wherefrom; sukham—happiness
translation_english: One who is not connected [with the Supreme] can have neither transcendental intelligence nor a steady mind, without which there is no possibility of peace. And how can there be any happiness without peace?
deep_dive_text: The 'Logic of Happiness'. No focus = No peace. No peace = No happiness. Krishna points out the simple truth that you can have all the wealth in the world, but if your mind is restless and stormy, 'happiness' is impossible.

original_script:
इन्द्रियाणां हि चरतां यन्मनोऽनुविधीयते।
तदस्य हरति प्रज्ञां वायुर्नावमिवाम्भसि।।2.67।।
transliteration:
indriyāṇāṁ hi charatāṁ yan mano ’nuvidhīyate
tad asya harati prajñāṁ vāyur nāvam ivāmbhasi
word_meanings: indriyāṇām—of the senses; hi—certainly; charatām—while wandering; yat—which; manaḥ—mind; anuvidhīyate—follows; tat—that; asya—his; harati—carries away; prajñām—wisdom; vāyuḥ—wind; nāvam—boat; iva—as; ambhasi—on the water
translation_english: Just as a strong wind sweeps away a boat on the water, even one of the wandering senses on which the mind focuses can carry away a man's wisdom.
deep_dive_text: A beautiful visual analogy. Even if you have 90% control, a single 'loose' sense can hijack the entire mind and wreck the 'boat' of your life. It emphasizes the need for total mindfulness.

original_script:
तस्माद्यस्य महाबाहो निगृहीतानि सर्वशः।
इन्द्रियाणीन्द्रियार्थेभ्यस्तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता।।2.68।।
transliteration:
tasmād yasya mahā-bāho nigṛihītāni sarvaśhaḥ
indriyāṇīndriyārthebhyas tasya prajñā pratiṣhṭhitā
word_meanings: tasmāt—therefore; yasya—whose; mahā-bāho—O mighty-armed one; nigṛihītāni—so restrained; sarvaśhaḥ—all around; indriyāṇi—the senses; indriya-arthebhyaḥ—from sense objects; tasya—his; prajñā—wisdom; pratiṣhṭhitā—is fixed
translation_english: Therefore, O mighty-armed one, whose senses are restrained from their objects, his intelligence is certainly fixed.
deep_dive_text: The final confirmation of the chapter's core theme: Mastery over the senses is the only gateway to permanent wisdom. Without this 'Master Key,' all other philosophical knowledge remains just theory.

original_script:
या निशा सर्वभूतानां तस्यां जागर्ति संयमी।
यस्यां जाग्रति भूतानि सा निशा पश्यतो मुनेः।।2.69।।
transliteration:
yā niśhā sarva-bhūtānāṁ tasyāṁ jāgarti saṁyamī
yasyāṁ jāgrati bhūtāni sā niśhā paśhyato muneḥ
word_meanings: yā—what; niśhā—night; sarva-bhūtānām—for all living beings; tasyām—in that; jāgarti—is awake; saṁyamī—the self-controlled; yasyām—in which; jāgrati—are awake; bhūtāni—all beings; sā—that; niśhā—night; paśhyataḥ—who sees; muneḥ—for the sage
translation_english: What is night for all beings is the time of awakening for the self-controlled; and the time of awakening for all beings is night for the introspective sage.
deep_dive_text: The 'Inversion of Reality'. Ordinary people are 'awake' to material gain and 'asleep' to spiritual truth. The sage is 'awake' to the soul and 'asleep' to the drama of the world. Their priorities are exactly opposite.

original_script:
आपूर्यमाणमचलप्रतिष्ठं
समुद्रमापः प्रविशन्ति यद्वत्।
तद्वत्कामा यं प्रविशन्ति सर्वे
स शान्तिमाप्नोति न कामकामी।।2.70।।
transliteration:
āpūryamāṇam achala-pratiṣhṭhaṁ
samudram āpaḥ praviśhanti yadvat
tadvat kāmā yaṁ praviśhanti sarve
sa śhāntim āpnoti na kāma-kāmī
word_meanings: āpūryamāṇam—filled from all sides; achala-pratiṣhṭham—steadily situated; samudram—the ocean; āpaḥ—waters; praviśhanti—enter; yadvat—as; tadvat—similarly; kāmāḥ—desires; yam—whom; praviśhanti—enter; sarve—all; saḥ—that person; śhāntim—peace; āpnoti—attains; na—not; kāma-kāmī—one who desires to fulfill desires
translation_english: As the ocean, though being ever filled by rivers from all sides, remains steady and undisturbed, so does the person into whom all desires enter without causing disturbance attain peace; not the one who strives to satisfy such desires.
deep_dive_text: The 'Ocean' analogy. A small pond overflows with a little rain, but the ocean absorbs massive rivers without changing its level. The sage's mind is so 'deep' and 'vast' that external desires can enter it without making a wave.

original_script:
विहाय कामान्यः सर्वान्पुमांश्चरति निःस्पृहः।
निर्ममो निरहङ्कारः स शान्तिमिगच्छति।।2.71।।
transliteration:
vihāya kāmān yaḥ sarvān pumānśh charati niḥspṛihaḥ
nirmamo nirahankāraḥ sa śhāntim adhigachchhati
word_meanings: vihāya—giving up; kāmān—material desires; yaḥ—who; sarvān—all; pumān—a person; charati—lives; niḥspṛihaḥ—free from cravings; nirmamaḥ—without a sense of 'mine'; nirahankāraḥ—without ego; saḥ—he; śhāntim—peace; adhigachchhati—attains
translation_english: That person who gives up all material desires and lives free from cravings, without any sense of 'mine' and 'I' (ego), attains true peace.
deep_dive_text: The formula for peace: Subtracting the Ego ($Ahamkara$) and the sense of Possession ($Mama$). When you stop saying 'I am the doer' and 'this is mine,' stress evaporates.

original_script:
एषा ब्राह्मी स्थितिः पार्थ नैनां प्राप्य विमुह्यति।
स्थित्वास्यामन्तकालेऽपि ब्रह्मनिर्वाणमृच्छति।।2.72।।
transliteration:
eṣhā brāhmī sthitiḥ pārtha naināṁ prāpya vimuhyati
sthitvāsyām anta-kāle ’pi brahma-nirvāṇam ṛichchhati
word_meanings: eṣhā—this; brāhmī—divine; sthitiḥ—state; pārtha—Arjun; na—never; enam—this; prāpya—attaining; vimuhyati—is deluded; sthitvā—being situated; asyām—in this; anta-kāle—at the end of life; api—even; brahma-nirvāṇam—liberation from material existence; ṛichchhati—attains
translation_english: This is the divine state, O Partha. Having attained this, one is never again deluded. Even if one is situated in this state at the final hour of life, one attains liberation from material existence.
deep_dive_text: The final destination. Krishna promises that once you reach this level of clarity, it stays with you forever. Even a few moments of this 'Brahmi-sthiti' before death are enough to break the cycle of suffering. This concludes the 'Gita in miniature'.

original_script: साङ्ख्ययोगः
transliteration: Sāṅkhya Yogaḥ
translation_english: Chapter 2: The Immortal Soul
deep_dive_text: Krishna begins his teaching by shifting Arjuna's perspective from the temporary body to the eternal Soul (Atman). He explains that the Soul cannot be burned, wet, or killed—it simply changes bodies like a person changes clothes. He introduces the concept of 'Sthitaprajna'—the person of steady wisdom who remains calm in both success and failure.

original_script: अथ तृतीयोऽध्यायः - कर्मयोगः
transliteration: Karma Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Karma—action; Yogaḥ—the path of synthesis.
translation_english: Chapter 3 Summary: The Yoga of Disciplined Action
deep_dive_text: Chapter 3 transitions the text from early Vedic ritualism to the internalization of sacrifice. Krishna explains that physical inaction is impossible; thus, the path to liberation lies in 'Karma Yoga'—performing action as 'Yajña' (selfless sacrifice). By acting in alignment with the cosmic cycle (*Chakra*), the individual contributes to the maintenance of the world (*Lokasamgraha*) without incurring personal karmic debt.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
ज्यायसी चेत्कर्मणस्ते मता बुद्धिर्जनार्दन।
तत्किं कर्मणि घोरे मां नियोजयसि केशव।।3.1।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
jyāyasī chet karmaṇas te matā buddhir janārdana
tat kiṁ karmaṇi ghore māṁ niyojayasi keśhava
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjun said; jyāyasī—superior; chet—if; karmaṇaḥ—than action; te—by you; matā—is thought; buddhiḥ—intellect; janārdana—Krishna; tat—then; kim—why; karmaṇi—in action; ghore—terrible; mām—me; niyojayasi—you engage; keśhava—Krishna
translation_english: Arjuna said: O Janardana, O Keshava, if You consider knowledge superior to action, why do You urge me to engage in this terrible war?
deep_dive_text: Arjuna is experiencing 'intellectual whiplash'. He heard Krishna praise the steady mind (Buddhi) in Chapter 2 and interpreted it as a call to sit in meditation. He is asking a classic human question: 'If I want to be spiritual, shouldn't I quit my stressful job/responsibilities?'

original_script:
व्यामिश्रेणेव वाक्येन बुद्धिं मोहयसीव मे।
तदेकं वद निश्चित्य येन श्रेयोऽहमाप्नुयाम्।।3.2।।
transliteration:
vyāmiśhreṇeva vākyena buddhiṁ mohayasīva me
tad ekaṁ vada niśhchitya yena śhreyo ’ham āpnuyām
word_meanings: vyāmiśhreṇa—ambiguous; iva—as if; vākyena—by words; buddhim—intellect; mohayasi—you are confusing; iva—as if; me—my; tat—therefore; ekam—one; vada—speak; niśhchitya—decisively; yena—by which; śhreyaḥ—the highest good; aham—I; āpnuyām—may attain
translation_english: My intellect is bewildered by Your seemingly ambiguous advice. Please tell me decisively which path is best for me.
deep_dive_text: Arjuna is begging for a 'Yes or No' answer. He wants a simple rule to follow because he is overwhelmed by the complexity of duty. Krishna will respond by showing that life isn't about 'Action vs. Knowledge,' but about 'Action *with* Knowledge'.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
लोकेऽस्मिन्द्विविधा निष्ठा पुरा प्रोक्ता मयानघ।
ज्ञानयोगेन साङ्ख्यानां कर्मयोगेन योगिनाम्।।3.3।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
loke ’smin dvi-vidhā niṣhṭhā purā proktā mayānagha
jñāna-yogena sāṅkhyānāṁ karma-yogena yoginām
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; loke—in the world; asmin—this; dvi-vidhā—two kinds of; niṣhṭhā—faith/paths; purā—formerly; proktā—were explained; mayā—by Me; anagha—O sinless one; jñāna-yogena—by the path of knowledge; sāṅkhyānām—for those inclined toward contemplation; karma-yogena—by the path of action; yoginām—for those inclined toward action
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: O sinless one, I have already explained that there are two paths in this world: the path of knowledge for those inclined toward contemplation, and the path of action for those inclined toward work.
deep_dive_text: Krishna acknowledges that people have different temperaments. Some are thinkers (Sankhyas), and some are doers (Yogis). However, he will soon explain that both paths eventually merge, and for most people, the path of action is more practical and safer.

original_script:
न कर्मणामनारम्भान्नैष्कर्म्यं पुरुषोऽश्नुते।
न च संन्यसनादेव सिद्धिं समधिगच्छति।।3.4।।
transliteration:
na karmaṇām anārambhān naiṣhkarmyaṁ puruṣho ’śhnute
na cha sannyasanād eva siddhiṁ samadhigachchhati
word_meanings: na—not; karmaṇām—of actions; anārambhāt—by non-performance; naiṣhkarmyam—freedom from karma; puruṣhaḥ—a person; aśhnute—attains; na—not; cha—also; sannyasanāt—by renunciation; eva—only; siddhim—perfection; samadhigachchhati—attains
translation_english: One cannot achieve freedom from karmic reactions by merely abstaining from work, nor can one attain perfection by renunciation alone.
deep_dive_text: A massive myth-buster. Krishna clarifies that 'not working' is not the same as 'freedom from work'. True renunciation is internal. If you stop working but your mind is still dreaming of the results, you haven't renounced anything; you've just become idle.

original_script:
न हि कश्चित्क्षणमपि जातु तिष्ठत्यकर्मकृत्।
कार्यते ह्यवशः कर्म सर्वः प्रकृतिजैर्गुणैः।।3.5।।
transliteration:
na hi kaśhchit kṣhaṇam api jātu tiṣhṭhatyakarmakṛit
kāryate hyavaśhaḥ karma sarvaḥ prakṛitijair guṇaiḥ
word_meanings: na—not; hi—certainly; kaśhchit—anyone; kṣhaṇam—a moment; api—even; jātu—at any time; tiṣhṭhati—remains; akarma-kṛit—without performing action; kāryate—is made to do; hi—certainly; avaśhaḥ—helplessly; karma—action; sarvaḥ—everyone; prakṛiti-jaiḥ—born of material nature; guṇaiḥ—by the modes
translation_english: No one can remain without performing action even for a moment. Everyone is driven to act helplessly by the qualities (gunas) born of material nature.
deep_dive_text: Action is a biological and cosmic necessity. Your heart beats, your lungs breathe, and your mind thinks—these are all actions. Krishna is telling Arjuna that he is 'helplessly' part of a moving universe, so the goal shouldn't be to stop, but to move in the right direction.

original_script:
कर्मेन्द्रियाणि संयम्य य आस्ते मनसा स्मरन्।
इन्द्रियार्थान्विमूढात्मा मिथ्याचारः स उच्यते।।3.6।।
transliteration:
karmendriyāṇi saṁyamya ya āste manasā smaran
indriyārthān vimūḍhātmā mithyāchāraḥ sa uchyate
word_meanings: karma-indriyāṇi—the organs of action; saṁyamya—restraining; yaḥ—who; āste—sits; manasā—by the mind; smaran—remembering; indriya-arthān—the objects of the senses; vimūḍha-ātmā—the deluded soul; mithyā-āchāraḥ—a hypocrite; saḥ—he; uchyate—is called
translation_english: One who restrains the organs of action but continues to dwell on sense objects in the mind is a deluded hypocrite.
deep_dive_text: Krishna warns against 'false renunciation'. If a person sits in a cave but is constantly thinking about food, money, or fame, they are worse off than someone who is working in the world honestly. Mental integrity is more important than physical posture.

original_script:
यस्त्विन्द्रियाणि मनसा नियम्यारभतेऽर्जुन।
कर्मेन्द्रियैः कर्मयोगमसक्तः स विशिष्यते।।3.7।।
transliteration:
yas tvindriyāṇi manasā niyamyārabhate ’rjuna
karmendriyaiḥ karma-yogam asaktaḥ sa viśhiṣhyate
word_meanings: yaḥ—who; tu—but; indriyāṇi—the senses; manasā—by the mind; niyamya—restraining; ārabhate—begins; arjuna—Arjun; karma-indriyaiḥ—by the organs of action; karma-yogam—the yoga of action; asaktaḥ—without attachment; saḥ—he; viśhiṣhyate—is superior
translation_english: But he who controls the senses by the mind and engages his active organs in the yoga of action without attachment, O Arjuna, is far superior.
deep_dive_text: This is the 'Working Sage'. Krishna clarifies that being 'spiritual' doesn't mean sitting still; it means moving and acting in the world while keeping your internal compass (the mind) steady and unattached to the results. This person is superior because they serve society while growing internally.

original_script:
नियतं कुरु कर्म त्वं कर्म ज्यायो ह्यकर्मणः।
शरीरयात्रापि च ते न प्रसिद्धयेदकर्मणः।।3.8।।
transliteration:
niyataṁ kuru karma tvaṁ karma jyāyo hyakarmaṇaḥ
śharīra-yātrāpi cha te na prasidhyed akarmaṇaḥ
word_meanings: niyatam—prescribed; kuru—perform; karma—duty; tvam—you; karma—action; jyāyaḥ—superior; hi—certainly; akarmaṇaḥ—than inaction; śharīra—body; yātrā—maintenance; api—even; cha—also; te—your; na prasidhyet—would not be possible; akarmaṇaḥ—without action
translation_english: Perform your prescribed duty, for action is superior to inaction. Even the maintenance of your body would be impossible without work.
deep_dive_text: The final verdict on the 'Work vs. No-Work' debate. Krishna gives a very grounded reason: you need to work just to survive. By doing your 'Niyatam Karma' (prescribed duty), you align yourself with the natural order of life.

original_script:
यज्ञार्थात्कर्मणोऽन्यत्र लोकोऽयं कर्मबन्धनः।
तदर्थं कर्म कौन्तेय मुक्तसङ्गः समाचर।।3.9।।
transliteration:
yajñārthāt karmaṇo ’nyatra loko ’yaṁ karma-bandhanaḥ
tad-arthaṁ karma kaunteya mukta-saṅgaḥ samāchara
word_meanings: yajña-arthāt—for the sake of sacrifice; karmaṇaḥ—than action; anyatra—otherwise; lokaḥ—world; ayam—this; karma-bandhanaḥ—bondage of action; tat-artham—for that sake; karma—action; kaunteya—son of Kunti; mukta-saṅgaḥ—free from attachment; samāchara—perform
translation_english: Work done as a sacrifice for Vishnu has to be performed, otherwise work causes bondage in this material world. Therefore, O son of Kunti, perform your prescribed duties for His satisfaction, and in that way you will always remain free from bondage.
deep_dive_text: The secret to 'Karmic Freedom'. Usually, every action we do creates a 'result' that binds us. But when we do our work as a 'Yajna' (an offering or service to the Divine/Higher Good), the 'glue' of attachment doesn't stick. We act, but we remain free.

original_script:
सहयज्ञाः प्रजाः सृष्ट्वा पुरोवाच प्रजापतिः।
अनेन प्रसविष्यध्वमेष वोऽस्त्विष्टकामधुक्।।3.10।।
transliteration:
saha-yajñāḥ prajāḥ sṛiṣhṭvā purovācha prajāpatiḥ
anena prasaviṣhyadhvam eṣha vo ’stv iṣhṭa-kāma-dhuk
word_meanings: saha—along with; yajñāḥ—sacrifices; prajāḥ—beings; sṛiṣhṭvā—having created; purā—in the beginning; uvācha—said; prajāpatiḥ—the Lord of creatures; anena—by this; prasaviṣhyadhvam—you shall prosper; eṣhaḥ—this; vaḥ—your; astu—let it be; iṣhṭa-kāma-dhuk—bestower of desired objects
translation_english: In the beginning of creation, the Lord of all creatures sent forth generations of men and demigods, along with sacrifices for Vishnu, and blessed them by saying, 'Be thou happy by this yajña [sacrifice] because its performance will bestow upon you everything desirable for living happily and achieving liberation.'
deep_dive_text: The verse links the prosperity of society to the spirit of 'Yajña' (selfless sacrifice). Krishna explains that at the dawn of creation, Prajāpati (the Progenitor) established duty and sacrifice as a single interconnected mechanism. In a historical context, this establishes the Vedic principle that resources are gifts to be shared and replenished through righteous action, rather than hoarded through greed.

original_script:
देवान्भावयतानेन ते देवा भावयन्तु वः।
परस्परं भावयन्तः श्रेयः परमवाप्स्यथ।।3.11।।
transliteration:
devān bhāvayatānena te devā bhāvayantu vaḥ
parasparaṁ bhāvayantaḥ śhreyaḥ param avāpsyatha
word_meanings: devān—celestial gods; bhāvayatā—pleasing; anena—by this; te—those; devāḥ—gods; bhāvayantu—may please; vaḥ—you; parasparam—mutually; bhāvayantaḥ—pleasing one another; śhreyaḥ—the highest good; param—the supreme; avāpsyatha—you shall achieve
translation_english: The demigods, being pleased by sacrifices, will also please you, and thus, by cooperation between men and demigods, prosperity will reign for all.
deep_dive_text: This is the 'Law of Reciprocity'. Whether you see 'Devas' as celestial beings or as the forces of nature (ecology), the message is the same: if we nourish the systems that support us, those systems will nourish us in return. Mutual care is the highest good.

original_script:
इष्टान्भोगान्हि वो देवा दास्यन्ते यज्ञभाविताः।
तैरदत्तानप्रदायैभ्यो यो भुङ्क्ते स्तेन एव सः।।3.12।।
transliteration:
iṣhṭān bhogān hi vo devā dāsyante yajña-bhāvitāḥ
tair adattān apradāyaibhyo yo bhuṅkte stena eva saḥ
word_meanings: iṣhṭān—desired; bhogān—necessities of life; hi—certainly; vaḥ—unto you; devāḥ—demigods; dāsyante—will give; yajña-bhāvitāḥ—being pleased by sacrifice; taiḥ—by them; adattān—without offering; apradāya—without giving; ebhyāḥ—to them; yaḥ—who; bhuṅkte—enjoys; stenaḥ—thief; eva—certainly; saḥ—he
translation_english: In charge of the various necessities of life, the demigods, being satisfied by the performance of yajña [sacrifice], will supply all necessities to you. But he who enjoys such gifts without offering them to the demigods in return is certainly a thief.
deep_dive_text: A powerful ethical warning. Taking from the world (resources, food, knowledge) without giving back is called 'theft'. A 'Karma Yogi' lives with a sense of gratitude, ensuring they contribute to the world as much as they consume.

original_script:
यज्ञशिष्टाशिनः सन्तो मुच्यन्ते सर्वकिल्बिषैः।
भुञ्जते ते त्वघं पापा ये पचन्त्यात्मकारणात्।।3.13।।
transliteration:
yajña-śhiṣhṭāśhinaḥ santo muchyante sarva-kilbiṣhaiḥ
bhuñjate te tv agham pāpā ye pachanty ātma-kāraṇāt
word_meanings: yajña-śhiṣhṭa—remnants of sacrifice; aśhinaḥ—eaters; santaḥ—the devotees; muchyante—get relief; sarva—from all; kilbiṣhaiḥ—sins; bhuñjate—eat; te—they; tu—but; agham—sin; pāpāḥ—sinners; ye—who; pachanti—cook; ātma-kāraṇāt—for their own sake
translation_english: The devotees of the Lord are released from all kinds of sins because they eat food which is offered first for sacrifice. Others, who prepare food for personal sense enjoyment, verily eat only sin.
deep_dive_text: Sanctifying everyday acts. Even the act of eating becomes spiritual when we recognize it as a gift. Cooking for 'selfish pleasure' binds the ego, but cooking and sharing as an act of service purifies the heart.

original_script:
अन्नाद्भवन्ति भूतानि पर्जन्यादन्नसम्भवः।
यज्ञाद्भवति पर्जन्यो यज्ञः कर्मसमुद्भवः।।3.14।।
transliteration:
annād bhavanti bhūtāni parjanyād anna-sambhavaḥ
yajñād bhavati parjanyo yajñaḥ karma-samudbhavaḥ
word_meanings: annāt—from food; bhavanti—subsist; bhūtāni—living beings; parjanyāt—from rains; anna—of food; sambhavaḥ—production; yajñāt—from sacrifice; bhavati—comes; parjanyaḥ—rain; yajñaḥ—sacrifice; karma—from action; samudbhavaḥ—born
translation_english: All living bodies subsist on food grains, which are produced from rains. Rains are produced by performance of yajña [sacrifice], and yajña is born of prescribed duties.
deep_dive_text: The 'Circle of Life'. Krishna connects our daily duties to the entire ecosystem. When humans perform their duties unselfishly (Yajna), it maintains the harmony of nature, which in turn sustains all life.

original_script:
कर्म ब्रह्मोद्भवं विद्धि ब्रह्माक्षरसमुद्भवम्।
तस्मात्सर्वगतं ब्रह्म नित्यं यज्ञे प्रतिष्ठितम्।।3.15।।
transliteration:
karma brahmodbhavaṁ viddhi brahmākṣhara-samudbhavam
tasmāt sarva-gataṁ brahma nityaṁ yajñe pratiṣhṭhitam
word_meanings: karma—duty; brahma—from the Vedas; udbhavam—born; viddhi—know; brahma—the Vedas; akṣhara—from the Imperishable; samudbhavam—born; tasmāt—therefore; sarva-gatam—all-pervading; brahma—the Divine; nitya—eternally; yajñe—in sacrifice; pratiṣhṭhitam—situated
translation_english: Regulated activities are prescribed in the Vedas, and the Vedas are directly manifested from the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Consequently the all-pervading Transcendence is eternally situated in acts of sacrifice.
deep_dive_text: Krishna links human work to the Divine source. He says the entire universe is an 'act of sacrifice' by God. Therefore, when *we* act selflessly, we are closest to the Divine nature. Sacrifice isn't a religious ritual; it's the signature of the Creator.

original_script:
एवं प्रवर्तितं चक्रं नानुवर्तयतीह यः।
अघायुरिन्द्रियारामो मोघं पार्थ स जीवति।।3.16।।
transliteration:
evaṁ pravartitaṁ chakraṁ nānuvartayatīha yaḥ
aghāyur indriyārāmo moghaṁ pārtha sa jīvati
word_meanings: evam—thus; pravartitam—set into motion; chakram—cycle; na anuvartayati—does not follow; iha—in this life; yaḥ—who; agha-āyuḥ—life of sin; indriya-ārāmaḥ—satisfied in sense objects; mogham—uselessly; pārtha—Arjun; saḥ—he; jīvati—lives
translation_english: My dear Arjuna, one who does not follow in human life the cycle of sacrifice thus established by the Vedas certainly lives a life full of sin. Living only for the satisfaction of the senses, such a person lives in vain.
deep_dive_text: The consequence of selfishness. To live only for yourself is to break the 'Wheel of Creation'. Such a person might be physically alive, but their life is 'in vain' because they have disconnected themselves from the greater purpose of existence.

original_script:
यस्त्वात्मरतिरेव स्यादात्मतृप्तश्च मानवः।
आत्मन्येव च सन्तुष्टस्तस्य कार्यं न विद्यते।।3.17।।
transliteration:
yas tvātma-ratir eva syād ātma-tṛiptaśh cha mānavaḥ
ātmanyeva cha santuṣhṭas tasya kāryaṁ na vidyate
word_meanings: yaḥ—who; tu—but; ātma-ratiḥ—rejoicing in the self; eva—certainly; syāt—is; ātma-tṛiptaḥ—satisfied in the self; cha—and; mānavaḥ—a person; ātmani—in the self; eva—only; cha—and; santuṣhṭaḥ—perfectly content; tasya—his; kāryam—duty; na—not; vidyate—exists
translation_english: But for one who rejoices only in the Self, who is satisfied with the Self, and who is fully content in the Self alone, there is no obligatory duty to perform.
deep_dive_text: Krishna describes the state of 'Atma-tripta'—one who is spiritually self-sufficient. Since this person has no personal desires or fears, they are no longer 'bound' by social or religious rules. They don't work because they *have* to; they work because they *choose* to.

original_script:
नैव तस्य कृतेनार्थो नाकृतेनेह कश्चन।
न चास्य सर्वभूतेषु कश्चिदर्थव्यपाश्रयः।।3.18।।
transliteration:
naiva tasya kṛitenārtho nākṛiteneha kaśhchana
na chāsya sarva-bhūteṣhu kaśhchid artha-vyapāśhrayaḥ
word_meanings: na—never; eva—certainly; tasya—his; kṛitena—by performance of duty; arthaḥ—purpose; na—nor; akṛitena—by non-performance; iha—in this world; kaśhchana—any; na—never; cha—also; asya—his; sarva-bhūteṣhu—among all living beings; kaśhchit—any; artha—purpose; vyapāśhrayaḥ—dependence
translation_english: A Self-realized person has no purpose to fulfill in the discharge of his prescribed duties, nor has he any reason not to perform such work. Nor is he dependent on any other living being for any purpose.
deep_dive_text: This is total spiritual independence. A sage doesn't gain anything from working and loses nothing by resting. They are completely detached from the 'transactional' nature of the world, yet they remain perfectly balanced in their existence.

original_script:
तस्मादसक्तः सततं कार्यं कर्म समाचर।
असक्तो ह्याचरन्कर्म परमाप्नोति पूरुषः।।3.19।।
transliteration:
tasmād asaktaḥ satataṁ kāryaṁ karma samāchara
asakto hyācharan karma param āpnoti pūruṣhaḥ
word_meanings: tasmāt—therefore; asaktaḥ—without attachment; satatam—constantly; kāryam—obligatory; karma—work; samāchara—perform; asaktaḥ—unattached; hi—certainly; ācharan—performing; karma—work; param—the Supreme; āpnoti—attains; pūruṣhaḥ—a person
translation_english: Therefore, without being attached to the fruits of activities, one should act as a matter of duty, for by working without attachment one attains the Supreme.
deep_dive_text: Krishna repeats his core advice: 'Asaktah' (detach yourself). Even if you aren't an enlightened sage yet, by practicing detachment in your daily work, you will eventually reach the same supreme state of peace and realization.

original_script:
कर्मणैव हि संसिद्धिमास्थिता जनकादयः।
लोकसंग्रहमेवापि सम्पश्यन्कर्तुमर्हसि।।3.20।।
transliteration:
karmaṇaiva hi saṁsiddhim āsthitā janakādayaḥ
loka-saṅgraham evāpi sampaśhyan kartum arhasi
word_meanings: karmaṇā—by performance of duty; eva—only; hi—certainly; saṁsiddhim—perfection; āsthitāḥ—attained; janaka-ādayaḥ—Janaka and others; loka-saṅgraham—welfare of the world; eva api—also; sampaśhyan—considering; kartum—to perform; arhasi—you should
translation_english: Kings such as Janaka attained perfection solely by performance of prescribed duties. Therefore, just for the sake of educating the people in general, you should perform your work.
deep_dive_text: Krishna introduces 'Lokasamgraha'—the maintenance and welfare of the world. He uses King Janaka (Sita's father) as an example of a 'Royal Sage' who was fully enlightened but continued to rule and work to keep society stable. Leaders have a responsibility to act so that others have a path to follow.

original_script:
यद्यदाचरति श्रेष्ठस्तत्तदेवेतरो जनः।
स यत्प्रमाणं कुरुते लोकस्तदनुवर्तते।।3.21।।
transliteration:
yad yad ācharati śhreṣhṭhas tat tad evetaro janaḥ
sa yat pramāṇaṁ kurute lokas tad anuvartate
word_meanings: yat yat—whatever; ācharati—performs; śhreṣhṭhaḥ—a great leader; tat tat—that (and that only); eva—certainly; itaraḥ—common; janaḥ—people; saḥ—he; yat—whatever; pramāṇam—example/standard; kurute—sets; lokaḥ—the world; tat—that; anuvartate—follows
translation_english: Whatever action a great man performs, common men follow. And whatever standards he sets by exemplary acts, all the world pursues.
deep_dive_text: The 'Leadership' verse. Krishna warns Arjuna that if a person of his stature abandons his duty, the common people will use it as an excuse to be lazy or irresponsible. A leader’s private actions have public consequences; integrity is non-negotiable for those at the top.

original_script:
न मे पार्थास्ति कर्तव्यं त्रिषु लोकेषु किञ्चन।
नानवाप्तमवाप्तव्यं वर्त एव च कर्मणि।।3.22।।
transliteration:
na me pārthāsti kartavyaṁ triṣhu lokeṣhu kiñchana
nānavāptam avāptavyaṁ varta eva cha karmaṇi
word_meanings: na—not; me—My; pārtha—Arjun; asti—there is; kartavyam—duty; triṣhu—in the three; lokeṣhu—worlds; kiñchana—any; na—not; anavāptam—wanting; avāptavyam—to be gained; varte—I am engaged; eva—certainly; cha—also; karmaṇi—in duty
translation_english: O son of Pritha, there is no work prescribed for Me within all the three heavenly systems. Nor am I in want of anything, nor have I a need to obtain anything—and yet I am engaged in prescribed duties.
deep_dive_text: Krishna uses himself as the ultimate example. As the Divine, he has no 'boss' and no 'desires,' yet he continues to participate in the world's work. If God is working, how can man justify being lazy?

original_script:
यदि ह्यहं न वर्तेयं जातु कर्मण्यतन्द्रितः।
मम वर्त्मानुवर्तन्ते मनुष्याः पार्थ सर्वशः।।3.23।।
transliteration:
yadi hyahaṁ na varteyaṁ jātu karmaṇyatandritaḥ
mama vartmānuvartante manuṣhyāḥ pārtha sarvaśhaḥ
word_meanings: yadi—if; hi—certainly; aham—I; na—not; varteyam—thus engage; jātu—ever; karmaṇi—in duty; atandritaḥ—carefully; mama—My; vartma—path; anuvartante—follow; manuṣhyāḥ—all men; pārtha—Arjun; sarvaśhaḥ—in all respects
translation_english: For if I ever failed to engage in carefully performing prescribed duties, O Partha, certainly all men would follow My path.
deep_dive_text: Krishna explains that his 'non-action' would lead to universal chaos. People tend to imitate those they admire; if the 'Highest' stops working, the 'Lower' will stop working too, leading to the collapse of civilization.

original_script:
उत्सीदेयुरिमे लोका न कुर्यां कर्म चेदहम्।
सङ्करस्य च कर्ता स्यामुपहन्यामिमाः प्रजाः।।3.24।।
transliteration:
utsīdeyur ime lokā na kuryāṁ karma ched aham
saṅkarasya cha kartā syām upahanyām imāḥ prajāḥ
word_meanings: utsīdeyuḥ—would be ruined; ime—all these; lokāḥ—worlds; na kuryām—I did not perform; karma—duty; chet—if; aham—I; saṅkarasya—of unwanted population; cha—and; kartā—creator; syām—would be; upahanyām—would destroy; imāḥ—all these; prajāḥ—beings
translation_english: If I did not perform prescribed duties, all these worlds would be put to finish. I would be the cause of creating irreligious population, and I would thereby destroy the peace of all living beings.
deep_dive_text: This highlights the 'Ripple Effect' of our actions. Krishna is showing that his work maintains the cosmic and social order ($Rta$). Action is the 'glue' that keeps the world from falling into ruin.

original_script:
सक्ताः कर्मण्यविद्वांसो यथा कुर्वन्ति भारत।
कुर्याद्विद्वांस्तथासक्तश्चिकीर्षुर्लोकसंग्रहम्।।3.25।।
transliteration:
saktāḥ karmaṇy avidvāṁso yathā kurvanti bhārata
kuryād vidvāns tathāsaktaśh chikīrṣhur loka-saṅgraham
word_meanings: saktāḥ—attached; karmaṇi—in duty; avidvāṁsaḥ—the ignorant; yathā—as; kurvanti—perform; bhārata—descendant of Bharat; kuryāt—should do; vidvān—the wise; tathā—similarly; asaktaḥ—unattached; chikīrṣhuḥ—wishing; loka-saṅgraham—welfare of the world
translation_english: As the ignorant perform their duties with attachment to results, the learned may also act, but without attachment, for the sake of leading people on the right path.
deep_dive_text: A comparison of 'Why' we work. The ignorant work for 'me and mine,' while the wise work for 'the world'. Externally, their work might look the same, but internally, the sage is free while the ignorant person is in chains.

original_script:
न बुद्धिभेदं जनयेदज्ञानां कर्मसङ्गिनाम्।
जोषयेत्सर्वकर्माणि विद्वान्युक्तः समाचरन्।।3.26।।
transliteration:
na buddhi-bhedaṁ janayed ajñānāṁ karma-saṅginām
joṣhayet sarva-karmāṇi vidvān yuktaḥ samācharan
word_meanings: na—not; buddhi-bhedam—confusion in mind; janayet—should cause; ajñānām—of the ignorant; karma-saṅginām—attached to the fruits of work; joṣhayet—should engage; sarva—all; karmāṇi—activities; vidvān—a wise person; yuktaḥ—engaged; samācharan—performing
translation_english: So as not to disrupt the minds of ignorant men attached to the fruitive results of prescribed duties, a learned person should not induce them to stop work. Rather, by working in the spirit of devotion, he should engage them in all kinds of activities [for the gradual development of Krishna consciousness].
deep_dive_text: Teaching by doing, not just talking. Krishna advises against confusing people by telling them to 'renounce work' if they aren't ready for it. Instead, a sage should set an example of how to work beautifully and unselfishly, letting others learn through observation.

original_script:
प्रकृतेः क्रियमाणानि गुणैः कर्माणि सर्वशः।
अहङ्कारविमूढात्मा कर्ताहमिति मन्यते।।3.27।।
transliteration:
prakṛiteḥ kriyamāṇāni guṇaiḥ karmāṇi sarvaśhaḥ
ahankāra-vimūḍhātmā kartāham iti manyate
word_meanings: prakṛiteḥ—of material nature; kriyamāṇāni—being done; guṇaiḥ—by the modes; karmāṇi—activities; sarvaśhaḥ—all kinds of; ahankāra-vimūḍha-ātmā—bewildered by false ego; kartā—doer; aham—I; iti—thus; manyate—thinks
translation_english: The spirit soul bewildered by the influence of false ego thinks himself the doer of activities that are in actuality carried out by the three modes of material nature.
deep_dive_text: The verse identifies 'Ahamkara' (ego-identity) as a secondary construct. Historically, it aligns with Samkhya physics: nature (Prakriti) is the only active force; the soul (Purusha) is merely the observer.

original_script:
तत्त्ववित्तु महाबाहो गुणकर्मविभागयोः।
गुणा गुणेषु वर्तन्त इति मत्वा न सज्जते।।3.28।।
transliteration:
tattva-vit tu mahā-bāho guṇa-karma-vibhāgayoḥ
guṇā guṇeṣhu vartanta iti matvā na sajjate
word_meanings: tattva-vit—knower of the Truth; tu—but; mahā-bāho—mighty-armed one; guṇa-karma—of the modes and actions; vibhāgayoḥ—of the divisions; guṇāḥ—the modes; guṇeṣhu—among the modes; vartante—are acting; iti—thus; matvā—thinking; na—not; sajjate—becomes attached
translation_english: One who knows the Truth about the divisions of material modes and actions does not become attached to them, O mighty-armed one, knowing that it is only the modes acting upon the modes.
deep_dive_text: The 'Detached Observer'. A knower of truth understands that their body and mind are just instruments of nature (Gunas) interacting with other objects of nature. By realizing 'I am not these moving parts,' they remain calm and unattached even while performing intense work.

original_script:
प्रकृतेर्गुणसम्मूढाः सज्जन्ते गुणकर्मसु।
तान्कृत्स्नविदो मन्दान्कृत्स्नविन्न विचालयेत्।।3.29।।
transliteration:
prakṛiter guṇa-sammūḍhāḥ sajjante guṇa-karmasu
tān akṛitsna-vido mandān kṛitsna-vin na vichālayet
word_meanings: prakṛiteḥ—of material nature; guṇa—by the modes; sammūḍhāḥ—bewildered; sajjante—become attached; guṇa-karmasu—in the functions of the modes; tān—those; akṛitsna-vidaḥ—persons with little knowledge; mandān—the ignorant; kṛitsna-vit—one who has complete knowledge; na—not; vichālayet—should unsettle
translation_english: Bewildered by the modes of material nature, the ignorant become attached to material activities and their results. But the wise should not unsettle those of small knowledge.
deep_dive_text: Krishna advises patience with those who don't yet understand. He warns the wise not to force higher philosophy onto people who are still emotionally attached to their work, as it might cause them to lose their direction entirely. Guidance should be gradual and compassionate.

original_script:
मयि सर्वाणि कर्माणि संन्यस्याध्यात्मचेतसा।
निराशीर्निर्ममो भूत्वा युध्यस्व विगतज्वरः।।3.30।।
transliteration:
mayi sarvāṇi karmāṇi sannyasyādhyātma-chetasā
nirāśhīr nirmamo bhūtvā yudhyasva vigata-jvaraḥ
word_meanings: mayi—unto Me; sarvāṇi—all; karmāṇi—activities; sannyasya—surrendering; adhyātma-chetasā—with mind fixed on the Self; nirāśhīḥ—free from desire for profit; nirmamaḥ—without a sense of ownership; bhūtvā—becoming; yudhyasva—fight; vigata-jvaraḥ—free from mental fever
translation_english: Therefore, surrendering all your works unto Me, with your mind intent on the Self, and free from desire for profit and ownership, fight without any mental fever.
deep_dive_text: This is a key instruction for high-pressure situations. 'Vigata-jvara' means 'free from fever' or 'cool-headed'. Krishna tells Arjuna that if he drops his ego and performs his duty as an offering to the Divine, his stress and anxiety will disappear, allowing him to fight with total clarity.

original_script:
ये मे मतमिदं नित्यमनुतिष्ठन्ति मानवाः।
श्रद्धावन्तोऽनसूयन्तो मुच्यन्ते तेऽपि कर्मभिः।।3.31।।
transliteration:
ye me matam idaṁ nityam anutiṣhṭhanti mānavāḥ
śhraddhāvanto ’nasūyanto muchyante te ’pi karmabhiḥ
word_meanings: ye—those; me—My; matam—teachings; idam—these; nityam—constantly; anutiṣhṭhanti—follow; mānavāḥ—men; śhraddhā-vantaḥ—with faith; anasūyantaḥ—without envy; muchyante—become free; te—they; api—also; karmabhiḥ—from the bondage of karma
translation_english: Those who constantly follow My teachings with faith and without envy are also freed from the bondage of karma.
deep_dive_text: The 'Seal of Approval'. Krishna promises that this path of selfless action works for anyone who approaches it with a sincere heart. You don't need to be a scholar; you just need to live the principles.

original_script:
ये त्वेतदभ्यसूयन्तो नानुतिष्ठन्ति मे मतम्।
सर्वज्ञानविमूढांस्तान्विद्धि नष्टानचेतसः।।3.32।।
transliteration:
ye tv-etad abhyasūyanto nānutiṣhṭhanti me matam
sarva-jñāna-vimūḍhāns tān viddhi naṣhṭān achetasaḥ
word_meanings: ye—those; tu—but; etat—this; abhyasūyantaḥ—who, out of envy; na anutiṣhṭhanti—do not follow; me—My; matam—teachings; sarva-jñāna—in all knowledge; vimūḍhān—bewildered; tān—them; viddhi—know; naṣhṭān—lost; achetasaḥ—without conscience
translation_english: But those who, out of envy, do not follow My teachings, know them to be bewildered in all knowledge, lost and devoid of conscience.
deep_dive_text: Krishna warns of the consequence of rejection. Without following these principles of action, a person remains stuck in the mental loops of ego and ignorance.

original_script:
सदृशं चेष्टते स्वस्याः प्रकृतेर्ज्ञानवानपि।
प्रकृतिं यान्ति भूतानि निग्रहः किं करिष्यति।।3.33।।
transliteration:
sadṛiśhaṁ cheṣhṭate svasyāḥ prakṛiter jñānavān api
prakṛitiṁ yānti bhūtāni nigrahaḥ kiṁ kariṣhyati
word_meanings: sadṛiśham—accordingly; cheṣhṭate—acts; svasyāḥ—of one's own; prakṛiteḥ—nature; jñāna-vān—the wise; api—even; prakṛitim—nature; yānti—follow; bhūtāni—all beings; nigrahaḥ—repression; kim—what; kariṣhyati—can do
translation_english: Even the wise act according to their own nature, for all beings follow their natural inclinations. What can repression accomplish?
deep_dive_text: Authentic nature vs. Repression. Krishna explains that you cannot 'force' yourself to be someone you are not. If a warrior tries to be a silent monk, he will likely fail because his nature (Prakriti) is active. The goal is to purify your nature, not suppress it.

original_script:
इन्द्रियस्येन्द्रियस्यार्थे रागद्वेषौ व्यवस्थितौ।
तयोर्न वशमागच्छेत्तौ ह्यस्य परिपन्थिनौ।।3.34।।
transliteration:
indriyasyendriyasyārthe rāga-dveṣhau vyavasthitau
tayor na vaśham āgachchhet tau hy asya paripanthinau
word_meanings: indriyasya—of the senses; indriyasya-arthe—in the sense objects; rāga—attachment; dveṣhau—aversion; vyavasthitau—situated; tayoḥ—of them; na—not; vaśham—control; āgachchhet—should come; tau—those two; hi—certainly; asya—his; paripanthinau—enemies
translation_english: The senses naturally feel attachment and aversion toward their objects. Do not come under the power of these two, for they are the enemies on the path.
deep_dive_text: The 'Tug of War'. Your eyes like certain things and hate others; your tongue craves certain tastes and avoids others. Krishna says this is 'mechanical'. The problem starts when you let these likes/dislikes (Raga/Dvesha) dictate your life. Freedom is being able to act based on duty, regardless of what the senses prefer.

original_script:
श्रेयान्स्वधर्मो विगुणः परधर्मात्स्वनुष्ठितात्।
स्वधर्मे निधनं श्रेयः परधर्मो भयावहः।।3.35।।
transliteration:
śhreyān swa-dharmo viguṇaḥ para-dharmāt svanuṣhṭhitāt
swa-dharme nidhanaṁ śhreyaḥ para-dharmo bhayāvahaḥ
word_meanings: śhreyān—better; swa-dharmaḥ—one's own duty; viguṇaḥ—imperfect; para-dharmāt—than another's duty; su-anuṣhṭhitāt—perfectly performed; swa-dharme—in one's own duty; nidhanam—death; śhreyaḥ—better; para-dharmaḥ—another's duty; bhaya-āvahaḥ—fraught with fear
translation_english: It is far better to perform one’s own duty, even if imperfectly, than to perform another’s duty perfectly. Even death in the performance of one’s own duty is better than the danger of following another’s path.
deep_dive_text: The 'Identity' verse. Krishna tells Arjuna that he must be true to himself. Trying to imitate someone else’s life or career because it looks 'holier' or 'easier' is dangerous for the soul. Your 'Svadharma' is the work that aligns with your unique nature and role in the world.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
अथ केन प्रयुक्तोऽयं पापं चरति पूरुषः।
अनिच्छन्नपि वार्ष्णेय बलादिव नियोजितः।।3.36।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
atha kena prayukto ’yaṁ pāpaṁ charati pūruṣhaḥ
anichchhann api vārṣhṇeya balād iva niyojitaḥ
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjun said; atha—then; kena—by what; prayuktaḥ—impelled; ayam—this; pāpam—sin; charati—commits; pūruṣhaḥ—a person; anichchhan—unwillingly; api—even; vārṣhṇeya—descendant of Vrishni (Krishna); balāt—by force; iva—as if; niyojitaḥ—compelled
translation_english: Arjuna said: O descendant of Vrishni, what is it that impels a person to commit sin, even against their will, as if driven by force?
deep_dive_text: One of the most relatable questions in the Gita. Arjuna asks: 'Why do I do things I know are wrong? Why do I feel like someone else is driving my car into a ditch?' He is asking about the force of addiction and compulsive behavior.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
काम एष क्रोध एष रजोगुणसमुद्भवः।
महाशनो महापाप्मा विद्ध्येनमिह वैरिणम्।।3.37।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
kāma eṣha krodha eṣha rajo-guṇa-samudbhavaḥ
mahāśhano mahā-pāpmā viddhy enam iha vairiṇam
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; kāmaḥ—lust; eṣhaḥ—this; krodhaḥ—anger; eṣhaḥ—this; rajaḥ-guṇa—the mode of passion; samudbhavaḥ—born of; mahā-aśhanaḥ—all-devouring; mahā-pāpmā—greatly sinful; viddhi—know; enam—this; iha—in this world; vairiṇam—the enemy
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: It is lust only, Arjuna, which is born of contact with the material mode of passion and later transformed into wrath, and which is the all-devouring sinful enemy of this world.
deep_dive_text: Krishna identifies the 'Criminal'. It is **Kama** (selfish desire/lust). Lust is never satisfied—it is 'all-devouring' like a fire. When a desire is blocked, it turns into 'Krodha' (anger). This pair is the root cause of every 'sin' and mistake in human history.

original_script:
धूमेनाव्रियते वह्निर्यथादर्शो मलेन च।
यथोल्बेनावृतो गर्भस्तथा तेनेदमावृतम्।।3.38।।
transliteration:
dhūmenāvriyate vahnir yathādarśho malena cha
yatholbenāvṛito garbhas tathā tenedam āvṛitam
word_meanings: dhūmena—by smoke; āvriyate—is covered; vahniḥ—fire; yathā—as; ādarśhaḥ—mirror; malena—by dust; cha—also; yathā—as; ulbena—by the womb; āvṛitaḥ—is covered; garbhaḥ—embryo; tathā—similarly; tena—by it (lust); idam—this (wisdom); āvṛitam—is covered
translation_english: As fire is covered by smoke, as a mirror is covered by dust, or as an embryo is covered by the womb, so wisdom is covered by lust.
deep_dive_text: The three degrees of 'covering'. Lust clouds our judgment to different extents. Smoke is easy to blow away (minor desire), dust needs a wipe (moderate), but a womb is a total enclosure (deep addiction). Krishna explains that our wisdom is still there, but it is 'hidden' behind our cravings.

original_script:
आवृतं ज्ञानमेतेन ज्ञानिनो नित्यवैरिणा।
कामरूपेण कौन्तेय दुष्पूरेणानलेन च।।3.39।।
transliteration:
āvṛitaṁ jñānam etena jñānino nitya-vairiṇā
kāma-rūpeṇa kaunteya duṣhpūreṇānalena cha
word_meanings: āvṛitam—covered; jñānam—pure consciousness; etena—by this; jñāninaḥ—of the knower; nitya-vairiṇā—by the eternal enemy; kāma-rūpeṇa—in the form of lust; kaunteya—O son of Kunti; duṣhpūreṇa—never to be satisfied; analena—by the fire; cha—also
translation_english: Thus the wise living entity's pure consciousness becomes covered by his eternal enemy in the form of lust, which is never satisfied and which burns like fire.
deep_dive_text: The Eternal Enemy. Krishna identifies the ultimate culprit behind our poor choices and suffering: Kama (lust or unchecked material desire). Even a 'jñāninaḥ' (wise person) can have their pure consciousness completely covered ('āvṛitam') by this force. Krishna compares lust to 'analena' (fire) because, just like a fire, it is 'duṣhpūreṇa' (never satisfied). Feeding a fire with fuel only makes it burn larger and hotter; similarly, trying to satisfy lust by fulfilling it only breeds deeper, more intense desires.

original_script:
इन्द्रियाणि मनो बुद्धिरस्याधिष्ठानमुच्यते।
एतैर्विमोहयत्येष ज्ञानमावृत्य देहिनम्।।3.40।।
transliteration:
indriyāṇi mano buddhir asyādhiṣhṭhānam uchyate
etair vimohayaty eṣha jñānam āvṛitya dehinam
word_meanings: indriyāṇi—the senses; manaḥ—the mind; buddhiḥ—the intellect; asya—of this (lust); adhiṣhṭhānam—sitting place; uchyate—is said; etaiḥ—by these; vimohayati—bewilders; eṣhaḥ—this (lust); jñānam—wisdom; āvṛitya—covering; dehinam—the embodied soul
translation_english: The senses, the mind, and the intellect are the sitting places of this lust. Through them, lust covers the real knowledge of the living entity and bewilders him.
deep_dive_text: Mapping the enemy’s 'Headquarters'. Desire enters through the **Senses** (seeing/touching), settles in the **Mind** (imagining), and eventually hijacks the **Intellect** (justifying). To win, you must secure these three 'gateways' of your own psychology.

original_script:
तस्मात्त्वमिन्द्रियाण्यादौ नियम्य भरतर्षभ।
पाप्मानं प्रजहि ह्येनं ज्ञानविज्ञाननाशनम्।।3.41।।
transliteration:
tasmāt tvam indriyāṇyādau niyamya bharatarṣhabha
pāpmānaṁ prajahi hy enaṁ jñāna-vijñāna-nāśhanam
word_meanings: tasmāt—therefore; tvam—you; indriyāṇi—the senses; ādau—in the beginning; niyamya—by regulating; bharata-ṛiṣhabha—best of the Bharatas; pāpmānam—the sinful (lust); prajahi—curb; hi—certainly; enam—this; jñāna—theoretical knowledge; vijñāna—practical realization; nāśhanam—the destroyer
translation_english: Therefore, O best of the Bharatas, in the very beginning curb this great symbol of sin [lust] by regulating the senses, and slay this destroyer of knowledge and self-realization.
deep_dive_text: The 'Counter-Strike'. Krishna tells Arjuna to start at the bottom: the senses. If you can control what you look at, listen to, and touch, the fire of lust cannot be fed. You must attack the desire before it grows into a monster that destroys your wisdom.

original_script:
इन्द्रियाणि पराण्याहुरिन्द्रियेभ्यः परं मनः।
मनसस्तु परा बुद्धिर्यो बुद्धेः परतस्तु सः।।3.42।।
transliteration:
indriyāṇi parāṇy āhur indriyebhyas paraṁ manaḥ
manasas tu parā buddhir yo buddheḥ paratas tu saḥ
word_meanings: indriyāṇi—the senses; parāṇi—superior; āhuḥ—they say; indriyebhyaḥ—than the senses; param—superior; manaḥ—the mind; manasaḥ—than the mind; tu—but; parā—superior; buddhiḥ—intellect; yaḥ—who; buddheḥ—than the intellect; parataḥ—superior; tu—but; saḥ—he (the soul)
translation_english: The working senses are superior to dull matter; mind is higher than the senses; intellect is still higher than the mind; and he [the soul] is even higher than the intellect.
deep_dive_text: The 'Hierarchy of Power'. Krishna gives Arjuna a tool to regain control: leverage the higher over the lower. If your senses want something, use your mind to redirect them. If your mind is wandering, use your intellect to focus it. And if your intellect is confused, remember that YOU are the immortal Soul, which is the master of it all.

original_script:
एवं बुद्धेः परं बुद्ध्वा संस्तभ्यात्मानमात्मना।
जहि शत्रुं महाबाहो कामरूपं दुरासदम्।।3.43।।
transliteration:
evaṁ buddheḥ paraṁ buddhvā saństabhyātmānam ātmanā
jahi śhatruṁ mahā-bāho kāma-rūpaṁ durāsadam
word_meanings: evam—thus; buddheḥ—than the intellect; param—superior; buddhvā—knowing; saństabhya—steadying; ātmānam—the mind; ātmanā—by the (purified) intellect; jahi—slay; śhatrum—the enemy; mahā-bāho—mighty-armed one; kāma-rūpam—in the form of lust; durāsadam—insatiable
translation_english: Thus knowing oneself to be transcendental to the material senses, mind, and intellect, O mighty-armed Arjuna, one should steady the mind by deliberate spiritual intelligence and thus—by spiritual strength—conquer this insatiable enemy known as lust.
deep_dive_text: The final victory of Chapter 3. Use your higher intelligence (spiritual 'Buddhi') to pin down your wandering mind. By remembering your true identity as the Soul, you gain the strength to defeat the 'insatiable enemy' (lust). Krishna has successfully moved Arjuna from paralysis to the possibility of disciplined action.

original_script: कर्मयोगः
transliteration: Karma Yogaḥ
translation_english: Chapter 3: The Path of Action
deep_dive_text: Krishna explains that since we cannot stop acting (even breathing is an action), the secret to freedom is not 'inaction,' but 'selfless action.' By performing our duties as a sacrifice (Yajna) for the world rather than for personal profit, we turn work into a spiritual practice. He identifies 'Lust' and 'Anger' as the primary enemies that cloud our judgment.

original_script: अथ चतुर्थोऽध्यायः - ज्ञानकर्मसंन्यासयोगः
transliteration: Jñāna Karma Sanyāsa Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Jñāna—knowledge; Karma—action; Sanyāsa—renunciation; Yogaḥ—the path of connection.
translation_english: Chapter 4 Summary: The Yoga of Knowledge and the Disciplines of Action
deep_dive_text: Summary: Krishna reveals the Gita as part of a primordial solar tradition, tracing its lineage back to Vivasvan. He explains the nature of His 'Avatara' (divine descent): appearing to restore the cosmic order (Dharma) when it falls into decay. The core lesson introduces the paradox of 'akarma' (inaction in action)—performing duty with such wisdom that the act itself becomes a sacrifice. The chapter concludes by presenting 'Jñāna' (transcendental knowledge) as the ultimate sword to sever the knots of doubt and ignorance.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
इमं विवस्वते योगं प्रोक्तवानहमव्ययम्।
विवस्वान्मनवे प्राह मनुरिक्ष्वाकवेऽब्रवीत्।।4.1।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
imaṁ vivasvate yogaṁ proktavān aham avyayam
vivasvān manave prāha manur ikṣhvākave ’bravīt
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; imam—this; vivasvate—to the sun-god; yogam—the science of Yoga; proktavān—instructed; aham—I; avyayam—imperishable; vivasvān—Vivasvan; manave—to Manu; prāha—told; manuḥ—Manu; ikṣhvākave—to Ikshvaku; abravīt—taught
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: I taught this eternal science of Yoga to the sun-god, Vivasvan, who passed it on to Manu, the father of mankind, and Manu in turn instructed Ikshvaku.
deep_dive_text: Krishna establishes the 'Parampara' or the chain of succession. By tracing the Gita's history back to the sun-god, He shows that this wisdom is as old as the solar system itself. It is not a new 'opinion' but a universal law that governs leaders and kings.

original_script:
एवं परम्पराप्राप्तमिमं राजर्षयो विदुः।
स कालेनेह महता योगो नष्टः परन्तप।।4.2।।
transliteration:
evaṁ paramparā-prāptam imaṁ rājarṣhayo viduḥ
sa kāleneha mahatā yogo naṣhṭaḥ parantapa
word_meanings: evam—thus; paramparā—discipilic succession; prāptam—received; imam—this; rāja-ṛiṣhayaḥ—the saintly kings; viduḥ—knew; saḥ—that; kālena—with the passage of time; iha—in this world; mahatā—great; yogaḥ—science of Yoga; naṣhṭaḥ—lost; parantapa—Arjun
translation_english: This supreme science was thus received through the chain of discipilic succession, and the saintly kings understood it in that way. But in the course of time, the succession was broken, and the science as it is appears to be lost.
deep_dive_text: Krishna addresses the 'Decay of Wisdom' over time. He explains that even the highest truths can be obscured if the chain of 'Paramparā' (discipilic succession) is broken by lack of integrity. His presence on the battlefield serves to restore the primordial connection between power (the Rajarshis) and wisdom (the Yoga).

original_script:
स एवायं मया तेऽद्य योगः प्रोक्तः पुरातनः।
भक्तोऽसि मे सखा चेति रहस्यं ह्येतदुत्तमम्।।4.3।।
transliteration:
sa evāyaṁ mayā te ’dya yogaḥ proktaḥ purātanaḥ
bhakto ’si me sakhā cheti rahasyaṁ hy etad uttamam
word_meanings: saḥ—that; eva—certainly; ayam—this; mayā—by Me; te—to you; adya—today; yogaḥ—science of Yoga; proktaḥ—is spoken; purātanaḥ—ancient; bhaktaḥ—devotee; asi—you are; me—My; sakhā—friend; cha—also; iti—thus; rahasyam—mystery/secret; hi—certainly; etat—this; uttamam—transcendental
translation_english: That very same ancient Yoga is being spoken by Me to you today because you are My devotee as well as My friend; therefore you can understand this transcendental mystery.
deep_dive_text: Qualification for Wisdom. Krishna isn't choosing Arjuna because he is a great scholar, but because of his 'attitude'—he is a friend and a sincere seeker (Bhakta). This teaches the user that the Gita's 'secrets' (Rahasya) open up to those who approach it with trust and humility rather than just cold logic.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
अपरं भवतो जन्म परं जन्म विवस्वतः।
कथमेतद्विजानीयां त्वमादौ प्रोक्तवानिति।।4.4।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
aparaṁ bhavato janma paraṁ janma vivasvataḥ
katham etad vijānīyāṁ tvam ādau proktavān iti
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjun said; aparam—later; bhavataḥ—Your; janma—birth; param—superior/earlier; janma—birth; vivasvataḥ—of the sun-god; katham—how; etat—this; vijānīyām—should I understand; tvam—You; ādau—in the beginning; proktavān—instructed; iti—thus
translation_english: Arjuna said: The sun-god Vivasvan was born much earlier than You. How then am I to understand that You instructed him in the beginning?
deep_dive_text: The 'Logic Gap'. Arjuna is looking at Krishna as a human contemporary. This question is vital because it triggers Krishna to reveal His divine nature. It represents the user's own doubt: 'How can a historical figure (or text) be relevant across all time?'

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
बहूनि मे व्यतीतानि जन्मानि तव चार्जुन।
तान्यहं वेद सर्वाणि न त्वं वेत्थ परन्तप।।4.5।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
bahūni me vyatītāni janmāni tava chārjuna
tāny ahaṁ veda sarvāṇi na tvaṁ vettha parantapa
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; bahūni—many; me—My; vyatītāni—passed; janmāni—births; tava—your; cha—and; arjuna—Arjun; tāni—those; aham—I; veda—know; sarvāṇi—all; na—not; tvam—you; vettha—know; parantapa—Arjun
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: Many, many births both you and I have passed. I can remember all of them, but you cannot, O subduer of the enemy!
deep_dive_text: Total Awareness. This is the difference between an 'Avatar' and a human being. While both have moved through many lives, the human 'forgets' due to the influence of material nature, while the Divine 'remembers' because He remains above nature. It highlights the continuity of the Soul.

original_script:
अजोऽपि सन्नव्ययात्मा भूतानामीश्वरोऽपि सन्।
प्रकृतिं स्वामधिष्ठाय सम्भवाम्यात्ममायया।।4.6।।
transliteration:
ajo ’pi sann avyayātmā bhūtānām īśhvaro ’pi san
prakṛitiṁ svām adhiṣhṭhāya sambhavāmy ātma-māyayā
word_meanings: ajaḥ—unborn; api—although; san—being; avyaya—imperishable; ātmā—soul; bhūtānām—of all beings; īśhvaraḥ—the Lord; api—although; san—being; prakṛitim—nature; svām—My own; adhiṣhṭhāya—situated; sambhavāmi—I manifest; ātma-māyayā—by My internal potency
translation_english: Although I am unborn and My transcendental body never deteriorates, and although I am the Lord of all living entities, I still appear in every millennium in My original transcendental form by My own internal power.
deep_dive_text: The mechanics of an Avatar. Unlike humans who are 'forced' into bodies by Karma, Krishna 'chooses' to manifest. He uses His 'Atma-maya' (divine energy) to appear in the world while remaining completely free from the laws of birth and death.

original_script:
यदा यदा हि धर्मस्य ग्लानिर्भवति भारत।
अभ्युत्थानमधर्मस्य तदात्मानं सृजाम्यहम्।।4.7।।
transliteration:
yadā yadā hi dharmasya glānir bhavati bhārata
abhyutthānam adharmasya tadātmānaṁ sṛijāmy aham
word_meanings: yadā yadā—whenever; hi—certainly; dharmasya—of righteousness; glāniḥ—decline; bhavati—is; bhārata—Arjun; abhyutthānam—rise; adharmasya—of unrighteousness; tadā—at that time; ātmānam—Myself; sṛijāmi—manifest; aham—I
translation_english: Whenever and wherever there is a decline in religious practice, O descendant of Bharata, and a predominant rise of irreligion—at that time I descend Myself.
deep_dive_text: The 'Universal Correction'. Krishna explains the 'Trigger' for His descent. When human systems fail and 'Adharma' (chaos/injustice) becomes too heavy, a divine intervention occurs to balance the scales. It is a promise of hope for the world during its darkest times.

original_script:
परित्राणाय साधूनां विनाशाय च दुष्कृताम्।
धर्मसंस्थापनार्थाय सम्भवामि युगे युगे।।4.8।।
transliteration:
paritrāṇāya sādhūnāṁ vināśhāya cha duṣhkṛitām
dharma-saństhāpanārthāya sambhavāmi yuge yuge
word_meanings: paritrāṇāya—for the protection; sādhūnām—of the virtuous; vināśhāya—for the destruction; cha—also; duṣhkṛitām—of the wicked; dharma—righteousness; saństhāpana-arthāya—to re-establish; sambhavāmi—I manifest; yuge yuge—age after age
translation_english: To deliver the pious and to annihilate the miscreants, as well as to re-establish the principles of religion, I Myself appear, millennium after millennium.
deep_dive_text: The 'Mission Statement' of the Divine. Krishna has a three-fold purpose: Protecting the good (Sadhus), removing the toxic (Duskrutam), and rebuilding the moral infrastructure (Dharma). This cycle repeats across history whenever the world needs a 'system update'.

original_script:
जन्म कर्म च मे दिव्यमेवं यो वेत्ति तत्त्वतः।
त्यक्त्वा देहं पुनर्जन्म नैति मामेति सोऽर्जुन।।4.9।।
transliteration:
janma karma cha me divyam evaṁ yo vetti tattvataḥ
tyaktvā dehaṁ punar janma naiti mām eti so ’rjuna
word_meanings: janma—birth; karma—activities; cha—also; me—My; divyam—divine; evam—thus; yaḥ—who; vetti—knows; tattvataḥ—in reality; tyaktvā—leaving; deham—the body; punaḥ—again; janma—birth; na—never; eti—attains; mām—Me; eti—attains; saḥ—he; arjuna—Arjun
translation_english: One who knows the transcendental nature of My appearance and activities does not, upon leaving the body, take his birth again in this material world, but attains My eternal abode, O Arjuna.
deep_dive_text: The 'Shortcut to Liberation'. Krishna reveals that simply 'understanding' the divine play is enough to break the cycle of reincarnation. By focusing the mind on the Divine's work rather than our own small dramas, our consciousness becomes 'divinized,' ensuring we return to the Source.

original_script:
वीतरागभयक्रोधा मन्मया मामुपाश्रिताः।
बहवो ज्ञानतपसा पूता मद्भावमागताः।।4.10।।
transliteration:
vīta-rāga-bhaya-krodhā man-mayā mām upāśhritāḥ
bahavo jñāna-tapasā pūtā mad-bhāvam āgatāḥ
word_meanings: vīta—free from; rāga—attachment; bhaya—fear; krodhāḥ—anger; mat-mayā—absorbed in Me; mām—in Me; upāśhritāḥ—taking shelter; bahavaḥ—many; jñāna—knowledge; tapasā—by penance; pūtāḥ—purified; mat-bhāvam—My nature; āgatāḥ—attained
translation_english: Being freed from attachment, fear, and anger, being fully absorbed in Me and taking refuge in Me, many, many persons in the past became purified by knowledge of Me—and thus they all attained transcendental love for Me.
deep_dive_text: The path of 'Purification'. Krishna explains how previous seekers reached the goal: by clearing the 'mental debris' of attachment, fear, and anger. This isn't just theory—it's a proven historical method of 'Jnana Tapasa' (the austerity of knowledge) that leads to oneness with the Divine.

original_script:
ये यथा मां प्रपद्यन्ते तांस्तथैव भजाम्यहम्।
मम वर्त्मानुवर्तन्ते मनुष्याः पार्थ सर्वशः।।4.11।।
transliteration:
ye yathā māṁ prapadyante tāns tathaiva bhajāmy aham
mama vartmānuvartante manuṣhyāḥ pārtha sarvaśhaḥ
word_meanings: ye—all who; yathā—as; mām—unto Me; prapadyante—surrender; tān—them; tathā—so; eva—certainly; bhajāmi—reward; aham—I; mama—My; vartma—path; anuvartante—follow; manuṣhyāḥ—all men; pārtha—Arjun; sarvaśhaḥ—in all respects
translation_english: As all surrender unto Me, I reward them accordingly. Everyone follows My path in all respects, O son of Pritha.
deep_dive_text: The Law of Reciprocation. Krishna explains that the Divine is like a mirror—He reflects back whatever attitude you bring to Him. Whether a person seeks God through fear, duty, love, or even logic, they are all interacting with the same Truth at different levels.

original_script:
काङ्क्षन्तः कर्मणां सिद्धिं यजन्त इह देवताः।
क्षिप्रं हि मानुषे लोके सिद्धिर्भवति कर्मजा।।4.12।।
transliteration:
kāṅkṣhantaḥ karmaṇāṁ siddhiṁ yajanta iha devatāḥ
kṣhipraṁ hi mānuṣhe loke siddhir bhavati karma-jā
