word_meanings: kāṅkṣhantaḥ—desiring; karmaṇām—of fruitive activities; siddhim—success; yajante—they worship; iha—in this world; devatāḥ—the celestial gods; kṣhipram—quickly; hi—certainly; mānuṣhe—in human; loke—society; siddhiḥ—success; bhavati—comes; karma-jā—from such work
translation_english: Men in this world desire success in fruitive activities, and therefore they worship the celestial gods. Quickly, of course, does success come from self-interested work in this world.
deep_dive_text: Short-term vs. Long-term success. Krishna acknowledges that 'transactional' worship for material gain (money, power) often brings fast results. However, he is preparing Arjuna for the higher path of Karma Yoga, which brings permanent spiritual freedom rather than just temporary physical comfort.

original_script:
चातुर्वर्ण्यं मया सृष्टं गुणकर्मविभागशः।
तस्य कर्तारमपि मां विद्ध्यकर्तारमव्ययम्।।4.13।।
transliteration:
chātur-varṇyaṁ mayā sṛiṣhṭaṁ guṇa-karma-vibhāgaśhaḥ
tasya kartāram api māṁ viddhy akartāram avyayam
word_meanings: chātuḥ-varṇyam—the four divisions of society; mayā—by Me; sṛiṣhṭam—originated; guṇa—quality/temperament; karma—and professional work; vibhāgaśhaḥ—according to the division; tasya—of that; kartāram—the originator; api—although; mām—Me; viddhi—know; akartāram—as the non-doer; avyayam—unchangeable
translation_english: The four divisions of society were originated by Me based on the distinction of temperament (*Guna*) and professional activity (*Karma*). Although I am the originator of this system, know Me as the unchangeable non-doer.
deep_dive_text: Critical historical marker: Krishna defines the four-fold social order (Varna) based on 'Guna' (innate quality) and 'Karma' (professional skill), explicitly deviating from birth-based biological inheritance patterns.

original_script:
न मां कर्माणि लिम्पन्ति न मे कर्मफले स्पृहा।
इति मां योऽभिजानाति कर्मभिर्न स बध्यते।।4.14।।
transliteration:
na māṁ karmāṇi limpanti na me karma-phale spṛihā
iti māṁ yo ’bhijānāti karmabhir na sa badhyate
word_meanings: na—never; mām—Me; karmāṇi—all kinds of work; limpanti—taint/bind; na—not; me—My; karma-phale—in the fruits of work; spṛihā—aspiration; iti—thus; mām—Me; yaḥ—who; abhijānāti—knows; karmabhiḥ—by the reactions of work; na—never; saḥ—he; badhyate—becomes entangled
translation_english: There is no work that affects Me, nor do I aspire for the fruits of action. One who understands this truth about Me also does not become entangled in the fruitive reactions of work.
deep_dive_text: Freedom through Understanding. Krishna says that because He has no selfish desires, His actions leave no 'karmic stains'. He invites Arjuna to mirror this Divine quality—to work for the world without 'clinging' to the prize—which is the only way to act without creating future suffering.

original_script:
एवं ज्ञात्वा कृतं कर्म पूर्वैरपि मुमुक्षुभिः।
कुरु कर्मैव तस्मात्त्वं पूर्वैः पूर्वतरं कृतम्।।4.15।।
transliteration:
evaṁ jñātvā kṛitaṁ karma pūrvair api mumukṣhubhiḥ
kuru karmaiva tasmāt tvaṁ pūrvaiḥ pūrvataraṁ kṛitam
word_meanings: evam—thus; jñātvā—knowing; kṛitam—performed; karma—work; pūrvaiḥ—by the ancients; api—also; mumukṣhubhiḥ—who attained liberation; kuru—perform; karma—duty; eva—certainly; tasmāt—therefore; tvam—you; pūrvaiḥ—by the ancestors; pūrvataram—ancient; kṛitam—as performed
translation_english: All the liberated souls in ancient times acted with this understanding of My transcendental nature. Therefore, you should perform your duty, following in their footsteps.
deep_dive_text: The Precedent of Sages. Krishna reminds Arjuna that 'liberation' doesn't mean escaping to a forest; the greatest saints of the past achieved freedom while staying active in their roles. Duty performed with wisdom is the ancient, proven path.

original_script:
किं कर्म किमकर्मेति कवयोऽप्यत्र मोहिताः।
तत्ते कर्म प्रवक्ष्यामि यज्ज्ञात्वा मोक्ष्यसेऽशुभात्।।4.16।।
transliteration:
kiṁ karma kim akarmeti kavayo ’py atra mohitāḥ
tat te karma pravakṣhyāmi yaj jñātvā mokṣhyase ’śhubhāt
word_meanings: kim—what is; karma—action; kim—what is; akarma—inaction; iti—thus; kavayaḥ—the intelligent; api—even; atra—in this matter; mohitāḥ—bewildered; tat—that; te—unto you; karma—action; pravakṣhyāmi—I shall explain; yat—which; jñātvā—knowing; mokṣhyase—you shall be liberated; aśhubhāt—from all ill-fortune
translation_english: Even the intelligent are bewildered as to what is action and what is inaction. Now I shall explain to you what action is, knowing which you shall be liberated from all misfortune.
deep_dive_text: The Paradox of Action. This is a profound philosophical deep-dive. Krishna admits that distinguishing between 'right work' and 'wrong work' is so subtle that even geniuses get confused. He promises to reveal the 'Internal Compass' that makes this distinction clear.

original_script:
कर्मणो ह्यपि बोद्धव्यं बोद्धव्यं च विकर्मणः।
अकर्मणश्च बोद्धव्यं गहना कर्मणो गतिः।।4.17।।
transliteration:
karmaṇo hy api boddhavyaṁ boddhavyaṁ cha vikarmaṇaḥ
akarmaṇaśh cha boddhavyaṁ gahanā karmaṇo gatiḥ
word_meanings: karmaṇaḥ—of action; hi—certainly; api—also; boddhavyam—should be understood; boddhavyam—should be understood; cha—also; vikarmaṇaḥ—of forbidden action; akarmaṇaḥ—of inaction; cha—also; boddhavyam—should be understood; gahanā—very difficult; karmaṇaḥ—of action; gatiḥ—the nature/path
translation_english: The intricacies of action are very hard to understand. Therefore one should know properly what action is, what forbidden action is, and what inaction is.
deep_dive_text: The three types of motion: 1. **Karma** (Action for duty); 2. **Vikarma** (Harmful/unethical action); 3. **Akarma** (Spiritual action that leaves no debt). Krishna warns that the 'Gati' (the flow) of how actions impact our life is complex and needs deep meditation to master.

original_script:
कर्मण्यकर्म यः पश्येदकर्मणि च कर्म यः।
स बुद्धिमान्मनुष्येषु स युक्तः कृत्स्नकर्मकृत्।।4.18।।
transliteration:
karmaṇy-akarma yaḥ paśhyed akarmaṇi cha karma yaḥ
sa buddhimān manuṣhyeṣhu sa yuktaḥ kṛitsna-karma-kṛit
word_meanings: karmaṇi—in action; akarma—inaction; yaḥ—who; paśhyet—observes; akarmaṇi—in inaction; cha—also; karma—action; yaḥ—who; saḥ—he; buddhimān—intelligent; manuṣhyeṣhu—among men; saḥ—he; yuktaḥ—in Yoga; kṛitsna-karma-kṛit—the performer of all actions
translation_english: One who sees inaction in action, and action in inaction, is intelligent among men, and he is in the transcendental position, although engaged in all sorts of activities.
deep_dive_text: The 'Inception' verse of the Gita. 'Inaction in Action' means a person who is working hard externally but is perfectly silent and detached internally. 'Action in Inaction' refers to someone who is sitting still but whose mind is racing with desires—they are actually working more than anyone. True intelligence is finding the 'Still Center' while moving through life.

original_script:
यस्य सर्वे समारम्भाः कामसङ्कल्पवर्जिताः।
ज्ञानाग्निदग्धकर्माणं तमाहुः पण्डितं बुधाः।।4.19।।
transliteration:
yasya sarve samārambhāḥ kāma-saṅkalpa-varjitāḥ
jñānāgni-dagdha-karmāṇaṁ tam āhuḥ paṇḍitaṁ budhāḥ
word_meanings: yasya—whose; sarve—all; samārambhāḥ—undertakings; kāma—desire; saṅkalpa—determination; varjitāḥ—devoid of; jñāna—knowledge; agni—fire; dagdha—burned; karmāṇam—actions; tam—him; āhuḥ—they say; paṇḍitam—learned sage; budhāḥ—the wise
translation_english: One is understood to be in full knowledge whose every endeavor is devoid of desire for sense gratification. He is said by sages to be a worker whose fruitive reactions have been burned up by the fire of perfect knowledge.
deep_dive_text: The Fire of Knowledge ($Jnanagni$). When you work without the 'I need this for myself' attitude, your actions don't create future destiny. They are 'burned up' the moment they are done, leaving you clean and free. This is the definition of a true 'Pandit' or scholar—not what they know, but how they act.

original_script:
त्यक्त्वा कर्मफलासङ्गं नित्यतृप्तो निराश्रयः।
कर्मण्यभिप्रवृत्तोऽपि नैव किञ्चित्करोति सः।।4.20।।
transliteration:
tyaktvā karma-phalāsaṅgaṁ nitya-tṛipto nirāśhrayaḥ
karmaṇy abhipravṛitto ’pi naiva kiñchit karoti saḥ
word_meanings: tyaktvā—giving up; karma-phala-āsaṅgam—attachment to the fruits of action; nitya—always; tṛiptaḥ—satisfied; nirāśhrayaḥ—without dependence; karmaṇi—in action; abhipravṛittaḥ—engaged; api—although; na—never; eva—certainly; kiñchit—anything; karoti—does; saḥ—he
translation_english: Abandoning all attachment to the results of his activities, ever satisfied and independent, he performs no fruitive action, although engaged in all kinds of undertakings.
deep_dive_text: Spiritual Sovereignty. This person is 'Nitya-tripta'—constantly full. Because they don't 'depend' ($Nirashraya$) on the world for their happiness, they can participate in the world's highest activities without any fear or anxiety about what happens next.

original_script:
निराशीर्यतचित्तात्मा त्यक्तसर्वपरिग्रहः।
शारीरं केवलं कर्म कुर्वन्नाप्नोति किल्बिषम्।।4.21।।
transliteration:
nirāśhīr yata-chittātmā tyakta-sarva-parigrahaḥ
śhārīraṁ kevalaṁ karma kurvan nāpnoti kilbiṣham
word_meanings: nirāśhīḥ—without desire for profit; yata—controlled; chitta-ātmā—mind and self; tyakta—giving up; sarva—all; parigrahaḥ—sense of ownership; śhārīram—for the body; kevalam—only; karma—work; kurvan—doing; na—never; āpnoti—incurs; kilbiṣham—sin
translation_english: Such a man of understanding acts with mind and intelligence perfectly controlled, gives up all sense of proprietorship over his possessions, and acts only for the bare necessities of life. Thus working, he is not affected by sinful reactions.
deep_dive_text: The Minimalist Warrior. By controlling the 'Parigraha' (possessiveness), a seeker simplifies their life. They act to maintain their role and their body, but they don't carry the mental weight of 'ownership'. This lightness of being prevents the buildup of negative Karma.

original_script:
यदृच्छालाभसन्तुष्टो द्वन्द्वातीतो विमत्सरः।
समः सिद्धावसिद्धौ च कृत्वापि न निबध्यते।।4.22।।
transliteration:
yadṛichchhā-lābha-santuṣhṭo dvandvātīto vimatsaraḥ
samaḥ siddhāv asiddhau cha kṛitvāpi na nibadhyate
word_meanings: yadṛichchhā—self-arrived; lābha—gain; santuṣhṭaḥ—satisfied; dvandva—dualities; atītaḥ—surpassed; vimatsaraḥ—free from envy; samaḥ—equanimous; siddhau—in success; asiddhau—in failure; cha—and; kṛitvā—performing; api—although; na—never; nibadhyate—is affected
translation_english: He who is satisfied with gain which comes of its own accord, who is free from duality and does not envy, who is steady in both success and failure, is never entangled, although performing actions.
deep_dive_text: The 'Free Flow' of Life. This verse describes a person who accepts what comes naturally without 'chasing' or 'rejecting'. They are 'Vimatsara' (free from the poison of envy) and 'Dvandvatita' (above the pairs of opposites). For your app, this is the 'Ultimate Peace' verse.

original_script:
गतसङ्गस्य मुक्तस्य ज्ञानावस्थितचेतसः।
यज्ञायाचरतः कर्म समग्रं प्रविलीयते।।4.23।।
transliteration:
gata-saṅgasya muktasya jñānāvasthita-chetasaḥ
yajñāyācharataḥ karma samagraṁ pravilīyate
word_meanings: gata-saṅgasya—of one who is unattached; muktasya—of the liberated; jñāna-avasthita—situated in knowledge; chetasaḥ—whose mind; yajñāya—as sacrifice; ācharataḥ—performing; karma—work; samagram—entirely; pravilīyate—dissolves
translation_english: The work of a man who is unattached to the modes of material nature and who is fully situated in transcendental knowledge merges entirely into transcendence.
deep_dive_text: Dissolving the 'Karmic Debt'. Krishna explains that when a person acts without a sense of 'I' and 'Mine,' the results of their actions don't create a future burden. They act, but the action leaves no footprint on their soul, effectively 'dissolving' into the divine consciousness.

original_script:
ब्रह्मार्पणं ब्रह्म हविर्ब्रह्माग्नौ ब्रह्मणा हुतम्।
ब्रह्मैव तेन गन्तव्यं ब्रह्मकर्मसमाधिना।।4.24।।
transliteration:
brahmārpaṇam brahma havir brahmāgnau brahmaṇā hutam
brahmaiva tena gantavyaṁ brahma-karma-samādhinā
word_meanings: brahma—the Divine; arpaṇam—the process of offering; brahma—the Divine; haviḥ—the offering; brahma-agnau—in the fire of the Divine; brahmaṇā—by the Divine; hutam—offered; brahma—the Divine; eva—only; tena—by him; gantavyam—to be reached; brahma-karma-samādhinā—by one who is absorbed in Divine action
translation_english: A person who is fully absorbed in Divine consciousness is sure to attain the Divine state because the offering is the Divine, the ritual is the Divine, and the fire is the Divine.
deep_dive_text: The 'Unity' Verse. This is a profound mantra used to sanctify food or work. It teaches that there is no separation between the actor, the action, and the goal. When you see the Divine in everything—the tools you use, the work you do, and the person you serve—your life becomes a continuous meditation (Samadhi).

original_script:
दैवमेवापरे यज्ञं योगिनः पर्युपासते।
ब्रह्माग्नावपरे यज्ञं यज्ञेनैवोपजुह्वति।।4.25।।
transliteration:
daivam evāpare yajñaṁ yoginaḥ paryupāsate
brahmāgnāv apare yajñaṁ yajñenaivopajuhvati
word_meanings: daivam—in worshiping the gods; eva—certainly; apare—some; yajñam—sacrifice; yoginaḥ—yogis; paryupāsate—worship perfectly; brahma-agnau—in the fire of the Supreme; apare—others; yajñam—sacrifice; yajñena—by sacrifice; eva—only; upajuhvati—offer
translation_english: Some yogis perfectly worship the demigods by offering sacrifices to them, and some offer sacrifice in the fire of the Supreme Brahman.
deep_dive_text: Diverse Paths to the Divine. Krishna acknowledges that people are at different stages of growth. Some perform external rituals (Daiva Yajna), while others perform internal rituals by offering their very identity into the 'fire' of universal consciousness. Both are valid steps toward the ultimate goal.

original_script:
श्रोत्रादीनीन्द्रियाण्यन्ये संयमाग्निषु जुह्वति।
शब्दादीन्विषयानन्य इन्द्रियाग्निषु जुह्वति।।4.26।।
transliteration:
śhrotrādīnīndriyāṇy anye saṁyamāgniṣhu juhvati
śhabdādīn viṣhayān anya indriyāganiṣhu juhvati
word_meanings: śhrotra-ādīni—hearing and other senses; indriyāṇi—senses; anye—others; saṁyama-agniṣhu—in the fires of restraint; juhvati—offer; śhabda-ādīn—sound and other objects; viṣhayān—objects of the senses; anye—others; indriya-agniṣhu—in the fires of the senses; juhvati—offer
translation_english: Some offer hearing and the other senses into the fire of restraint, and others offer sound and other objects of the senses into the fire of the senses.
deep_dive_text: The Sacrifice of Senses. Krishna describes two lifestyles: those who practice strict withdrawal of senses and those who live in the world but use their senses in a regulated, sacred way.

original_script:
सर्वाणीन्द्रियकर्माणि प्राणकर्माणि चापरे।
आत्मसंयमयोगाग्नौ जुह्वति ज्ञानदीपिते।।4.27।।
transliteration:
sarvāṇīndriya-karmāṇi prāṇa-karmāṇi chāpare
ātma-saṁyama-yogāgnau juhvati jñāna-dīpite
word_meanings: sarvāṇi—all; indriya—of the senses; karmāṇi—functions; prāṇa-karmāṇi—functions of the life breath; cha—also; apare—others; ātma-saṁyama—of self-control; yoga-agnau—in the fire of Yoga; juhvati—offer; jñāna-dīpite—illuminated by knowledge
translation_english: Others, who are interested in achieving self-realization through control of the mind and senses, offer the functions of all the senses, and of the life breath, as oblations into the fire of the self-controlled mind.
deep_dive_text: Mental Alchemy. This refers to deep 'Pranayama' and meditation. The practitioner offers their biological urges and even their breath into the fire of 'Atma-Samyama' (self-control), which is fueled by 'Jnana' (wisdom). It is the internal labor of the yogi.

original_script:
द्रव्ययज्ञास्तपोयज्ञा योगयज्ञास्तथापरे।
स्वाध्यायज्ञानयज्ञाश्च यतयः संशितव्रताः।।4.28।।
transliteration:
dravya-yajñās tapo-yajñā yoga-yajñās tathāpare
swādhyāya-jñāna-yajñāśh cha yatayaḥ saṁśhita-vratāḥ
word_meanings: dravya-yajñāḥ—sacrifice of possessions; tapaḥ-yajñāḥ—sacrifice in austerities; yoga-yajñāḥ—sacrifice in eightfold yoga; tathā—also; apare—others; swādhyāya—sacrifice in study of the Vedas; jñāna-yajñāḥ—sacrifice in transcendental knowledge; cha—also; yatayaḥ—enlightened; saṁśhita-vratāḥ—taken to strict vows
translation_english: Having accepted strict vows, some become enlightened by sacrificing their possessions, and others by performing severe austerities, by practicing the yoga of eightfold mysticism, or by studying the Vedas to advance in transcendental knowledge.
deep_dive_text: The Menu of Discipline. Krishna lists five types of seekers: 1. Charitable (possession sacrifice); 2. Ascetic (austerity); 3. Mystic (Yoga practice); 4. Academic (scriptural study); and 5. Philosophical (wisdom-seeking). Every person can find a 'Yajna' that fits their nature.

original_script:
सर्वेऽप्येते यज्ञविदो यज्ञक्षपितकल्मषाः।
यज्ञशिष्टामृतभुजो यान्ति ब्रह्म सनातनम्।।4.30।।
transliteration:
sarve ’py ete yajñavido yajña-kṣhapita-kalmaṣhāḥ
yajña-śhiṣhṭāmṛita-bhujo yānti brahma sanātanam
word_meanings: sarve—all; api—also; ete—those; yajña-vidaḥ—conversant with sacrifice; yajña-kṣhapita—purified by sacrifice; kalmaṣhāḥ—of sinful reactions; yajña-śhiṣhṭa—of the remnants of sacrifice; amṛita-bhujaḥ—those who taste the nectar; yānti—attain; brahma—the Supreme; sanātanam—eternal
translation_english: All these performers who know the meaning of sacrifice become cleansed of sinful reactions, and, having tasted the nectar of the remnants of sacrifice, they advance toward the supreme eternal atmosphere.
deep_dive_text: The Nectar of Work. Krishna promises that the 'remnants' (the peace and joy left over after a selfless act) are like nectar ($Amrita$). This nectar purifies the consciousness and eventually leads the seeker back to the Eternal Truth.

original_script: नायं लोकोऽस्त्ययज्ञस्य कुतोऽन्यः कुरुसत्तम।।4.31।।
transliteration: nāyaṁ loko ’sty ayajñasya kuto ’nyaḥ kuru-sattama
word_meanings: na—never; ayam—this; lokaḥ—planet/world; asti—there is; ayajñasya—for one who performs no sacrifice; kutaḥ—where is; anyaḥ—the other (world); kuru-sat-tama—O best among the Kurus
translation_english: O best of the Kuru dynasty, without sacrifice one can never live happily on this planet or in this life: what then of the next?
deep_dive_text: Sacrifice is Mandatory. Krishna argues that a life without giving/sacrifice is miserable even at a material level. Selfishness leads to isolation and stress in 'this' world. If you can't manage happiness here through giving, how can you expect to find it in a higher spiritual realm?

original_script:
एवं बहुविधा यज्ञा वितता ब्रह्मणो मुखे।
कर्मजान्विद्धि तान्सर्वानेवं ज्ञात्वा विमोक्ष्यसे।।4.32।।
transliteration:
evaṁ bahu-vidhā yajñā vitatā brahmaṇo mukhe
karma-jān viddhi tān sarvān evaṁ jñātvā vimokṣhyase
word_meanings: evam—thus; bahu-vidhāḥ—various; yajñāḥ—sacrifices; vitatāḥ—spread out; brahmaṇaḥ—of the Vedas/Supreme; mukhe—in the mouth; karma-jān—born of action; viddhi—know; tān—them; sarvān—all; evam—thus; jñātvā—knowing; vimokṣhyase—you shall be liberated
translation_english: All these different types of sacrifice are approved by the Vedas, and all of them are born of different types of work. Knowing them as such, you will become liberated.
deep_dive_text: Validation of Action. Krishna emphasizes that all these high spiritual states are 'born of action' ($Karma-jan$). You don't get there by thinking; you get there by working in the right spirit. This provides the ultimate justification for Arjuna to perform his duty.

original_script:
श्रेयान्द्रव्यमयाद्यज्ञाज्ज्ञानयज्ञः परन्तप।
सर्वं कर्माखिलं पार्थ ज्ञाने परिसमाप्यते।।4.33।।
transliteration:
śhreyān dravyamayād yajñāj jñāna-yajñaḥ parantapa
sarvaṁ karmākhilaṁ pārtha jñāne parisamāpyate
word_meanings: śhreyān—greater; dravya-mayāt—of material possessions; yajñāt—than sacrifice; jñāna-yajñaḥ—sacrifice in knowledge; parantapa—Arjun; sarvam—all; karma—work; akhilam—entire; pārtha—Arjun; jñāne—in knowledge; parisamāpyate—ends
translation_english: O chastiser of the enemy, the sacrifice performed in knowledge is greater than the mere sacrifice of material possessions. After all, O son of Pritha, all sacrifices of work culminate in transcendental knowledge.
deep_dive_text: The Goal of Work is Wisdom. While giving money or doing rituals is good, the 'Yajna of Knowledge' (studying the self and the truth) is superior. Why? Because the purpose of all action is to eventually reach a state of perfect understanding. Once you have the wisdom, the need for the 'tool' of action may change.

original_script:
तद्विद्धि प्रणिपातेन परिप्रश्नेन सेवया।
उपदेक्ष्यन्ति ते ज्ञानं ज्ञानिनस्तत्त्वदर्शिनः।।4.34।।
transliteration:
tad viddhi praṇipātena paripraśhnena sevayā
upadekṣhyanti te jñānaṁ jñāninastattva-darśhinaḥ
word_meanings: tat—that knowledge; viddhi—try to learn; praṇipātena—by surrendering to a spiritual master; paripraśhnena—by submissive inquiries; sevayā—by service; upadekṣhyanti—they will initiate; te—unto you; jñānam—knowledge; jñāninaḥ—the self-realized; tattva-darśhinaḥ—the seers of the truth
translation_english: Just try to learn the truth by approaching a spiritual master. Inquire from him submissively and render service unto him. The self-realized souls can impart knowledge unto you because they have seen the truth.
deep_dive_text: The Protocol for Wisdom. Krishna explains that transcendental knowledge isn't found in a search engine; it requires a relationship. He lists three requirements: 1. **Pranipat** (Humility); 2. **Pariprashna** (Sincere questioning); and 3. **Seva** (Service). This ensures the student is mentally ready to receive the truth without ego blocking the way.

original_script:
यज्ज्ञात्वा न पुनर्मोहमेवं यास्यसि पाण्डव।
येन भूतान्यशेषेण द्रक्ष्यस्यात्मन्यथो मयि।।4.35।।
transliteration:
yaj jñātvā na punar moham evaṁ yāsyasi pāṇḍava
yena bhūtāny aśheṣheṇa drakṣhyasy ātmany atho mayi
word_meanings: yat—which; jñātvā—knowing; na—never; punaḥ—again; moham—delusion; evam—in this way; yāsyasi—you shall go; pāṇḍava—Arjun; yena—by which; bhūtāni—living beings; aśheṣheṇa—all; drakṣhyasi—you will see; ātmani—in the self; atho—and; mayi—in Me
translation_english: Having obtained real knowledge from a self-realized soul, you will never again fall into such confusion, for by this knowledge you will see that all living beings are but part of the Supreme, or, in other words, that they are Mine.
deep_dive_text: The End of Delusion. Krishna promises that once you 'see' the underlying unity of all life, you can never be fooled by the 'separateness' of the material world again. You realize that every person you meet is a part of the same Divine energy, making hatred or fear impossible.

original_script:
अपि चेदसि पापेभ्यः सर्वेभ्यः पापकृत्तमः।
सर्वं ज्ञानप्लवेनैव वृजिनं सन्तरिष्यसि।।4.36।।
transliteration:
api ched asi pāpebhyaḥ sarvebhyaḥ pāpa-kṛittamaḥ
sarvaṁ jñāna-plavenaiva vṛijinaṁ santariṣhyasi
word_meanings: api—even; chet—if; asi—you are; pāpebhyaḥ—of sinners; sarvebhyaḥ—of all; pāpa-kṛit-tamaḥ—the most sinful; sarvam—all; jñāna-plavena—by the boat of knowledge; eva—certainly; vṛijinam—the ocean of miseries; santariṣhyasi—you will cross over
translation_english: Even if you are considered the most sinful of all sinners, when you are situated in the boat of transcendental knowledge you will be able to cross over the ocean of miseries.
deep_dive_text: The 'Boat of Knowledge'. This is an incredibly hopeful verse. Krishna says that your 'past' doesn't matter once you attain 'real-time' wisdom. Knowledge is like a boat that keeps you dry even if you are in the middle of a vast ocean of past mistakes (Karma).

original_script:
यथैधांसि समिद्धोऽग्निर्भस्मसात्कुरुतेऽर्जुन।
ज्ञानाग्निः सर्वकर्माणि भस्मसात्कुरुते तथा।।4.37।।
transliteration:
yathaidhāṁsi samiddho ’gnir bhasmasāt kurute ’rjuna
jñānāgniḥ sarva-karmāṇi bhasmasāt kurute tathā
word_meanings: yathā—as; edhāṁsi—firewood; samiddhaḥ—blazing; agniḥ—fire; bhasmasāt—to ashes; kurute—turns; arjuna—Arjun; jñāna-agniḥ—the fire of knowledge; sarva-karmāṇi—all reactions to material activities; bhasmasāt—to ashes; kurute—turns; tathā—similarly
translation_english: As a blazing fire turns firewood to ashes, O Arjuna, so does the fire of knowledge burn to ashes all reactions to material activities.
deep_dive_text: The Fire of Wisdom. In the previous verse, knowledge was a boat; now it is a fire. Krishna explains that wisdom actually 'consumes' the seeds of Karma. Once you truly understand your nature, the 'why' behind your past actions is understood, and their power to bind you to a future of suffering is destroyed.

original_script:
न हि ज्ञानेन सदृशं पवित्रमिह विद्यते।
तत्स्वयं योगसंसिद्धः कालेनात्मनि विन्दति।।4.38।।
transliteration:
na hi jñānena sadṛiśhaṁ pavitram iha vidyate
tat svayaṁ yoga-saṁsiddhaḥ kālenātmani vindati
word_meanings: na—never; hi—certainly; jñānena—with knowledge; sadṛiśham—comparison; pavitram—sanctified/pure; iha—in this world; vidyate—exists; tat—that; svayam—himself; yoga-saṁsiddhaḥ—one who is mature in Yoga; kālena—in course of time; ātmani—in himself; vindati—finds
translation_english: In this world, there is nothing so sublime and pure as transcendental knowledge. One who has become accomplished in the practice of Yoga enjoys this knowledge within himself in due course of time.
deep_dive_text: The Ultimate Purifier. Krishna states that knowledge is the 'gold standard' of purity. It doesn't come overnight; it is the 'fruit' of consistent practice ($Yoga-samsiddhah$). Eventually, the seeker doesn't need to look at books; they find the truth vibrating inside their own consciousness.

original_script:
श्रद्धावॉंल्लभते ज्ञानं तत्परः संयतेन्द्रियः।
ज्ञानं लब्ध्वा परां शान्तिमचिरेणाधिगच्छति।।4.39।।
transliteration:
śhraddhāvān labhate jñānaṁ tat-paraḥ saṁyatendriyaḥ
jñānaṁ labdhvā parāṁ śhāntim achireṇādhigachchhati
word_meanings: śhraddhā-vān—a faithful man; labhate—achieves; jñānam—knowledge; tat-paraḥ—very much attached to it; saṁyata—controlled; indriyaḥ—senses; jñānam—knowledge; labdhvā—having achieved; parām—transcendental; śhāntim—peace; achireṇa—very soon; adhigachchhati—attains
translation_english: A faithful man who is dedicated to transcendental knowledge and who subdues his senses is eligible to achieve such knowledge, and having achieved it he quickly attains the supreme spiritual peace.
deep_dive_text: The Three Pillars of Success: 1. **Shraddha** (Faith/Trust); 2. **Tat-parah** (Dedication); and 3. **Samyatendriyah** (Control of senses). If you have these three, Krishna promises 'Achirena'—that peace will come very, very soon.

original_script:
अज्ञश्चाश्रद्दधानश्च संशयात्मा विनश्यति।
नायं लोकोऽस्ति न परो न सुखं संशयात्मनः।।4.40।।
transliteration:
ajñaśh chāśhraddadhānaśh cha saṁśhayātmā vinaśhyati
nāyaṁ loko ’sti na paro na sukhaṁ saṁśhayātmanaḥ
word_meanings: ajñaḥ—ignorant; cha—and; aśhraddadhānaḥ—without faith; cha—and; saṁśhaya-ātmā—a doubting person; vinaśhyati—falls back; na—never; ayam—this; lokaḥ—world; asti—is; na—not; paraḥ—next; na—not; sukham—happiness; saṁśhaya-ātmanaḥ—for the doubting soul
translation_english: But ignorant and faithless persons who doubt the revealed scriptures do not attain God consciousness; they fall down. For the doubting soul there is happiness neither in this world nor in the next.
deep_dive_text: The Danger of Chronic Doubt. Krishna distinguishes between 'sincere inquiry' and 'cynical doubt'. A person who refuses to trust anything can never be happy because they have no foundation to stand on. Doubt is like a virus that eats away at your ability to experience either material or spiritual success.

original_script:
योगसंन्यस्तकर्माणं ज्ञानछिन्नसंशयम्।
आत्मवन्तं न कर्माणि निबध्नन्ति धनञ्जय।।4.41।।
transliteration:
yoga-sannyasta-karmāṇaṁ jñāna-chhinna-saṁśhayam
ātmavantaṁ na karmāṇi nibadnanti dhanañjaya
word_meanings: yoga—by devotional service; sannyasta—renounced; karmāṇam—actions; jñāna—by knowledge; chhinna—cut; saṁśhayam—doubts; ātma-vantam—situated in the self; na—never; karmāṇi—actions; nibadnanti—bind; dhanañjaya—Arjun
translation_english: One who acts in devotional service, renouncing the fruits of his actions, and whose doubts have been destroyed by transcendental knowledge, is situated freely in the self. Thus he is not bound by the reactions of work, O conqueror of riches.
deep_dive_text: The Formula for Freedom. This verse brings together the two themes of the chapter: **Yoga** (Action) and **Jnana** (Knowledge). When you work without ego AND your doubts are removed by wisdom, you become 'Atmavantam'—fully anchored in your true self. Nothing in the world can 'bind' you anymore.

original_script:
तस्मादज्ञानसम्भूतं हृत्स्थं ज्ञानअसिनात्मनः।
छित्त्वैनं संशयं योगमातिष्ठोत्तिष्ठ भारत।।4.42।।
transliteration:
tasmād ajñāna-sambhūtaṁ hṛit-sthaṁ jñānāsinātmanaḥ
chittvainaṁ saṁśhayaṁ yogam ātiṣhṭhottiṣhṭha bhārata
word_meanings: tasmāt—therefore; ajñāna-sambhūtam—born of ignorance; hṛit-stham—situated in the heart; jñāna—of knowledge; asinā—by the sword; ātmanaḥ—of the self; chittvā—cutting; enam—this; saṁśhayam—doubt; yogam—in Yoga; ātiṣhṭha—be situated; uttiṣhṭha—stand up; bhārata—Arjun
translation_english: Therefore the doubts which have arisen in your heart out of ignorance should be slashed by the weapon of knowledge. Armed with Yoga, O Bharata, stand up and fight.
deep_dive_text: The Sword of Knowledge ($Jnanasina$). Krishna ends the chapter with a final call to action. Doubt is an internal enemy living in the heart. He commands Arjuna to use the 'Sword of Wisdom' he has just received to slash that doubt once and for all. The final instruction is the same as in Chapter 2: **Uttishtha** (Stand up!).

original_script: ज्ञानकर्मसंन्यासयोगः
transliteration: Jñāna Karma Sanyāsa Yogaḥ
translation_english: Chapter 4: The Fire of Wisdom
deep_dive_text: Krishna reveals the history of the Gita as a timeless science. He explains his own divine nature as an Avatar who descends to restore balance when unrighteousness rises. He introduces the 'Sword of Knowledge,' which has the power to burn away all past karmic debt and cut through the bonds of doubt once and for all.

original_script: अथ पञ्चमोऽध्यायः - कर्मसंन्यासयोगः
transliteration: Karma Sanyāsa Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Karma—action; Sanyāsa—renunciation; Yogaḥ—the path of connection.
translation_english: Chapter 5 Summary: The Yoga of Renunciation
deep_dive_text: Summary: Arjuna is still torn between two paths: should he meditate in a forest or fight a war? Krishna reveals that the 'Renunciation of Action' and 'Action in Devotion' lead to the same goal, but the path of action is easier and more practical. A true 'Sanyasi' isn't someone who wears orange robes, but someone who works in the world without any sense of ownership or ego. This chapter teaches how to be 'in the world, but not of it,' like a lotus leaf that stays dry even in the middle of a pond.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
संन्यासं कर्मणां कृष्ण पुनर्योगं च शंससि।
यच्छ्रेय एतयोरेकं तन्मे ब्रूहि सुनिश्चितम्।।5.1।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
sannyāsaṁ karmaṇāṁ kṛiṣhṇa punar yogaṁ cha śhaṁsasi
yach chhreya etayor ekaṁ tan me brūhi su-niśhchitam
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjun said; sannyāsam—renunciation; karmaṇām—of actions; kṛiṣhṇa—Krishna; punaḥ—again; yogam—devotional action; cha—also; śhaṁsasi—You are praising; yat—which; śhreyaḥ—better; etayoḥ—of these two; ekam—one; tat—that; me—to me; brūhi—tell; su-niśhchitam—decisively
translation_english: Arjuna said: O Krishna, first You ask me to renounce work, and then again You recommend work with devotion. Now will You kindly tell me decisively which of the two is more beneficial?
deep_dive_text: The Conflict of Paths. Arjuna is confused by the subtle difference between 'quitting work' and 'working without ego'. To his mind, these seem like opposites. He is asking for a definitive choice between a life of contemplation and a life of active service.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
संन्यासः कर्मयोगश्च निःश्रेयसकरावुभौ।
तयोस्तु कर्मसंन्यासात्कर्मयोगो विशिष्यते।।5.2।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
sannyāsaḥ karma-yogaśh cha niḥśhreya-sakarāv ubhau
tayos tu karma-sannyāsāt karma-yogo viśhiṣhyate
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; sannyāsaḥ—renunciation of work; karma-yogaḥ—work in devotion; cha—also; niḥśhreya-sakarau—leading to the path of liberation; ubhau—both; tayoḥ—of the two; tu—but; karma-sannyāsāt—than the renunciation of work; karma-yogaḥ—work in devotion; viśhiṣhyate—is better
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: Both the renunciation of work and work in devotion are good for liberation. But, of the two, work in devotional service is better than renunciation of work.
deep_dive_text: The Verdict. Krishna states that while both paths lead to the same destination, **Karma Yoga** (active service) is superior. Why? Because for most humans, sitting still and controlling the mind is incredibly difficult, while working for a higher cause naturally purifies the mind through action.

original_script:
ज्ञेयः स नित्यसंन्यासी यो न द्वेष्टि न काङ्क्षति।
निर्द्वन्द्वो हि महाबाहो सुखं बन्धात्प्रमुच्यते।।5.3।।
transliteration:
jñeyaḥ sa nitya-sannyāsī yo na dveṣhṭi na kāṅkṣhati
nirdvandvo hi mahā-bāho sukhaṁ bandhāt pramuchyate
word_meanings: jñeyaḥ—should be known; saḥ—he; nitya—always; sannyāsī—renunciant; yaḥ—who; na—neither; dveṣhṭi—abhors; na—nor; kāṅkṣhati—desires; nirdvandvaḥ—free from all dualities; hi—certainly; mahā-bāho—O mighty-armed one; sukham—happily; bandhāt—from bondage; pramuchyate—is completely liberated
translation_english: One who neither hates nor desires the fruits of his activities is known to be always renounced. Such a person, free from all dualities, easily overcomes material bondage and is completely liberated, O mighty-armed Arjuna.
deep_dive_text: The Definition of a True Renunciant. Krishna redefines 'Sanyas'. You don't need to change your clothes or your location to be a monk; you just need to change your heart. If you can live in the world without being driven by 'I want this' or 'I hate that,' you are already a 'Nitya-Sanyasi' (permanent renunciant).

original_script:
साङ्ख्ययोगौ पृथग्बालाः प्रवदन्ति न पण्डिताः।
एकंमप्यास्थितः सम्यगुभयोर्विन्दते फलम्।।5.4।।
transliteration:
sāṅkhya-yogau pṛithag bālāḥ pravadanti na paṇḍitāḥ
ekaṁ apy āsthitaḥ samyag ubhayor vindate phalam
word_meanings: sāṅkhya—analytical study; yogau—and work in devotion; pṛithak—different; bālāḥ—the less intelligent; pravadanti—say; na—never; paṇḍitāḥ—the learned; ekam—in one; api—even; āsthitaḥ—being situated; samyak—completely; ubhayoḥ—of both; vindate—enjoys; phalam—the result
translation_english: Only the ignorant speak of analytical study of the material world [Sankhya] as being different from devotional service [Karma Yoga]. Those who are actually learned say that he who applies himself well to one of these paths achieves the results of both.
deep_dive_text: The Unity of Logic and Action. Krishna calls those who think these paths are different 'Balah' (childlike/ignorant). Whether you reach the truth through deep philosophical analysis (Sankhya) or through selfless work (Yoga), the 'view from the top' is exactly the same.

original_script:
यत्साङ्ख्यैः प्राप्यते स्थानं तद्यौगैरपि गम्यते।
एकं साङ्ख्यं च योगं च यः पश्यति स पश्यति।।5.5।।
transliteration:
yat sāṅkhyaiḥ prāpyate sthānaṁ tad yogair api gamyate
ekaṁ sāṅkhyaṁ cha yogaṁ cha yaḥ paśhyati sa paśhyati
word_meanings: yat—what; sāṅkhyaiḥ—by the followers of Sankhya; prāpyate—is achieved; sthānam—place; tat—that; yogaiḥ—by the followers of Yoga; api—also; gamyate—is attained; ekam—one; sāṅkhyam—analytical study; cha—and; yogam—action; cha—and; yaḥ—who; paśhyati—sees; saḥ—he; paśhyati—actually sees
translation_english: One who knows that the position reached by means of analytical study can also be attained by devotional service, and who therefore sees analytical study and devotional service to be on the same level, sees things as they are.
deep_dive_text: True Vision. This verse emphasizes that 'Knowledge' and 'Action' are not two separate things but two sides of a single reality. To 'see' correctly is to realize that the silent meditation of the sage and the active duty of the warrior can both be expressions of the same Divine connection.

original_script:
संन्यासस्तु महाबाहो दुःखमाप्तुमयोगतः।
योगयुक्तो मुनिर्ब्रह्म नचिरेणाधिगच्छति।।5.6।।
transliteration:
sannyāsas tu mahā-bāho duḥkham āptum ayogataḥ
yoga-yukto munir brahma na chireṇādhigachchhati
word_meanings: sannyāsaḥ—renunciation; tu—but; mahā-bāho—O mighty-armed one; duḥkham—distress; āptum—to derive; ayogataḥ—without devotional service; yoga-yuktaḥ—one engaged in Yoga; muniḥ—a sage; brahma—the Supreme; na chireṇa—without delay; adhigachchhati—attains
translation_english: Merely renouncing all activities without engaging in the devotional service of the Lord cannot make one happy. But a thoughtful person engaged in devotional service can reach the Supreme without delay.
deep_dive_text: The Risk of 'Empty' Renunciation. Krishna warns that 'Sanyas' (quitting work) without 'Yoga' (devotion/service) leads only to distress. If you don't have something higher to focus on, your mind will just eat itself in the silence. Service provides the structure that allows a seeker to reach God 'Na chirena' (without delay).

original_script:
योगयुक्तो विशुद्धात्मा विजितात्मा जितेन्द्रियः।
सर्वभूतात्मभूतात्मा कुर्वन्नपि न लिप्यते।।5.7।।
transliteration:
yoga-yukto viśhuddhātmā vijitātmā jitendriyaḥ
sarva-bhūtātma-bhūtātmā kurvann api na lipyate
word_meanings: yoga-yuktaḥ—engaged in Yoga; viśhuddha-ātmā—a purified soul; vijita-ātmā—self-controlled; jita-indriyaḥ—having conquered the senses; sarva-bhūta—of all living entities; ātma-bhūta-ātmā—whose soul is the soul; kurvan api—although working; na—never; lipyate—is entangled
translation_english: One who works in devotion, who is a pure soul, and who controls his mind and senses is dear to everyone, and everyone is dear to him. Though always working, such a man is never entangled.
deep_dive_text: The Magnetic Personality of a Yogi. A person who has mastered their senses ($Jitendriyah$) and feels a connection to the soul of all beings ($Sarva-bhutatma$) becomes naturally lovable. They don't have personal 'enemies' because they see their own self in everyone. Their work becomes a light for others rather than a trap for themselves.

original_script:
नैव किञ्चित्करोमीति युक्तो मन्येत तत्त्ववित्।
पश्यञ्शृण्वन्स्पृशञ्जिघ्रन्नश्नन्गच्छन्स्वपञ्श्वसन्।।5.8।।
transliteration:
naiva kiñchit karomīti yukto manyeta tattva-vit
paśhyañ śhṛiṇvan spṛiśhañ jighrann aśhnan gachchhan svapañ śhvasan
word_meanings: na—never; eva—certainly; kiñchit—anything; karomi—I do; iti—thus; yuktaḥ—engaged in Yoga; manyeta—thinks; tattva-vit—knower of truth; paśhyan—seeing; śhṛiṇvan—hearing; spṛiśhan—touching; jighran—smelling; aśhnan—eating; gachchhan—moving; svapan—dreaming; śhvasan—breathing
translation_english: A person in the divine consciousness, although engaged in seeing, hearing, touching, smelling, eating, moving about, sleeping and breathing, always knows within himself that he actually does nothing at all.
deep_dive_text: The 'Divine Non-Doer'. This is a high state of awareness. The Yogi realizes that the 'Body' is performing all these biological functions like a machine, while the 'Self' (the Soul) is merely the silent observer. Even while eating or walking, they remain anchored in the truth that the Spirit is motionless and untouched.

original_script:
प्रलपन्विसृजन्गृह्णन्नुन्मिषन्निमिषन्नपि।
इन्द्रियाणीन्द्रियार्थेषु वर्तन्त इति धारयन्।।5.9।।
transliteration:
pralapan visṛijan gṛihṇann unmiṣhan nimiṣhann api
indriyāṇīndriyārtheṣhu vartanta iti dhārayan
word_meanings: pralapan—talking; visṛijan—giving up/releasing; gṛihṇan—accepting; unmiṣhan—opening eyes; nimiṣhan—closing eyes; api—also; indriyāṇi—the senses; indriya-artheṣhu—in the sense objects; vartante—are acting; iti—thus; dhārayan—concluding
translation_english: A person in divine consciousness, while speaking, releasing, grasping, or opening and closing their eyes, always knows that only the material senses are engaged with their objects.
deep_dive_text: The Anatomy of Awareness. This completes the list started in Verse 8. It covers even the most involuntary actions like blinking. The Yogi lives with a dual-consciousness: the body performs its biological program, while the inner self remains a 'Witness' ($Sakshi$), untouched by the mechanics of the material world.

original_script:
ब्रह्मण्याधाय कर्माणि सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा करोति यः।
लिप्यते न स पापेन पद्मपत्रमिवाम्भसा।।5.10।।
transliteration:
brahmaṇy ādhāya karmāṇi saṅgaṁ tyaktvā karoti yaḥ
lipyate na sa pāpena padma-patram ivāmbhasā
word_meanings: brahmaṇi—unto the Supreme; ādhāya—resigning; karmāṇi—all works; saṅgam—attachment; tyaktvā—giving up; karoti—performs; yaḥ—who; lipyate—is affected; na—never; saḥ—he; pāpena—by sin; padma-patram—a lotus leaf; iva—like; ambhasā—by water
translation_english: One who performs his duty without attachment, surrendering the results unto the Supreme Lord, is unaffected by sinful action, as the lotus leaf is untouched by water.
deep_dive_text: The Lotus Leaf Analogy. This is the iconic image of Chapter 5. A lotus grows in muddy water, and water droplets sit on its leaves, yet the leaf itself never gets 'wet'. Similarly, a Yogi lives in the middle of a messy, complicated world, but because they have surrendered their ego, the 'mud' of the world never sticks to their soul.

original_script:
कायेन मनसा बुद्ध्या केवलैरिन्द्रियैरपि।
योगिनः कर्म कुर्वन्ति सङ्गं त्यक्त्वात्मशुद्धये।।5.11।।
transliteration:
kāyena manasā buddhyā kevalair indriyair api
yoginaḥ karma kurvanti saṅgaṁ tyaktvātma-śhuddhaye
word_meanings: kāyena—with the body; manasā—with the mind; buddhyā—with the intellect; kevalaiḥ—only; indriyaiḥ—with the senses; api—even; yoginaḥ—the yogis; karma—action; kurvanti—perform; saṅgam—attachment; tyaktvā—giving up; ātma-śhuddhaye—for the purpose of purification
translation_english: The yogis, abandoning attachment, act with body, mind, intelligence and even with the senses, only for the purpose of purification.
deep_dive_text: Work as Therapy. Krishna explains that for a seeker, work is a tool for 'Atma-shuddhaye' (self-purification). Every task is an opportunity to scrub away the ego. By acting without 'clinging' to the result, the yogi uses their daily life as a spiritual laboratory to become cleaner and more transparent in spirit.

original_script:
युक्तः कर्मफलं त्यक्त्वा शान्तिमाप्नोति नैष्ठिकीम्।
अयुक्तः कामकारेण फले सक्तो निबध्यते।।5.12।।
transliteration:
yuktaḥ karma-phalaṁ tyaktvā śhāntim āpnoti naiṣhṭhikīm
ayuktaḥ kāma-kāreṇa phale sakto nibadhyate
word_meanings: yuktaḥ—one engaged in Yoga; karma-phalam—results of action; tyaktvā—giving up; śhāntim—peace; āpnoti—attains; naiṣhṭhikīm—unwavering; ayuktaḥ—one not connected; kāma-kāreṇa—driven by desire; phale—in the result; saktaḥ—attached; nibadhyate—becomes entangled
translation_english: The steadily devoted soul attains unadulterated peace because he offers the result of all activities to Me; whereas a person who is not in union with the Divine, who is greedy for the fruits of his labor, becomes entangled.
deep_dive_text: The Source of Peace vs. Anxiety. Krishna contrasts two types of workers. The 'Yukta' (the connected) offers up the result and finds 'Naishtikim Shantim' (deep, permanent peace). The 'Ayukta' (the disconnected) is a slave to their desires and is constantly stressed by the 'What if I fail?' thought, which creates a karmic prison.

original_script:
सर्वकर्माणि मनसा संन्यस्यास्ते सुखं वशी।
नवद्वारे पुरे देही नैव कुर्वन्न कारयन्।।5.13।।
transliteration:
sarva-karmāṇi manasā sannyasyāste sukhaṁ vaśhī
nava-dvāre pure dehī naiva kurvan na kārayan
word_meanings: sarva—all; karmāṇi—actions; manasā—by the mind; sannyasya—renouncing; āste—remains; sukham—happily; vaśhī—controlled; nava-dvāre—in the nine-gated; pure—in the city; dehī—the 'Dehi' (tenant/citizen soul); na—never; eva—certainly; kurvan—doing; na—nor; kārayan—causing to be done
translation_english: When the Self-controlled tenant (*Dehi*) mentally renounces the fruits of all actions, they reside happily in the city of nine gates (the body), neither acting as the primary agent nor inducing others to act.
deep_dive_text: The metaphor of the 'City of Nine Gates' reflects Upanishadic influence. The soul is presented as the 'Dehi'—a citizen or tenant of the physical structure—maintaining a position of observation over the body's mechanical activities.

original_script:
न कर्तृत्वं न कर्माणि लोकस्य सृजति प्रभुः।
न कर्मफलसंयोगं स्वभावस्तु प्रवर्तते।।5.14।।
transliteration:
na kartṛitvaṁ na karmāṇi lokasya sṛijati prabhuḥ
na karma-phala-saṅyogaṁ swabhāvas tu pravartate
word_meanings: na—never; kartṛitvam—authorship/sense of doership; na—never; karmāṇi—actions; lokasya—of the world; sṛijati—creates; prabhuḥ—the Lord of the city (soul); na—never; karma-phala—results of actions; saṅyogam—connection; swabhāvaḥ—nature; tu—but; pravartate—enacts
translation_english: The embodied spirit, master of the city of his body, does not create activities, nor does he induce people to act, nor does he create the fruits of action. All this is enacted by the modes of material nature.
deep_dive_text: Nature as the Engine. Krishna clarifies that the Soul doesn't 'micro-manage' the body. 'Swabhava' (nature/conditioning) is what drives our actions and connects them to results. Realizing this removes the ego's false pride of 'I did this' and the false guilt of 'I messed this up'.

original_script:
नादत्ते कस्यचित्पापं न चैव सुकृतं विभुः।
अज्ञानेनावृतं ज्ञानं तेन मुह्यन्ति जन्तवः।।5.15।।
transliteration:
nādatte kasyachit pāpaṁ na chaiva sukṛitaṁ vibhuḥ
ajñānenāvṛitaṁ jñānaṁ tena muhyanti jantavaḥ
word_meanings: na—never; ādatte—accepts; kasyachit—anyone’s; pāpam—sin; na—never; cha—also; eva—certainly; sukṛitam—pious deeds; vibhuḥ—the Supreme Lord; ajñānena—by ignorance; āvṛitam—covered; jñānam—knowledge; tena—by that; muhyanti—are bewildered; jantavaḥ—living beings
translation_english: The Supreme Lord does not assume anyone’s sinful or pious activities. Embodied beings, however, are bewildered because of the ignorance which covers their real knowledge.
deep_dive_text: The Neutrality of God. Krishna explains that the Divine doesn't 'take sides' in our personal Karma. If we suffer, it's not because God is punishing us; it's because our own 'Ignorance' ($Ajñāna$) is covering our wisdom, leading us to make choices that bind us. The sun shines on everyone, but if you close your eyes, you are in darkness.

original_script:
ज्ञानेन तु तदज्ञानं येषां नाशितमात्मनः।
तेषामादित्यवज्ज्ञानं प्रकाशयति तत्परम्।।5.16।।
transliteration:
jñānena tu tad ajñānaṁ yeṣhāṁ nāśhitam ātmanaḥ
teṣhām ādityavaj jñānaṁ prakāśhayati tat param
word_meanings: jñānena—by knowledge; tu—but; tat—that; ajñānam—ignorance; yeṣhām—whose; nāśhitam—is destroyed; ātmanaḥ—of the self; teṣhām—for them; āditya-vat—like the rising sun; jñānam—knowledge; prakāśhayati—reveals; tat param—the Supreme
translation_english: When, however, one is enlightened with the knowledge by which nescience is destroyed, then his knowledge reveals everything, as the sun enlightens everything in the daytime.
deep_dive_text: The Sunrise of the Soul. Once you apply 'Jnana' (wisdom) to your life, the ignorance simply vanishes. You don't have to 'fight' the darkness; you just have to turn on the light. This wisdom reveals the 'Tat Param' (The Supreme) as clearly as the sun reveals the landscape.

original_script:
तद्बुद्धयस्तदात्मानस्तन्निष्ठास्तत्परायणाः।
गच्छन्त्यपुनरावृत्तिं ज्ञाननिर्धूतकल्मषाः।।5.17।।
transliteration:
tad-buddhayas tad-ātmānas tan-niṣhṭhās tat-parāyaṇāḥ
gachchha nty apunar-āvṛittiṁ jñāna-nirdhūta-kalmaṣhāḥ
word_meanings: tat-buddhayaḥ—those whose intelligence is fixed in the Supreme; tat-ātmānaḥ—whose minds are absorbed in the Supreme; tat-niṣhṭhāḥ—whose faith is in the Supreme; tat-parāyaṇāḥ—whose goal is the Supreme; gachchhanti—attain; apunaḥ-āvṛittim—liberation from rebirth; jñāna—by knowledge; nirdhūta—cleansed; kalmaṣhāḥ—of sins
translation_english: When one’s intelligence, mind, faith and refuge are all fixed in the Supreme, then one becomes fully cleansed of misgivings through complete knowledge and thus proceeds straight on the path of liberation.
deep_dive_text: The Four-Fold Focus. Krishna lists what needs to be aligned: Intellect, Mind, Faith, and Goal. When all these point toward the Divine, the 'Kalmaṣhāḥ' (misgivings/sins) are washed away by the sheer force of knowledge, and the cycle of rebirth ends.

original_script:
विद्याविनयसम्पन्ने ब्राह्मणे गवि हस्तिनि।
शुनि चैव श्वपाके च पण्डिताः समदर्शिनः।।5.18।।
transliteration:
vidyā-vinaya-sampanne brāhmaṇe gavi hastini
śhuni chaiva śhva-pāke cha paṇḍitāḥ sama-darśhinaḥ
word_meanings: vidyā—knowledge; vinaya—humility; sampanne—equipped with; brāhmaṇe—in a priest; gavi—in a cow; hastini—in an elephant; śhuni—in a dog; cha—and; eva—certainly; śhva-pāke—in a dog-eater/outcaste; cha—also; paṇḍitāḥ—the learned; sama-darśhinaḥ—see with equal vision
translation_english: The humble sages, by virtue of true knowledge, see with equal vision a learned and gentle brahmana, a cow, an elephant, a dog and a dog-eater [outcaste].
deep_dive_text: Equal Vision ($Sama-darśhinaḥ$). This is the hallmark of a sage. They don't see the 'labels' of society or the 'species' of the body. They see the same divine Spark (the Soul) residing in a scholar, an animal, and a beggar. True wisdom is the death of prejudice.

original_script:
इहैव तैर्जितः सर्गो येषां साम्ये स्थितं मनः।
निर्दोषं हि समं ब्रह्म तस्माद्ब्रह्मणि ते स्थिताः।।5.19।।
transliteration:
ihaiva tair jitaḥ sargo yeṣhāṁ sāmye sthitaṁ manaḥ
nirdoṣhaṁ hi samaṁ brahma tasmād brahmaṇi te sthitāḥ
word_meanings: iha—in this life; eva—certainly; taiḥ—by them; jitaḥ—conquered; sargaḥ—birth and death; yeṣhām—whose; sāmye—in equanimity; sthitam—situated; manaḥ—mind; nirdoṣham—flawless; hi—certainly; samam—equal; brahma—the Supreme; tasmāt—therefore; brahmaṇi—in the Supreme; te—they; sthitāḥ—are situated
translation_english: Those whose minds are established in sameness and equanimity have already conquered the conditions of birth and death. They are flawless like Brahman, and thus they are already situated in Brahman.
deep_dive_text: Victory in 'This' Life. Krishna makes a bold promise: You don't have to wait for death to be liberated. If you can achieve 'Samya' (equanimity) right now, you have already 'conquered' the world. Being steady in the face of change is the same as being Divine.

original_script:
न प्रहृष्येत्प्रियं प्राप्य नोद्विजेत्प्राप्य चाप्रियम्।
स्थिरबुद्धिरसम्मूढो ब्रह्मविद्ब्रह्मणि स्थितः।।5.20।।
transliteration:
na prahṛiṣhyet priyaṁ prāpya nodvijet prāpya chāpriyam
sthira-buddhir asammūḍho brahma-vid brahmaṇi sthitaḥ
word_meanings: na—never; prahṛiṣhyet—rejoices; priyam—pleasant; prāpya—obtaining; na—never; udvijet—becomes agitated; prāpya—obtaining; cha—also; apriyam—unpleasant; sthira-buddhiḥ—steady intellect; asammūḍhaḥ—unbewildered; brahma-vit—knower of the Supreme; brahmaṇi—in the Supreme; sthitaḥ—situated
translation_english: A person who neither rejoices upon achieving something pleasant nor laments upon obtaining something unpleasant, who is self-intelligent, who is unbewildered, and who knows the science of God, is already situated in transcendence.
deep_dive_text: The Balanced Intellect ($Sthira-buddhiḥ$). This verse defines the 'behavior' of a liberated soul. They aren't cold or robotic, but they have a 'shock absorber' for life's volatility. They don't let external 'Likes' or 'Dislikes' shake their internal peace.

original_script:
बाह्यस्पर्शेष्वसक्तात्मा विन्दत्यात्मनि यत्सुखम्।
स ब्रह्मयोगयुक्तात्मा सुखमक्षयमश्नुते।।5.21।।
transliteration:
bāhya-sparśheṣhv asaktātmā vindaty ātmani yat sukham
sa brahma-yoga-yuktātmā sukham akṣhayam aśhnute
word_meanings: bāhya-sparśheṣhu—in external sense contacts; asakta-ātmā—unattached soul; vindati—finds; ātmani—in the self; yat—which; sukham—happiness; saḥ—he; brahma-yoga—concentration on the Supreme; yukta-ātmā—self-connected; sukham—happiness; akṣhayam—unlimited/endless; aśhnute—enjoys
translation_english: Such a liberated person is not attracted to material sense pleasure but is always in trance, enjoying the pleasure within. In this way, the self-realized person enjoys unlimited happiness, for he concentrates on the Supreme.
deep_dive_text: Unlimited Happiness ($Akṣhayam Sukham$). Krishna explains that sensory pleasures have a beginning and an end (and usually lead to pain). Internal spiritual joy, however, is 'Akshayam'—it never decays or runs out. The more you connect to the Self, the less you need external 'fixes' for happiness.

original_script:
ये हि संस्पर्शजा भोगा दुःखयोनय एव ते।
आद्यन्तवन्तः कौन्तेय न तेषु रमते बुधः।।5.22।।
transliteration:
ye hi saṁsparśha-jā bhogā duḥkha-yonaya eva te
ādy-antavantaḥ kaunteya na teṣhu ramate budhaḥ
word_meanings: ye—those; hi—certainly; saṁsparśha-jāḥ—born of contact with the material senses; bhogāḥ—enjoyments; duḥkha—misery; yonayaḥ—sources of; eva—certainly; te—they are; ādi—beginning; anta-vantaḥ—having an end; kaunteya—Arjun; na—never; teṣhu—in those; ramate—takes delight; budhaḥ—the intelligent person
translation_english: An intelligent person does not take part in the sources of misery, which are due to contact with the material senses. O son of Kunti, such pleasures have a beginning and an end, and so the wise man does not delight in them.
deep_dive_text: The Trap of Instant Gratification. Krishna calls sensory pleasures 'Duḥkha-yonaya' (wombs of misery). Because they are 'Ady-antavantah' (having a start and an end), the 'high' is always followed by a 'low.' A wise user of this app learns to look for a deeper, more stable satisfaction that doesn't rely on flickering external objects.

original_script:
शक्नोतीहैव यः सोढुं प्राक्शरीरविमोक्षणात्।
कामक्रोधोद्भवं वेगं स युक्तः स सुखी नरः।।5.23।।
transliteration:
śhaknotīhaiva yaḥ soḍhuṁ prāk śharīra-vimokṣhaṇāt
kāma-krodhodbhavaṁ vegaṁ sa yuktaḥ sa sukhī naraḥ
word_meanings: śhaknoti—is able; iha eva—in this life; yaḥ—who; soḍhum—to tolerate; prāk—before; śharīra—the body; vimokṣhaṇāt—quitting; kāma—desire; krodha—and anger; udbhavam—generated from; vegam—urges; saḥ—he; yuktaḥ—in trance; saḥ—he; sukhī—happy; naraḥ—human being
translation_english: Before giving up this present body, if one is able to tolerate the urges of the material senses and check the force of desire and anger, he is well situated and is happy in this world.
deep_dive_text: Taming the Urge ($Vegam$). Krishna uses the word 'Vega' (force/velocity) to describe how desire and anger hit us. They are like emotional storms. The one who can 'tolerate' ($Sodhum$) these impulses without acting on them is defined as 'Sukhi'—truly happy. It’s about emotional regulation as a path to freedom.

original_script:
योऽन्तःसुखोऽन्तरारामस्तथान्तर्ज्योतिरेव यः।
स योगी ब्रह्मनिर्वाणम् ब्रह्मभूतोऽधिगच्छति।।5.24।।
transliteration:
yo ’ntaḥ-sukho ’ntar-ārāmas tathāntar-jyotir eva yaḥ
sa yogī brahma-nirvāṇaṁ brahma-bhūto ’dhigachchhati
word_meanings: yaḥ—who; antaḥ-sukhaḥ—happy from within; antar-ārāmaḥ—rejoicing within; tathā—as well as; antar-jyotiḥ—illuminated within; eva—certainly; yaḥ—who; saḥ—he; yogī—a yogi; brahma-nirvāṇam—liberation in the Supreme; brahma-bhūtaḥ—being self-realized; adhigachchhati—attains
translation_english: One whose happiness is within, who is active within, and who rejoices within and is illuminated within, is actually the perfect mystic. He is liberated in the Supreme, and ultimately he attains the Supreme.
deep_dive_text: The Internal Compass ($Antaḥ-sukhaḥ$). Krishna explains that a true yogi has found a 'wireless' connection to joy. They don't need external entertainment or validation because their source of light and happiness is entirely internal. They have reached the state of 'Brahma-bhuta,' where their individual consciousness merges with the universal.

original_script:
लभन्ते ब्रह्मनिर्वाणमृषयः क्षीणकल्मषाः।
छिन्नद्वैधा यतात्मानः सर्वभूतहिते रताः।।5.25।।
transliteration:
labhante brahma-nirvāṇam ṛiṣhayaḥ kṣhīṇa-kalmaṣhāḥ
chhinna-dvaidhā yatātmānaḥ sarva-bhūta-hite ratāḥ
word_meanings: labhante—achieve; brahma-nirvāṇam—liberation in the Supreme; ṛiṣhayaḥ—those who are active within; kṣhīṇa-kalmaṣhāḥ—who are devoid of all sins; chhinna—having torn away; dvaidhāḥ—duality; yata-ātmānaḥ—self-engaged; sarva-bhūta—for all living entities; hite—in welfare; ratāḥ—engaged
translation_english: Those who are beyond the dualities that arise from doubts, whose minds are engaged within, who are always busy working for the welfare of all living beings, and who are free from all sins achieve liberation in the Supreme.
deep_dive_text: Compassion as a Sign of Wisdom. A person who is 'internally' liberated doesn't become selfish; instead, they naturally engage in 'Sarva-bhuta-hite'—the welfare of all beings. Because they have no personal needs, their entire energy is spent making the world better for others.

original_script:
कामक्रोधवियुक्तानां यतीनां यतचेतसाम्।
अभितो ब्रह्मनिर्वाणं वर्तते विदितात्मनाम्।।5.26।।
transliteration:
kāma-krodha-viyuktānāṁ yatīnāṁ yata-chetasām
abhito brahma-nirvāṇaṁ vartate viditātmanām
word_meanings: kāma—from desire; krodha—and anger; viyuktānām—of those who are freed; yatīnām—of the saintly persons; yata-chetasām—who have full control over the mind; abhitaḥ—assured in the near future; brahma-nirvāṇam—liberation in the Supreme; vartate—is there; vidita-ātmanām—of those who are self-realized
translation_english: Those who are free from anger and all material desires, who are self-realized, self-disciplined and constantly endeavoring for perfection, are assured of liberation in the Supreme in the very near future.
deep_dive_text: The Nearness of Peace ($Abhitaḥ$). For those who have mastered the twin storms of Desire ($Kāma$) and Anger ($Krodha$), peace is not a distant dream; it is 'Abhitah'—encompassing them on all sides. It shows that spiritual liberation is a present reality, not just an afterlife reward.

original_script:
स्पर्शान्कृत्वा बहिर्बाह्यांश्चक्षुश्चैवान्तरे भ्रुवोः।
प्राणापानौ समौ कृत्वा नासाभ्यन्तरचारिणौ।।5.27।।
transliteration:
sparśhān kṛitvā bahir bāhyānśh chakṣhuśh chaivāntare bhruvoḥ
prāṇāpānau samau kṛitvā nāsābhyantara-chāriṇau
word_meanings: sparśhān—external sense objects; kṛitvā—keeping; bahiḥ—outside; bāhyān—external; chakṣhuḥ—eyes; cha—also; eva—certainly; antare—between; bhruvoḥ—the eyebrows; prāṇa-apānau—upward and downward breaths; samau—equal; kṛitvā—making; nāsa-abhyantara—within the nostrils; chāriṇau—moving
translation_english: Shutting out all external sense objects, keeping the eyes and vision concentrated between the two eyebrows, suspending the inward and outward breaths within the nostrils...
deep_dive_text: The Mechanics of Focus. Krishna introduces the physical posture of meditation ($Aṣhṭāṅga$ $Yoga$). By fixing the gaze at the 'third eye' and balancing the 'Prana' (in-breath) and 'Apana' (out-breath), the yogi physically stabilizes the nervous system to prepare for deep mental silence.

original_script:
यतेन्द्रियमनोबुद्धिर्मुनिर्मोक्षपरायणः।
विगतच्छाभयक्रोधो यः सदा मुक्त एव सः।।5.28।।
transliteration:
yatendriya-mano-buddhir munir mokṣha-parāyaṇaḥ
vigatechchhā-bhaya-krodho yaḥ sadā mukta eva saḥ
word_meanings: yata—controlled; indriya—senses; manaḥ—mind; buddhiḥ—intellect; muniḥ—the sage; mokṣha—liberation; parāyaṇaḥ—being dedicated to; vigata—discarded; ichchhā—desire; bhaya—fear; krodhaḥ—anger; yaḥ—who; sadā—always; muktaḥ—liberated; eva—certainly; saḥ—he
translation_english: ...and thus controlling the mind, senses and intelligence, the transcendentalist seeking liberation becomes free from desire, fear and anger. One who is always in this state is certainly liberated.
deep_dive_text: The State of Eternal Freedom ($Sadā Muktaḥ$). This verse concludes the technical instruction. When the hierarchy of Senses -> Mind -> Intellect is aligned toward liberation, and the 'poisons' of fear and anger are gone, the person is not 'becoming' free; they are *already* free ($Sadā$ $Muktaḥ$).

original_script:
भोक्तारं यज्ञतपसां सर्वलोकमहेश्वरम्।
सुहृदं सर्वभूतानां ज्ञात्वा मां शान्तिमृच्छति।।5.29।।
transliteration:
bhoktāraṁ yajña-tapasāṁ sarva-loka-maheśhvaram
suhṛidaṁ sarva-bhūtānāṁ jñātvā māṁ śhāntim ṛichchhati
word_meanings: bhoktāram—the beneficiary; yajña—of sacrifices; tapasām—and penances; sarva-loka—of all worlds; mahā-īśhvaram—the Supreme Lord; su-hṛidam—the benefactor/friend; sarva-bhūtānām—of all living entities; jñātvā—knowing; mām—Me; śhāntim—peace; ṛichchhati—attains
translation_english: A person in full consciousness of Me, knowing Me to be the ultimate beneficiary of all sacrifices and penances, the Supreme Lord of all planets and demigods, and the benefactor and well-wisher of all living entities, attains peace from the pangs of material miseries.
deep_dive_text: The Peace Formula ($Śhāntim Ṛichchhati$). This is the concluding verse of Chapter 5. It provides three realizations for total peace: 1. God is the ultimate 'Enjoyer' of all work; 2. God is the 'Owner' of all that exists; 3. God is the 'Best Friend' ($Suhridam$) of every living being. When you realize the Universe is managed by a friend, all anxiety vanishes.

original_script: अथ षष्ठोऽध्यायः - आत्मसंयमयोगः
transliteration: Ātma Saṁyama Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Ātma—self; Saṁyama—control/meditation; Yogaḥ—the path of synthesis.
translation_english: Chapter 6 Summary: The Yoga of Meditation
deep_dive_text: This chapter codifies the physical and mental techniques for meditation (*Dhyana*). It outlines proto-Yoga practices, such as choosing a clean place and sitting on 'kusha' grass, while emphasizing that the mind can be either the soul's greatest ally or its most relentless adversary. It introduces the doctrine of spiritual 'Samskaras'—the latent impressions that allow a seeker to carry spiritual progress across multiple lifetimes.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
अनाश्रितः कर्मफलं कार्यं कर्म करोति यः।
स संन्यासी च योगी च न निरग्निर्न चाक्रियः।।6.1।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
anāśhritaḥ karma-phalaṁ kāryaṁ karma karoti yaḥ
sa sannyāsī cha yogī cha na niragnir na chākriyaḥ
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; anāśhritaḥ—without taking shelter; karma-phalam—the result of work; kāryam—obligatory; karma—work; karoti—performs; yaḥ—who; saḥ—he; sannyāsī—a renunciant; cha—also; yogī—a yogi; cha—also; na—not; niragniḥ—one who maintains no fire; na—not; cha—also; akriyaḥ—without activity
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: One who is unattached to the fruits of his work and who works as he is obligated is in the renounced order of life, and he is the true mystic, not he who lights no fire and performs no work.
deep_dive_text: The Active Monk. Krishna starts the chapter by clarifying that 'Meditation' doesn't mean escaping responsibility. A true renunciant isn't someone who stops doing chores or lighting the sacrificial fire ($Niragnir$); it is someone who performs their 'Karyam Karma' (obligatory duty) with zero attachment to the outcome. Mental renunciation is the prerequisite for formal meditation.

original_script:
यं संन्यासमिति प्राहुर्योगं तं विद्धि पाण्डव।
न ह्यसंन्यस्तसङ्कल्पो योगी भवति कश्चन।।6.2।।
transliteration:
yaṁ sannyāsam iti prāhur yogaṁ taṁ viddhi pāṇḍava
na hy asannyasta-saṅkalpo yogī bhavati kaśhchana
word_meanings: yam—what; sannyāsam—renunciation; iti—thus; prāhuḥ—they say; yogam—Yoga; tam—that; viddhi—know; pāṇḍava—Arjun; na—never; hi—certainly; asannyasta—without renouncing; saṅkalpaḥ—desire for self-satisfaction; yogī—a yogi; bhavati—becomes; kaśhchana—anyone
translation_english: What is called renunciation is the same as Yoga, or linking oneself with the Supreme, O son of Pandu, for no one can become a yogi unless he renounces the desire for self-satisfaction.
deep_dive_text: Renouncing the 'Agenda'. To be a yogi, one must renounce 'Sankalpa'—the mental habit of planning for one's own pleasure. If your mind is busy calculating how an action will benefit 'me,' it cannot be steady enough for meditation. This 'letting go' of the personal agenda is the common ground between the path of knowledge and the path of action.

original_script:
आरुरुक्षोर्मुनेर्योगं कर्म कारणमुच्यते।
योगारूढस्य तस्यैव शमः कारणमुच्यते।।6.3।।
transliteration:
ārurukṣhor muner yogaṁ karma kāraṇam uchyate
yogārūḍhasya tasyaiva śhamaḥ kāraṇam uchyate
word_meanings: ārurukṣhoḥ—who is just beginning the path; muneḥ—of the sage; yogam—Yoga; karma—action; kāraṇam—the means; uchyate—is said; yoga-ārūḍhasya—of one who has attained the summit; tasya—his; eva—certainly; śhamaḥ—quiescence/stillness; kāraṇam—the means; uchyate—is said
translation_english: For one who is a beginner in the eightfold yoga system, work is said to be the means; and for one who is already elevated in yoga, cessation of all material activities is said to be the means.
deep_dive_text: The Two Stages of Practice. Krishna provides a roadmap: for the beginner ($Aruruksho$), action is the medicine. You use selfless work to burn away the restless energy of the mind. However, once you reach the 'summit' ($Yogārūḍha$), the focus shifts to 'Shama' (tranquility and silence). You don't start with silence; you work your way into it.

original_script:
यदा हि नेन्द्रियार्थेषु न कर्मस्वनुषज्जते।
सर्वसङ्कल्पसंन्यासी योगारूढस्तदोच्यते।।6.4।।
transliteration:
yadā hi nendriyārtheṣhu na karmasv anuṣhajjate
sarva-saṅkalpa-sannyāsī yogārūḍhas tadochyate
word_meanings: yadā—when; hi—certainly; na—neither; indriya-artheṣhu—in sense objects; na—nor; karmasu—in activities; anuṣhajjate—is attached; sarva-saṅkalpa—of all material desires; sannyāsī—renunciant; yoga-ārūḍhaḥ—elevated in Yoga; tadā—at that time; uchyate—is said
translation_english: A person is said to be elevated in yoga when, having renounced all material desires, he neither acts for sense gratification nor engages in fruitive activities.
deep_dive_text: Signs of Mastery. How do you know if you are 'elevated' in Yoga? The marker is a lack of 'clinging' ($Anuṣhajjate$). You no longer feel the compulsive need to engage the senses for a 'dopamine hit.' You act because it is necessary, not because you are chasing a thrill. This state of 'Sankalpa-Sannyasi' is the gateway to deep meditation.

original_script:
उद्धरेदात्मनात्मानं नात्मानमवसादयेत्।
आत्मैव ह्यात्मनो बन्धुरात्मैव रिपुरात्मनः।।6.5।।
transliteration:
uddhared ātmanātmānaṁ nātmānam avasādayet
ātmāiva hyātmano bandhur ātmāiva ripur ātmanaḥ
word_meanings: uddharet—one must deliver; ātmanā—by the mind; ātmānam—the self; na—never; ātmānam—the self; avasādayet—degrade; ātmā—mind; eva—certainly; hi—indeed; ātmanaḥ—of the self; bandhuḥ—friend; ātmā—mind; eva—certainly; ripuḥ—enemy; ātmanaḥ—of the self
translation_english: One must deliver himself with the help of his mind, and not degrade himself. The mind is the friend of the conditioned soul, and his enemy as well.
deep_dive_text: The Mind: Your Greatest Asset or Liability. This is one of the most psychological verses in the Gita. Krishna emphasizes personal responsibility. You are your own primary project. Depending on how you train it, your mind can be the 'Bandhu' (friend) that pulls you out of suffering, or the 'Ripu' (enemy) that traps you in it. No one can do this work for you.

original_script:
बन्धुरात्मात्मनस्तस्य येनात्मैवात्मना जितः।
अनात्मनस्तु शत्रुत्वे वर्तेतात्मैव शत्रुवत्।।6.6।।
transliteration:
bandhur ātmātmanas tasya yenātmāivātmanā jitaḥ
anātmanas tu śhatrutve vartetātmāiva śhatru-vat
word_meanings: bandhuḥ—friend; ātmā—the mind; ātmanaḥ—of the self; tasya—of him; yena—by whom; ātmā—the mind; eva—certainly; ātmā—by the self; jitaḥ—is conquered; anātmanaḥ—of one who has failed to control the mind; tu—but; śhatrutve—for an enemy; varteta—remains; ātmā eva—the mind itself; śhatru-vat—like an enemy
translation_english: For him who has conquered the mind, the mind is the best of friends; but for one who has failed to do so, his mind will remain the greatest enemy.
deep_dive_text: The Internal Civil War. If your mind is 'Jita' (conquered), it obeys your higher intellect. If not, it wanders into 'Shatrutve' (hostility), creating intrusive thoughts, anxiety, and distractions. Meditation is the process of ending this internal conflict and making the mind a disciplined servant of the soul.

original_script:
जितात्मनः प्रशान्तस्य परमात्मा समाहितः।
शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु तथा मानापमानयोः।।6.7।।
transliteration:
jitātmanaḥ praśhāntasya paramātmā samāhitaḥ
śhītoṣhṇa-sukha-duḥkheṣhu tathā mānāpamānayoḥ
word_meanings: jita-ātmanaḥ—of one who has conquered the mind; praśhāntasya—who has attained tranquility; parama-ātmā—the Supreme Self; samāhitaḥ—perfectly situated; śhīta—cold; uṣhṇa—heat; sukha—happiness; duḥkheṣhu—in distress; tathā—also; māna—honor; apamānayoḥ—in dishonor
translation_english: For one who has conquered the mind, the Supersoul is already reached, for he has attained tranquility. To such a man happiness and distress, heat and cold, honor and dishonor are all the same.
deep_dive_text: The Calm After the Storm. Once the mind is conquered, the seeker reaches 'Prashanta'—perfect tranquility. In this state, external dualities like the weather (heat/cold), physical state (pleasure/pain), or social ego (honor/dishonor) no longer disrupt the internal connection. You are 'Samahita'—fixed in your true center.

original_script:
ज्ञानविज्ञानतृप्तात्मा कूटस्थो विजितेन्द्रियः।
युक्त इत्युच्यते योगी समलोष्टाश्मकाञ्चनः।।6.8।।
transliteration:
jñāna-vijñāna-tṛiptātmā kūṭa-stho vijitendriyaḥ
yukta ity uchyate yogī sama-loṣhṭāśhma-kāñchanaḥ
word_meanings: jñāna—knowledge; vijñāna—realized knowledge; tṛipta—satisfied; ātmā—soul; kūṭa-sthaḥ—unchanging; vijita-indriyaḥ—having conquered the senses; yuktaḥ—united; iti—thus; uchyate—is said; yogī—a yogi; sama—equal; loṣhṭa—pebble; aśhma—stone; kāñchanaḥ—gold
translation_english: A person is said to be established in self-realization and is called a yogi when he is fully satisfied by virtue of acquired knowledge and realization. Such a person is situated in transcendence and is self-controlled. He sees everything—whether it be pebbles, stones or gold—as the same.
deep_dive_text: Equal Value of All Matter. A yogi is 'Jnana-Vijnana Tripta'—satisfied by both theory and experience. Because their inner world is so rich, they are no longer mesmerized by the 'value' humans place on things. A piece of gold and a common stone are seen with the same 'Sama' (equal) vision, because the yogi is focused on the spirit that lies behind both.

original_script:
सुहृन्मित्रार्युदासीनमध्यस्थद्वेष्यबन्धुषु।
साधुष्वपि च पापेषु समबुद्धिर्विशिष्यते।।6.9।।
transliteration:
suhṛin-mitrāry-udāsīna-madhyastha-dveṣhya-bandhuṣhu
sādhuṣhv api cha pāpeṣhu sama-buddhir viśhiṣhyate
word_meanings: su-hṛit—well-wishers; mitra—friends; ari—enemies; udāsīna—the indifferent; madhyastha—mediators; dveṣhya—the envious; bandhuṣhu—relatives; sādhuṣhu—the pious; api—even; cha—also; pāpeṣhu—the sinners; sama-buddhiḥ—having equal vision; viśhiṣhyate—is considered even higher
translation_english: A person is considered still further advanced when he regards honest well-wishers, affectionate friends, enemies, the indifferent, mediators, the envious, relatives, the pious and the sinners all with an equal mind.
deep_dive_text: The Social Test of Yoga. In verse 6.8, Krishna spoke of seeing gold and stones as equal; here, he applies that 'Sama-buddhi' to people. True spiritual advancement is not just about inner peace, but about losing the 'bias' that labels people as friends or enemies. The yogi sees the soul in everyone, making their social vision unshakeable.

original_script:
योगी युञ्जीत सततमात्मानं रहसि स्थितः।
एकाकी यतचित्तात्मा निराशीरपरिग्रहः।।6.10।।
transliteration:
yogī yuñjīta satatam ātmānaṁ rahasi sthitaḥ
ekākī yata-chittātmā nirāśhīr aparigrahaḥ
word_meanings: yogī—a yogi; yuñjīta—should concentrate; satatam—constantly; ātmānam—himself (the mind); rahasi—in a secluded place; sthitaḥ—situated; ekākī—alone; yata-chitta-ātmā—having controlled mind and self; nirāśhīḥ—free from desires; aparigrahaḥ—free from possessiveness
translation_english: A yogi should constantly engage his mind in meditation on the Supreme Self; he should live alone in a secluded place and should always carefully control his mind. He should be free from desires and feelings of possessiveness.
deep_dive_text: The Outer Environment. Krishna begins the practical 'tutorial.' For deep meditation, one needs 'Rahasi' (seclusion). He emphasizes 'Aparigraha'—non-possessiveness. If your mind is worried about your house, car, or phone, it cannot travel inward. Meditation requires shedding all 'ownership' for a brief time to be truly alone with the self.

original_script:
शुचौ देशे प्रतिष्ठाप्य स्थिरमासनमात्मनः।
नात्युच्छ्रितं नातिनीचं चैलाजिनकुशोत्तरम्।।6.11।।
transliteration:
śhuchau deśhe pratiṣhṭhāpya sthiram āsanam ātmanaḥ
nāty-uchchhritaṁ nāti-nīchaṁ chailājina-kuśhottaram
word_meanings: śhuchau—in a clean; deśhe—place; pratiṣhṭhāpya—having established; sthiram—steady; āsanam—seat; ātmanaḥ—his own; na—not; ati-uchchhritam—too high; na—not; ati-nīcham—too low; chaila-ajina-kuśha-uttaram—covered with cloth, deerskin, and kusha grass
translation_english: To practice yoga, one should go to a secluded place and should lay kusha grass on the ground and then cover it with a deerskin and a soft cloth. The seat should be neither too high nor too low and should be situated in a sacred place.
deep_dive_text: The Seat of Stability ($Asanam$). Krishna gets very specific about ergonomics. The seat must be 'Sthiram' (steady). In ancient times, the layers of grass, skin, and cloth were used to regulate temperature and provide insulation from the ground. The instruction 'neither too high nor too low' ensures physical safety and comfort, preventing the mind from worrying about a fall or discomfort.

original_script:
तत्रैकाग्रं मनः कृत्वा यतचित्तेन्द्रियक्रियः।
उपविश्यासने युञ्ज्याद्योगमात्मविशुद्धये।।6.12।।
transliteration:
tatraikāgraṁ manaḥ kṛitvā yata-chittendriya-kriyaḥ
upaviśhyāsane yuñjyād yogam ātma-viśhuddhaye
word_meanings: tatra—there; eka-agram—one-pointed; manaḥ—mind; kṛitvā—making; yata-chitta-indriya-kriyaḥ—controlling the mind, senses, and activities; upaviśhya—sitting; āsane—on the seat; yuñjyāt—should practice; yogam—Yoga; ātma-viśhuddhaye—for self-purification
translation_english: The yogi should then sit on it and practice yoga by controlling the mind, senses and activities and fixing the mind on one point to purify the self.
deep_dive_text: One-Pointed Focus ($Ekāgram$). Once the body is settled, the mental work begins. The goal of this specific sitting is 'Atma-vishuddhaye'—to clear the 'static' from the consciousness. By funneling all mental energy into a single point, the 'waves' of the mind settle, revealing the pure self beneath.

original_script:
समं कायशिरोग्रीवं धारयन्नचलं स्थिरः।
सम्प्रेक्ष्य नासिकाग्रं स्वं दिशश्चानवलोकयन्।।6.13।।
transliteration:
samaṁ kāya-śhiro-grīvaṁ dhārayann achalaṁ sthiraḥ
samprekṣhya nāsikāgraṁ svaṁ diśhaśh chānavalokayan
word_meanings: samam—straight; kāya—body; śhiraḥ—head; grīvam—neck; dhārayan—holding; achalam—unmoving; sthiraḥ—steady; samprekṣhya—gazing; nāsika-agram—at the tip of the nose; swam—own; diśhaḥ—all directions; cha—also; anavalokayan—not looking
translation_english: One should hold one’s body, neck and head erect in a straight line and stare steadily at the tip of the nose. Thus, with an unagitated, subdued mind, devoid of fear, completely free from sex life, one should meditate upon Me within the heart and make Me the ultimate goal of life.
deep_dive_text: The Geometry of Meditation. Krishna describes the 'Vajrasana' or 'Siddhasana' alignment: head, neck, and spine in a vertical line. This allows for the free flow of energy (Prana). By fixing the eyes on the tip of the nose ($Nasikagram$), the yogi prevents the eyes from wandering and 'locking' onto external distractions.

original_script:
प्रशान्तात्मा विगतभीर्ब्रह्मचारिव्रते स्थितः।
मनः संयम्य मच्चित्तॊ युक्त आसीत मत्परः।।6.14।।
transliteration:
praśhāntātmā vigata-bhīr brahmachāri-vrate sthitah
manaḥ saṁyamya mach-chitto yukta āsīta mat-paraḥ
word_meanings: praśhānta-ātmā—serene soul; vigata-bhīḥ—fearless; brahmachāri-vrate—in the vow of celibacy; sthitaḥ—situated; manaḥ—mind; saṁyamya—subduing; mat-chittaḥ—thinking of Me; yuktaḥ—united; āsīta—should sit; mat-paraḥ—devoted to Me
translation_english: With a serene and fearless mind, firm in the vow of celibacy, the yogi should subdue the mind, fix the heart on Me, and make Me the ultimate goal.
deep_dive_text: Internal Requirements. Along with the posture, Krishna lists three mental states: 1. **Prashanta** (Peaceful); 2. **Vigata-bhi** (Fearless); and 3. **Mat-para** (Devoted). Fear is the biggest disturber of meditation. By shifting the focus to the Divine ($Mach-chitta$), the yogi finds a 'safe harbor' that allows the mind to finally let go of its defenses.

original_script:
युञ्जन्नेवं सदात्मानं योगी नियतमानसः।
शान्तिं निर्वाणपरमां मत्संस्थामधिगच्छति।।6.15।।
transliteration:
yuñjann evaṁ sadātmānaṁ yogī niyata-mānasaḥ
śhāntiṁ nirvāṇa-paramāṁ mat-saństhām adhigachchhati
word_meanings: yuñjan—practicing; evam—thus; sadā—constantly; ātmānam—the self (mind); yogī—the yogi; niyata-mānasaḥ—with a disciplined mind; śhāntim—peace; nirvāṇa-paramām—culminating in liberation; mat-saństhām—existing in Me; adhigachchhati—attains
translation_english: Thus practicing constant control of the body, mind and activities, the mystic transcendentalist, his mind regulated, attains to the kingdom of God [or the abode of Krishna] by cessation of material existence.
deep_dive_text: The Result of Consistency. 'Yuñjann evaṁ sadā' (practicing thus constantly) is the key. Meditation is not a one-time event but a lifestyle of regulation. The result is a peace ($Shanti$) that is not just a temporary feeling, but a permanent state of being situated in the Divine ($Mat-saństhā$).

original_script:
नात्यश्नतस्तु योगोऽस्ति न चैकान्तमनश्नतः।
न चाति स्वप्नशीलस्य जाग्रतो नैव चार्जुन।।6.16।।
transliteration:
nāty-aśhnatas tu yogo ’sti na chaikāntam anaśhnataḥ
na chāti svapna-śhīlasya jāgrato naiva chārjuna
word_meanings: na—not; ati—too much; aśhnataḥ—of one who eats; tu—but; yogaḥ—Yoga; asti—there is; na—not; cha—also; ekāntam—at all; anaśhnataḥ—of one who does not eat; na—not; cha—also; ati—too much; svapna-śhīlasya—of one who sleeps; jāgrataḥ—of one who keeps awake; na eva—not at all; cha—and; arjuna—Arjun
translation_english: There is no possibility of one’s becoming a yogi, O Arjuna, if one eats too much or eats too little, sleeps too much or does not sleep enough.
deep_dive_text: The Path of Moderation. Krishna rejects extreme asceticism. You cannot meditate if you are bloated, but you also cannot meditate if you are starving and light-headed. The same applies to sleep. Yoga is for the 'balanced' person. This verse is the foundation for a healthy, sustainable spiritual lifestyle.

original_script:
युक्ताहारविहारस्य युक्तचेष्टस्य कर्मसु।
युक्तस्वप्नावबोधस्य योगो भवति दुःखहा।।6.17।।
transliteration:
yuktāhāra-vihārasya yukta-cheṣhṭasya karmasu
yukta-svapnāvabodhasya yogo bhavati duḥkha-hā
word_meanings: yukta—temperate; āhāra—eating; vihārasya—recreation; yukta—temperate; cheṣhṭasya—of activities; karmasu—in performing duties; yukta—temperate; svapna-avabodhasya—sleep and wakefulness; yogaḥ—Yoga; bhavati—becomes; duḥkha-hā—the destroyer of sorrows
translation_english: He who is temperate in his habits of eating, sleeping, working and recreation can mitigate all material pains by practicing the yoga system.
