deep_dive_text: The Destroyer of Sorrows ($Duḥkha-hā$). Krishna promises that Yoga actually works to end suffering, but only if life is balanced. 'Yukta' means appropriate or regulated. If your 'recreation' is draining or your 'work' is obsessive, meditation becomes impossible. Yoga is not an escape from life; it is the art of regulating life so that the mind can finally rest.

original_script:
यदा विनियतं चित्तमात्मन्येवावतिष्ठते।
निःस्पृहः सर्वकामेभ्यो युक्त इत्युच्यते तदा।।6.18।।
transliteration:
yadā viniyataṁ chittam ātmany evāvatiṣhṭhate
niḥspṛihaḥ sarva-kāmebhyo yukta ity uchyate tadā
word_meanings: yadā—when; viniyatam—perfectly disciplined; chittam—the mind; ātmani—in the self; eva—only; avatiṣhṭhate—becomes situated; niḥspṛihaḥ—free from cravings; sarva—all; kāmebhyaḥ—for material desires; yuktaḥ—united in Yoga; iti—thus; uchyate—is said; tadā—at that time
translation_english: When the yogi, by practice of yoga, disciplines his mental activities and becomes situated in transcendence—devoid of all material desires—he is said to be well established in yoga.
deep_dive_text: Mental Stillness. This verse defines the 'Success' of a meditation session. It is when the 'Chitta' (the mind-stuff) is no longer leaking energy toward outside objects and settles into the 'Atman' (the self). 'Niḥspṛihaḥ'—the absence of craving—is the primary indicator of a successful practice.

original_script:
यथा दीपो निवातस्थो नेङ्गते सोपमा स्मृता।
योगिनो यतचित्तस्य युञ्जतो योगमात्मनः।।6.19।।
transliteration:
yathā dīpo nivāta-stho neṅgate sopamā smṛitā
yogino yata-chittasya yuñjato yogam ātmanaḥ
word_meanings: yathā—as; dīpaḥ—a lamp; nivāta-sthaḥ—in a place without wind; na—not; iṅgate—flickers; sa—that; upamā—comparison; smṛitā—is considered; yoginaḥ—of the yogi; yata-chittasya—whose mind is controlled; yuñjataḥ—constantly engaged; yogam—in meditation; ātmanaḥ—on the self
translation_english: As a lamp in a windless place does not waver, so the transcendentalist, whose mind is subdued, remains always steady in his meditation on the transcendent Self.
deep_dive_text: The Windless Flame Analogy. One of the most beautiful images in the Gita. Desires are like winds that make the flame of our awareness flicker and jump. In the 'windless place' of deep meditation, the consciousness becomes a single, steady, unmoving beam of light. For your app, this is the 'Visual Meditation' verse.

original_script:
यत्रोपरमते चित्तं निरुद्धं योगसेवया।
यत्र चैवात्मनात्मानं पश्यन्नात्मनि तुष्यति।।6.20।।
transliteration:
yatroparamate chittaṁ niruddhaṁ yoga-sevayā
yatra chaivātmanātmānaṁ paśhyann ātmani tuṣhyati
word_meanings: yatra—where; uparamate—cease; chittam—the mind; niruddham—restrained from material jetsam; yoga-sevayā—by performance of Yoga; yatra—where; cha—also; eva—certainly; ātmanā—by the purified mind; ātmānam—the self; paśhyan—seeing; ātmani—in the self; tuṣhyati—is satisfied
translation_english: In the stage of perfection called trance, or samādhi, one’s mind is completely restrained from material mental activities by practice of yoga. This perfection is characterized by one’s ability to see the Self by the pure mind and to relish and rejoice in the Self.
deep_dive_text: Rejoicing in the Self ($Tuṣhyati$). In Samādhi, the mind stops 'looking' for things. It becomes so pure that it acts like a clear mirror, allowing you to see your own soul. The result is a deep, internal 'relishing'—a joy that doesn't need a reason or an object.

original_script:
सुखमात्यन्तिकं यत्तद्बुद्धिग्राह्यमतीन्द्रियम्।
वेत्ति यत्र न चैवायं स्थितश्चलति तत्त्वतः।।6.21।।
transliteration:
sukham ātyantikaṁ yat tad buddhi-grāhyam atīndriyam
vetti yatra na chaivāyaṁ sthitaśh chalati tattvataḥ
word_meanings: sukham—happiness; ātyantikam—unlimited; yat—which; tat—that; buddhi—by intellect; grāhyam—accessible; atīndriyam—transcendental to the senses; vetti—knows; yatra—where; na—never; cha—also; eva—certainly; ayam—this; sthitaḥ—situated; chalati—moves; tattvataḥ—from the truth
translation_english: In that joyous state, one is situated in boundless transcendental happiness, established through transcendental senses. Established thus, one never departs from the truth.
deep_dive_text: Boundless Happiness ($Ātyantikam Sukham$). Krishna distinguishes this from sensory pleasure. It is 'Atīndriyam' (beyond the senses). It is a mental state of such high clarity and joy that once you experience it, you never want to slide back into the 'moving' world of confusion again.

original_script:
यं लब्ध्वा चापरं लाभं मन्यते नाधिकं ततः।
यस्मिन्स्थितो न दुःखेन गुरुणापि विचाल्यते।।6.22।।
transliteration:
yaṁ labdhvā chāparaṁ lābhaṁ manyate nādhikaṁ tataḥ
yasmin sthito na duḥkhena guruṇāpi vichālyate
word_meanings: yam—which; labdhvā—having attained; cha—also; aparam—other; lābham—gain; manyate—considers; na—not; adhikam—greater; tataḥ—than that; yasmin—in which; sthitaḥ—situated; na—never; duḥkhena—by miseries; guruṇā api—even the greatest; vichālyate—is shaken
translation_english: Upon gaining this, he thinks there is no greater gain. Being situated in such a position, one is never shaken, even in the midst of greatest difficulty.
deep_dive_text: Unshakeable Peace. This is the ultimate 'ROI' (Return on Investment) of meditation. Once you find this inner center, even 'Guruṇāpi duḥkhena' (the heaviest sorrow) cannot knock you off balance. This is the goal of the 'Gita Scroll' app: to help users find that unshakeable spot.

original_script:
तं विद्याद्दुःखसंयोगवियोगं योगसंज्ञितम्।
स निश्चयेन योक्तव्यो योगोऽनिर्विण्णचेतसा।।6.23।।
transliteration:
taṁ vidyād duḥkha-saṅyoga-viyogaṁ yoga-saṅjñitam
sa niśhchayena yoktavyo yogo ’nirviṇṇa-chetasā
word_meanings: tam—that; vidyāt—know; duḥkha-saṅyoga—contact with misery; viyogam—severance; yoga-saṅjñitam—called Yoga; saḥ—that; niśhchayena—with determination; yoktavyaḥ—must be practiced; yogaḥ—Yoga; anirviṇṇa-chetasā—without deviation
translation_english: This, indeed, is actual freedom from all miseries arising from material contact. One should practice yoga with determination and should not be deviated from the path.
deep_dive_text: Defining Yoga as 'Severance'. Paradoxically, Krishna defines Yoga (Union) as the 'disconnection' ($Viyoga$) from suffering ($Duḥkha$). It is the act of cutting the cord between our consciousness and our pain. He advises practicing with 'Niśhchayena'—iron-clad determination.

original_script:
सङ्कल्पप्रभवान्कामांस्त्यक्त्वा सर्वानशेषतः।
मनसैवेन्द्रियग्रामं विनियम्य समन्ततः।।6.24।।
transliteration:
saṅkalpa-prabhavān kāmāns tyaktvā sarvān aśheṣhataḥ
manasaivendriya-grāmaṁ viniyamya samantataḥ
word_meanings: saṅkalpa—mental imagination; prabhavān—born of; kāmān—desires; tyaktvā—abandoning; sarvān—all; aśheṣhataḥ—completely; manasā—by the mind; eva—certainly; indriya-grāmam—the group of senses; viniyamya—restraining; samantataḥ—from all sides
translation_english: One should engage oneself in the practice of yoga with determination and faith and not be deviated from the path. One should abandon, without exception, all material desires born of mental speculation and thus control the all-pervading senses on all sides by the mind.
deep_dive_text: The Source of Desires ($Saṅkalpa$). Krishna notes that desires aren't just 'there'; they are born of mental imagination/speculation. By curbing the 'story-telling' habit of the mind, the senses lose their fuel. Meditation is the process of shutting down the 'factory' of cravings.

original_script:
शनैः शनैरुपरमेद्बुद्ध्या धृतिगृहीतया।
आत्मसंस्थं मनः कृत्वा न किञ्चिदपि चिन्तयेत्।।6.25।।
transliteration:
śhanaiḥ śhanair uparamed buddhyā dhṛiti-gṛihītayā
ātma-saństhaṁ manaḥ kṛitvā na kiñchid api chintayet
word_meanings: śhanaiḥ śhanaiḥ—gradually, step by step; uparamet—one should withdraw; buddhyā—by the intellect; dhṛiti-gṛihītayā—held with determination; ātma-saństham—situated in the self; manaḥ—mind; kṛitvā—making; na—not; kiñchit—anything; api—even; chintayet—should think
translation_english: Gradually, step by step, with full conviction, one should become situated in trance by means of intelligence sustained by steady conviction, and thus the mind should be fixed on the Self alone and should think of nothing else.
deep_dive_text: The Patience of Practice ($Shanaiḥ Shanaiḥ$). Krishna emphasizes that meditation is not an 'instant' result but a gradual withdrawal. The secret is to use the 'Dhriti' (determination) of the intellect to keep the mind anchored. 'Thinking of nothing else' is not a forced void, but a state where the Self is so interesting that other thoughts simply lose their appeal.

original_script:
यतो यतो निश्चलति मनश्चञ्चलमस्थिरम्।
ततस्ततो नियम्यैतदात्मन्येव वशं नयेत्।।6.26।।
transliteration:
yato yato niśhcharati manaśh chañchalam asthiram
tatas tato niyamyaitad ātmany eva vaśhaṁ nayet
word_meanings: yataḥ yataḥ—wherever; niśhcharati—wanders; manaḥ—the mind; chañchalam—flickering; asthiram—unsteady; tataḥ tataḥ—from there; niyamya—regulating; etat—this; ātmani—in the self; eva—certainly; vaśham—control; nayet—must bring
translation_english: From wherever the mind wanders due to its flickering and unsteady nature, one must certainly withdraw it and bring it back under the control of the Self.
deep_dive_text: The 'Gentle Tug' Method. This is the most practical advice for any meditator. Krishna acknowledges the mind is 'Chanchalam' (flickering). He doesn't say 'stop the mind from wandering'; he says 'whenever it wanders, bring it back.' Meditation is the repeated act of returning. Each time you bring the mind back, you are strengthening your 'spiritual muscle.'

original_script:
प्रशान्तमनसं ह्येनं योगिनं सुखमुत्तमम्।
उपैति शान्तरजसं ब्रह्मभूतमकल्मषम्।।6.27।।
transliteration:
praśhānta-manasaṁ hy enaṁ yoginaṁ sukham uttamam
upaiti śhānta-rajasaṁ brahma-bhūtam akalmaṣham
word_meanings: praśhānta—peaceful; manasam—mind; hi—certainly; enam—this; yoginam—yogi; sukham—happiness; uttamam—highest; upaiti—attains; śhānta-rajasam—whose passion is quieted; brahma-bhūtam—liberated in Brahman; akalmaṣham—free from sin
translation_english: The yogi whose mind is fixed on Me verily attains the highest perfection of transcendental happiness. He is beyond the mode of passion, he realizes his qualitative identity with the Supreme, and thus he is freed from all reactions to past deeds.
deep_dive_text: Quieting the Passion ($Śhānta-rajasam$). True happiness ($Sukham Uttamam$) is only possible when 'Rajas' (restless passion/agitation) is stilled. When the mind is 'Prashanta' (serene), the soul realizes it is not the ego but a part of the vast 'Brahma-bhutam.' This realization acts as a universal solvent, washing away past karmic burdens.

original_script:
युञ्जन्नेवं सदात्मानं योगी विगतकल्मषः।
सुखेन ब्रह्मसंस्पर्शमत्यन्तं सुखमश्नुते।।6.28।।
transliteration:
yuñjann evaṁ sadātmānaṁ yogī vigata-kalmaṣhaḥ
sukhena brahma-saṁsparśham atyantaṁ sukham aśhnute
word_meanings: yuñjan—practicing; evam—thus; sadā—constantly; ātmānam—the self (mind); yogī—the yogi; vigata—freed from; kalmaṣhaḥ—sin; sukhena—happily; brahma-saṁsparśham—contact with the Supreme; atyantam—unlimited; sukham—happiness; aśhnute—enjoys
translation_english: Thus the self-controlled yogi, constantly engaged in yoga practice, becomes free from all material contamination and achieves the highest stage of perfect happiness in transcendental loving service to the Lord.
deep_dive_text: Contact with the Infinite ($Brahma-saṁsparśham$). Krishna describes a state where the soul 'touches' the Divine. 'Sukhena' implies this shouldn't be a painful struggle, but a joyful attainment. This contact produces 'Atyantam Sukham'—happiness that has no end and no boundary.

original_script:
सर्वभूतस्थमात्मानं सर्वभूतानि चात्मनि।
ईक्षते योगयुक्तात्मा सर्वत्र समदर्शनः।।6.29।।
transliteration:
sarva-bhūta-stham ātmānaṁ sarva-bhūtāni chātmani
īkṣhate yoga-yuktātmā sarvatra sama-darśhanaḥ
word_meanings: sarva-bhūta-stham—situated in all beings; ātmānam—the self; sarva—all; bhūtāni—beings; cha—also; ātmani—in the self; īkṣhate—sees; yoga-yukta-ātmā—one whose soul is united in Yoga; sarvatra—everywhere; sama-darśhanaḥ—equal vision
translation_english: A true yogi observes Me in all beings and also sees every being in Me. Indeed, the self-realized person sees Me, the same Supreme Lord, everywhere.
deep_dive_text: The Universal Vision. This is the 'Optical' result of Samadhi. The yogi stops seeing people as 'separate' or 'other.' They see the same Soul ($Atman$) in the heart of everyone, and they see the entire world existing within that same Divine fabric. This is 'Sama-darshana'—the end of all division.

original_script:
यो मां पश्यति सर्वत्र सर्वं च मयि पश्यति।
तस्याहं न प्रणश्यामि स च मे न प्रणश्यति।।6.30।।
transliteration:
yo māṁ paśhyati sarvatra sarvaṁ cha mayi paśhyati
tasyāhaṁ na praṇaśhyāmi sa cha me na praṇaśhyati
word_meanings: yaḥ—who; mām—Me; paśhyati—sees; sarvatra—everywhere; sarvam—everything; cha—and; mayi—in Me; paśhyati—sees; tasya—for him; aham—I; na—not; praṇaśhyāmi—am lost; saḥ—he; cha—also; me—to Me; na—not; praṇaśhyati—is lost
translation_english: For one who sees Me everywhere and sees everything in Me, I am never lost, nor is he ever lost to Me.
deep_dive_text: The Eternal Bond. This is one of the most comforting verses in the Gita. Krishna promises that once you achieve this vision, the connection becomes 'unbreakable.' You are never alone again. No matter where you go or what you do, the Divine presence is visible, and the Divine is always 'watching' over you.

original_script:
सर्वभूतस्थितं यो मां भजत्येकत्वमास्थितः।
सर्वथा वर्तमानोऽपि स योगी मयि वर्तते।।6.31।।
transliteration:
sarva-bhūta-sthitaṁ yo māṁ bhajaty ekatvam āsthitaḥ
sarvathā vartamāno ’pi sa yogī mayि vartate
word_meanings: sarva-bhūta-sthitam—situated in all beings; yaḥ—who; mām—Me; bhajati—worships; ekatvam—in oneness; āsthitaḥ—situated; sarvathā—in all respects; vartamānaḥ—remains; api—although; saḥ—he; yogī—the yogi; mayi—in Me; vartate—dwells
translation_english: Such a yogi, who engages in the worshipful service of the Supersoul, knowing that I and the Supersoul are one, remains always in Me in all circumstances.
deep_dive_text: Oneness in Action. A yogi who has realized this 'Oneness' ($Ekatvam$) doesn't need to be in a temple or a cave to be spiritual. 'Sarvatha vartamano api'—even while living an ordinary life or doing a job, they are always dwelling 'in' the Divine because their fundamental perception has changed.

original_script:
आत्मौपम्येण सर्वत्र समं पश्यति योऽर्जुन।
सुखं वा यदि वा दुःखं स योगी परमो मतः।।6.32।।
transliteration:
ātmaupamyeṇa sarvatra samaṁ paśhyati yo ’rjuna
sukhaṁ vā yadi vā duḥkhaṁ sa yogī paramo mataḥ
word_meanings: ātma-aupamyeṇa—by comparison with oneself; sarvatra—everywhere; samam—equally; paśhyati—sees; yaḥ—who; arjuna—Arjun; sukham—happiness; vā—or; yadi—if; vā—or; duḥkham—distress; saḥ—he; yogī—the yogi; paramaḥ—highest; mataḥ—is considered
translation_english: He is a perfect yogi who, by comparison to his own self, sees the true equality of all beings, in both their happiness and their distress, O Arjuna!
deep_dive_text: The Golden Rule of Yoga. Krishna defines the 'Highest' ($Paramaḥ$) yogi as the one who feels the pain and joy of others as if it were their own. This is the peak of empathy. You don't hurt others because you 'see' yourself in them. Spirituality here is not an escape from others, but a total, compassionate union with them.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
योऽयं योगस्त्वया प्रोक्तः साम्येन मधुसूदन।
एतस्याहं न पश्यामि चञ्चलत्वात्स्थितिं स्थिराम्।।6.33।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
yo ’yaṁ yogas tvayā proktaḥ sāmyena madhusūdana
etasyāhaṁ na paśhyāmi chañchalatvāt sthitiṁ sthirām
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjun said; yaḥ ayam—this; yogaḥ—system of Yoga; tvayā—by You; proktaḥ—described; sāmyena—in terms of equanimity; madhusūdana—O Krishna; etasya—of this; aham—I; na—not; paśhyāmi—see; chañchalatvāt—due to restlessness; sthitim—situation; sthirām—steady
translation_english: Arjuna said: O Madhusudana, the system of yoga which You have summarized appears impractical and unendurable to me, for the mind is restless and unsteady.
deep_dive_text: The Voice of the Student. Arjuna is honest about his struggle. He finds the idea of 'Equal Vision' beautiful but impossible because his mind is too 'Chanchalam' (flickering). This makes the text incredibly relatable—even a great warrior feels defeated by his own thoughts.

original_script:
चञ्चलं हि मनः कृष्ण प्रमाथि बलवद्दृढम्।
तस्याहं निग्रहं मन्ये वायोरिव सुदुष्करम्।।6.34।।
transliteration:
chañchalaṁ hi manaḥ kṛiṣhṇa pramāthi balavad dṛiḍham
tasyāhaṁ nigrahaṁ manye vāyor iva su-duṣhkaram
word_meanings: chañchalam—restless; hi—certainly; manaḥ—the mind; kṛiṣhṇa—Krishna; pramāthi—agitating; balavat—strong; dṛiḍham—obstinate; tasya—of it; aham—I; nigraham—control; manye—think; vāyoḥ—of the wind; iva—like; su-duṣhkaram—difficult
translation_english: For the mind is restless, turbulent, obstinate and very strong, O Krishna, and to subdue it, I think, is more difficult than controlling the wind.
deep_dive_text: Catching the Wind. Arjuna uses four adjectives for the mind: 1. **Chanchalam** (flickering); 2. **Pramathi** (agitating/turbulent); 3. **Balavat** (strong); and 4. **Dridham** (stubborn). He compares controlling thoughts to trying to grab a handful of wind—it slips through your fingers every time you think you've caught it.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
असंशयं महाबाहो मनो दुर्निग्रहं चलम्।
अभ्यासेन तु कौन्तेय वैराग्येण च गृह्यते।।6.35।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
asaṁśhayaṁ mahā-bāho mano durnigrahaṁ chalam
abhyāsena tu kaunteya vairāgyeṇā cha gṛihyate
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; asaṁśhayam—undoubtedly; mahā-bāho—mighty-armed one; manaḥ—the mind; durnigraham—difficult to curb; chalam—flickering; abhyāsena—by practice; tu—but; kaunteya—Arjun; vairāgyeṇa—by detachment; cha—and; gṛihyate—can be controlled
translation_english: Lord Sri Krishna said: O mighty-armed son of Kunti, it is undoubtedly very difficult to curb the restless mind, but it is possible by suitable practice and by detachment.
deep_dive_text: The Two-Pillar Solution. Krishna agrees with Arjuna—the mind *is* hard to control. But he gives the antidote: 1. **Abhyāsa** (consistent practice) and 2. **Vairāgya** (detachment from sensory results). Practice builds the 'muscle' of focus, while detachment removes the 'distractions' that pull the mind away in the first place.

original_script:
असंयतात्मना योगो दुष्प्राप इति मे मतिः।
वश्यात्मना तु यतता शक्योऽवाप्तुमुपायतः।।6.36।।
transliteration:
asaṁyatātmanā yogo duṣhprāpa iti me matiḥ
vaśhyātmanā tu yatatā śhakyo ’vāptum upāyataḥ
word_meanings: asaṁyata—unrestrained; ātmanā—by the self; yogaḥ—Yoga; duṣhprāpaḥ—difficult to obtain; iti—thus; me—My; matiḥ—opinion; vaśhya—controlled; ātmanā—by the self; tu—but; yatatā—by striving; śhakyaḥ—possible; avāptum—to achieve; upāyataḥ—by appropriate means
translation_english: For one whose mind is unbridled, self-realization is difficult work. But he whose mind is controlled and who strives by appropriate means is assured of success. That is My opinion.
deep_dive_text: Success through 'Upāya' (Means). Yoga is not a matter of luck or divine favor; it is a technology. If you follow the 'Upāya' (correct methods), success is mechanical. If you don't control the mind first, all the rituals in the world won't bring peace.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
अयतिः श्रद्धयोपेतो योगाच्चलितमानसः।
अप्राप्य योगसंसिद्धिं कां गतिं कृष्ण गच्छति।।6.37।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
ayatiḥ śhraddhayopeto yogāch chalita-mānasaḥ
aprāpyā yoga-saṁsiddhiṁ kāṁ gatiṁ kṛiṣhṇa gachchhati
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjun said; ayatiḥ—unsuccessful; śhraddhayā—with faith; upetaḥ—engaged; yogāt—from Yoga; chalita—deviated; mānasaḥ—whose mind; aprāpya—without achieving; yoga-saṁsiddhim—perfection in Yoga; kām—which; gatim—destination; kṛiṣhṇa—Krishna; gachchhati—attains
translation_english: Arjuna said: O Krishna, what is the destination of the unsuccessful transcendentalist, who in the beginning takes to the process of self-realization with faith but who later desists due to worldly-mindedness and thus does not attain perfection in mysticism?
deep_dive_text: The Fear of Failure. Arjuna asks: 'What if I try and fail?'. He's worried that if he leaves his material life but fails at Yoga, he will be left with nothing—like a cloud torn apart by the wind. It's a question about 'Spiritual Sunk Cost'.

original_script:
कच्चिन्नोभयविभ्रष्टश्छिन्नाभ्रमिव नश्यति।
अप्रतिष्ठो महाबाहो विमूढो ब्रह्मणः पथि।।6.38।।
transliteration:
kachchin nobhay-vibhraṣhṭaśh chhinnābhram iva naśhyati
apratiṣhṭho mahā-bāho vimūḍho brahmaṇaḥ pathi
word_meanings: kachchit—whether; na—not; ubhaya—both (material and spiritual); vibhraṣhṭaḥ—fallen from; chhinna—torn; abhram—cloud; iva—like; naśhyati—perishes; apratiṣhṭhaḥ—without a foundation; mahā-bāho—O mighty-armed one; vimūḍhaḥ—bewildered; brahmaṇaḥ—of the Supreme; pathi—on the path
translation_english: O mighty-armed Krishna, does not such a man, who is bewildered from the path of transcendence, fall away from both spiritual and material success and perish like a torn cloud, with no position in any sphere?
deep_dive_text: The Torn Cloud Metaphor. Arjuna worries that a failed yogi loses both worlds. They lose the material pleasures they renounced AND the spiritual goal they missed. He fears ending up in 'no-man's land,' perishing without a foundation.

original_script:
एतन्मे संशयं कृष्ण छेत्तुमर्हस्यशेषतः।
त्वदन्यः संशयस्यास्य छेत्ता न ह्युपपद्यते।।6.39।।
transliteration:
etan me saṁśhayaṁ kṛiṣhṇa chhettum arhasy aśheṣhataḥ
tvad-anyaḥ saṁśhayasyāsya chhettā na hy upapadyate
word_meanings: etat—this; me—my; saṁśhayam—doubt; kṛiṣhṇa—Krishna; chhettum—to clear; arhasi—You deserve; aśheṣhataḥ—completely; tvat—than You; anyaḥ—other; saṁśhayasya—of doubt; asya—of this; chhettā—remover; na—not; hi—certainly; upapadyate—is to be found
translation_english: This is my doubt, O Krishna, and I ask You to dispel it completely. But for You, no one is to be found who can destroy this doubt.
deep_dive_text: Total Trust. Arjuna realizes that only Krishna, who knows the secrets of life and death, can answer what happens after the body dies. This shows the deep bond of 'Guru-Shishya' (Teacher-Student) established throughout the chapters.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
पार्थ नैवेह नामुत्र विनाशस्तस्य विद्यते।
न हि कल्याणकृत्कश्चिद्दुर्गतिं तात गच्छति।।6.40।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
pārtha naiveha nāmutra vināśhas tasya vidyate
na hi kalyāṇa-kṛit kaśhchid durgatiṁ tāta gachchhati
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; pārtha—Arjun; na eva—never; iha—in this life; na—not; amutra—in the next life; vināśhaḥ—destruction; tasya—for him; vidyate—exists; na—never; hi—certainly; kalyāṇa-kṛit—one who does good; kaśhchit—anyone; durgatim—to a bad destination; tāta—my friend; gachchhati—goes
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: Son of Pritha, a transcendentalist engaged in auspicious activities does not meet with destruction either in this world or in the spiritual world; one who does good, My friend, is never overcome by evil.
deep_dive_text: The Divine Guarantee. This is an insurance policy for the soul. Krishna reassures Arjuna that 'Kalyāṇa-kṛit' (one who performs good/auspicious acts) can never be ruined. Spiritual progress is permanent; it stays in your 'soul's bank account' and carries over, even if you don't finish the job in one lifetime.

original_script:
प्राप्य पुण्यकृतां लोकानुषित्वा शाश्वतीः समाः।
शुचीनां श्रीमतां गेहे योगभ्रष्टोऽभिजायते।।6.41।।
transliteration:
prāpya puṇya-kṛitāṁ lokān uṣhitvā śhāśhvatīḥ samāḥ
śhuchīnāṁ śhrīmatāṁ gehe yoga-bhraṣhṭo ’bhijāyate
word_meanings: prāpya—attaining; puṇya-kṛitām—of the righteous; lokān—worlds; uṣhitvā—having dwelt; śhāśhvatīḥ—for many; samāḥ—years; śhuchīnām—of the pious; śhrī-matām—of the prosperous; gehe—in the house; yoga-bhraṣhṭaḥ—one who fell from the path of Yoga; abhijāyate—is born
translation_english: The unsuccessful yogi, after many, many years of enjoyment on the planets of the pious living entities, is born into a family of righteous people, or into a family of rich aristocracy.
deep_dive_text: The Safety Net of Karma. Krishna explains that even an 'unsuccessful' yogi earns great merit. They first enjoy heavenly rewards and then are given a 'head start' in their next life by being born into an environment (pious or prosperous) where they have the leisure and values to pursue spirituality again.

original_script:
अथवा योगिनामेव कुले भवति धीमताम्।
एतद्धि दुर्लभतरं लोके जन्म यदीदृशम्।।6.42।।
transliteration:
athavā yoginām eva kule bhavati dhīmatām
etad dhi durlabhataraṁ loke janma yad īdṛiśham
word_meanings: athavā—or; yoginām—of yogis; eva—certainly; kule—in the family; bhavati—takes birth; dhī-matām—of those with great wisdom; etat—this; hi—certainly; durlabha-taram—very rare; loke—in this world; janma—birth; yat—which; īdṛiśham—like this
translation_english: Or [if unsuccessful after long practice] he takes his birth in a family of wise yogis. Certainly such a birth is rare in this world.
deep_dive_text: The Rare Birth. For a soul that was very advanced but didn't quite finish the journey, they are born directly into a family of 'Dhimatam' (wise yogis). This is considered more valuable than being born into wealth, as the spiritual atmosphere from birth allows for rapid progress without material distractions.

original_script:
तत्र तं बुद्धिसंयोगं लभते पौर्वदेहिकम्।
यतते च ततो भूयः संसिद्धौ कुरुनन्दन।।6.43।।
transliteration:
tatra taṁ buddhi-saṅyogaṁ labhate paurva-dehikam
yatate cha tato bhūyaḥ saṁsiddhau kuru-nandana
word_meanings: tatra—there; tam—that; buddhi-saṅyogam—revival of consciousness; labhate—attains; paurva-dehikam—from the previous body; yatate—strives; cha—and; tataḥ—from that point; bhūyaḥ—again; saṁsiddhau—for perfection; kuru-nandana—son of the Kurus
translation_english: On taking such a birth, he revives the divine consciousness of his previous life, and he again tries to make further progress in order to achieve complete success, O son of Kuru.
deep_dive_text: Spiritual Muscle Memory. This is a foundational verse for the concept of 'Samskaras' (latent impressions). Krishna explains that you don't start from scratch; your 'Buddhi-sanyoga' (intellectual connection) from your past life is reactivated. This explains child prodigies or people who feel a natural, 'unexplained' pull toward meditation.

original_script:
पूर्वाभ्यासेन तेनैव ह्रियते ह्यवशोऽपि सः।
जिज्ञासुरपि योगस्य शब्दब्रह्मातिवर्तते।।6.44।।
transliteration:
pūrvābhyāsena tenaiva hriyate hy avaśho ’pi saḥ
jijñāsur api yogasya śhabda-brahmātivartate
word_meanings: pūrva—previous; abhyāsena—by practice; tena—by that; eva—certainly; hriyate—is attracted; hi—surely; avaśhaḥ—helplessly; api—even; saḥ—he; jijñāsuḥ—inquisitive; api—even; yogasya—about Yoga; śhabda-brahma—ritualistic principles of scripture; ativartate—transcends
translation_english: By virtue of the divine consciousness of his previous life, he automatically becomes attracted to the yogic principles—even without seeking them. Such an inquisitive transcendentalist stands always above the ritualistic principles of the scriptures.
deep_dive_text: The Internal Compass. A 'Yoga-bhrashta' (fallen yogi) is 'helplessly' ($Avaśhaḥ$) drawn back to the path. Their past practice creates a gravitational pull toward wisdom that transcends mere religious rituals ($Shabda-brahma$). They seek the essence of truth because their soul remembers its previous flavor.

original_script:
प्रयत्नाद्यतमानस्तु योगी संशुद्धकिल्बिषः।
अनेकजन्मसंसिद्धस्ततो याति परां गतिम्।।6.45।।
transliteration:
prayatnād yatamānas tu yogī saṁśhuddha-kilbiṣhaḥ
aneka-janma-saṁsiddhas tato yāti parāṁ gatim
word_meanings: prayatnāt—with great effort; yatamānaḥ—striving; tu—but; yogī—the yogi; saṁśhuddha—purified; kilbiṣhaḥ—from all sins; aneka—after many; janma—births; saṁsiddhaḥ—having achieved perfection; tataḥ—then; yāti—attains; parām—the highest; gatim—destination
translation_english: And when the yogi engages himself with sincere endeavor in making further progress, being washed of all contaminations, then ultimately, after many, many births of practice, he attains the supreme goal.
deep_dive_text: The Compound Interest of Soul. Sincere effort ($Prayatnāt$) combined with the momentum of many births ($Aneka-janma$) eventually leads to the finish line. This verse encourages the user to be patient; spiritual evolution is a marathon, not a sprint, and every step counts toward the 'Param Gatim' (highest destination).

original_script:
तपस्विभ्योऽधिको योगी ज्ञानिभ्योऽपि मतोऽधिकः।
कर्मिभ्यश्चाधिको योगी तस्माद्योगी भवार्जुन।।6.46।।
transliteration:
tapasvibhyo ’dhiko yogī jñānibhyo ’pi mato ’dhikaḥ
karmibhyo chādhiko yogī tasmād yogī bhavārjuna
word_meanings: tapasvibhyaḥ—than the ascetics; adhikaḥ—greater; yogī—the yogi; jñānibhyaḥ—than the wise; api—also; mataḥ—is considered; adhikaḥ—greater; karmibhyaḥ—than the fruitive workers; cha—also; adhikaḥ—greater; yogī—the yogi; tasmāt—therefore; yogī—a yogi; bhava—become; arjuna—Arjun
translation_english: A yogi is greater than the ascetic, greater than the empiricist and greater than the fruitive worker. Therefore, O Arjuna, in all circumstances, be a yogi.
deep_dive_text: The Supremacy of Yoga. Krishna compares different types of seekers. The Yogi is superior to the 'Tapasvi' (who only does physical penance), the 'Jnani' (who only has theoretical knowledge), and the 'Karmi' (who only does good deeds for rewards). Why? Because the Yogi *connects* all these—action, knowledge, and discipline—into a unified relationship with the Divine.

original_script:
योगिनामपि सर्वेषां मद्गतेनान्तरात्मना।
श्रद्धावान्भजते यो मां स मे युक्ततमो मतः।।6.47।।
transliteration:
yoginām api sarveṣhāṁ mad-gatenāntar-ātmanā
śhraddhāvān bhajate yo māṁ sa me yuktatamo mataḥ
word_meanings: yoginām—of yogis; api—also; sarveṣhām—of all; mat-gatena—abiding in Me; antaḥ-ātmanā—within the self; śhraddhā-vān—full of faith; bhajate—renders service; yaḥ—who; mām—unto Me; saḥ—he; me—by Me; yukta-tamaḥ—the greatest yogi; mataḥ—is considered
translation_english: And of all yogis, the one with great faith who always abides in Me, thinks of Me within himself, and renders transcendental loving service to Me—he is the most intimately united with Me in yoga and is the highest of all. That is My opinion.
deep_dive_text: The Highest Yogi ($Yuktatamaḥ$). The chapter ends with a climax. While there are many types of Yogis, the one who adds **Bhakti** (devotion/love) to their meditation is the 'Yuktatama' (the most united). Krishna is subtly moving Arjuna toward the next chapters, where the path of Love and Devotion will be explained in detail.

original_script: अथ सप्तमोऽध्यायः - ज्ञानविज्ञानयोगः
transliteration: Jñāna Vijñāna Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Jñāna—theory/knowledge; Vijñāna—realization/experience; Yogaḥ—the path of connection.
translation_english: Chapter 7 Summary: Knowledge of the Ultimate
deep_dive_text: Summary : How do we recognize the Divine in the physical world? Krishna begins by revealing that there is nothing higher than Him. He explains that the world is composed of two energies: the material (earth, water, fire, etc.) and the spiritual (the life force). This chapter is the 'Grand Unified Theory' of the Gita, showing that everything we see, hear, and touch is actually a thread in the cosmic fabric of the Supreme.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
मय्यासक्तमनाः पार्थ योगं युञ्जन्मदाश्रयः।
असंशयं समग्रं मां यथा ज्ञास्यसि तच्छृणु।।7.1।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
mayy āsakta-manāḥ pārtha yogaṁ yuñjan mad-āśhrayaḥ
asaṁśhayaṁ samagraṁ māṁ yathā jñāsyasi tach chhṛiṇu
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; mayi—in Me; āsakta-manāḥ—mind attached; pārtha—Arjun; yogam—Yoga; yuñjan—practicing; mat-āśhrayaḥ—taking shelter of Me; asaṁśhayam—without doubt; samagram—completely; mām—Me; yathā—how; jñāsyasi—you shall know; tat—that; śhṛiṇu—listen
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: Now listen, O son of Pritha, how by practicing yoga in full consciousness of Me, with mind attached to Me, you can know Me fully, free from doubt.
deep_dive_text: The Complete Knowledge ($Samagram$). Krishna promises Arjuna that he won't just learn 'about' God, but will know Him 'Samagram'—totally and in full. The requirement is 'Mayyāsakta-manāḥ' (a mind attached to Me). This shift from self-effort (Chapter 6) to Divine-reliance (Chapter 7) is the bridge to higher realization.

original_script:
ज्ञानं तेऽहं सविज्ञानमिदं वक्ष्याम्यशेषतः।
यज्ज्ञात्वा नेह भूयोऽन्यज्ज्ञातव्यमवशिष्यते।।7.2।।
transliteration:
jñānaṁ te ’haṁ sa-vijñānam idaṁ vakṣhyāmy aśheṣhataḥ
yaj jñātvā neha bhūyo ’nyaj jñātavyam avaśhiṣhyate
word_meanings: jñānam—phenomenal knowledge; te—unto you; aham—I; sa—with; vijñānam—realized knowledge; idam—this; vakṣhyāmi—shall explain; aśheṣhataḥ—completely; yat—which; jñātvā—knowing; na—not; iha—in this world; bhūyaḥ—further; anyat—anything else; jñātavyam—to be known; avaśhiṣhyate—remains
translation_english: I shall now declare unto you in full this knowledge, both phenomenal and spiritous. This being known, nothing further shall remain for you to know.
deep_dive_text: Theory vs. Realization. Krishna distinguishes between **Jñāna** (knowledge of the material world and its mechanics) and **Vijñāna** (the intuitive, lived experience of the Divine). He claims that this specific wisdom is exhaustive; it is the 'master key' that makes all other information redundant.

original_script:
मनुष्याणां सहस्रेषु कश्चिद्यतति सिद्धये।
यततामपि सिद्धानां कश्चिन्मां वेत्ति तत्त्वतः।।7.3।।
transliteration:
manuṣhyāṇāṁ sahasreṣhu kaśhchid yatati siddhaye
yatatām api siddhāṇāṁ kaśhchin māṁ vetti tattvataḥ
word_meanings: manuṣhyāṇām—of men; sahasreṣhu—among thousands; kaśhchit—someone; yatati—strives; siddhaye—for perfection; yatatām—of those striving; api—even; siddhānām—of those who have achieved perfection; kaśhchit—someone; mām—Me; vetti—knows; tattvataḥ—in truth
translation_english: Out of many thousands among men, one may endeavor for perfection, and of those who have achieved perfection, hardly one knows Me in truth.
deep_dive_text: The Rarity of Realization. This verse highlights how difficult the journey is. Most people never start; many who start never finish; and even among those who reach high spiritual states, only a rare few 'Vetti Tattvataḥ'—know the absolute Truth as it really is. It encourages the user to be one of those dedicated few.

original_script:
भूमिरापोऽनलो वायुः खं मनो बुद्धिरेव च।
अहङ्कार इतीयं मे भिन्ना प्रकृतिरष्टधा।।7.4।।
transliteration:
bhūmir āpo ’nalo vāyuḥ khaṁ mano buddhir eva cha
ahaṅkāra itīyaṁ me bhinnā prakṛitir aṣhṭadhā
word_meanings: bhūmiḥ—earth; āpaḥ—water; analaḥ—fire; vāyuḥ—air; kham—ether; manaḥ—mind; buddhiḥ—intellect; eva—certainly; cha—and; ahaṅkāraḥ—ego; iti—thus; iyam—all these; me—My; bhinnā—separated; prakṛitiḥ—energies; aṣhṭadhā—eightfold
translation_english: Earth, water, fire, air, ether, mind, intellect, and ego—these eight constitute the divided components of My material energy (*Prakriti*).
deep_dive_text: This provides the standard Samkhya categorization of matter: the five gross elements plus the three subtle elements (Mind, Intellect, and Ego). It serves as the Gita's formal ontology of the material world.

original_script:
अपरेयमितस्त्वन्यां प्रकृतिं विद्धि मे पराम्।
जीवभूतां महाबाहो ययेदं धार्यते जगत्।।7.5।।
transliteration:
apareyam itas tvanyāṁ prakṛitiṁ viddhi me parām
jīva-bhūtāṁ mahā-bāho yayedaṁ dhāryate jagat
word_meanings: aparā—inferior; iyam—this; itaḥ—besides this; tu—but; anyām—another; prakṛitim—energy; viddhi—know; me—My; parām—superior; jīva-bhūtām—comprising the living entities; mahā-bāho—O mighty-armed one; yayā—by which; idam—this; dhāryate—is sustained; jagat—the universe
translation_english: Besides these, O mighty-armed Arjuna, there is another, superior energy of Mine, which comprises the living entities who are exploiting the resources of this material, inferior nature.
deep_dive_text: The Life Force ($Parā Prakṛiti$). Beyond the 8 material elements is the 'Para' or higher energy—Consciousness. This is the 'Jiva' or the soul. Krishna explains that the dead matter (lower nature) is animated and sustained by this higher spiritual energy. Without the observer, the observed has no meaning.

original_script:
एतद्योनीनि भूतानि सर्वाणीत्युपधारय।
अहं कृत्स्नस्य जगतः प्रभवः प्रलयस्तथा।।7.6।।
transliteration:
etad-yonīni bhūtāni sarvāṇīty upadhāraya
ahaṁ kṛitsnasya jagataḥ prabhavaḥ pralaya s tathā
word_meanings: etat—these two; yonīni—whose source of birth; bhūtāni—beings; sarvāṇi—all; iti—thus; upadhāraya—know; aham—I; kṛitsnasya—of the entire; jagataḥ—of the world; prabhavaḥ—the source of manifestation; pralayaḥ—the source of dissolution; tathā—also
translation_english: All created beings have their source in these two natures. Know that of all that is material and all that is spiritual in this world, I am both the origin and the dissolution.
deep_dive_text: The Alpha and Omega. Krishna claims total authorship. Every being in the universe is a combination of His lower (material) and higher (spiritual) energies. He is the 'Womb' from which everything emerges and the 'Void' into which everything eventually returns.

original_script:
मत्तः परतरं नान्यत्किञ्चिदस्ति धनञ्जय।
मयि सर्वमिदं प्रोतं सूत्रे मणिगणा इव।।7.7।।
transliteration:
mattaḥ parataraṁ nānyat kiñchid asti dhanañjaya
mayi sarvam idaṁ protaṁ sūtre maṇi-gaṇā iva
word_meanings: mattaḥ—beyond Me; para-taram—superior; na—not; anyat kiñchit—anything else; asti—there is; dhanañjaya—Arjun; mayi—in Me; sarvam—all; idam—this; protam—is strung; sūtre—on a thread; maṇi-gaṇāḥ—pearls; iva—like
translation_english: O conqueror of wealth, there is no truth superior to Me. Everything rests upon Me, as pearls are strung on a thread.
deep_dive_text: The Thread of Reality ($Sūtre$). This is one of the most famous metaphors in the Gita. We see the 'pearls' (stars, people, trees, events), but we don't see the 'thread' (Divine energy) that holds them all together. Krishna is that invisible thread; without Him, the universe would fall apart into disconnected pieces.

original_script:
रसोऽहमप्सु कौन्तेय प्रभास्मि शशिसूर्ययोर।
प्रणवः सर्ववेदेषु शब्दः खे पौरुषं नृषु।।7.8।।
transliteration:
raso ’ham apsu kaunteya prabhāsmi śhaśhi-sūryayoḥ
praṇavaḥ sarva-vedeṣhu śhabdaḥ khe pauruṣhaṁ nṛiṣhu
word_meanings: rasaḥ—taste; aham—I; apsu—in water; kaunteya—son of Kunti; prabhā—light; asmi—I am; śhaśhi-sūryayoḥ—of the moon and the sun; praṇavaḥ—the syllable Om; sarva—in all; vedeṣhu—Vedas; śhabdaḥ—sound; khe—in ether; pauruṣham—ability; nṛiṣhu—in men
translation_english: O son of Kunti, I am the taste of water, the light of the sun and the moon, the syllable Om in the Vedic mantras; I am the sound in ether and ability in man.
deep_dive_text: Divine Essence in the Ordinary. Krishna explains how to 'see' Him without needing a temple. He is the very 'quality' that makes things what they are. When you taste water, that refreshing 'Rasa' is God. When you see light, that brilliance is God. When you see someone excel, that 'Paurusham' (talent/ability) is the Divine shining through.

original_script:
पुण्यो गन्धः पृथिव्यां च तेजश्चास्मि विभावसौ।
जीवनं सर्वभूतेषु तपश्चास्मि तपस्विषु।।7.9।।
transliteration:
puṇyo gandhaḥ pṛithivyāṁ cha tejaśh chāsmi vibhāvasau
jīvanaṁ sarva-bhūteṣhu tapaśh chāsmi tapasviṣhu
word_meanings: puṇyaḥ—pure; gandhaḥ—fragrance; pṛithivyām—in the earth; cha—and; tejaḥ—brilliance; cha—also; asmi—I am; vibhāvasau—in fire; jīvanam—life; sarva-bhūteṣhu—in all living entities; tapaḥ—austerity; cha—also; asmi—I am; tapasviṣhu—in ascetics
translation_english: I am the original fragrance of the earth, and I am the heat in fire. I am the life of all that lives, and I am the penances of all ascetics.
deep_dive_text: The Essence of Existence. Krishna continues the list of His 'signatures' in nature. The 'Puṇya' (original/pure) scent of the earth after rain, the raw energy of fire, and the very 'will to live' in every creature are all manifestations of His presence. He is also the internal strength ($Tapaḥ$) that allows a disciplined person to endure hardship.

original_script:
बीजं मां सर्वभूतानां विद्धि पार्थ सनातनम्।
बुद्धिर्बुद्धिमतामस्मि तेजस्तेजस्विनामहम्।।7.10।।
transliteration:
bījaṁ māṁ sarva-bhūtānāṁ viddhi pārtha sanātanam
buddhir buddhimatām asmi tejas tejasvinām aham
word_meanings: bījam—the seed; mām—Me; sarva-bhūtānām—of all living entities; viddhi—know; pārtha—Arjun; sanātanam—eternal; buddhiḥ—intelligence; buddhi-matām—of the intelligent; asmi—I am; tejaḥ—prowess; tejasvinām—of the powerful; aham—I am
translation_english: O son of Pritha, know that I am the eternal seed of all existences, the intelligence of the intelligent, and the prowess of all powerful men.
deep_dive_text: The Eternal Seed ($Sanātanam Bījam$). This implies that the potential for everything—every tree, every human, every galaxy—resides in Him. Furthermore, human excellence is not an ego-achievement; the 'IQ' of a genius and the 'Charisma' of a leader are actually Divine traits being channeled through a human form.

original_script:
बलं बलवतां चाहं कामरागविवर्जितम्।
धर्माविरुद्धो भूतेषु कामोऽस्मि भरतर्षभ।।7.11।।
transliteration:
balaṁ balavatāṁ chāhaṁ kāma-rāga-vivarjitam
dharmāviruddho bhūteṣhu kāmo ’smi bharatarṣhabha
word_meanings: balam—strength; bala-vatām—of the strong; cha—and; aham—I am; kāma—desire; rāga—and attachment; vivarjitam—devoid of; dharma-aviruddhaḥ—not conflicting with duty; bhūteṣhu—in all beings; kāmaḥ—desire; asmi—I am; bharata-ṛiṣhabha—O lord of the Bharatas
translation_english: I am the strength of the strong, devoid of passion and desire. I am sex life which is not contrary to religious principles, O lord of the Bharatas [Arjuna].
deep_dive_text: Sanctified Desire. This is a crucial distinction. Krishna isn't against strength or desire ($Kāma$); He is against *selfish* strength and *unregulated* desire. Strength used for protection ($Dharma$) and desire used for the continuation of life are Divine. He validates human nature by showing how it can be spiritually aligned.

original_script:
ये चैव सात्त्विका भावा राजसास्तामसाश्च ये।
मत्त एवेति तान्विद्धि न त्वहं तेषु ते मयि।।7.12।।
transliteration:
ye chaiva sāttvikā bhāvā rājasās tāmasāśh cha ye
matta eveti tān viddhi na tv ahaṁ teṣhu te mayi
word_meanings: ye—all which; cha—also; eva—certainly; sāttvikāḥ—in the mode of goodness; bhāvāḥ—states of being; rājasāḥ—in the mode of passion; tāmasāḥ—in the mode of ignorance; cha—also; ye—which; mattaḥ—from Me; eva—certainly; iti—thus; tān—those; viddhi—know; na—not; tu—but; aham—I; teṣhu—in them; te—they; mayi—in Me
translation_english: Know that all states of being—be they of goodness, passion or ignorance—are manifested by My energy. I am, in one sense, everything, but I am independent. I am not under the modes of material nature, for they, on the contrary, are within Me.
deep_dive_text: The Source of the Gunas. Everything in the world falls under three categories ($Sattva$, $Rajas$, $Tamas$). Krishna says these 'programs' come from Him, but He is not 'inside' the program. Like an actor who isn't actually the character they play, the Divine is the source of the world but remains untouched by its limitations.

original_script:
त्रिभिर्गुणमयैर्भावैरेभिः सर्वमिदं जगत्।
मोहितं नाभिजानाति मामेभ्यः परमव्ययम्।।7.13।।
transliteration:
tribhir guṇa-mayair bhāvair ebhiḥ sarvam idaṁ jagat
mohitaṁ nābhijānāti mām ebhyaḥ param avyayam
word_meanings: tribhiḥ—by three; guṇa-mayaiḥ—consisting of the gunas; bhāvaiḥ—by states of being; ebhiḥ—all these; sarvam—all; idam—this; jagat—universe; mohitam—deluded; na abhijānāti—does not know; mām—Me; ebhyaḥ—above these; param—the Supreme; avyayam—inexhaustible
translation_english: Deluded by the three modes [goodness, passion and ignorance], the whole world does not know Me, who am above the modes and inexhaustible.
deep_dive_text: The Veil of the Modes. Why don't people see the 'Thread' mentioned in 7.7? Because the three Gunas create a 'mental smoke' or delusion ($Mohitam$). We get so caught up in the drama of goodness, passion, and darkness that we fail to see the source that is 'Param Avyayam' (Supreme and Inexhaustible) above it all.

original_script:
दैवी ह्येषा गुणमयी मम माया दुरत्यया।
मामेव ये प्रपद्यन्ते मायामेतां तरन्ति ते।।7.14।।
transliteration:
daivī hy eṣhā guṇa-mayī mama māyā duratyayā
mām eva ye prapadyante māyām etāṁ taranti te
word_meanings: daivī—divine; hi—certainly; eṣhā—this; guṇa-mayī—consisting of the three modes; mama—My; māyā—energy; duratyayā—very difficult to overcome; mām—unto Me; eva—only; ye—those who; prapadyante—surrender; māyām—energy; etām—this; taranti—cross over; te—they
translation_english: This divine energy of Mine, consisting of the three modes of material nature, is difficult to overcome. But those who have surrendered unto Me can easily cross over it.
deep_dive_text: Crossing the Ocean of Illusion ($Māyā$). Krishna admits that the illusion of the world is 'Duratyayā'—extremely difficult to bypass with just logic or willpower. However, he provides a 'Cheat Code': Surrender. When a soul turns toward the Source, the illusion loses its power, and they 'cross over' into reality.

original_script:
न मां दुष्कृतिनो मूढाः प्रपद्यन्ते नराधमाः।
माययापहृतज्ञाना आसुरं भावमाश्रिताः।।7.15।।
transliteration:
na māṁ duṣhkṛitino mūḍhāḥ prapadyante narādhamāḥ
māyayāpahṛita-jñānā āsuraṁ bhāvam āśhritāḥ
word_meanings: na—not; mām—unto Me; duṣhkṛitinaḥ—miscreants; mūḍhāḥ—foolish; prapadyante—surrender; nara-adhamāḥ—the lowest among mankind; māyayā—by the illusory energy; apahṛita—stolen; jñānāḥ—whose knowledge; āsuram—demoniac; bhāvam—nature; āśhritāḥ—accepting
translation_english: Those miscreants who are grossly foolish, who are lowest among mankind, whose knowledge is stolen by illusion, and who partake of the atheistic nature of demons do not surrender unto Me.
deep_dive_text: The Obstacles to Surrender. Krishna categorizes those who refuse the spiritual path. It isn't just about 'not believing'; it's about being 'Apahṛita-jñānā'—having your wisdom stolen by pride, ego, or purely materialistic focus. They are anchored in 'Asuric' (selfish/destructive) tendencies that make them blind to the 'Thread.'

original_script:
चतुर्विधा भजन्ते मां जनाः सुकृतिनोऽर्जुन।
आर्तो जिज्ञासुरर्थार्थी ज्ञानी च भरतर्षभ।।7.16।।
transliteration:
chatur-vidhā bhajante māṁ janāḥ sukṛitino ’rjuna
ārto jijñāsur arthārthī jñānī cha bharatarṣhabha
word_meanings: chatuḥ-vidhāḥ—four kinds of; bhajante—worship; mām—Me; janāḥ—people; sukṛitinaḥ—pious; arjuna—Arjun; ārtaḥ—the distressed; jijñāsuḥ—the inquisitive; artha-arthī—the seeker of wealth; jñānī—the one in knowledge; cha—and; bharata-ṛiṣhabha—O lord of the Bharatas
translation_english: O best among the Bharatas, four kinds of pious men begin to render devotional service unto Me—the distressed, the desirer of wealth, the inquisitive, and he who is searching for knowledge of the Absolute.
deep_dive_text: The Four Gateways to the Divine. Krishna identifies why people start their spiritual journey: 1. **Ārtaḥ** (Suffering/Grief); 2. **Arthārthī** (Need for material gain); 3. **Jijñāsuḥ** (Curiosity); and 4. **Jñānī** (Intellectual pursuit of Truth). Even if the motive starts as selfish (wealth or help with pain), the fact that they are looking toward the Divine makes them 'Sukṛitino' (pious).

original_script:
तेषां ज्ञानी नित्ययुक्त एकभक्तिर्विशिष्यते।
प्रियो हि ज्ञानिनोऽत्यर्थमहं स च मम प्रियः।।7.17।।
transliteration:
teṣhāṁ jñānī nitya-yukta eka-bhaktir viśhiṣhyate
priyo hi jñānino ’tyartham ahaṁ sa cha mama priyaḥ
word_meanings: teṣhām—of them; jñānī—the one in knowledge; nitya-yuktaḥ—always united; eka-bhaktiḥ—in exclusive devotion; viśhiṣhyate—is special; priyaḥ—dear; hi—certainly; jñāninaḥ—of the wise; atyartham—highly; aham—I am; saḥ—he; cha—also; mama—to Me; priyaḥ—dear
translation_english: Of these, the one in full knowledge who is always engaged in pure devotional service is the best. For I am very dear to him, and he is dear to Me.
deep_dive_text: The Favorite Seeker. While Krishna accepts all four types of seekers, the Jñānī stands out. Unlike the distressed or the seeker of wealth, the Jñānī doesn't want anything *from* God; they only want God. This 'Eka-bhakti' (one-pointed devotion) creates a mutual bond of love that is the highest state of Yoga.

original_script:
उदाराः सर्व एवैते ज्ञानी त्वात्मैव मे मतम्।
आस्थितः स हि युक्तात्मा मामेवानुत्तमां गतिम्।।7.18।।
transliteration:
udārāḥ sarva evaite jñānī tvātmaiva me matam
āsthitaḥ sa hi yuktātmā mām evānuttamāṁ gatim
word_meanings: udārāḥ—noble; sarve—all; eva—certainly; ete—these; jñānī—the wise; tu—but; ātmā eva—just like My own self; me—My; matam—opinion; āsthitaḥ—situated; saḥ—he; hi—certainly; yukta-ātmā—united with the self; mām—in Me; eva—certainly; anuttamām—the highest; gatim—destination
translation_english: All these devotees are undoubtedly magnanimous souls, but he who is situated in knowledge of Me I consider to be as good as My own self. Being engaged in My transcendental service, he is sure to attain Me, the highest and most perfect goal.
deep_dive_text: As Good as Myself. Krishna calls all seekers 'Udārāḥ' (noble/magnanimous). However, He says the Jñānī is His 'Atma' (very soul). This is because the wise person has no separation in their mind; they have surrendered their ego so completely that they live in constant union with the Divine.

original_script:
बहूनां जन्मनामन्ते ज्ञानवान्मां प्रपद्यते।
वासुदेवः सर्वमिति स महात्मा सुदुर्लभः।।7.19।।
transliteration:
bahūnāṁ janmanām ante jñānavān māṁ prapadyate
vāsudevaḥ sarvam iti sa mahātmā su-durlabhaḥ
word_meanings: bahūnām—many; janmanām—of births; ante—at the end; jñāna-vān—the wise; mām—unto Me; prapadyante—surrenders; vāsudevaḥ—the Divine; sarvam—everything; iti—thus; saḥ—that; mahā-ātmā—great soul; su-durlabhaḥ—is very rare
translation_english: After many births and deaths, he who is actually in knowledge surrenders unto Me, knowing Me to be the cause of all causes and all that is. Such a great soul is very rare.
deep_dive_text: The Rare Realization ($Su-durlabhaḥ$). It takes many lifetimes of trial and error to reach the conclusion that 'Vasudevaḥ Sarvam'—the Divine is everything. This is the ultimate 'Mahatma' (Great Soul). They no longer see a world separate from God; they see only God manifesting as the world.

original_script:
कामैस्तैस्तैर्हृतज्ञानाः प्रपद्यन्तेऽन्यदेवताः।
तं तं नियममास्थाय प्रकृत्या नियताः स्वया।।7.20।।
transliteration:
kāmais tais tair hṛita-jñānāḥ prapadyante ’nyā-devatāḥ
taṁ taṁ niyamam āsthāya prakṛityā niyatāḥ svayā
word_meanings: kāmaiḥ—by desires; taiḥ taiḥ—various; hṛita—stolen; jñānāḥ—knowledge; prapadyante—surrender; anya—to other; devatāḥ—gods; tam tam—various; niyamam—regulations; āsthāya—following; prakṛityā—by nature; niyatāḥ—controlled; svayā—by their own
translation_english: Those whose intelligence has been stolen by material desires surrender unto demigods and follow the particular rules and regulations of worship according to their own natures.
deep_dive_text: Stolen Intelligence ($Hṛita-jñānāḥ$). Krishna describes people who worship 'Anya-devatāḥ' (other powers/deities) for quick material fixes. He says their wisdom is 'stolen' by their cravings. They are so eager for a specific result (wealth, health, fame) that they miss the big picture—the Source of all power.

original_script:
यो यो यां यां तनुं भक्तः श्रद्धयार्चितुमिच्छति।
तस्य तस्याचलां श्रद्धां तामेव विदधाम्यहम्।।7.21।।
transliteration:
yo yo yāṁ yāṁ tanuṁ bhaktaḥ śhraddhayārchitum ichchhati
tasya tasyāchalāṁ śhraddhāṁ tām eva vidadhāmy aham
word_meanings: yaḥ yaḥ—whoever; yām yām—whichever; tanum—form; bhaktaḥ—devotee; śhraddhayā—with faith; architum—to worship; ichchhati—desires; tasya tasya—his; achalām—steady; śhraddhām—faith; tām—that; eva—certainly; vidadhāmi—bestow; aham—I
translation_english: I am in everyone’s heart as the Supersoul. As soon as one desires to worship some demigod, I make his faith steady so that he can devote himself to that particular deity.
deep_dive_text: The Neutral Facilitator. This is a subtle point: even when someone worships a 'lesser' power, it is Krishna (the Source) who provides the 'faith' to do so. He respects human free will so much that He helps the seeker be successful in whatever they choose, even if it's not the highest path.

original_script:
स तया श्रद्धया युक्तस्तस्याराधनमीहते।
लभते च ततः कामान्मयैव विहितान्हि तान्।।7.22।।
transliteration:
sa tayā śhraddhayā yuktas tasyārādhanam īhate
labhate cha tataḥ kāmān mayaiva vihitān hi tān
word_meanings: saḥ—he; tayā—with that; śhraddhayā—faith; yuktaḥ—endowed; tasya—of that; ārādhanam—worship; īhate—aspires; labhate—attains; cha—also; tataḥ—from that; kāmān—desires; mayā—by Me; eva—alone; vihitān—ordained; hi—certainly; tān—those
translation_english: Endowed with such faith, he endeavors to worship a particular demigod and obtains his desires. But in actuality these benefits are bestowed by Me alone.
deep_dive_text: The Hidden Provider. A person might think their success came from a specific ritual or a specific deity, but Krishna reveals that He is the one fulfilling the order behind the scenes. All 'paychecks' in the universe are ultimately signed by the Supreme, regardless of which 'department' (deity) you work for.

original_script:
अन्तवत्तु फलं तेषां तद्भवत्यल्पमेधसाम्।
देवान्देवयजो यान्ति मद्भक्ता यान्ति मामपि।।7.23।।
transliteration:
antavat tu phalaṁ teṣhāṁ tad bhavaty alpa-medhasām
devān deva-yajo yānti mad-bhaktā yānti mām api
word_meanings: antavat—perishable; tu—but; phalam—fruit; teṣhām—their; tat—that; bhavati—becomes; alpa-medhasām—for those of small intelligence; devān—to the gods; deva-yajaḥ—the worshippers of gods; yānti—go; mat—My; bhaktāḥ—devotees; yānti—attain; mām—Me; api—also
translation_english: Men of small intelligence worship the demigods, and their fruits are limited and temporary. Those who worship the demigods go to the planets of the demigods, but My devotees ultimately reach My supreme planet.
deep_dive_text: Perishable Rewards ($Antavat$). Krishna calls this 'Alpa-medhasām' (small intelligence) because people work so hard for things that will eventually rot or be lost. Why trade your life for a 'temporary planet' when you could attain the 'Supreme Abode'? It is a call to invest in the eternal rather than the fleeting.

original_script:
अव्यक्तं व्यक्तिमापन्नं मन्यन्ते मामबुद्धयः।
परं भावमजानन्तो ममाव्ययमनुत्तमम्।।7.24।।
transliteration:
avyaktaṁ vyaktim āpannaṁ manyante mām abuddhayaḥ
paraṁ bhāvam ajānanto mamāvyayam anuttamam
word_meanings: avyaktam—unmanifested; vyaktim—personality; āpannam—attained; manyante—think; mām—Me; abuddhayaḥ—the unintelligent; param—higher; bhāvam—existence; ajānantaḥ—not knowing; mama—My; avyayam—imperishable; anuttamam—the finest
translation_english: Unintelligent men, who do not know Me perfectly, think that I, the Supreme Personality of Godhead, Krishna, was impersonal before and have now assumed this personality. Due to their small knowledge, they do not know My higher nature, which is imperishable and supreme.
deep_dive_text: The Mystery of Form. This is a deep philosophical point: Krishna is not just a human who became a 'God'. He is the Infinite ($Avyakta$) that has manifested as a person ($Vyakti$) for the sake of the devotee. People who think He is 'only' the physical body in front of them miss His 'Paraṁ Bhāvam'—His eternal, all-pervading reality.

original_script: अथ अष्टमोऽध्यायः - अक्षरब्रह्मयोगः
transliteration: Akṣhara Brahma Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Akṣhara—indestructible; Brahma—the Absolute; Yogaḥ—the path of synthesis.
translation_english: Chapter 8 Summary: The Yoga of the Indestructible Absolute
deep_dive_text: This chapter codifies the technical process of 'Anta-kale' (transition at death). Historically, it reflects the transition from Vedic external ritualism to Upanishadic internalization. It details 'Yoga-dharana'—the concentration of the life-breath (Prana)—as a systematic discipline rather than a mystical occurrence. It establishes the 'Akshara' (Imperishable) as the ultimate destination, distinguishing it from the 'Kshara' (Perishable) material cycle.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
किं तद्ब्रह्म किमध्यात्मं किं कर्म पुरुषोत्तम।
अधिभूतं च किं प्रोक्तमधिदैवं किमुच्यते।।8.1।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
kiṁ tad brahma kim adhyātmaṁ kiṁ karma puruṣhottama
adhibhūtaṁ cha kiṁ proktam adhidaivaṁ kim uchyate
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjun said; kim—what; tat—that; brahma—Brahman; kim—what; adhyātmam—the self; kim—what; karma—fruitive activities; puruṣha-uttama—O Supreme Person; adhibhūtam—the material manifestation; cha—and; kim—what; proktam—is called; adhidaivam—the demigods; kim—what; uchyate—is called
translation_english: Arjuna inquired: O my Lord, O Supreme Person, what is Brahman? What is the self? What are fruitive activities? What is this material manifestation? And what are the demigods? Please explain this to me.
deep_dive_text: The Seeker's Vocabulary. Arjuna asks for clarity on the 'meta-physical' framework of the universe. He is looking for the 'definitions' behind the cosmic architecture. This sets the stage for Krishna to define the relationship between the Individual, the World, and the Divine.

original_script:
अधियज्ञः कथं कोऽत्र देहेऽस्मिन्मधुसूदन।
प्रयाणकाले च कथं ज्ञेयोऽसि नियतात्मभिः।।8.2।।
transliteration:
adhiyajñaḥ kathaṁ ko ’tra dehe ’smin madhusūdana
prayāṇa-kāle cha kathaṁ jñeyo ’si niyatātmabhiḥ
word_meanings: adhiyajñaḥ—the Lord of sacrifice; katham—how; kaḥ—who; atra—here; dehe—in the body; asmin—this; madhusūdana—O Madhusudana; prayāṇa-kāle—at the time of death; cha—and; katham—how; jñeyaḥ asi—can You be known; niyata-ātmabhiḥ—by those of self-controlled mind
translation_english: Who is the Lord of sacrifice, and how does He live in the body, O Madhusudana? And how can those engaged in devotional service know You at the time of death?
deep_dive_text: The Ultimate Question. Arjuna asks the most practical question for any mortal: how do we maintain our spiritual connection during the trauma and confusion of leaving the body? He wants to know the secret of a 'conscious death.'

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
अक्षरं ब्रह्म परमं स्वभावोऽध्यात्ममुच्यते।
भूतभावोद्भवकरो विसर्गः कर्मसंज्ञिततः।।8.3।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
akṣharaṁ brahma paramaṁ svabhāvo ’dhyātmam uchyate
bhūta-bhāvodbhava-karo visargaḥ karma-sañjñitaḥ
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; akṣharam—indestructible; brahma—Brahman; paramam—transcendental; svabhāvaḥ—eternal nature; adhyātmam—the self; uchyate—is called; bhūta-bhāva-udbhava-karaḥ—producing the bodies of living beings; visargaḥ—creation; karma—action; sañjñitaḥ—is called
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: The indestructible, transcendental living entity is called Brahman, and his eternal nature is called adhyātma, the self. Action pertaining to the development of the material bodies of the living entities is called karma, or fruitive activities.
deep_dive_text: The Definitions. Krishna defines **Brahman** as the indestructible spirit. **Adhyātma** is the individual soul's nature. **Karma** is the creative force ($Visargaḥ$) that causes the manifestation of physical bodies and their consequences. He is explaining the 'software' and 'hardware' of existence.

original_script:
अधिभूतं क्षरो भावः पुरुषश्चाधिदैवतम्।
अधियज्ञोऽहमेवात्र देहे देहभृतां वर।।8.4।।
transliteration:
adhibhūtaṁ kṣharo bhāvaḥ puruṣhaśh chādhidaivatam
adhiyajño ’ham evātra dehe deha-bhṛitāṁ vara
word_meanings: adhibhūtam—the physical manifestation; kṣharaḥ—constantly changing; bhāvaḥ—nature; puruṣhaḥ—the universal form; cha—and; adhidaivatam—the governing divine power; adhiyajñaḥ—the Lord of sacrifice; aham—I am; eva—certainly; atra—here; dehe—in the body; deha-bhṛitām—of the embodied; vara—O best
translation_english: O best of the embodied beings, the physical nature, which is constantly changing, is called adhibhūta [the material manifestation]. The universal cosmic form is called adhidaiva, and I, the Supreme Lord, represented as the Supersoul in the heart of every embodied being, am called adhiyajña [the Lord of sacrifice].
deep_dive_text: The Three Aspects of Reality. 1. **Adhibhūta**: The perishable world of matter. 2. **Adhidaiva**: The cosmic intelligence behind the demigods. 3. **Adhiyajña**: Krishna Himself, residing in the heart of every being as the witness and the guide. This completes the map of the universe.

original_script:
अन्तकाले च मामेव स्मरन्मुक्त्वा कलेवरम्।
यः प्रयाति स मद्भावं याति नास्त्यत्र संशयः।।8.5।।
transliteration:
anta-kāle cha mām eva smaran muktvā kalevaram
yaḥ prayāti sa mad-bhāvaṁ yāti nāsty atra sañśhayaḥ
word_meanings: anta-kāle—at the end of life; cha—also; mām—Me; eva—certainly; smaran—remembering; muktvā—quitting; kalevaram—the body; yaḥ—who; prayāti—goes; saḥ—he; mat-bhāvam—My nature; yāti—achieves; na—not; asti—there is; atra—here; sañśhayaḥ—doubt
translation_english: And whoever, at the end of his life, quits his body remembering Me alone at once attains My nature. Of this there is no doubt.
deep_dive_text: The Law of the Last Thought. This is the central thesis of Chapter 8. Your consciousness at the moment of death ($Anta-kāle$) acts as a 'coordinate' for your next destination. If you are focused on the Divine, you go to the Divine. It is the spiritual equivalent of 'saving your game' at the highest level.

original_script:
यं यं वापि स्मरन्भावं त्यजत्यन्ते कलेवरम्।
तं तमेवैति कौन्तेय सदा तद्भावभावितः।।8.6।।
transliteration:
yaṁ yaṁ vāpi smaran bhāvaṁ tyajaty ante kalevaram
taṁ tam evaiti kaunteya sadā tad-bhāva-bhāvitaḥ
word_meanings: yam yam—whatever; vā api—at all; smaran—remembering; bhāvam—nature; tyajati—gives up; ante—at the end; kalevaram—the body; tam tam—that; eva—certainly; eti—attains; kaunteya—son of Kunti; sadā—always; tat—that; bhāva—state of being; bhāvitaḥ—absorbed
translation_english: Whatever state of being one remembers when he quits his body, O son of Kunti, that state he will attain without fail.
deep_dive_text: The Momentum of Mind. Krishna explains that the last thought isn't a random accident; it is the sum total of what you have focused on your whole life ($Sadā tad-bhāva-bhāvitaḥ$). If you spend your life thinking of money, family, or anger, that is what will surface at the end. You become what you contemplate.

original_script:
तस्मात्सर्वेषु कालेषु मामनुस्मर युध्य च।
मय्यर्पितमनोबुद्धिर्मामेवैष्यस्यसंशयम्।।8.7।।
transliteration:
tasmāt sarveṣhu kāleṣhu mām anusmara yudhya cha
mayy arpita-mano-buddhir mām evaiṣhyasy asaṅśhayaḥ
word_meanings: tasmāt—therefore; sarveṣhu—at all; kāleṣhu—times; mām—Me; anusmara—remember; yudhya—fight; cha—also; mayi—unto Me; arpita—surrender; manaḥ—mind; buddhiḥ—intellect; mām—Me; eva—certainly; eṣhyasi—you will attain; asaṅśhayaḥ—beyond doubt
translation_english: Therefore, Arjuna, you should always think of Me in the form of Krishna and at the same time carry out your prescribed duty of fighting. With your activities dedicated to Me and your mind and intelligence fixed on Me, you will attain Me without doubt.
deep_dive_text: The 'Fight and Remember' Strategy. This is the ultimate 'work-life balance' verse. Krishna doesn't tell Arjuna to stop the war to meditate. He says: 'Yudhya' (Fight/Do your job) and 'Anusmara' (Remember Me) simultaneously. Use your hands for the world, but keep your mind anchored in the Divine.

original_script:
अभ्यासयोगयुक्तेन चेतसा नान्यगामिना।
परमं पुरुषं दिव्यं याति पार्थानुचिन्तयन्।।8.8।।
transliteration:
abhyāsa-yoga-yuktena chetasā nānya-gāminā
paramaṁ puruṣhaṁ divyaṁ yāti pārthānuchintayan
word_meanings: abhyāsa-yoga—by practice of Yoga; yuktena—engaged; chetasā—by the mind; na anya-gāminā—without deviating; paramam—the Supreme; puruṣham—Person; divyam—Divine; yāti—attains; pārtha—Arjun; anuchintayan—constantly meditating
translation_english: He who meditates on Me as the Supreme Personality of Godhead, his mind constantly engaged in remembering Me, undeviated from the path, he, O Partha, is sure to reach Me.
deep_dive_text: The Power of Abhyāsa. 'Abhyāsa' (practice) is what makes the impossible possible. By training the mind every day to return to the Divine, you create a 'default setting.' When the body eventually fails, this trained mind will naturally 'upload' itself to the Supreme Presence.

original_script:
कविं पुराणमनुशासितार-
मणोरणीयंसमनुस्मरेद्यः।
सर्वस्य धातारमचिन्त्यरूप-
मादित्यवर्णं तमसः परस्तात्।।8.9।।
transliteration:
kaviṁ purāṇam anuśhāsitāram
aṇor aṇīyānsam anusmared yaḥ
sarvasya dhātāram achintya-rūpam
āditya-varṇaṁ tamasaḥ parastāt
word_meanings: kavim—the omniscient; purāṇam—the oldest; anuśhāsitāram—the controller; aṇoḥ—than the atom; aṇīyānsam—smaller; anusmaret—remembers; yaḥ—who; sarvasya—of everything; dhātāram—the maintainer; achintya—inconceivable; rūpam—form; āditya-varṇam—effulgent like the sun; tamasaḥ—to darkness; parastāt—transcendental
translation_english: One should meditate upon the Supreme Person as the one who knows everything, as He who is the oldest, who is the controller, who is smaller than the smallest, who is the maintainer of everything, who is beyond all material conception, who is inconceivable, and who is always a person. He is luminous like the sun, and He is transcendental, beyond this material nature.
deep_dive_text: The Attributes of the Absolute. Krishna provides a mental 'anchor' for meditation. By describing the Divine as both 'smaller than an atom' and the 'maintainer of all,' He shows that God is present in every scale of reality. The 'Aditya-varnam' (sun-like) imagery suggests that just as the sun dispels darkness, focusing on this form dispels the ignorance of the mind.

original_script:
प्रयाणकाले मनसाचलेन
भक्त्या युक्तो योगबलेन चैव।
भ्रुवोर्मध्ये प्राणमावेश्य सम्यक्
स तं परं पुरुषमुपैति दिव्यम्।।8.10।।
transliteration:
prayāṇa-kāle manasāchalena
bhaktyā yukto yoga-balena chaiva
bhruvor madhye prāṇam āveśhya samyak
sa taṁ paraṁ puruṣham upaiti divyam
word_meanings: prayāṇa-kāle—at the time of transition; manasā—by the mind; achalena—unmoving; bhaktyā—with focused intent; yuktaḥ—engaged; yoga-balena—by the strength of disciplined practice; cha—also; eva—certainly; bhruvoḥ—the eyebrows; madhye—between; prāṇam—the life-force; āveśhya—establishing; samyak—completely; saḥ—he; tam—that; param—Supreme; puruṣham—Divine Being; upaiti—reaches; divyam—transcendental
translation_english: At the moment of transition, one who fixes the life-force between the eyebrows with an unmoving mind and the strength of disciplined practice, reaches the Supreme Divine Being.
deep_dive_text: Historically, this verse provides a specific 'technique of the Self.' It references the 'Ajna Chakra' (the point between the brows) not as a mystical metaphor, but as a technical focal point in proto-Yoga systems for directing consciousness during the dissolution of the physical body.

original_script:
यदक्षरं वेदविदो वदन्ति
विशन्ति यद्यतयो वीतरागाः।
यदिच्छन्तो ब्रह्मचर्यं चरन्ति
तत्ते पदं संग्रहेण प्रवक्ष्ये।।8.11।।
transliteration:
yad akṣharaṁ veda-vido vadanti
viśhanti yad yatayo vīta-rāgāḥ
yad ichchhanto brahmacharyaṁ charanti
tat te padaṁ saṅgraheṇa pravakṣye
word_meanings: yat—which; akṣharam—imperishable; veda-vidaḥ—knowers of the Vedas; vadanti—say; viśhanti—enter; yat—which; yatayaḥ—great sages; vīta-rāgāḥ—in the renounced order of life; yat—which; ichchhantaḥ—desiring; brahmacharyam—celibacy; charanti—practice; tat—that; te—unto you; padam—attainment/goal; saṅgraheṇa—in summary; pravakṣye—I shall explain
translation_english: Persons learned in the Vedas, who utter omkara and who are great sages in the renounced order, enter into Brahman. Desiring such perfection, one practices celibacy. I shall now explain to you this process by which one may attain salvation.
deep_dive_text: The Ancient Goal. Krishna references the Upanishadic sages to show that this 'imperishable' state is the ultimate destination of all spiritual inquiry. He is offering Arjuna the 'concentrated' version of centuries of wisdom on how to reach the goal of 'Aksharam' (the Indestructible).

original_script:
सर्वद्वाराणि संयम्य मनो हृदि निरुध्य च।
मूर्ध्न्याधायात्मनः प्राणमास्थितो योगधारणाम्।।8.12।।
transliteration:
sarva-dvārāṇi saṁyamya mano hṛidi nirudhya cha
mūrdhny ādhāyātmanaḥ prāṇam āsthito yoga-dhāraṇām
word_meanings: sarva-dvārāṇi—all the gates (of the body); saṁyamya—controlling; manaḥ—the mind; hṛidi—in the heart; nirudhya—confining; cha—also; mūrdhni—on the head; ādhāya—fixing; ātmanaḥ—of the self; prāṇam—the life-air; āsthitaḥ—situated; yoga-dhāraṇām—the yogic state
translation_english: The yogic situation is that of detachment from all sensual engagements. Closing all the doors of the senses and fixing the mind on the heart and the life air at the top of the head, one establishes himself in yoga.
deep_dive_text: Closing the Gates. The 'nine gates' of the body (eyes, ears, etc.) are the ports through which consciousness leaks out. In 'Yoga-dhāraṇā,' the yogi closes these gates, pulls the mind into the heart, and then pushes the Prana to the crown of the head ($Mūrdhni$). This is the 'launchpad' for the soul's liberation.

original_script:
ओमित्येकाक्षरं ब्रह्म व्याहरन्मामनुस्मरन्।
यः प्रयाति त्यजन्देहं स याति परमां गतिम्।।8.13।।
transliteration:
om ity ekākṣharaṁ brahma vyāharan mām anusmaran
yaḥ prayāti tyajan dehaṁ sa yāti parāṁ gatim
word_meanings: om—the sacred syllable Om; iti—thus; eka-akṣharam—the one syllable; brahma—the Supreme; vyāharan—vibrating; mām—Me; anusmaran—remembering; yaḥ—who; prayāti—leaves; tyajan—quitting; deham—the body; saḥ—he; yāti—attains; parām—the highest; gatim—destination
translation_english: After being situated in this yoga practice and vibrating the sacred syllable om, the supreme combination of letters, if one thinks of the Supreme Personality of Godhead and quits his body, he will certainly reach the spiritual planets.
deep_dive_text: The Sound of the Infinite. 'Om' is the sonic representation of the Divine. By vibrating this sound while focusing on Krishna, the yogi aligns their frequency with the Absolute. This verse provides a simple, powerful mantra to accompany the technical yoga of breath control.

original_script:
अनन्यचेताः सततं यो मां स्मरति नित्यशः।
तस्याहं सुलभः पार्थ नित्ययुक्तस्य योगिनः।।8.14।।
transliteration:
ananya-chetāḥ satataṁ yo māṁ smarati nityaśhaḥ
tasyāhaṁ sulabhaḥ pārtha nitya-yuktasya yoginaḥ
word_meanings: ananya-chetāḥ—without deviation of the mind; satatam—always; yaḥ—who; mām—Me; smarati—remembers; nityaśhaḥ—regularly; tasya—for him; aham—I am; su-labhaḥ—easy to obtain; pārtha—Arjun; nitya—constantly; yuktasya—engaged; yoginaḥ—for the yogi
translation_english: For one who remembers Me without deviation, I am easy to obtain, O son of Pritha, because of his constant engagement in devotional service.
deep_dive_text: Easy to Obtain ($Su-labhaḥ$). After describing the difficult technical yoga in verses 10-13, Krishna offers a 'grace-based' alternative. For the person who is simply 'Ananya-chetāḥ' (constantly, lovingly focused on Me), the Divine becomes 'easy' to reach. Devotion makes the technical requirements of yoga secondary to the sincerity of the heart.

original_script:
मामुपेत्य पुनर्जन्म दुःखालयमशाश्वतम्।
नाप्नुवन्ति महात्मानः संसिद्धिं परमां गताः।।8.15।।
transliteration:
mām upetya punar janma duḥkhālayam aśhāśhvatam
nāpnuvanti mahātmānaḥ saṁsiddhiṁ parāṁ gatāḥ
word_meanings: mām—Me; upetya—attaining; punaḥ—again; janma—birth; duḥkha-ālayam—place of miseries; aśhāśhvatam—temporary; na—never; āpnuvanti—attain; mahā-ātmānaḥ—the great souls; saṁsiddhim—perfection; parām—the highest; gatāḥ—having achieved
translation_english: After attaining Me, the great souls, who are yogis in devotion, never return to this temporary world, which is full of miseries, because they have attained the highest perfection.
deep_dive_text: The Nature of the World ($Duḥkhālayam$). Krishna pulls no punches here. He calls the material world a 'place of misery' and 'temporary.' Just as a hospital is a place for the sick, this world is a place for the conditioned soul. Liberation is not just 'going somewhere else'; it is graduating from a school of suffering into a state of 'Samsiddhim' (perfection).

original_script:
आब्रह्मभुवनाल्लोकाः पुनरावर्तिनोऽर्जुन।
मामुपेत्य तु कौन्तेय पुनर्जन्म न विद्यते।।8.16।।
transliteration:
ā-brahma-bhuvanāl lokāḥ punar āvartino ’rjuna
mām upetya tu kaunteya punar janma na vidyate
word_meanings: ā-brahma-bhuvanāt—up to the planet of Brahma; lokāḥ—the planetary systems; punaḥ—again; āvartinaḥ—returning; arjuna—Arjun; mām—Me; upetya—attaining; tu—but; kaunteya—son of Kunti; punar janma—rebirth; na—not; vidyate—exists
translation_english: From the highest planet in the material world down to the lowest, all are places of misery wherein repeated birth and death take place. But one who attains to My abode, O son of Kunti, never takes birth again.
deep_dive_text: No Return. Even the 'heavenly' realms of the universe (like Brahma's world) are temporary. Eventually, one's merit runs out, and they must 'return' ($Punar āvartinaḥ$). Krishna contrasts this with 'His Abode'—a transcendental state that is permanent. This verse is a call to stop aiming for 'better' material lives and start aiming for the Eternal.

original_script:
सहस्रयुगपर्यन्तमहर्यद्ब्रह्मणो विदुः।
रात्रिं युगसहस्रान्तां तेऽहोरात्रविदो जनाः।।8.17।।
transliteration:
sahasra-yuga-paryantam ahar yad brahmaṇo viduḥ
rātriṁ yuga-sahasrāntāṁ te ’ho-rātra-vido janāḥ
word_meanings: sahasra—one thousand; yuga—ages; paryantam—including; ahaḥ—day; yat—which; brahmaṇaḥ—of Brahma; viduḥ—they know; rātrim—night; yuga—ages; sahasra-antām—ending after one thousand; te—they; aho-rātra—day and night; vidaḥ—who know; janāḥ—people
translation_english: By human calculation, a thousand ages taken together form the duration of Brahma’s one day. And such also is the duration of his night.
deep_dive_text: Cosmic Time-Scaling. Krishna introduces the concept of a 'Kalpa'. One day of Brahma lasts 4.32 billion years, followed by a night of equal length. By providing these staggering numbers, Krishna helps Arjuna realize that human problems—and even human civilizations—are incredibly fleeting in the grand design of the cosmos.

original_script:
अव्यक्ताद्व्यक्तयः सर्वाः प्रभवन्त्यहरागमे।
रात्र्यागमे प्रलीयन्ते तत्रैवाव्यक्तसंज्ञके।।8.18।।
transliteration:
avyaktād vyaktayaḥ sarvāḥ prabhavanty ahar-āgame
rātry-āgame pralīyante tatraivāvyakta-sañjñake
word_meanings: avyaktāt—from the unmanifest; vyaktayaḥ—manifested beings; sarvāḥ—all; prabhavanti—become manifest; ahaḥ-āgame—at the beginning of the day; rātri-āgame—at the fall of night; pralīyante—are annihilated; tatra—there; eva—certainly; avyakta—the unmanifest; sañjñake—called
translation_english: At the beginning of Brahma’s day, all living entities become manifest from the unmanifest state, and thereafter, when the night falls, they are merged into the unmanifest again.
deep_dive_text: The Breath of the Universe. This verse describes the 'Big Bang' and 'Big Crunch' as a recurring cycle. Matter is not destroyed; it just cycles between a 'Vyakta' (manifest) state and an 'Avyakta' (dormant/unmanifest) state. This cycle is mechanical and happens automatically with the passage of cosmic time.

original_script:
भूतग्रामः स एवायं भूत्वा भूत्वा प्रलीयते।
रात्र्यागमेऽवशः पार्थ प्रभवत्यहरागमे।।8.19।।
transliteration:
bhūta-grāmaḥ sa evāyaṁ bhūtvā bhūtvā pralīyate
rātry-āgame ’vaśhaḥ pārtha prabhavaty ahar-āgame
word_meanings: bhūta-grāmaḥ—the aggregate of all living entities; saḥ—this; eva—certainly; ayam—this; bhūtvā bhūtvā—repeatedly taking birth; pralīyate—is annihilated; rātri-āgame—at the arrival of night; avaśhaḥ—helplessly; pārtha—Arjun; prabhavati—is manifest; ahaḥ-āgame—at the beginning of the day
translation_english: Again and again, when Brahma’s day arrives, all living entities come into being, and with the arrival of Brahma’s night they are helplessly annihilated.
deep_dive_text: The Helplessness of Material Life ($Avaśhaḥ$). Krishna points out the tragedy of the material cycle: living beings are 'helplessly' dragged into existence and 'helplessly' dissolved. This 'bhūtvā bhūtvā pralīyate' (repeating birth and death) is the very bondage that the soul seeks to escape.

original_script:
परस्तस्मात्तु भावोऽन्योऽव्यक्तोऽव्यक्तात्सनातनः।
यः स सर्वेषु भूतेषु नश्यत्सु न विनश्यति।।8.20।।
transliteration:
paras tasmāt tu bhāvo ’nyo ’vyakto ’vyaktāt sanātanaḥ
yaḥ sa sarveṣhu bhūteṣhu naśhyatsu na vinaśhyati
word_meanings: paraḥ—transcendental; tasmāt—to that; tu—but; bhāvaḥ—nature; anyaḥ—another; avyaktaḥ—unmanifest; avyaktāt—to the unmanifest; sanātanaḥ—eternal; yaḥ—which; saḥ—that; sarveṣhu—all; bhūteṣhu—manifestations; naśhyatsu—being annihilated; na—never; vinaśhyati—is annihilated
translation_english: Yet there is another unmanifest nature, which is eternal and is transcendental to this manifested and unmanifested matter. It is supreme and is never annihilated. When all in this world is annihilated, that part remains as it is.
deep_dive_text: The Imperishable Realm. Beyond the cycling universe of Brahma lies the 'Sanātana' (eternal) unmanifest. This is the 'Spiritual Sky' or the Absolute Truth. While the material world is like a movie being turned on and off, this higher reality is the screen that remains untouched by the drama.

original_script:
अव्यक्तोऽक्षर इत्युक्तस्तमाहुः परमां गतिम्।
यं प्राप्य न निवर्तन्ते तद्धाम परमं मम।।8.21।।
transliteration:
avyakto ’kṣhara ity uktas tam āhuḥ paramāṁ gatim
yaṁ prāpya na nivartante tad dhāma paramaṁ mama
word_meanings: avyaktaḥ—unmanifest; akṣharaḥ—imperishable; iti—thus; uktaḥ—is said; tam—that; āhuḥ—is known; paramām—the ultimate; gatim—destination; yam—which; prāpya—attaining; na—never; nivartante—come back; tat—that; dhāma—abode; paramam—supreme; mama—My
translation_english: That which the Vedantists describe as unmanifest and infallible, that which is known as the supreme destination, that place from which, having attained it, one never returns—that is My supreme abode.
deep_dive_text: The Destination of No-Return. Krishna identifies Himself with this eternal realm ($Dhāma$). This is the 'Paramāṁ Gatim' (Ultimate Goal). The promise is 'Na nivartante'—once you reach this level of consciousness, you are no longer subject to the 'helpless' cycles of cosmic birth and death.

original_script:
पुरुषः स परः पार्थ भक्त्या लभ्यस्त्वनन्यया।
यस्यान्तःस्थानि भूतानि येन सर्वमिदं ततम्।।8.22।।
transliteration:
puruṣhaḥ sa paraḥ pārtha bhaktyā labhyas tv ananyayā
yasyāntaḥ-sthāni bhūtāni yena sarvam idaṁ tatam
word_meanings: puruṣhaḥ—the Supreme Personality; saḥ—He; paraḥ—the Supreme; pārtha—Arjun; bhaktyā—by devotion; labhyaḥ—can be attained; tu—but; ananyayā—unalloyed; yasya—of whom; antaḥ-sthāni—within; bhūtāni—all living entities; yena—by whom; sarvam—all; idam—this; tatam—is pervaded
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead, who is greater than all, is attainable by unalloyed devotion. Although He is present in His abode, He is all-pervading, and everything is situated within Him.
deep_dive_text: Attainment through Devotion ($Ananyayā Bhaktyā$). How does one reach this eternal realm? Not just by calculation or cosmic observation, but by unalloyed love. Krishna explains a paradox: He is 'localized' in His abode but simultaneously 'all-pervading' ($Tatam$). Love is the frequency that connects the soul to this pervasive presence.

original_script:
यत्र काले त्वनावृत्तिमावृत्तिं चैव योगिनः।
प्रयाता यान्ति तं कालं वक्ष्यामि भरतर्षभ।।8.23।।
transliteration:
yatra kāle tv anāvṛittim āvṛittiṁ chaiva yoginaḥ
prayātā yānti taṁ kālaṁ vakṣhyāmi bharatarṣhabha
