God is not just the creator; He is 'Sarva-hara'—the ultimate thief called Death, who steals away all material attachments, resetting the board for the future. In beautiful contrast, He identifies with seven feminine qualities (often personified as goddesses): fame, beauty/fortune, eloquence, memory, intelligence, determination, and forgiveness. Where these virtues reside, the Divine is palpably present.

original_script:
बृहत्साम तथा साम्नां गायत्री छन्दसामहम्।
मासानां मार्गशीर्षोऽहमृतूनां कुसुमाकरः।।10.35।।
transliteration:
bṛihat-sāma tathā sāmnāṁ gāyatrī chhandasām aham
māsānāṁ mārga-śhīrṣho ’ham ṛitūnāṁ kusumākaraḥ
word_meanings: bṛihat-sāma—the Brihat-sama; tathā—also; sāmnām—of the Sama Veda songs; gāyatrī—the Gayatri hymns; chhandasām—of all poetry; aham—I am; māsānām—of months; mārga-śhīrṣhaḥ—the month of November-December; aham—I am; ṛitūnām—of all seasons; kusuma-ākaraḥ—spring
translation_english: Of the hymns in the Sama Veda I am the Brihat-sama, and of poetry I am the Gayatri. Of months I am Margashirsha [November-December], and of seasons I am flower-bearing spring.
deep_dive_text: The Rhythm of Life. The Divine is not chaotic; it operates with perfect rhythm and beauty. The Brihat-sama is sung at midnight for high spiritual connection. The Gayatri mantra is the most sacred meter of Vedic poetry, a daily rhythm of enlightenment. Among the calendar months, He is Margashirsha (harvest time, full of abundance), and among seasons, He is Spring (Kusumakara)—the joyful explosion of life, color, and renewal.

original_script:
द्यूतं छलयतामस्मि तेजस्तेजस्विनामहम्।
जयोऽस्मि व्यवसायोऽस्मि सत्त्वं सत्त्ववतामहम्।।10.36।।
transliteration:
dyūtaṁ chhalayatām asmi tejas tejasvinām aham
jayo ’smi vyavasāyo ’smi sattvaṁ sattvavatām aham
word_meanings: dyūtam—gambling; chhalayatām—of all cheats; asmi—I am; tejaḥ—the splendor; tejasvinām—of everything splendid; aham—I am; jayaḥ—victory; asmi—I am; vyavasāyaḥ—enterprise or effort; asmi—I am; sattvam—the strength; sattva-vatām—of the strong; aham—I am
translation_english: I am also the gambling of cheats, and of the splendid I am the splendor. I am victory, I am adventure, and I am the strength of the strong.
deep_dive_text: The Gambler and the Victor. This is one of the most provocative verses. Krishna says, 'I am the gambling of cheats.' He is the inherent unpredictability and risk in the universe; even the cunning of a cheat is a spark of His supreme intelligence, albeit misused. On the brighter side, He is the 'Tejas' (charisma/splendor) of brilliant people. When you see a breathtaking victory, a bold adventure, or pure strength of character, you are witnessing the Divine in action.

original_script:
वृष्णीनां वासुदेवोऽस्मि पाण्डवानां धनञ्जयः।
मुनीनामप्यहं व्यासः कवीनामुशना कविः।।10.37।।
transliteration:
vṛiṣhṇīnāṁ vāsudevo ’smi pāṇḍavānāṁ dhanañjayaḥ
munīnām apy ahaṁ vyāsaḥ kavīnām uśhanā kaviḥ
word_meanings: vṛiṣhṇīnām—of the descendants of Vrishni; vāsudevaḥ—Krishna in Dwarka; asmi—I am; pāṇḍavānām—of the Pandavas; dhanañjayaḥ—Arjuna; munīnām—of the sages; api—also; aham—I am; vyāsaḥ—Vyasa, the compiler of all Vedic literature; kavīnām—of all great thinkers; uśhanā—Ushana; kaviḥ—the thinker
translation_english: Of the descendants of Vrishni I am Vasudeva, and of the Pandavas I am Arjuna. Of the sages I am Vyasa, and among great thinkers I am Ushana.
deep_dive_text: The Divine Mirror. Krishna gets intimately personal here. He identifies as Himself (Vasudeva) among His own tribe, but stunningly, He tells Arjuna, 'Among the Pandavas, I am YOU (Dhananjaya).' He is telling Arjuna that his martial prowess and righteousness are not his own, but a divine manifestation. He is also Vyasa (the compiler of the Vedas) and Ushana (Shukracharya, the brilliant strategist).

original_script:
दण्डो दमयतामस्मि नीतिरस्मि जिगीषताम्।
मौनं चैवास्मि गुह्यानां ज्ञानं ज्ञानवतामहम्।।10.38।।
transliteration:
daṇḍo damayatām asmi nītir asmi jigīṣhatām
maunaṁ chaivāsmi guhyānāṁ jñānaṁ jñānavatām aham
word_meanings: daṇḍaḥ—punishment; damayatām—of all means of suppression; asmi—I am; nītiḥ—morality; asmi—I am; jigīṣhatām—of those who seek victory; maunam—silence; cha—and; eva—also; asmi—I am; guhyānām—of secrets; jñānam—knowledge; jñāna-vatām—of the wise; aham—I am
translation_english: Among all means of suppressing lawlessness I am punishment, and of those who seek victory I am morality. Of secret things I am silence, and of the wise I am the wisdom.
deep_dive_text: The Ultimate Strategy. For those who enforce order, the Divine is the pure 'Danda' (justice/punishment) that corrects society. For those seeking victory, He is 'Niti' (ethics/morality)—true victory is only won through right action, not deceit. Most beautifully, among secrets, He is 'Maunam' (Silence). The deepest spiritual truths cannot be spoken; they are guarded and revealed only in profound inner silence.

original_script:
यच्चापि सर्वभूतानां बीजं तदहमर्जुन।
न तदस्ति विना यत्स्यान्मया भूतं चराचरम्।।10.39।।
transliteration:
yach chāpi sarva-bhūtānāṁ bījaṁ tad aham arjuna
na tad asti vinā yat syān mayā bhūtaṁ charācharam
word_meanings: yat—whatever; cha—also; api—may be; sarva-bhūtānām—of all creations; bījam—seed; tat—that; aham—I am; arjuna—O Arjuna; na—not; tat—that; asti—there is; vinā—without; yat—which; syāt—exists; mayā—Me; bhūtam—created being; chara-acharam—moving and nonmoving
translation_english: Furthermore, O Arjuna, I am the generating seed of all existences. There is no being—moving or nonmoving—that can exist without Me.
deep_dive_text: The Universal Seed. Krishna brings the long list of specific opulences back to a singular, all-encompassing truth. Instead of looking for Him only in the 'best' or 'most powerful' things, He states that He is the 'Bijam' (the core seed) of literally everything. Nothing—whether a moving animal or a stationary rock—has any independent existence apart from the Divine.

original_script:
नान्तोऽस्ति मम दिव्यानां विभूतीनां परन्तप।
एष तूद्देशतः प्रोक्तो विभूतेर्विस्तरो मया।।10.40।।
transliteration:
nānto ’sti mama divyānāṁ vibhūtīnāṁ parantapa
eṣha tūddeśhataḥ prokto vibhūter vistaro mayā
word_meanings: na—not; antaḥ—limit; asti—there is; mama—My; divyānām—of divine; vibhūtīnām—opulences; parantapa—O conqueror of enemies; eṣhaḥ—this; tu—but; uddeśhataḥ—briefly; proktaḥ—spoken; vibhūteḥ—of opulences; vistaraḥ—the extent; mayā—by Me
translation_english: O mighty conqueror of enemies, there is no end to My divine manifestations. What I have spoken to you is but a mere brief indication of My infinite opulences.
deep_dive_text: The Limits of Language. Krishna acknowledges that the infinite cannot be captured in a finite list. The 39 verses prior were not an exhaustive encyclopedia, but merely a 'brief indication' to help Arjuna start seeing the divine in the everyday world.

original_script:
यद्यद्विभूतिमत्सत्त्वं श्रीमदूर्जितमेव वा।
तत्तदेवावगच्छ त्वं मम तेजोंऽशसम्भवम्।।10.41।।
transliteration:
yad yad vibhūtimat sattvaṁ śhrīmad ūrjitam eva vā
tat tad evāvagachchha tvaṁ mama tejo ’nśha-sambhavam
word_meanings: yat yat—whatever; vibhūti-mat—opulent; sattvam—being; śhrī-mat—splendid; ūrjitam—powerful; eva—certainly; vā—or; tat tat—all that; eva—certainly; avagachchha—know; tvam—you; mama—My; tejaḥ—effulgence; anśha—fragment; sambhavam—born of
translation_english: Understand that every opulent, splendid, or powerful being originates from but a fragment of My effulgence.
deep_dive_text: This verse establishes the historical 'Formula of Excellence.' It dictates that extraordinary human or natural phenomena are not to be worshipped as independent deities, but as 'Amsha' (fragments) of the single underlying Reality. It is a unifying philosophical statement intended to resolve sectarian diversity.

original_script:
अथवा बहुनैतेन किं ज्ञातेन तवार्जुन।
विष्टभ्याहमिदं कृत्स्नमेकांशेन स्थितो जगत्।।10.42।।
transliteration:
athavā bahunaitena kiṁ jñātena tavārjuna
viṣhṭabhyāham idaṁ kṛitsnam ekānśhena sthito jagat
word_meanings: athavā—or; bahunā—many; etena—by this; kim—what; jñātena—knowing; tava—your; arjuna—O Arjuna; viṣhṭabhya—pervading; aham—I; idam—this; kṛitsnam—entire; eka—by one; anśhena—part; sthitaḥ—am situated; jagat—universe
translation_english: But what need is there, Arjuna, for all this detailed knowledge? With a single fragment of Myself I pervade and support this entire universe.
deep_dive_text: The Mic Drop. After a long, beautiful description of His glories, Krishna collapses the entire universe into perspective. He says, 'Why bother memorizing all the details? Just know this: the entire cosmos you see is supported by just one tiny fraction of My energy.' This leaves Arjuna in a state of absolute awe, perfectly setting the stage for Chapter 11.

original_script: विभूतियोगः
transliteration: Vibhūti Yogaḥ
translation_english: Chapter 10: The Yoga of Divine Glories
deep_dive_text: In Chapter 10, Krishna changes the way Arjuna sees the world. Instead of seeing a collection of random objects, Arjuna learns to see the universe as an exhibition of Divine opulence. Krishna reveals that He is the seed of all existence, the intelligence of the wise, and the strength of the strong. He concludes by stating that all beautiful and glorious things spring from just a single spark of His infinite splendor, shifting Arjuna from doubt into profound devotion.

original_script: अथ एकादशोऽध्यायः - विश्वरूपदर्शनयोगः
transliteration: Viśhvarūpa Darśhana Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Viśhvarūpa—Universal Form; Darśhana—Vision/Revelation; Yogaḥ—the path of synthesis.
translation_english: Chapter 11 Summary: The Yoga of the Vision of the Universal Form
deep_dive_text: Chapter 11 is the literary and theological climax of the Gita. It presents a 'Theophany'—a direct revelation of the Divine as 'Kāla' (Time/Destruction). Historically, it transitions Krishna from a personal teacher and tribal hero to the 'Ishvara' (Supreme Lord) of the entire cosmos. The terrifying nature of the vision serves to destroy Arjuna's remaining ego-attachments by showing the inevitability of the temporal cycle.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
मदनुग्रहाय परमं गुह्यमध्यात्मसंज्ञितम्।
यत्त्वयोक्तं वचस्तेन मोहोऽयं विगतो मम।।11.1।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
mad-anugrahāya paramaṁ guhyam adhyātma-sañjñitam
yat tvayoktaṁ vachas tena moho ’yaṁ vigato mama
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjuna said; mat-anugrahāya—out of compassion for me; paramam—supreme; guhyam—secret; adhyātma—spiritual; sañjñitam—subject matter; yat—which; tvayā—by You; uktam—spoken; vachaḥ—words; tena—by that; mohaḥ—illusion; ayam—this; vigataḥ—is dispelled; mama—my
translation_english: Arjuna said: By my hearing the profoundly secret spiritual subjects You have mercifully explained to me, my illusion has now been dispelled.
deep_dive_text: The Cure for Illusion. Arjuna acknowledges the 'Anugraha' (grace/compassion) of his teacher. The profound knowledge of chapters 7 through 10 was the medicine that cured his 'Moha' (delusion). He no longer identifies purely with the temporary physical reality, having understood the eternal spiritual truth.

original_script:
भवाप्ययौ हि भूतानां श्रुतौ विस्तरशो मया।
त्वत्तः कमलपत्राक्ष माहात्म्यमपि चाव्ययम्।।11.2।।
transliteration:
bhavāpyayau hi bhūtānāṁ śhrutau vistaraśho mayā
tvattaḥ kamala-patrākṣha māhātmyam api chāvyayam
word_meanings: bhava—appearance; apyayau—and disappearance; hi—certainly; bhūtānām—of all living beings; śhrutau—have been heard; vistaraśhaḥ—in detail; mayā—by me; tvattaḥ—from You; kamala-patra-akṣha—O lotus-eyed one; māhātmyam—glories; api—also; cha—and; avyayam—inexhaustible
translation_english: O lotus-eyed one, I have heard from You in detail about the appearance and disappearance of every living entity and have realized Your inexhaustible glories.
deep_dive_text: The Lotus-Eyed Observer. Arjuna affectionately calls Krishna 'Kamala-patraksha' (lotus-eyed). He confirms he has understood the mechanics of creation and dissolution (Bhava and Apyaya). He now grasps the theory: that everything emerges from and returns to the Divine.

original_script:
एवमेतद्यथात्थ त्वमात्मानं परमेश्वर।
द्रष्टुमिच्छामि ते रूपमैश्वरं पुरुषोत्तम।।11.3।।
transliteration:
evam etad yathāttha tvam ātmānaṁ parameśhvara
draṣhṭum ichchhāmi te rūpam aiśhvaraṁ puruṣhottama
word_meanings: evam—thus; etat—this; yathā—as; āttha—have spoken; tvam—You; ātmānam—Yourself; parama-īśhvara—O Supreme Lord; draṣhṭum—to see; ichchhāmi—I wish; te—Your; rūpam—form; aiśhvaram—divine; puruṣha-uttama—O Supreme Personality
translation_english: O greatest of all personalities, O supreme form, though I see You here before me in Your actual position, as You have described Yourself, I wish to see how You have entered into this cosmic manifestation. I want to see that of Your divine form.
deep_dive_text: From Theory to Experience. Arjuna believes the theory perfectly, but human nature craves experience. 'I have heard it; now I want to see it.' He wants the ultimate empirical proof of the philosophy he has just learned. He asks to witness the 'Aishvaram Rupam'—the majestic, cosmic form of God.

original_script:
मन्यसे यदि तच्छक्यं मया द्रष्टुमिति प्रभो।
योगेश्वर ततो मे त्वं दर्शयात्मानमव्ययम्।।11.4।।
transliteration:
manyase yadi tach chhakyaṁ mayā draṣhṭum iti prabho
yogeśhvara tato me tvaṁ darśhayātmānam avyayam
word_meanings: manyase—You think; yadi—if; tat—that; śhakyam—is able; mayā—by me; draṣhṭum—to be seen; iti—thus; prabho—O Lord; yoga-īśhvara—O master of all mystic power; tataḥ—then; me—unto me; tvam—You; darśhaya—show; ātmānam—Yourself; avyayam—eternal
translation_english: If You think that I am able to behold Your cosmic form, O my Lord, O master of all mystic power, then kindly show me that unlimited universal Self.
deep_dive_text: The Humility of the Seeker. Arjuna doesn't demand the vision; he requests it with a condition: 'If You think I am capable.' He acknowledges that the infinite cannot be seen with ordinary human capacity unless the 'Yogeshvara' (Master of Mysticism) grants the grace to reveal it.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
पश्य मे पार्थ रूपाणि शतशोऽथ सहस्रशः।
नानाविधानि दिव्यानि नानावर्णाकृतीनि च।।11.5।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
paśhya me pārtha rūpāṇi śhataśho ’tha sahasraśhaḥ
nānā-vidhāni divyāni nānā-varṇākṛitīni cha
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Blessed Lord said; paśhya—behold; me—My; pārtha—O son of Pritha; rūpāṇi—forms; śhataśhaḥ—hundreds; atha—also; sahasraśhaḥ—thousands; nānā-vidhāni—various kinds; divyāni—divine; nānā—many; varṇa—colors; ākṛitīni—shapes; cha—and
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: My dear Arjuna, O son of Pritha, see now My opulences, hundreds of thousands of varied divine and multicolored forms.
deep_dive_text: The Veil Lifts. Krishna immediately grants Arjuna's request. With the word 'Pashya' (Behold!), Krishna shatters the boundaries of normal perception. He begins to display a multidimensional reality containing hundreds of thousands of forms, colors, and shapes—a visual overload of the entire cosmic code.

original_script:
पश्यादित्यान्वसून्रुद्रानश्विनौ मरुतस्तथा।
बहून्यदृष्टपूर्वाणि पश्याश्चर्याणि भारत।।11.6।।
transliteration:
paśhyādityān vasūn rudrān aśhvinau marutas tathā
bahūny adṛiṣhṭa-pūrvāṇi paśhyāśhcharyāṇi bhārata
word_meanings: paśhya—behold; ādityān—the twelve Adityas; vasūn—the eight Vasus; rudrān—the eleven Rudras; aśhvinau—the twin Ashvinis; marutaḥ—the forty-nine Maruts; tathā—also; bahūni—many; adṛiṣhṭa-pūrvāṇi—never seen before; paśhya—behold; āśhcharyāṇi—marvels; bhārata—O best of the Bharatas
translation_english: O best of the Bharatas, see here the different manifestations of Adityas, Vasus, Rudras, Ashwini-kumaras and all the other demigods. Behold the many wonderful things which no one has ever seen or heard of before.
deep_dive_text: The Cosmic Assembly. Krishna invites Arjuna to look into His form and see the entire pantheon of Vedic gods—the Adityas, Vasus, Rudras, and Ashvinis—all coexisting in one place. These represent the fundamental forces of nature (light, elements, storms, healing). Arjuna is about to witness 'Adrishta-purvani'—marvels never seen before by human eyes, proving that the universe is far stranger and more magnificent than the human mind can conceive.

original_script:
इहैकस्थं जगत्कृत्स्नं पश्याद्य सचराचरम्।
मम देहे गुडाकेश यच्चान्यद्द्रष्टुमिच्छसि।।11.7।।
transliteration:
ihaika-sthaṁ jagat kṛitsnaṁ paśhyādya sa-charācharam
mama dehe guḍākeśha yach chānyad draṣhṭum ichchhasi
word_meanings: iha—here; eka-stham—situated in one place; jagat—the universe; kṛitsnam—entire; paśhya—behold; adya—now; sa—with; chara—the moving; acharam—and not moving; mama—in My; dehe—body; guḍākeśha—O Arjuna; yat—whatever; cha—also; anyat—else; draṣhṭum—to see; ichchhasi—you wish
translation_english: O Arjuna, whatever you wish to see, behold at once in this body of Mine! This universal form can show you whatever you now desire to see and whatever you may want to see in the future. Everything—moving and nonmoving—is here completely, in one place.
deep_dive_text: The Universal Matrix. Krishna's body becomes a multi-dimensional screen. 'Eka-stham' means 'in one place.' Arjuna doesn't have to travel across galaxies or through time to see the universe; everything that has ever existed, exists now, or will exist in the future is condensed into this singular, overwhelming vision. It is the ultimate expression of omnipresence.

original_script:
न तु मां शक्यसे द्रष्टुमनेनैव स्वचक्षुषा।
दिव्यं ददामि ते चक्षुः पश्य मे योगमैश्वरम्।।11.8।।
transliteration:
na tu māṁ śhakyase draṣhṭum anenaiva sva-chakṣhuṣhā
divyaṁ dadāmi te chakṣhuḥ paśhya me yogam aiśhvaram
word_meanings: na—never; tu—but; mām—Me; śhakyase—are you able; draṣhṭum—to see; anena—with these; eva—certainly; sva-chakṣhuṣhā—your own eyes; divyam—divine; dadāmi—I give; te—to you; chakṣhuḥ—eyes; paśhya—behold; me—My; yogam aiśhvaram—inconceivable mystic power
translation_english: But you cannot see Me with your present eyes. Therefore I give you divine eyes. Behold My mystic opulence!
deep_dive_text: The Upgrade of Perception. Human eyes are calibrated to see a very narrow spectrum of reality—just 3D space and linear time. To see a multi-dimensional, infinite reality, Arjuna needs a hardware upgrade. Krishna grants him 'Divyam Chakshuh' (divine vision). This signifies that the Ultimate Truth cannot be grasped by material senses alone; it requires divine grace and a profound shift in consciousness.

original_script:
सञ्जय उवाच
एवमुक्त्वा ततो राजन्महायोगेश्वरो हरिः।
दर्शयामास पार्थाय परमं रूपमैश्वरम्।।11.9।।
transliteration:
sañjaya uvācha
evam uktvā tato rājan mahā-yogeśhvaro hariḥ
darśhayām āsa pārthāya paramaṁ rūpam aiśhvaram
word_meanings: sañjayaḥ uvācha—Sanjaya said; evam—thus; uktvā—having spoken; tataḥ—thereafter; rājan—O King; mahā-yoga-īśhvaraḥ—the great Lord of mystic power; hariḥ—the Supreme Personality of Godhead; darśhayām āsa—showed; pārthāya—unto Arjuna; paramam—the divine; rūpam aiśhvaram—universal form
translation_english: Sanjaya said: O King, having spoken thus, the Supreme Lord of all mystic power, the Personality of Godhead, displayed His universal form to Arjuna.
deep_dive_text: The Shift in Narrative. The perspective pulls back to Sanjaya, who is narrating this mind-bending event to the blind King Dhritarashtra. Sanjaya calls Krishna 'Maha-yogeshvara' (the Master of all mystics). The display of the 'Paramam Rupam' (Supreme Form) begins, marking the transition from philosophical dialogue to pure, awe-inspiring revelation.

original_script:
अनेकवक्त्रनयनमनेकाद्भुतदर्शनम्।
अनेकदिव्याभरणं दिव्यानेकोद्यतायुधम्।।11.10।।
transliteration:
aneka-vaktra-nayanam anekādbhuta-darśhanam
aneka-divyābharaṇaṁ divyānekodyatāyudham
word_meanings: aneka—many; vaktra—faces; nayanam—and eyes; aneka—many; adbhuta—wonderful; darśhanam—sights; aneka—many; divya—divine; ābharaṇam—ornaments; divya—divine; aneka—many; udyata—uplifted; āyudham—weapons
translation_english: Arjuna saw in that universal form unlimited mouths, unlimited eyes, unlimited wonderful visions. The form was decorated with many celestial ornaments and bore many divine upraised weapons.
deep_dive_text: The Visual Overload. As the divine vision activates, Arjuna's mind is flooded with impossible imagery. 'Aneka' (unlimited/many) is repeated to emphasize the sheer scale. He sees countless faces and eyes, representing the infinite awareness of God. The raised weapons symbolize the Divine's power to destroy ego, ignorance, and adharma on a cosmic level.

original_script:
दिव्यमाल्याम्बरधरं दिव्यगन्धानुलेपनम्।
सर्वाश्चर्यमयं देवमनन्तं विश्वतोमुखम्।।11.11।।
transliteration:
divya-mālyāmbara-dharaṁ divya-gandhānulepanam
sarvāśhcharyamayaṁ devam anantaṁ viśhvato-mukham
word_meanings: divya—celestial; mālya—garlands; ambara—and garments; dharam—wearing; divya—celestial; gandha—fragrances; anulepanam—smeared with; sarva—all; āśhcharya-mayam—wonderful; devam—shining; anantam—unlimited; viśhvataḥ-mukham—all-pervading
translation_english: He wore celestial garlands and garments, and many divine scents were smeared over His body. All was wondrous, brilliant, boundless, all-expanding.
deep_dive_text: The Infinite Splendor. The Vishvarupa is not just terrifying; it is profoundly beautiful. The celestial garlands, garments, and fragrances represent the perfect purity and opulence of the Divine. The term 'Vishvato-mukham' (faces everywhere) means that there is no 'behind' or 'blind spot' to God. The Divine is observing and experiencing reality from every possible angle simultaneously.

original_script:
दिवि सूर्यसहस्रस्य भवेद्युगपदुत्थिता।
यदि भाः सदृशी सा स्याद्भासस्तस्य महात्मनः।।11.12।।
transliteration:
divi sūrya-sahasrasya bhaved yugapad utthitā
yadi bhāḥ sadṛiśhī sā syād bhāsas tasya mahātmanaḥ
word_meanings: divi—in the sky; sūrya—of suns; sahasrasya—of thousands; bhavet—there were; yugapat—simultaneously; utthitā—present; yadi—if; bhāḥ—light; sadṛiśhī—like that; sā—that; syāt—might be; bhāsaḥ—effulgence; tasya—of Him; mahā-ātmanaḥ—the great Lord
translation_english: If hundreds of thousands of suns were to rise at once into the sky, their radiance might resemble the effulgence of the Supreme Person in that universal form.
deep_dive_text: The Splendor of the Vishwarūpa. Sanjaya uses the metaphor of 'a thousand suns' to describe the Divine Vision (Divya-chakṣhu). Historically, this represents the transition from a personal deity to the absolute, cosmic totality of Time (Kāla) that governs the rise and fall of civilizations.

original_script:
तत्रैकस्थं जगत्कृत्स्नं प्रविभक्तमनेकधा।
अपश्यद्देवदेवस्य शरीरे पाण्डवस्तदा।।11.13।।
transliteration:
tatraika-sthaṁ jagat kṛitsnaṁ pravibhaktam anekadhā
apaśhyad deva-devasya śharīre pāṇḍavas tadā
word_meanings: tatra—there; eka-stham—in one place; jagat—the universe; kṛitsnam—complete; pravibhaktam—divided; anekadhā—into many; apaśhyat—could see; deva-devasya—of the Supreme Personality of Godhead; śharīre—in the universal body; pāṇḍavaḥ—Arjuna; tadā—at that time
translation_english: At that time Arjuna could see in the universal form of the Lord the unlimited expansions of the universe situated in one place although divided into many, many thousands.
deep_dive_text: The Paradox of One and Many. Arjuna witnesses the ultimate unified field theory. He sees the 'Jagat Kritsnam' (entire universe) with all its infinite diversity, galaxies, and lifeforms ('Pravibhaktam anekadha' - divided in countless ways), yet it is all perfectly contained 'Eka-stham' (in one single place) within the body of the 'Deva-devasya' (God of gods). Multiplicity and Unity exist simultaneously.

original_script:
ततः स विस्मयाविष्टो हृष्टरोमा धनञ्जयः।
प्रणम्य शिरसा देवं कृताञ्जलिरभाषत।।11.14।।
transliteration:
tataḥ sa vismayāviṣhṭo hṛiṣhṭa-romā dhanañjayaḥ
praṇamya śhirasā devaṁ kṛitāñjalir abhāṣhata
word_meanings: tataḥ—then; saḥ—he; vismaya-āviṣhṭaḥ—overwhelmed with wonder; hṛiṣhṭa-romā—with his bodily hairs standing on end; dhanañjayaḥ—Arjuna; praṇamya—bowing down; śhirasā—with his head; devam—to the Supreme Lord; kṛita-añjaliḥ—with folded hands; abhāṣhata—spoke
translation_english: Then, bewildered and astonished, his hair standing on end, Arjuna bowed his head to offer obeisances and with folded hands began to pray to the Supreme Lord.
deep_dive_text: The Physical Reaction to Awe. The sheer scale of the vision bypasses Arjuna's intellect and strikes his physiology. 'Hrishta-roma' means his hair stood on end, a classic sign of overwhelming spiritual ecstasy and fear. Stripped of his ego by the infinite vastness before him, the great warrior spontaneously folds his hands ('Kritanjali') and bows his head in absolute surrender.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
पश्यामि देवांस्तव देव देहे
सर्वांस्तथा भूतविशेषसङ्घान्।
ब्रह्माणमीशं कमलासनस्थ-
मृषींश्च सर्वानुरगांश्च दिव्यान्।।11.15।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
paśhyāmi devāns tava deva dehe
sarvāns tathā bhūta-viśheṣha-saṅghān
brahmāṇam īśhaṁ kamalāsana-stham
ṛiṣhīṅśh cha sarvān uragāṅśh cha divyān
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjuna said; paśhyāmi—I see; devān—all the demigods; tava—Your; deva—O Lord; dehe—in the body; sarvān—all; tathā—also; bhūta—living entities; viśheṣha-saṅghān—specifically assembled; brahmāṇam—Lord Brahma; īśham—Lord Shiva; kamala-āsana-stham—sitting on the lotus flower; ṛiṣhīn—great sages; cha—also; sarvān—all; uragān—serpents; cha—also; divyān—divine
translation_english: Arjuna said: My dear Lord Krishna, I see assembled in Your body all the demigods and various other living entities. I see Brahma sitting on the lotus flower, as well as Lord Shiva and all the sages and divine serpents.
deep_dive_text: Arjuna's Testimony. Arjuna finds his voice and begins to describe the indescribable. He serves as our eyes, narrating the vision. He confirms seeing the highest creator gods, Brahma and Shiva, alongside the ancient sages and cosmic serpents (like Vasuki). He realizes that all 'deities' and 'forces' humans worship are merely organs operating within the single, colossal body of the Supreme.

original_script:
अनेकबाहूदरवक्त्रनेत्रं
पश्यामि त्वां सर्वतोऽनन्तरूपम्।
नान्तं न मध्यं न पुनस्तवादिं
पश्यामि विश्वेश्वर विश्वरूप।।11.16।।
transliteration:
aneka-bāhūdara-vaktra-netraṁ
paśhyāmi tvāṁ sarvato ’nanta-rūpam
nāntaṁ na madhyaṁ na punas tavādiṁ
paśhyāmi viśhveśhvara viśhva-rūpa
word_meanings: aneka—many; bāhu—arms; udara—bellies; vaktra—mouths; netram—eyes; paśhyāmi—I see; tvām—You; sarvataḥ—on all sides; ananta-rūpam—unlimited form; na antam—no end; na madhyam—no middle; na punaḥ—nor again; tava—Your; ādim—beginning; paśhyāmi—I see; viśhva-īśhvara—O Lord of the universe; viśhva-rūpa—O universal form
translation_english: O Lord of the universe, O universal form, I see in Your body many, many arms, bellies, mouths and eyes, expanded everywhere, without limit. I see in You no end, no middle and no beginning.
deep_dive_text: The Infinite Expanse. Arjuna scans the vastness of the Vishvarupa. Human brains naturally look for boundaries—a start, a middle, an end—to make sense of an object. But Arjuna is completely disoriented because this form defies physical logic. It stretches endlessly in every direction. It is a terrifying and sublime realization that the Divine cannot be contained, measured, or mapped.

original_script:
किरीटिनं गदिनं चक्रिणं च
तेजोराशिं सर्वतो दीप्तिमन्तम्।
पश्यामि त्वां दुर्निरीक्ष्यं समन्ताद्
दीप्तानलार्कद्युतिमप्रमेयम्।।11.17।।
transliteration:
kirīṭinaṁ gadinaṁ chakriṇaṁ cha
tejo-rāśhiṁ sarvato dīptimantam
paśhyāmi tvāṁ durnirīkṣhyaṁ samantād
dīptānalārka-dyutim aprameyam
word_meanings: kirīṭinam—with helmets; gadinam—with maces; chakriṇam—with discs; cha—and; tejaḥ-rāśhim—effulgence; sarvataḥ—on all sides; dīpti-mantam—luminous; paśhyāmi—I see; tvām—You; durnirīkṣhyam—difficult to see; samantāt—everywhere; dīpta-anala—blazing fire; arka—of the sun; dyutim—the sunshine; aprameyam—immeasurable
translation_english: Your form is difficult to see because of its glaring effulgence, spreading on all sides, like blazing fire or the immeasurable glare of the sun. Yet I see this glowing form everywhere, adorned with various crowns, clubs and discs.
deep_dive_text: The Blinding Majesty. Despite the unbearable brightness ('Durnirikshyam' - difficult to look at), Arjuna forces himself to witness the details. He spots the traditional symbols of Vishnu: the crown (sovereignty), the mace (power/discipline), and the glowing chakra (the wheel of time/dharma). The Divine is not just an abstract energy; it is the ultimate ruling authority of reality, armed and crowned.

original_script:
त्वमक्षरं परमं वेदितव्यं
त्वमस्य विश्वस्य परं निधानम्।
त्वमव्ययः शाश्वतधर्मगोप्ता
सनातनस्त्वं पुरुषो मतो मे।।11.18।।
transliteration:
tvam akṣharaṁ paramaṁ veditavyaṁ
tvam asya viśhvasya paraṁ nidhānam
tvam avyayaḥ śhāśhvata-dharma-goptā
sanātanas tvaṁ puruṣho mato me
word_meanings: tvam—You; akṣharam—the inexhaustible; paramam—supreme; veditavyam—to be understood; tvam—You; asya—of this; viśhvasya—universe; param—supreme; nidhānam—basis; tvam—You; avyayaḥ—inexhaustible; śhāśhvata-dharma-goptā—maintainer of the eternal religion; sanātanaḥ—eternal; tvam—You; puruṣhaḥ—the Supreme Personality; mataḥ me—is my opinion
translation_english: You are the supreme primal objective. You are the ultimate resting place of all this universe. You are inexhaustible, and You are the oldest. You are the maintainer of the eternal religion, the Personality of Godhead. This is my opinion.
deep_dive_text: Arjuna's Declaration of Faith. Having seen the empirical proof, Arjuna's intellect catches up with his vision. He declares Krishna as 'Aksharam' (imperishable) and the 'Param Nidhanam' (ultimate resting place) of the cosmos. He realizes that the chaos of the universe is actually under the strict protection of the 'Shashvata-dharma-gopta'—the eternal guardian of cosmic order.

original_script:
अनादिमध्यान्तमनन्तवीर्य-
मनन्तबाहुं शशिसूर्यनेत्रम्।
पश्यामि त्वां दीप्तहुताशवक्त्र-
स्वतेजसा विश्वमिदं तपन्तम्।।11.19।।
transliteration:
anādi-madhyāntam ananta-vīryam
ananta-bāhuṁ śhaśhi-sūrya-netram
paśhyāmi tvāṁ dīpta-hutāśha-vaktraṁ
sva-tejasā viśhvam idaṁ tapantam
word_meanings: anādi—without beginning; madhya—middle; antam—or end; ananta—unlimited; vīryam—glories; ananta—unlimited; bāhum—arms; śhaśhi—the moon; sūrya—and sun; netram—eyes; paśhyāmi—I see; tvām—You; dīpta—blazing; hutāśha-vaktram—fire coming out of Your mouth; sva-tejasā—by Your radiance; viśhvam—universe; idam—this; tapantam—heating
translation_english: You are without origin, middle or end. Your glory is unlimited. You have numberless arms, and the sun and moon are Your eyes. I see You with blazing fire coming forth from Your mouth, burning this entire universe by Your own radiance.
deep_dive_text: The Heat of the Divine. The vision intensifies from majestic to intimidating. The Sun and Moon, previously described as His eyes, now become literal cosmic lenses. The most striking element is the 'Dipta-hutasha-vaktram'—blazing fire roaring from His mouths, heating the entire universe. This represents the relentless, consuming power of Time that slowly burns away all material creations.

original_script:
द्यावापृथिव्योरिदमन्तरं हि
व्याप्तं त्वयैकेन दिशश्च सर्वाः।
दृष्ट्वाद्भुतं रूपमुग्रं तवेदं
लोकत्रयं प्रव्यथितं महात्मन्।।11.20।।
transliteration:
dyāv ā pṛithivyor idam antaraṁ hi
vyāptaṁ tvayaikena diśhaśh cha sarvāḥ
dṛiṣhṭvādbhutaṁ rūpam ugraṁ tavedaṁ
loka-trayaṁ pravyathitaṁ mahātman
word_meanings: dyau—from heaven; ā pṛithivyoḥ—to the earth; idam—this; antaram—space between; hi—certainly; vyāptam—pervaded; tvayā—by You; ekena—alone; diśhaḥ—directions; cha—and; sarvāḥ—all; dṛiṣhṭvā—by seeing; adbhutam—wonderful; rūpam—form; ugram—terrible; tava—Your; idam—this; loka-trayam—all the planetary systems; pravyathitam—perturbed; mahā-ātman—O great one
translation_english: Although You are one, You spread throughout the sky and the planets and all space between. O great one, seeing this wondrous and terrible form, all the planetary systems are perturbed.
deep_dive_text: The Universal Tremor. Arjuna is not the only one reacting. The 'Ugram' (fierce/terrible) nature of this form is causing a shockwave across 'Loka-trayam' (the three worlds: heaven, earth, and the nether regions). When the ultimate reality is exposed, tearing away the comfortable illusions of normal life, it is deeply unsettling and terrifying for all beings, not just humans.

original_script:
अमी हि त्वां सुरसङ्घा विशन्ति
केचिद्भीताः प्राञ्जलयो गृणन्ति।
स्वस्तीत्युक्त्वा महर्षिसिद्धसङ्घाः
स्तुवन्ति त्वां स्तुतिभिः पुष्कलाभिः।।11.21।।
transliteration:
amī hi tvāṁ sura-saṅghā viśhanti
kechid bhītāḥ prāñjalayo gṛiṇanti
svastīty uktvā maharṣhi-siddha-saṅghāḥ
stuvanti tvāṁ stutibhiḥ puṣhkalābhiḥ
word_meanings: amī—all those; hi—certainly; tvām—You; sura-saṅghāḥ—groups of demigods; viśhanti—are entering; kechit—some of them; bhītāḥ—out of fear; prāñjalayaḥ—with folded hands; gṛiṇanti—are offering prayers; svasti—all peace; iti—thus; uktvā—speaking; mahā-ṛiṣhi—great sages; siddha-saṅghāḥ—perfect beings; stuvanti—are singing hymns; tvām—unto You; stutibhiḥ—with prayers; puṣhkalābhiḥ—Vedic hymns
translation_english: All the hosts of demigods are surrendering before You and entering into You. Some of them, very much afraid, are offering prayers with folded hands. Hosts of great sages and perfected beings, crying 'All peace!' are praying to You by singing the Vedic hymns.
deep_dive_text: The Surrender of the Gods. Even the 'Sura-sangha' (hosts of demigods or celestial beings) are entirely overpowered by this vision. They realize their powers are minuscule compared to the Supreme. While some enter Him in fearful surrender, the 'Maharshis' (enlightened sages) maintain their composure. They chant 'Svasti' (let there be peace), acting as the stabilizing force of wisdom amidst cosmic terror.

original_script:
रुद्रादित्या वसवो ये च साध्या
विश्वेऽश्विनौ मरुतश्चोष्मपाश्च।
गन्धर्वयक्षासुरसिद्धसङ्घा
वीक्षन्ते त्वां विस्मिताश्चैव सर्वे।।11.22।।
transliteration:
rudrādityā vasavo ye cha sādhyā
viśhve ’śhvinau marutaśh choṣhmapāśh cha
gandharva-yakṣhāsura-siddha-saṅghā
vīkṣhante tvāṁ vismitāśh chaiva sarve
word_meanings: rudra—manifestations of Lord Shiva; ādityāḥ—the Adityas; vasavaḥ—the Vasus; ye—all those; cha—and; sādhyāḥ—the Sadhyas; viśhve—the Vishvedevas; aśhvinau—the Ashwini-kumaras; marutaḥ—the Maruts; cha—and; uṣhma-pāḥ—the forefathers; cha—and; gandharva—of the Gandharvas; yakṣha—the Yakshas; asura—the demons; siddha—and the perfected demigods; saṅghāḥ—the assemblies; vīkṣhante—are beholding; tvām—You; vismitāḥ—in wonder; cha—also; eva—certainly; sarve—all
translation_english: All the various manifestations of Lord Shiva, the Adityas, the Vasus, the Sadhyas, the Vishvedevas, the two Ashvis, the Maruts, the forefathers, the Gandharvas, the Yakshas, the Asuras and the perfected demigods are beholding You in wonder.
deep_dive_text: The Audience of the Infinite. Arjuna lists the exact demographic of the cosmic audience witnessing this event. It's an unprecedented gathering: angels, demons (Asuras), forefathers, and elements are all united in one shared emotion—'Vismitah' (awe/wonder). In the presence of the absolute truth, all celestial politics, rivalries, and hierarchies dissolve into pure astonishment.

original_script:
रूपं महत्ते बहुवक्त्रनेत्रं
महाबाहो बहुबाहूरुपादम्।
बहूदरं बहुदंष्ट्राकरालं
दृष्ट्वा लोकाः प्रव्यथितास्तथाहम्।।11.23।।
transliteration:
rūpaṁ mahat te bahu-vaktra-netraṁ
mahā-bāho bahu-bāhūru-pādam
bahūdaraṁ bahu-danṣhṭrā-karālaṁ
dṛiṣhṭvā lokāḥ pravyathitās tathāham
word_meanings: rūpam—form; mahat—very great; te—Your; bahu—many; vaktra—faces; netram—and eyes; mahā-bāho—O mighty-armed one; bahu—many; bāhu—arms; ūru—thighs; pādam—and legs; bahu-udaram—many bellies; bahu-danṣhṭrā—many teeth; karālam—terrible; dṛiṣhṭvā—seeing; lokāḥ—all the planets; pravyathitāḥ—perturbed; tathā—similarly; aham—I
translation_english: O mighty-armed one, all the planets with their demigods are disturbed at seeing Your great form, with its many faces, eyes, arms, thighs, legs, and bellies and Your many terrible teeth; and as they are disturbed, so am I.
deep_dive_text: The Creeping Panic. The initial wonder is fully giving way to dread. Arjuna describes the 'Karalam' (terrible) features: massive thighs, many bellies, and ghastly teeth. He admits that he is no longer just a passive observer; he is 'Pravyathitah' (deeply distressed/terrified). The reality of God is not just a comforting hug; it is a staggering, overwhelming force of nature.

original_script:
नभःस्पृशं दीप्तमनेकवर्णं
व्यात्ताननं दीप्तविशालनेत्रम्।
दृष्ट्वा हि त्वां प्रव्यथितान्तरात्मा
धृतिं न विन्दामि शमं च विष्णो।।11.24।।
transliteration:
nabhaḥ-spṛiśhaṁ dīptam aneka-varṇaṁ
vyāttānanaṁ dīpta-viśhāla-netram
dṛiṣhṭvā hi tvāṁ pravyathitāntar-ātmā
dhṛitiṁ na vindāmi śhamaṁ cha viṣhṇo
word_meanings: nabhaḥ-spṛiśham—touching the sky; dīptam—glowing; aneka—many; varṇam—colors; vyātta—open; ānanam—mouths; dīpta—glowing; viśhāla—very great; netram—eyes; dṛiṣhṭvā—seeing; hi—certainly; tvām—You; pravyathita—perturbed; antaḥ-ātmā—within the soul; dhṛitim—steadiness; na—not; vindāmi—I find; śhamam—mental tranquility; cha—and; viṣhṇo—O Lord Vishnu
translation_english: O all-pervading Vishnu, seeing You with Your many radiant colors touching the sky, Your gaping mouths, and Your great glowing eyes, my mind is perturbed by fear. I can no longer maintain my steadiness or equilibrium of mind.
deep_dive_text: The Loss of Equilibrium. Arjuna directly confesses his breakdown. This is the greatest warrior of his era, a man who faces armies without flinching, but looking at this 'Nabha-sprisham' (sky-touching) entity with open, glowing mouths, his 'Antaratma' (inner soul) is shaking. He has lost 'Dhritim' (courage/steadiness) and 'Shamam' (mental peace). The ego is entirely crushed by the sheer weight of the Infinite.

original_script:
दंष्ट्राकरालानि च ते मुखानि
दृष्ट्वैव कालानलसन्निभानि।
दिशो न जाने न लभे च शर्म
प्रसीद देवेश जगन्निवास।।11.25।।
transliteration:
danṣhṭrā-karālāni cha te mukhāni
dṛiṣhṭvaiva kālānala-sannibhāni
diśho na jāne na labhe cha śharma
prasīda deveśha jagan-nivāsa
word_meanings: danṣhṭrā—teeth; karālāni—terrible; cha—also; te—Your; mukhāni—faces; dṛiṣhṭvā—seeing; eva—thus; kāla-anala—the fire of death; sannibhāni—as if; diśhaḥ—the directions; na—not; jāne—I know; na—not; labhe—I obtain; cha—and; śharma—grace; prasīda—be pleased; deva-īśha—O Lord of all the demigods; jagat-nivāsa—O refuge of the universe
translation_english: O Lord of lords, O refuge of the worlds, please be gracious to me. I cannot keep my balance seeing thus Your blazing deathlike faces and awful teeth. In all directions I am bewildered.
deep_dive_text: The Begging for Grace. Arjuna is now fully experiencing spiritual vertigo ('Disho na jane' - I have lost my sense of direction). He sees the mouths of the Divine resembling 'Kalanala'—the cosmic fire that burns at the end of time. Driven to the edge of his sanity, he pleads 'Prasida' (have mercy/be gracious). He desperately needs the terrifying cosmic reality to soften back into the compassionate friend he once knew.

original_script:
अमी च त्वां धृतराष्ट्रस्य पुत्राः
सर्वे सहैवावनिपालसङ्घैः।
भीष्मो द्रोणः सूतपुत्रस्तथासौ
सहास्मदीयैरपि योधमुख्यैः।।11.26।।
वक्त्राणि ते त्वरमाणा विशन्ति
दंष्ट्राकरालानि भयानकानि।
केचिद्विलग्ना दशनान्तरेषु
सन्दृश्यन्ते चूर्णितैरुत्तमाङ्गैः।।11.27।।
transliteration:
amī cha tvāṁ dhṛitarāṣhṭrasya putrāḥ
sarve sahaivāvani-pāla-saṅghaiḥ
bhīṣhmo droṇaḥ sūta-putras tathāsau
sahāsmadīyair api yodha-mukhyaiḥ
vaktrāṇi te tvaramāṇā viśhanti
danṣhṭrā-karālāni bhayānakāni
kechid vilagnā daśhanāntareṣhu
sandṛiṣhyante chūrṇitair uttamāṅgaiḥ
word_meanings: amī—these; cha—and; tvām—You; dhṛitarāṣhṭrasya—of Dhritarashtra; putrāḥ—the sons; sarve—all; saha—with; eva—indeed; avani-pāla—of warrior kings; saṅghaiḥ—the groups; bhīṣhmaḥ—Bhishma; droṇaḥ—Drona; sūta-putraḥ—Karna; tathā—also; asau—that; saha—with; asmadīyaiḥ—our; api—also; yodha-mukhyaiḥ—chief soldiers; vaktrāṇi—mouths; te—Your; tvaramāṇāḥ—rushing; viśhanti—are entering; danṣhṭrā-karālāni—terrible with teeth; bhayānakāni—very fearful; kechit—some of them; vilagnāḥ—caught; daśhana-antareṣhu—between the teeth; sandṛiṣhyante—are seen; chūrṇitaiḥ—smashed; uttama-aṅgaiḥ—with their heads
translation_english: All the sons of Dhritarashtra, along with their allied kings, and Bhishma, Drona, Karna—and our chief soldiers also—are rushing into Your fearful mouths. And some I see trapped with heads smashed between Your teeth.
deep_dive_text: The Inevitability of Destiny. Arjuna receives a terrifying preview of the war's outcome. He sees the greatest warriors of his time—his beloved grandfather Bhishma, his teacher Drona, his rival Karna, and even his own allies—rushing headlong into the crushing jaws of the Universal Form. It is a stark visualization of destiny: the war has practically already happened in the cosmic timeline, and the destruction of the ego-driven kings is absolute and inescapable.

original_script:
यथा नदीनां बहवोऽम्बुवेगाः
समुद्रमेवाभिमुखा द्रवन्ति।
तथा तवामी नरलोकवीरा
विशन्ति वक्त्राण्यभिविज्वलन्ति।।11.28।।
transliteration:
yathā nadīnāṁ bahavo ’mbu-vegāḥ
samudram evābhimukhā dravanti
tathā tavāmī nara-loka-vīrā
viśhanti vaktrāṇy abhivijvalanti
word_meanings: yathā—as; nadīnām—of the rivers; bahavaḥ—the many; ambu-vegāḥ—waves of the waters; samudram—the ocean; eva—certainly; abhimukhāḥ—towards; dravanti—glide; tathā—similarly; tava—Your; amī—all these; nara-loka-vīrāḥ—kings of human society; viśhanti—are entering; vaktrāṇi—the mouths; abhivijvalanti—blazing
translation_english: As the many waves of the rivers flow into the ocean, so do all these great warriors enter blazing into Your mouths.
deep_dive_text: The Flow of Time. Arjuna uses a powerful natural analogy. Just as rivers are helplessly drawn by gravity toward the vast, swallowing ocean, the lives of these powerful kings and warriors are being pulled relentlessly toward their end by the current of Time. They think they are fighting for land and glory, but they are simply rushing toward the ultimate dissolution.

original_script:
यथा प्रदीप्तं ज्वलनं पतङ्गा
विशन्ति नाशाय समृद्धवेगाः।
तथैव नाशाय विशन्ति लोका-
स्तवापि वक्त्राणि समृद्धवेगाः।।11.29।।
transliteration:
yathā pradīptaṁ jvalanaṁ pataṅgā
viśhanti nāśhāya samṛiddha-vegāḥ
tathaiva nāśhāya viśhanti lokās
tavāpi vaktrāṇi samṛiddha-vegāḥ
word_meanings: yathā—as; pradīptam—blazing; jvalanam—a fire; pataṅgāḥ—moths; viśhanti—enter; nāśhāya—for destruction; samṛiddha-vegāḥ—with full speed; tathā eva—similarly; nāśhāya—for destruction; viśhanti—are entering; lokāḥ—all people; tava—Your; api—also; vaktrāṇi—mouths; samṛiddha-vegāḥ—with full speed
translation_english: I see all people rushing full speed into Your mouths, as moths dash to destruction in a blazing fire.
deep_dive_text: The Fatal Attraction of Ego. While the river analogy highlights natural inevitability, the moth analogy highlights fatal illusion. Moths are drawn to the flame out of a blinding, hypnotic attraction, unaware that their desire will destroy them. Similarly, these warriors, driven by greed, ego, and the thrill of battle, are willingly throwing themselves into the jaws of death.

original_script:
लेलिह्यसे ग्रसमानः समन्ताल्-
लोकान्समग्रान्वदनैर्ज्वलद्भिः।
तेजोभिरापूर्य जगत्समग्रं
भासस्तवोग्राः प्रतपन्ति विष्णो।।11.30।।
transliteration:
lelihyase grasamānaḥ samantāl
lokān samagrān vadanair jvaladbhiḥ
tejobhir āpūrya jagat samagraṁ
bhāsas tavogrāḥ pratapanti viṣhṇo
word_meanings: lelihyase—You are licking; grasamānaḥ—devouring; samantāt—from all directions; lokān—people; samagrān—all; vadanaiḥ—by the mouths; jvaladbhiḥ—blazing; tejobhiḥ—by effulgence; āpūrya—covering; jagat—the universe; samagram—all; bhāsaḥ—illuminating rays; tava—Your; ugrāḥ—terrible; pratapanti—are scorching; viṣhṇo—O all-pervading Lord
translation_english: O Vishnu, I see You devouring all people from all sides with Your flaming mouths. Covering all the universe with Your immeasurable rays, You are manifest with terrible, scorching rays.
deep_dive_text: The Devouring Absolute. The vision reaches its absolute peak of terror. 'Lelihyase' implies a licking of lips, a horrifying, almost casual consumption of entire armies and civilizations. The Divine is not just sitting passively; it is an active, all-consuming force of cosmic recycling. The 'Ugra' (terrible) heat of Vishnu leaves Arjuna entirely breathless and desperate for an explanation.

original_script:
आख्याहि मे को भवानुग्ररूपो
नमोऽस्तु ते देववर प्रसीद।
विज्ञातुमिच्छामि भवन्तमाद्यं
न हि प्रजानामि तव प्रवृत्तिम्।।11.31।।
transliteration:
ākhyāhi me ko bhavān ugra-rūpo
namo ’stu te deva-vara prasīda
vijñātum ichchhāmi bhavantam ādyaṁ
na hi prajānāmi tava pravṛittim
word_meanings: ākhyāhi—please explain; me—unto me; kaḥ—who; bhavān—You; ugra-rūpaḥ—in a fierce form; namaḥ astu—obeisances; te—unto You; deva-vara—O great one amongst the demigods; prasīda—be gracious; vijñātum—to know; ichchhāmi—I wish; bhavantam—You; ādyam—the original; na—not; hi—certainly; prajānāmi—do I know; tava—Your; pravṛittim—mission
translation_english: O Lord of lords, so fierce of form, please tell me who You are. I offer my obeisances unto You; please be gracious to me. You are the primal Lord. I want to know about You, for I do not know what Your mission is.
deep_dive_text: The Desperate Plea for Meaning. Arjuna's psychological breaking point arrives. He knows this is Krishna, but this terrifying entity feels utterly alien to the friend who used to drive his chariot. He begs, 'Akhyahi me' (Tell me!), 'Who are you, and what are you doing?' He needs to understand the 'Pravrittim' (mission or purpose) behind this horrifying cosmic slaughter.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
कालोऽस्मि लोकक्षयकृत्प्रवृद्धो
लोकान्समाहर्तुमिह प्रवृत्तः।
ऋतेऽपि त्वां न भविष्यन्ति सर्वे
येऽवस्थिताः प्रत्यनीकेषु योधाः।।11.32।।
transliteration:
śhrī-bhagavān uvācha
kālo ’smi loka-kṣhaya-kṛit pravṛiddho
lokān samāhartum iha pravṛittaḥ
ṛite ’pi tvāṁ na bhaviṣhyanti sarve
ye ’vasthitāḥ pratyanīkeṣhu yodhāḥ
word_meanings: kālaḥ—Time; asmi—I am; loka-kṣhaya-kṛit—the destroyer of worlds; pravṛiddhaḥ—mighty; lokān—the worlds; samāhartum—to annihilate; iha—here; pravṛittaḥ—engaged; ṛite—without; api—even; tvām—you; na bhaviṣhyanti—will cease to be; sarve—all; ye—who; avasthitāḥ—situated; pratyanīkeṣhu—in the opposing armies; yodhāḥ—the warriors
translation_english: I am Kāla (Time), the great destroyer of worlds, currently manifest to engage in the dissolution of these people.
deep_dive_text: Historically, 'Kāla' is the supreme governing principle. This verse removes the moral burden from the warrior by demonstrating that time has already processed the outcome; the individual is merely an instrument.

original_script:
तस्मात्त्वमुत्तिष्ठ यशो लभस्व
जित्वा शत्रून्भुङ्क्ष्व राज्यं समृद्धम्।
मयैवैते निहताः पूर्वमेव
निमित्तमात्रं भव सव्यसाचिन्।।11.33।।
transliteration:
tasmāt tvam uttiṣhṭha yaśho labhasva
jitvā śhatrūn bhuṅkṣhva rājyaṁ samṛiddham
mayaivaite nihatāḥ pūrvam eva
nimitta-mātraṁ bhava savya-sāchin
word_meanings: tasmāt—therefore; tvam—you; uttiṣhṭha—get up; yaśhaḥ—glory; labhasva—gain; jitvā—conquering; śhatrūn—enemies; bhuṅkṣhva—enjoy; rājyam—kingdom; samṛiddham—flourishing; mayā—by Me; eva—certainly; ete—all these; nihatāḥ—killed; pūrvam eva—by previous arrangement; nimitta-mātram—just the instrument; bhava—become; savya-sāchin—O Savyasachi (ambidextrous archer)
translation_english: Therefore, get up. Prepare to fight and win glory. Conquer your enemies and enjoy a flourishing kingdom. They are already put to death by My arrangement, and you, O Savyasachi, can be but an instrument in the fight.
deep_dive_text: The Concept of 'Nimitta-mātram'. Krishna tells Arjuna to be 'merely an instrument.' A historian would note this as the resolution of the tension between free will and destiny: the war is already 'won' in the realm of Kāla (Time), and Arjuna is invited to align his action with the inevitable historical flow.

original_script:
द्रोणं च भीष्मं च जयद्रथं च
कर्णं तथान्यानपि योधवीरान्।
मया हतांस्त्वं जहि मा व्यथिष्ठा
युध्यस्व जेतासि रणे सपत्नान्।।11.34।।
transliteration:
droṇaṁ cha bhīṣhmaṁ cha jayadrathaṁ cha
karṇaṁ tathānyān api yodha-vīrān
mayā hatāns tvaṁ jahi mā vyathiṣhṭhā
yudhyasva jetāsi raṇe sapatnān
word_meanings: droṇam—Drona; cha—also; bhīṣhmam—Bhishma; cha—also; jayadratham—Jayadratha; cha—also; karṇam—Karna; tathā—also; anyān—others; api—certainly; yodha-vīrān—great warriors; mayā—by Me; hatān—already killed; tvam—you; jahi—destroy; mā—do not; vyathiṣhṭhāḥ—be disturbed; yudhyasva—just fight; jetāsi—you will conquer; raṇe—in the fight; sapatnān—enemies
translation_english: Drona, Bhishma, Jayadratha, Karna and the other great warriors have already been destroyed by Me. Therefore, kill them and do not be disturbed. Simply fight, and you will vanquish your enemies in battle.
deep_dive_text: The Guarantee of Victory. Arjuna's deepest anxiety was having to kill his invincible mentors (Bhishma, Drona) and fierce rivals (Karna, Jayadratha). Krishna explicitly names Arjuna's nightmares and tells him, 'I have already handled them.' The command 'Ma vyathishtha' (Do not be disturbed) is a directive to let go of the paralyzing fear of failure. When you align your will with the Divine Will, victory is a foregone conclusion.

original_script:
सञ्जय उवाच
एतच्छ्रुत्वा वचनं केशवस्य
कृताञ्जलिर्वेपमानः किरीटी।
नमस्कृत्वा भूय एवाह कृष्णं
सगद्गदं भीतभीतः प्रणम्य।।11.35।।
transliteration:
sañjaya uvācha
etach chhrutvā vachanaṁ keśhavasya
kṛitāñjalir vepamānaḥ kirīṭī
namaskṛitvā bhūya evāha kṛiṣhṇaṁ
sa-gadgadaṁ bhīta-bhītaḥ praṇamya
word_meanings: sañjayaḥ uvācha—Sanjaya said; etat—thus; śhrutvā—hearing; vachanam—the speech; keśhavasya—of Krishna; kṛita-añjaliḥ—with folded hands; vepamānaḥ—trembling; kirīṭī—Arjuna; namaskṛitvā—offering obeisances; bhūyaḥ—again; eva—also; āha—spoke; kṛiṣhṇam—to Krishna; sa-gadgadam—with a faltering voice; bhīta-bhītaḥ—fearful; praṇamya—bowing down
translation_english: Sanjaya said to Dhritarashtra: O King, after hearing these words from the Supreme Personality of Godhead, the trembling Arjuna offered obeisances with folded hands again and again. He fearfully spoke to Lord Krishna in a faltering voice, as follows.
deep_dive_text: The Trembling Warrior. The perspective briefly shifts back to Sanjaya narrating to the blind King. We see the physical toll the vision has taken on Arjuna. He is 'Vepamana' (trembling) and speaks 'Sa-gadgadam' (with a choking, faltering voice). This sets the stage for Arjuna's beautiful, spontaneous prayer of pacification, where he tries to reconcile his terrifying vision of God with the loving friend standing before him.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
स्थाने हृषीकेश तव प्रकीर्त्या
जगत्प्रहृष्यत्यनुरज्यते च।
रक्षांसि भीतानि दिशो द्रवन्ति
सर्वे नमस्यन्ति च सिद्धसङ्घाः।।11.36।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
sthāne hṛiṣhīkeśha tava prakīrtyā
jagat prahṛiṣhyaty anurajyate cha
rakṣhānsi bhītāni diśho dravanti
sarve namasyanti cha siddha-saṅghāḥ
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjuna said; sthāne—rightly; hṛiṣhīka-īśha—O master of all senses; tava—Your; prakīrtyā—by the glories; jagat—the entire world; prahṛiṣhyati—rejoices; anurajyate—is attached; cha—and; rakṣhānsi—the demons; bhītāni—out of fear; diśhaḥ—in all directions; dravanti—flee; sarve—all; namasyanti—are offering respects; cha—also; siddha-saṅghāḥ—the perfect human beings
translation_english: Arjuna said: O master of the senses, the world becomes joyful upon hearing Your name, and thus everyone becomes attached to You. Although the perfected beings offer You their respectful homage, the demons are afraid, and they flee here and there. All this is rightly done.
deep_dive_text: The Cosmic Harmony. Arjuna regains a fraction of his composure and begins to pray. He realizes that the terrifying form he is seeing is not evil; it is pure, absolute truth. He says 'Sthane' (it is appropriate/right). It is entirely appropriate that the 'Siddhas' (perfected beings) are rejoicing and bowing down, while the 'Rakshasas' (ego-driven demons) are fleeing in terror. The presence of God naturally repels the impure and intensely attracts the pure.

original_script:
कस्माच्च ते न नमेरन्महात्मन्
गरीयसे ब्रह्मणोऽप्यादिकर्त्रे।
अनन्त देवेश जगन्निवास
त्वमक्षरं सदसत्तत्परं यत्।।11.37।।
transliteration:
kasmāch cha te na nameran mahātman
garīyase brahmaṇo ’py ādi-kartre
ananta deveśha jagan-nivāsa
tvam akṣharaṁ sad-asat tat paraṁ yat
word_meanings: kasmāt—why; cha—also; te—unto You; na—not; nameran—they should offer proper obeisances; mahā-ātman—O great one; garīyase—who are better; brahmaṇaḥ—than Brahma; api—although; ādi-kartre—to the supreme creator; ananta—O unlimited; deva-īśha—O God of the gods; jagat-nivāsa—O refuge of the universe; tvam—You; akṣharam—imperishable; sat-asat—cause and effect; tat param—transcendental; yat—because
translation_english: O great one, greater even than Brahma, You are the original creator. Why then should they not offer their respectful obeisances unto You? O limitless one, God of gods, refuge of the universe! You are the invincible source, the cause of all causes, transcendental to this material manifestation.
deep_dive_text: The Supreme Logic of Worship. Arjuna reasons out loud. Why wouldn't everyone bow to You? You are the 'Adi-kartre'—the original creator who even created Lord Brahma (the engineer of the universe). You are 'Aksharam' (the imperishable reality) that exists beyond both 'Sat' (the manifested world) and 'Asat' (the unmanifested void). To bow to You is simply to acknowledge the factual reality of the universe.

original_script:
त्वमादिदेवः पुरुषः पुराणस्-
त्वमस्य विश्वस्य परं निधानम्।
वेत्तासि वेद्यं च परं च धाम
त्वया ततं विश्वमनन्तरूप।।11.38।।
transliteration:
tvam ādi-devaḥ puruṣhaḥ purāṇas
tvam asya viśhvasya paraṁ nidhānam
vettāsi vedyaṁ cha paraṁ cha dhāma
tvayā tataṁ viśhvam ananta-rūpa
word_meanings: tvam—You; ādi-devaḥ—the original Supreme God; puruṣhaḥ—personality; purāṇaḥ—the oldest; tvam—You; asya—of this; viśhvasya—universe; param—transcendental; nidhānam—refuge; vettā—the knower; asi—You are; vedyam—the knowable; cha—and; param—transcendental; cha—and; dhāma—refuge; tvayā—by You; tatam—pervaded; viśhvam—the universe; ananta-rūpa—O unlimited form
translation_english: You are the original Personality of Godhead, the oldest, the ultimate sanctuary of this manifested cosmic world. You are the knower of everything, and You are all that is knowable. You are the supreme refuge, above the material modes. O limitless form! This whole cosmic manifestation is pervaded by You!
deep_dive_text: The Knower and the Known. Arjuna continues his ecstatic praise. He acknowledges Krishna as 'Purana Purusha' (the most ancient person). He perfectly summarizes the philosophy of knowledge: God is the 'Vetta' (the ultimate observer/knower), the 'Vedyam' (the ultimate truth to be known), and the 'Param Dhama' (the ultimate destination). The Divine is the journey, the destination, and the traveler all at once.

original_script:
वायुर्यमोऽग्निर्वरुणः शशाङ्कः
प्रजापतिस्त्वं प्रपितामहश्च।
नमो नमस्तेऽस्तु सहस्रकृत्वः
पुनश्च भूयोऽपि नमो नमस्ते।।11.39।।
transliteration:
vāyur yamo ’gnir varuṇaḥ śhaśhāṅkaḥ
prajāpatis tvaṁ prapitāmahaśh cha
namo namas te ’stu sahasra-kṛitvaḥ
punaśh cha bhūyo ’pi namo namas te
word_meanings: vāyuḥ—air; yamaḥ—the controller; agniḥ—fire; varuṇaḥ—water; śhaśha-aṅkaḥ—the moon; prajāpatiḥ—Brahma; tvam—You; prapitāmahaḥ—the great-grandfather; cha—also; namaḥ—My respects; namaḥ—again my respects; te—unto You; astu—let there be; sahasra-kṛitvaḥ—a thousand times; punaḥ cha—and again; bhūyaḥ—again; api—also; namaḥ—offering respects; namaḥ te—offering respects unto You
translation_english: You are air, and You are the supreme controller! You are fire, You are water, and You are the moon! You are Brahma, the first living creature, and You are the great-grandfather. I therefore offer my respectful obeisances unto You a thousand times, and again and yet again!
deep_dive_text: The Avalanche of Obeisances. Overwhelmed by love and fear, Arjuna's words break down into pure devotion. He rapidly lists the elemental forces—air, fire, water, the moon—realizing they are all just Krishna. He calls Him 'Prapitamaha' (the great-grandfather of all). Unable to adequately express his awe, he just repeats 'Namo Namah' (I bow to You) a thousand times over, trapped in a loop of profound reverence.

original_script:
नमः पुरस्तादथ पृष्ठतस्ते
नमोऽस्तु ते सर्वत एव सर्व।
अनन्तवीर्यामितविक्रमस्त्वं
सर्वं समाप्नोषि ततोऽसि सर्वः।।11.40।।
transliteration:
namaḥ purastād atha pṛiṣhṭhatas te
namo ’stu te sarvata eva sarva
ananta-vīryāmita-vikramas tvaṁ
sarvaṁ samāpnoṣhi tato ’si sarvaḥ
word_meanings: namaḥ—offering obeisances; purastāt—from the front; atha—also; pṛiṣhṭhataḥ—from behind; te—unto You; namaḥ astu—I offer my respects; te—unto You; sarvataḥ—from all sides; eva—indeed; sarva—because You are everything; ananta-vīrya—unlimited potency; amita-vikramaḥ—and unlimited force; tvam—You; sarvam—everything; samāpnoṣhi—You cover; tataḥ—therefore; asi—You are; sarvaḥ—everything
translation_english: Obeisances to You from the front, from behind and from all sides! O unbounded power, You are the master of limitless might! You are all-pervading, and thus You are everything!
deep_dive_text: Surrounded by God. Because the Universal Form has no back or front, and no blind spots, Arjuna realizes he cannot just bow in one direction. He bows to the front, the back, and in all 360 degrees. He concludes with the ultimate Vedantic realization: 'Sarvam samapnoshi tato ’si sarvah'—You pervade everything, therefore, You ARE everything. There is no reality outside of Him.

original_script:
सखेति मत्वा प्रसभं यदुक्तं
हे कृष्ण हे यादव हे सखेति।
अजानता महिमानं तवेदं
मया प्रमादात्प्रणयेन वापि।।11.41।।
यच्चावहासार्थमसत्कृतोऽसि
विहारशय्यासनभोजनेषु।
एकोऽथवाप्यच्युत तत्समक्षं
तत्क्षामये त्वामहमप्रमेयम्।।11.42।।
transliteration:
sakheti matvā prasabhaṁ yad uktaṁ
he kṛiṣhṇa he yādava he sakheti
ajānatā mahimānaṁ tavedaṁ
mayā pramādāt praṇayena vāpi
yach chāvahāsārtham asat-kṛito ’si
vihāra-śhayyāsana-bhojaneṣhu
eko ’thavāpy achyuta tat-samakṣhaṁ
tat kṣhāmaye tvām aham aprameyam
word_meanings: sakhā—friend; iti—thus; matvā—thinking; prasabham—presumptuously; yat—whatever; uktam—said; he kṛiṣhṇa—O Krishna; he yādava—O Yadava; he sakhe—O my dear friend; iti—thus; ajānatā—without knowing; mahimānam—glories; tava—Your; idam—this; mayā—by me; pramādāt—out of foolishness; praṇayena—out of love; vā api—either; yat—whatever; cha—also; avahāsa-artham—for joking; asat-kṛitaḥ—dishonored; asi—You have been; vihāra—in relaxation; śhayyā—in lying down; āsana—in sitting; bhojaneṣhu—or while eating together; ekaḥ—alone; athavā—or; api—also; achyuta—O infallible one; tat-samakṣham—in front of others; tat—all those; kṣhāmaye—ask forgiveness; tvām—from You; aham—I; aprameyam—immeasurable
translation_english: Thinking of You as my friend, I have rashly addressed You 'O Krishna,' 'O Yadava,' 'O my friend,' not knowing Your glories. Please forgive whatever I may have done in madness or in love. I have dishonored You many times, joking as we relaxed, lay on the same bed, or sat or ate together, sometimes alone and sometimes in front of many friends. O infallible one, please excuse me for all those offenses.
deep_dive_text: The Panic of Familiarity. (Grouped together as they form one continuous apology). Arjuna is suddenly struck by intense embarrassment and regret. For his whole life, he treated Krishna like a buddy—slapping his back, calling him casual names ('Hey Yadava'), joking around while eating, and resting together. Having just realized he was treating the terrifying Creator of the Universe like a frat brother, Arjuna frantically begs for forgiveness for his 'Pramadat' (foolishness) and familiar 'Pranayena' (casual love).

original_script:
पितासि लोकस्य चराचरस्य
त्वमस्य पूज्यश्च गुरुर्गरीयान्।
न त्वत्समोऽस्त्यभ्यधिकः कुतोऽन्यो
लोकत्रयेऽप्यप्रतिमप्रभाव।।11.43।।
transliteration:
pitāsi lokasya charācharasya
tvam asya pūjyaśh cha gurur garīyān
na tvat-samo ’sty abhyadhikaḥ kuto ’nyo
loka-traye ’py apratima-prabhāva
word_meanings: pitā—the father; asi—You are; lokasya—of the entire world; chara-acharasya—of the moving and nonmoving; tvam—You; asya—of this; pūjyaḥ—worshipable; cha—also; guruḥ—master; garīyān—glorious; na—never; tvat-samaḥ—equal to You; asti—there is; abhyadhikaḥ—greater; kutaḥ—how is it possible; anyaḥ—other; loka-traye—in the three planetary systems; api—also; apratima-prabhāva—O immeasurable power
translation_english: You are the father of this complete cosmic manifestation, of the moving and the nonmoving. You are its worshipable chief, the supreme spiritual master. No one is equal to You, nor can anyone be one with You. How then could there be anyone greater than You within the three worlds, O Lord of immeasurable power?
deep_dive_text: The Unmatched Superiority. Arjuna firmly establishes the hierarchy of reality. Krishna is not just a powerful king or an avatar; He is the 'Pita' (Father) and the 'Guru-gariyan' (the most glorious spiritual master) of the entire cosmos. Arjuna states a logical absolute: if God is infinite, there cannot be two infinites. Therefore, no one can be equal to Him ('na tvat-samo asti'), let alone greater than Him.

original_script:
तस्मात्प्रणम्य प्रणिधाय कायं
प्रसादये त्वामहमीशमीड्यम्।
पितेव पुत्रस्य सखेव सख्युः
प्रियः प्रियायार्हसि देव सोढुम्।।11.44।।
transliteration:
tasmāt praṇamya praṇidhāya kāyaṁ
prasādaye tvām aham īśham īḍyam
piteva putrasya sakheva sakhyuḥ
priyaḥ priyāyārhasi deva soḍhum
word_meanings: tasmāt—therefore; praṇamya—offering obeisances; praṇidhāya—laying down; kāyam—the body; prasādaye—to beg mercy; tvām—unto You; aham—I; īśham—unto the Supreme Lord; īḍyam—worshipable; pitā iva—like a father; putrasya—with a son; sakhā iva—like a friend; sakhyuḥ—with a friend; priyaḥ—a lover; priyāyāḥ—with the dearmost; arhasi—You should; deva—O Lord; soḍhum—tolerate
translation_english: You are the Supreme Lord, to be worshiped by every living being. Thus I fall down to offer You my respectful obeisances and ask Your mercy. As a father tolerates the impudence of his son, or a friend tolerates the impertinence of a friend, or a wife tolerates the familiarity of her partner, please tolerate the wrongs I may have done You.
deep_dive_text: The Plea for Tolerance. Throwing his entire body to the ground in total submission ('Pranidhaya Kayam'), Arjuna asks the terrifying Universal Form to remember their personal, intimate relationships. He uses three deeply emotional analogies: please forgive my past mistakes the way a loving father excuses his toddler, the way a best friend brushes off an insult, or the way a lover excuses the flaws of their beloved.

original_script:
अदृष्टपूर्वं हृषितोऽस्मि दृष्ट्वा
भयेन च प्रव्यथितं मनो मे।
तदेव मे दर्शय देव रूपं
प्रसीद देवेश जगन्निवास।।11.45।।
transliteration:
adṛiṣhṭa-pūrvaṁ hṛiṣhito ’smi dṛiṣhṭvā
bhayena cha pravyathitaṁ mano me
tad eva me darśhaya deva rūpaṁ
prasīda deveśha jagan-nivāsa
word_meanings: adṛiṣhṭa-pūrvam—never seen before; hṛiṣhitaḥ—gladdened; asmi—I am; dṛiṣhṭvā—by seeing; bhayena—out of fear; cha—and; pravyathitam—perturbed; manaḥ—mind; me—my; tat—that; eva—certainly; me—unto me; darśhaya—show; deva—O Lord; rūpam—the form; prasīda—just be gracious; deva-īśha—O Lord of lords; jagat-nivāsa—O refuge of the universe
translation_english: After seeing this universal form, which I have never seen before, I am gladdened, but at the same time my mind is disturbed with fear. Therefore please bestow Your grace upon me and reveal again Your form as the Personality of Godhead, O Lord of lords, O abode of the universe.
deep_dive_text: The Limit of Awe. Arjuna admits his psychological duality. He is 'Hrishitah' (thrilled/gladdened) to have witnessed the ultimate truth that no human has ever seen, but his mind is completely 'Pravyathitam' (shaken with fear). The infinite is too vast for the human heart to comfortably love. He begs Krishna to 'Prasida' (have mercy) and shrink back down into His reassuring, familiar form.

original_script:
किरीटिनं गदिनं चक्रहस्त-
मिच्छामि त्वां द्रष्टुमर्हं तथैव।
तेनैव रूपेण चतुर्भुजेन
सहस्रबाहो भव विश्वमूर्ते।।11.46।।
transliteration:
kirīṭinaṁ gadinaṁ chakra-hastam
ichchhāmi tvāṁ draṣhṭum ahaṁ tathaiva
tenaiva rūpeṇa chatur-bhujena
sahasra-bāho bhava viśhva-mūrte
word_meanings: kirīṭinam—with helmet; gadinam—with club; chakra-hastam—disc in hand; ichchhāmi—I wish; tvām—You; draṣhṭum—to see; aham—I; tathā eva—in that position; tena eva—in that; rūpeṇa—form; chatuḥ-bhujena—four-handed; sahasra-bāho—O thousand-handed Lord; bhava—just become; viśhva-mūrte—O universal form
translation_english: O universal form, O thousand-armed Lord, I wish to see You in Your four-armed form, with helmeted head and with club, wheel, conch and lotus in Your hands. I long to see You in that form.
deep_dive_text: The Need for the Personal. Arjuna makes a specific request. He cannot relate to the 'Sahasra-baho' (thousand-armed) cosmic entity; it is too abstract and terrifying. He asks Krishna to return to His four-armed 'Narayana' or 'Vishnu' form—a form that still represents supreme divinity and majesty (with the crown, club, and disc), but is contained, beautiful, and capable of a personal, loving relationship.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
मया प्रसन्नेन तवार्जुनेदं
रूपं परं दर्शितमात्मयोगात्।
तेजोमयं विश्वमनन्तमाद्यं
यन्मे त्वदन्येन न दृष्टपूर्वम्।।11.47।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
mayā prasannena tavārjunedaṁ
rūpaṁ paraṁ darśhitam ātma-yogāt
tejomayaṁ viśhvam anantam ādyaṁ
yan me tvad-anyena na dṛiṣhṭa-pūrvam
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Personality of Godhead said; mayā—by Me; prasannena—happily; tava—unto you; arjuna—O Arjuna; idam—this; rūpam—form; param—transcendental; darśhitam—shown; ātma-yogāt—by My internal mystic power; tejaḥ-mayam—full of effulgence; viśhvam—the entire universe; anantam—unlimited; ādyam—original; yat—which; me—My; tvat anyena—besides you; na dṛiṣhṭa-pūrvam—no one has previously seen
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: My dear Arjuna, happily have I shown you, by My internal mystic power, this supreme universal form within the material world. No one before you has ever seen this primal form, unlimited and full of glaring effulgence.
deep_dive_text: The Grace of the Vision. Krishna reassures Arjuna. He did not show this form to frighten him, but 'Prasannena' (happily or out of grace). It was a special privilege granted purely out of love. This 'Tejomayam' (effulgent) and 'Adyam' (original) form is not accessible to anyone through ordinary means; it was revealed exclusively to Arjuna because of his pure devotion.

original_script:
न वेदयज्ञाध्ययनैर्न दानैर्-
न च क्रियाभिर्न तपोभिरुग्रैः।
एवंरूपः शक्य अहं नृलोके
द्रष्टुं त्वदन्येन कुरुप्रवीर।।11.48।।
transliteration:
na veda-yajñādhyayanair na dānair
na cha kriyābhir na tapobhir ugraiḥ
evaṁ-rūpaḥ śhakya ahaṁ nṛi-loke
draṣhṭuṁ tvad-anyena kuru-pravīra
word_meanings: na—never; veda-yajña—by sacrifice; adhyayanaiḥ—or Vedic study; na—never; dānaiḥ—by charity; na—never; cha—also; kriyābhiḥ—by pious activities; na—never; tapobhiḥ—by serious penances; ugraiḥ—severe; evam-rūpaḥ—in this form; śhakyaḥ—can; aham—I; nṛi-loke—in this material world; draṣhṭum—be seen; tvat—than you; anyena—by another; kuru-pravīra—O best among the Kuru warriors
translation_english: O best of the Kuru warriors, no one before you has ever seen this universal form of Mine, for neither by studying the Vedas, nor by performing sacrifices, nor by charity, nor by pious activities, nor by severe penances can I be seen in this form in the material world.
deep_dive_text: The Inadequacy of Human Effort. Krishna explicitly lists the highest spiritual achievements of human civilization: studying scripture, massive charities, rituals, and agonizing physical penances. None of these acts as a currency to 'buy' a vision of the Infinite. The Ultimate Reality cannot be commanded or unlocked by mechanical human effort; it only reveals itself through grace.

original_script:
मा ते व्यथा मा च विमूढभावो
दृष्ट्वा रूपं घोरमीदृङ्ममेदम्।
व्यपेतभीः प्रीतमनाः पुनस्त्वं
तदेव मे रूपमिदं प्रपश्य।।11.49।।
transliteration:
mā te vyathā mā cha vimūḍha-bhāvo
dṛiṣhṭvā rūpaṁ ghoram īdṛiṅ mamedam
vyapeta-bhīḥ prīta-manāḥ punas tvaṁ
tad eva me rūpam idaṁ prapaśhya
word_meanings: mā—let it not be; te—unto you; vyathā—trouble; mā—let it not be; cha—also; vimūḍha-bhāvaḥ—bewilderment; dṛiṣhṭvā—by seeing; rūpam—this form; ghoram—horrible; īdṛik—as it is; mama—My; idam—this; vyapeta-bhīḥ—free from all fear; prīta-manāḥ—pleased in mind; punaḥ—again; tvam—you; tat—that; eva—thus; me—My; rūpam—form; idam—this; prapaśhya—just see
translation_english: You have been perturbed and bewildered by seeing this horrible feature of Mine. Now let it be finished. My devotee, be free again from all disturbances. With a peaceful mind you can now see the form you desire.
deep_dive_text: The Return to Comfort. Like a parent calming a terrified child after a nightmare, Krishna soothes Arjuna. He acknowledges the form was 'Ghoram' (horrible/terrifying). He tells Arjuna to let go of his 'Vyatha' (trouble/fear) and 'Vimudha-bhavo' (bewilderment). With a 'Prita-manah' (pleased/peaceful mind), Arjuna is invited to look again as Krishna transitions back to a manageable, loving form.

original_script:
सञ्जय उवाच
इत्यर्जुनं वासुदेवस्तथोक्त्वा
स्वकं रूपं दर्शयामास भूयः।
आश्वासयामास च भीतमेनं
भूत्वा पुनः सौम्यवपुर्महात्मा।।11.50।।
transliteration:
sañjaya uvācha
ity arjunaṁ vāsudevas tathoktvā
svakaṁ rūpaṁ darśhayām āsa bhūyaḥ
āśhvāsayām āsa cha bhītam enaṁ
bhūtvā punaḥ saumya-vapur mahātmā
word_meanings: sañjayaḥ uvācha—Sanjaya said; iti—thus; arjunam—unto Arjuna; vāsudevaḥ—Krishna; tathā—in that way; uktvā—speaking; svakam—His own; rūpam—form; darśhayām āsa—showed; bhūyaḥ—again; āśhvāsayām āsa—encouraged; cha—also; bhītam—fearful; enam—him; bhūtvā—becoming; punaḥ—again; saumya-vapuḥ—the beautiful form; mahā-ātmā—the great one
translation_english: Sanjaya said to Dhritarashtra: The Supreme Personality of Godhead, Krishna, having spoken thus to Arjuna, displayed His real four-armed form and at last showed His two-armed form, thus encouraging the fearful Arjuna.
deep_dive_text: The De-escalation of the Divine. Sanjaya narrates the transition. Krishna first scales down from the infinite, terrifying Vishvarupa to the majestic four-armed Vishnu form (as Arjuna requested). But seeing Arjuna is still deeply shaken, the 'Mahatma' (Great Soul) shrinks down even further, returning to His 'Saumya-vapuh'—the gentle, sweet, two-armed human-like form of Krishna, the charioteer, to fully comfort his friend.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
दृष्ट्वेदं मानुषं रूपं तव सौम्यं जनार्दन।
इदानीमस्मि संवृत्तः सचेताः प्रकृतिं गतः।।11.51।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
dṛiṣhṭvedaṁ mānuṣhaṁ rūpaṁ tava saumyaṁ janārdana
idānīm asmi saṁvṛittaḥ sa-chetāḥ prakṛitiṁ gataḥ
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjuna said; dṛiṣhṭvā—seeing; idam—this; mānuṣham—human; rūpam—form; tava—Your; saumyam—very beautiful; janārdana—O chastiser of the enemies; idānīm—now; asmi—I am; saṁvṛittaḥ—settled; sa-chetāḥ—in my consciousness; prakṛitim—to my own nature; gataḥ—returned
translation_english: When Arjuna thus saw Krishna in His original form, he said: O Janardana, seeing this humanlike form, so very beautiful, I am now composed in mind, and I am restored to my original nature.
deep_dive_text: The Return to Sanity. Arjuna exhales. Seeing the 'Manusham Rupam' (human-like form) that is 'Saumyam' (gentle and beautiful), his psychological crisis ends. He declares that he has 'Prakritim gatah' (returned to his normal nature). The human mind is not built to permanently dwell in the infinite; it requires the anchor of the personal and the beautiful to function and love properly.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
सुदुर्दर्शमिदं रूपं दृष्टवानसि यन्मम।
देवा अप्यस्य रूपस्य नित्यं दर्शनकाङ्क्षिणः।।11.52।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
su-durdarśham idaṁ rūpaṁ dṛiṣhṭavān asi yan mama
devā apy asya rūpasya nityaṁ darśhana-kāṅkṣhiṇaḥ
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Personality of Godhead said; su-durdarśham—very difficult to see; idam—this; rūpam—form; dṛiṣhṭavān asi—as you have seen; yat—which; mama—of Mine; devāḥ—the demigods; api—also; asya—this; rūpasya—form; nityam—eternally; darśhana-kāṅkṣhiṇaḥ—aspiring to see
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: My dear Arjuna, this form of Mine which you are now seeing is very difficult to behold. Even the demigods are ever seeking the opportunity to see this form, which is so dear.
deep_dive_text: The Rarity of the Two-Armed Form. This is a profound twist. We might think the Universal Form was the ultimate revelation, but Krishna clarifies that His original, sweet, two-armed form is actually the most 'Su-durdarsham' (extremely difficult to see). Even the highest demigods (Devas) long eternally just to catch a glimpse of this personal, loving form, because it represents the highest pinnacle of spiritual intimacy, far beyond cosmic power.

original_script:
नाहं वेदैर्न तपसा न दानेन न चेज्यया।
शक्य एवंविधो द्रष्टुं दृष्टवानसि मां यथा।।11.53।।
transliteration:
nāhaṁ vedair na tapasā na dānena na chejyayā
śhakya evaṁ-vidho draṣhṭuṁ dṛiṣhṭavān asi māṁ yathā
word_meanings: na—never; aham—I; vedaiḥ—by study of the Vedas; na—never; tapasā—by serious penances; na—never; dānena—by charity; na—never; cha—also; ijyayā—by worship; śhakyaḥ—it is possible; evam-vidhaḥ—like this; draṣhṭum—to see; dṛiṣhṭavān—seeing; asi—you are; mām—Me; yathā—as
translation_english: The form you are seeing with your transcendental eyes cannot be understood simply by studying the Vedas, nor by undergoing serious penances, nor by charity, nor by worship. It is not by these means that one can see Me as I am.
deep_dive_text: The Rejection of Mechanics. Krishna repeats his earlier statement (from verse 48) but applies it specifically to His original, loving form. You cannot 'hack' your way to God through sheer willpower, massive philanthropy, or intellectual mastery of scriptures. If the goal is a relationship with the Supreme Person, impersonal, mechanical methods will always fail.

original_script:
भक्त्या त्वनन्यया शक्य अहमेवंविधोऽर्जुन।
ज्ञातुं द्रष्टुं च तत्त्वेन प्रवेष्टुं च परन्तप।।11.54।।
transliteration:
bhaktyā tv ananyayā śhakya aham evaṁ-vidho ’rjuna
jñātuṁ draṣhṭuṁ cha tattvena praveṣhṭuṁ cha parantapa
word_meanings: bhaktyā—by devotional service; tu—but; ananyayā—without being mixed with fruitive activities or empirical speculation; śhakyaḥ—possible; aham—I; evam-vidhaḥ—like this; arjuna—O Arjuna; jñātum—to be known; draṣhṭum—to be seen; cha—and; tattvena—in fact; praveṣhṭum—to enter into; cha—also; parantapa—O mighty-armed one
translation_english: My dear Arjuna, only by undivided devotional service can I be understood as I am, standing before you, and can thus be seen directly. Only in this way can you enter into the mysteries of My understanding.
deep_dive_text: The Master Key: Ananya Bhakti. If penance and study don't work, what does? 'Bhaktya tv ananyaya'—by undivided, unalloyed devotion. Love is the only currency that matters in the spiritual realm. When devotion is pure (not asking for material rewards or spiritual superpowers), it unlocks the ability to truly know (Jnatum), see (Drashtum), and eventually enter into (Praveshtum) the ultimate reality of the Divine.

original_script:
मत्कर्मकृन्मत्परमो मद्भक्तः सङ्गवर्जितः।
निर्वैरः सर्वभूतेषु यः स मामेति पाण्डव।।11.55।।
transliteration:
mat-karma-kṛin mat-paramo mad-bhaktaḥ saṅga-varjitaḥ
nirvairaḥ sarva-bhūteṣhu yaḥ sa mām eti pāṇḍava
word_meanings: mat-karma-kṛit—engaged in doing My work; mat-paramaḥ—considering Me the supreme; mat-bhaktaḥ—engaged in My devotional service; saṅga-varjitaḥ—freed from the contamination of fruitive activities and mental speculation; nirvairaḥ—without an enemy; sarva-bhūteṣhu—among all living entities; yaḥ—one who; saḥ—he; mām—unto Me; eti—comes; pāṇḍava—O son of Pandu
translation_english: My dear Arjuna, he who engages in My pure devotional service, free from the contaminations of fruitive activities and mental speculation, he who works for Me, who makes Me the supreme goal of his life, and who is friendly to every living being—he certainly comes to Me.
deep_dive_text: The Blueprint of the Perfect Yogi. Krishna summarizes the entire 11th chapter in one actionable formula. To reach the Divine, one must: 1. Work for God (Mat-karma-krit), 2. Make Him the ultimate goal (Mat-paramo), 3. Be devoted (Mad-bhakta), 4. Detach from toxic associations and worldly outcomes (Sanga-varjita), and crucially, 5. Be completely free from malice toward any living being (Nirvairah). This concludes the profound revelations of the Eleventh Chapter.

original_script: विश्वरूपदर्शनयोगः
transliteration: Viśhvarūpa Darśhana Yogaḥ
translation_english: Chapter 11: The Yoga of the Vision of the Universal Form
deep_dive_text: Chapter 11 is the awe-inspiring climax of the Bhagavad Gita's visual theology. Arjuna receives 'divine eyes' and witnesses the staggering, terrifying Vishvarupa—the Universal Form where all of space, time, gods, demons, and the inevitable destruction of the cosmic order are contained within Krishna. Overwhelmed and terrified by this blazing, all-devouring form of Time, Arjuna begs Krishna to return to His familiar, gentle shape. Krishna obliges, concluding with the ultimate lesson: the Infinite cannot be purchased by intelligence or austerity; it can only be realized through pure, undivided love.

original_script: अथ द्वादशोऽध्यायः - भक्तियोगः
transliteration: Bhakti Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Bhakti—devotion/participation; Yogaḥ—the path of synthesis.
translation_english: Chapter 12 Summary: The Yoga of Devotion
deep_dive_text: This chapter addresses the historical debate between 'Saguna' (worship of the Divine with form) and 'Nirguna' (meditation on the formless Absolute). Krishna identifies the 'Saguna' path as more practical for embodied beings. It concludes with a list of 'Bhakta-lakshanas'—the behavioral traits of an ideal practitioner—emphasizing equanimity, lack of malice, and internal independence.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
एवं सततयुक्ता ये भक्तास्त्वां पर्युपासते।
ये चाप्यक्षरमव्यक्तं तेषां के योगवित्तमाः।।12.1।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
evaṁ satata-yuktā ye bhaktās tvāṁ paryupāsate
ye chāpy akṣharam avyaktaṁ teṣhāṁ ke yoga-vittamāḥ
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjuna said; evam—thus; satata-yuktāḥ—always engaged; ye—who; bhaktāḥ—devotees; tvām—You; paryupāsate—worship; ye—who; cha—also; api—certainly; akṣharam—the imperishable; avyaktam—the unmanifested; teṣhām—of them; ke—who; yoga-vit-tamāḥ—the most perfect in knowledge of yoga
translation_english: Arjuna inquired: Which are considered to be more perfect, those who are always properly engaged in Your devotional service or those who worship the unmanifested, the imperishable?
deep_dive_text: The Ultimate Spiritual Debate. Arjuna asks the question that has divided philosophers for millennia: Personal vs. Impersonal. Should we worship a God with a form, a personality, and qualities (Saguna), or should we meditate on a formless, invisible, abstract universal energy (Nirguna)? He wants to know who the 'Yoga-vittamah' (the most perfect yogis) truly are.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
मय्यावेश्य मनो ये मां नित्ययुक्ता उपासते।
श्रद्धया परयोपेतास्ते मे युक्ततमा मताः।।12.2।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
mayy āveśhya mano ye māṁ nitya-yuktā upāsate
śhraddhayā parayopetās te me yukta-tamā matāḥ
