word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Personality of Godhead said; mayi—upon Me; āveśhya—fixing; manaḥ—the mind; ye—who; mām—unto Me; nitya-yuktāḥ—always engaged; upāsate—worship; śhraddhayā—with faith; parayā—transcendental; upetāḥ—endowed; te—they; me—by Me; yukta-tamāḥ—most perfect in yoga; matāḥ—are considered
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: Those who fix their minds on My personal form and are always engaged in worshiping Me with great and transcendental faith are considered by Me to be most perfect.
deep_dive_text: The Verdict: Devotion Wins. Krishna answers without hesitation. He calls those who fix their minds on His personal form 'Yukta-tamah' (the highest yogis). The key ingredients here are 'Nitya-yukta' (constant engagement) and 'Paraya Shraddhaya' (supreme, transcendental faith). It is not about intellectual gymnastics; it is about anchoring the wandering mind through the gravity of deep, personal love.

original_script:
ये त्वक्षरमनिर्देश्यमव्यक्तं पर्युपासते।
सर्वत्रगमचिन्त्यं च कूटस्थमचलं ध्रुवम्।।12.3।।
transliteration:
ye tv akṣharam anirdeśhyam avyaktaṁ paryupāsate
sarvatra-gam achintyaṁ cha kūṭa-stham achalaṁ dhruvam
word_meanings: ye—those who; tu—but; akṣharam—the imperishable; anirdeśhyam—the indefinable; avyaktam—the unmanifested; paryupāsate—completely worship; sarvatra-gam—all-pervading; achintyam—inconceivable; cha—also; kūṭa-stham—unchanging; achalam—immovable; dhruvam—fixed
translation_english: But those who fully worship the unmanifested, that which lies beyond the perception of the senses, the all-pervading, inconceivable, unchanging, fixed and immovable—
deep_dive_text: The Abstract Path. Krishna does not dismiss the formless path. He perfectly describes the 'Nirguna Brahman'—the imperishable (Aksharam), undefinable (Anirdeshyam), inconceivable (Achintyam) reality that pervades everything. It is the raw, unmanifested software code of the universe. He acknowledges that people do worship this abstract absolute.

original_script:
सन्नियम्येन्द्रियग्रामं सर्वत्र समबुद्धयः।
ते प्राप्नुवन्ति मामेव सर्वभूतहिते रताः।।12.4।।
transliteration:
sanniyamyendriya-grāmaṁ sarvatra sama-buddhayaḥ
te prāpnuvanti mām eva sarva-bhūta-hite ratāḥ
word_meanings: sanniyamya—controlling; indriya-grāmam—all the senses; sarvatra—everywhere; sama-buddhayaḥ—equally disposed; te—they; prāpnuvanti—achieve; mām—Me; eva—certainly; sarva-bhūta-hite—for the welfare of all living entities; ratāḥ—engaged
translation_english: Such persons, by controlling the various senses and being equally disposed to everyone, and engaged in the welfare of all, at last achieve Me.
deep_dive_text: Both Paths Reach the Summit. If you follow the path of the formless, you still reach the exact same goal ('prapnuvanti mam eva'—they achieve Me). However, Krishna lists the strict prerequisites for this path: rigorous, total control of the senses (Sanniyamya indriya-gramam) and absolute equality of mind. They must also be actively engaged in the welfare of all beings. It is a path of severe, intellectual asceticism.

original_script:
क्लेशोऽधिकतरस्तेषामव्यक्तासक्तचेतसाम्।
अव्यक्ता हि गतिर्दुःखं देहवद्भिरवाप्यते।।12.5।।
transliteration:
kleśho ’dhikataras teṣhām avyaktāsakta-chetasām
avyaktā hi gatir duḥkhaṁ dehavadbhir avāpyate
word_meanings: kleśhaḥ—trouble; adhikataraḥ—very much; teṣhām—of them; avyakta—to the unmanifested; āsakta—attached; chetasām—of those whose minds; avyaktā—the unmanifested; hi—certainly; gatiḥ—progress; duḥkham—with trouble; deha-vadbhiḥ—by the embodied; avāpyate—is achieved
translation_english: For those whose minds are attached to the unmanifested, impersonal feature of the Supreme, advancement is very troublesome. To make progress in that discipline is always difficult for those who are embodied.
deep_dive_text: The Friction of the Formless. This is a crucial psychological insight. Krishna says the path of the abstract is 'Klesha adhikatara' (extremely troublesome). Why? Because we are 'Dehavadbhih' (embodied beings). We have forms, emotions, and relationships. Trying to force a human heart to love an invisible, formless void goes against our very nature. It requires a painful suppression of our natural emotional gravity, whereas Bhakti utilizes our emotions to propel us upward.

original_script:
ये तु सर्वाणि कर्माणि मयि संन्यस्य मत्पराः।
अनन्येनैव योगेन मां ध्यायन्त उपासते।।12.6।।
transliteration:
ye tu sarvāṇi karmāṇi mayi sannyasya mat-parāḥ
ananyenaiva yogena māṁ dhyāyanta upāsate
word_meanings: ye—those who; tu—but; sarvāṇi—all; karmāṇi—activities; mayi—unto Me; sannyasya—giving up; mat-parāḥ—being attached to Me; ananyena—without division; eva—certainly; yogena—by practice of yoga; mām—upon Me; dhyāyantaḥ—meditating; upāsate—worship
translation_english: But those who worship Me, giving up all their activities unto Me and being devoted to Me without deviation, engaged in devotional service and always meditating upon Me—
deep_dive_text: The Blueprint of the Devotee. Krishna contrasts the difficult ascetic path with the path of Bhakti. The formula is beautifully simple: 'Sannyasya mat-parah'—renounce the results of your actions by offering them to Me, and make Me the supreme goal. It doesn't require fleeing to a cave; it requires operating in the world with 'Ananya Yoga' (undivided focus), turning your daily job, relationships, and duties into acts of worship.

original_script:
तेषामहं समुद्धर्ता मृत्युसंसारसागरात्।
भवामि नचिरात्पार्थ मय्यावेशितचेतसाम्।।12.7।।
transliteration:
teṣhām ahaṁ samuddhartā mṛityu-saṁsāra-sāgarāt
bhavāmi na chirāt pārtha mayy āveśhita-chetasām
word_meanings: teṣhām—of them; aham—I; samuddhartā—the deliverer; mṛityu—of death; saṁsāra—in material existence; sāgarāt—from the ocean; bhavāmi—I become; na—not; chirāt—after a long time; pārtha—O son of Pritha; mayi—upon Me; āveśhita—fixed; chetasām—of those whose minds
translation_english: O son of Pritha, for those whose minds are fixed upon Me, I swiftly become the deliverer from the ocean of birth and death.
deep_dive_text: The Divine Rescue Operation. This is the ultimate promise of Bhakti Yoga. If you try to cross the 'Samsara-sagara' (the turbulent ocean of repeated birth and death) through your own intellectual effort, you might drown. But if you fix your mind on the Divine, Krishna promises to act as the 'Samuddharta' (the rescuer/lifeguard). And He does it 'Na chirat'—swiftly, without delay. You don't save yourself; God reaches down and pulls you out.

original_script:
मय्येव मन आधत्स्व मयि बुद्धिं निवेशय।
निवसिष्यसि मय्येव अत ऊर्ध्वं न संशयः।।12.8।।
transliteration:
mayy eva mana ādhatsva mayi buddhiṁ niveśhaya
nivasiṣhyasi mayy eva ata ūrdhvaṁ na sanśhayaḥ
word_meanings: mayi—upon Me; eva—certainly; manaḥ—mind; ādhatsva—fix; mayi—upon Me; buddhim—intelligence; niveśhaya—apply; nivasiṣhyasi—you will live; mayi—in Me; eva—certainly; ataḥ ūrdhvam—thereafter; na—never; sanśhayaḥ—doubt
translation_english: Just fix your mind upon Me, the Supreme Personality of Godhead, and engage all your intelligence in Me. Thus you will live in Me always, without a doubt.
deep_dive_text: The Highest Instruction. This is Plan A. Krishna tells Arjuna to lock his 'Mana' (emotional mind) and 'Buddhi' (intellectual mind) completely onto the Divine. If you can align both your feelings and your logic toward the Supreme, you achieve instant spiritual residence. You won't have to wait for the afterlife; you will 'Nivasishyasi' (live) in the Divine right here, right now.

original_script:
अथ चित्तं समाधातुं न शक्नोषि मयि स्थिरम्।
अभ्यासयोगेन ततो मामिच्छाप्तुं धनञ्जय।।12.9।।
transliteration:
atha chittaṁ samādhātuṁ na śhaknoṣhi mayi sthiram
abhyāsa-yogena tato mām ichchhāptuṁ dhanañjaya
word_meanings: atha—if, therefore; chittam—mind; samādhātum—to fix; na—not; śhaknoṣhi—you are able; mayi—upon Me; sthiram—steadily; abhyāsa-yogena—by the practice of yoga; tataḥ—then; mām—Me; ichchhā—desire; āptum—to get; dhanañjaya—O winner of wealth, Arjuna
translation_english: My dear Arjuna, O winner of wealth, if you cannot fix your mind upon Me without deviation, then follow the regulative principles of yoga. In this way develop a desire to attain Me.
deep_dive_text: Plan B: The Power of Practice. Krishna is the ultimate pragmatic teacher. He knows that instantly fixing the mind on God (Plan A) is incredibly difficult for most people. So, He introduces Plan B: 'Abhyasa Yoga' (the yoga of practice). If your mind wanders, gently bring it back through routines, chanting, rules, and rituals. The repetitive practice itself will eventually forge a genuine desire ('Ichchha') to attain the Supreme.

original_script:
अभ्यासेऽप्यसमर्थोऽसि मत्कर्मपरमो भव।
मदर्थमपि कर्माणि कुर्वन्सिद्धिमवाप्स्यसि।।12.10।।
transliteration:
abhyāse ’py asamartho ’si mat-karma-paramo bhava
mad-artham api karmāṇi kurvan siddhim avāpsyasi
word_meanings: abhyāse—in practice; api—even if; asamarthaḥ—unable; asi—you are; mat-karma—My work; paramaḥ—dedicated to; bhava—become; mat-artham—for My sake; api—even; karmāṇi—actions; kurvan—performing; siddhim—perfection; avāpsyasi—you will achieve
translation_english: If you cannot practice the regulations of bhakti-yoga, then just try to work for Me, because by working for Me you will come to the perfect stage.
deep_dive_text: Plan C: The Path of Dedicated Action. Krishna is infinitely accommodating. If Plan B (regulative practice and meditation) is too difficult, He offers Plan C. You don't need to change your career or sit in a temple all day. Just change the beneficiary of your work. Dedicate your projects, your art, your business, and your daily labor to the Divine. When you work for a higher purpose ('Mat-artham'), the work itself becomes the purifying practice that leads to perfection.

original_script:
अथैतदप्यशक्तोऽसि कर्तुं मद्योगमाश्रितः।
सर्वकर्मफलत्यागं ततः कुरु यतात्मवान्।।12.11।।
transliteration:
athaitad apy aśhakto ’si kartuṁ mad-yogam āśhritaḥ
sarva-karma-phala-tyāgaṁ tataḥ kuru yatātmavān
word_meanings: atha—if, therefore; etat—this; api—also; aśhaktaḥ—unable; asi—you are; kartum—to perform; mat-yogam—taking shelter of My yoga; āśhritaḥ—taking refuge; sarva-karma—of all activities; phala—of the results; tyāgam—renunciation; tataḥ—then; kuru—do; yata-ātma-vān—self-situated
translation_english: If, however, you are unable to work in this consciousness of Me, then try to act giving up all results of your work and try to be self-situated.
deep_dive_text: Plan D: The Safety Net of Detachment. What if your ego is too strong to say 'I am working for God'? Krishna offers the final safety net. If you can't work for the Divine, at least stop working purely for your own selfish greed. 'Karma-phala-tyagam' means letting go of the obsession with the results. Give your wealth to a good cause, help others, and detach your self-worth from your material successes and failures. Even secular detachment purifies the mind.

original_script:
श्रेयो हि ज्ञानमभ्यासाज्ज्ञानाद्ध्यानं विशिष्यते।
ध्यानात्कर्मफलत्यागस्त्यागाच्छान्तिरनन्तरम्।।12.12।।
transliteration:
śhreyo hi jñānam abhyāsāj jñānād dhyānaṁ viśhiṣhyate
dhyānāt karma-phala-tyāgas tyāgāch chhāntir anantaram
word_meanings: śhreyaḥ—better; hi—certainly; jñānam—knowledge; abhyāsāt—than practice; jñānāt—than knowledge; dhyānam—meditation; viśhiṣhyate—is considered better; dhyānāt—than meditation; karma-phala-tyāgaḥ—renunciation of the results of action; tyāgāt—by such renunciation; śhāntiḥ—peace; anantaram—immediately
translation_english: If you cannot take to this practice, then engage yourself in the cultivation of knowledge. Better than knowledge, however, is meditation, and better than meditation is renunciation of the fruits of action, for by such renunciation one can attain peace of mind.
deep_dive_text: The Hierarchy of Peace. Krishna ranks the spiritual tools. Mechanical practice without understanding is low. Knowledge (Jnana) is better. Meditation (Dhyana) with that knowledge is even better. But the absolute highest is 'Tyaga'—renouncing the anxiety of results. Why? Because the moment you stop obsessing over 'what's in it for me,' you achieve 'Shantir anantaram' (immediate, instant peace). Detachment is the fastest shortcut to tranquility.

original_script:
अद्वेष्टा सर्वभूतानां मैत्रः करुण एव च।
निर्ममो निरहङ्कारः समदुःखसुखः क्षमी।।12.13।।
सन्तुष्टः सततं योगी यतात्मा दृढनिश्चयः।
मय्यर्पितमनोबुद्धिर्यो मद्भक्तः स मे प्रियः।।12.14।।
transliteration:
adveṣhṭā sarva-bhūtānāṁ maitraḥ karuṇa eva cha
nirmamo nirahaṅkāraḥ sama-duḥkha-sukhaḥ kṣhamī
santuṣhṭaḥ satataṁ yogī yatātmā dṛiḍha-niśhchayaḥ
mayy arpita-mano-buddhir yo mad-bhaktaḥ sa me priyaḥ
word_meanings: adveṣhṭā—nonenvious; sarva-bhūtānām—toward all living entities; maitraḥ—friendly; karuṇaḥ—compassionate; eva—certainly; cha—and; nirmamaḥ—with no sense of proprietorship; nirahaṅkāraḥ—without false ego; sama—equal; duḥkha—in distress; sukhaḥ—and happiness; kṣhamī—forgiving; santuṣhṭaḥ—satisfied; satatam—always; yogī—one engaged in devotion; yata-ātmā—self-controlled; dṛiḍha-niśhchayaḥ—with determination; mayi—upon Me; arpita—engaged; manaḥ—mind; buddhiḥ—and intelligence; yaḥ—one who; mat-bhaktaḥ—My devotee; saḥ—he; me—to Me; priyaḥ—dear
translation_english: One who is not envious but is a kind friend to all living entities, who does not think himself a proprietor and is free from false ego, who is equal in both happiness and distress, who is tolerant, always satisfied, self-controlled, and engaged in devotional service with determination, his mind and intelligence fixed on Me—such a devotee of Mine is very dear to Me.
deep_dive_text: The Anatomy of a Devotee. (Grouped to capture the continuous flow of traits). Krishna begins listing the 39 qualities that make someone truly 'dear' to Him. A true yogi isn't defined by their ability to levitate; they are defined by their emotional intelligence. They are 'Adveshta' (free from hatred), 'Maitrah' (friendly), and 'Nirmamo' (free from the toxic 'mine' mentality). True devotion softens the heart towards others while steeling the mind's determination towards the Divine.

original_script:
यस्मान्नोद्विजते लोको लोकान्नोद्विजते च यः।
हर्षामर्षभयोद्वेगैर्मुक्तो यः स च मे प्रियः।।12.15।।
transliteration:
yasmān nodvijate loko lokān nodvijate cha yaḥ
harṣhāmarṣha-bhayodvegair mukto yaḥ sa cha me priyaḥ
word_meanings: yasmāt—from whom; na—not; udvijate—are agitated; lokaḥ—people; lokāt—from people; na—not; udvijate—is agitated; cha—and; yaḥ—anyone who; harṣha—from gladness; amarṣha—anger; bhaya—fear; udvegaiḥ—and anxiety; muktaḥ—freed; yaḥ—who; saḥ—he; cha—also; me—to Me; priyaḥ—very dear
translation_english: He for whom no one is put into difficulty and who is not disturbed by anyone, who is equipoised in happiness and distress, fear and anxiety, is very dear to Me.
deep_dive_text: The Master of Emotional Boundaries. This verse is a masterpiece of social and emotional psychology. A perfect person has two defining relational traits: 1. They don't agitate or cause anxiety to anyone else. 2. They refuse to be agitated by the chaotic behavior of the world. Because they have mastered their own joy, anger, and fear ('Harsha-amarsha-bhaya'), they are a source of profound stability in a turbulent society.

original_script:
अनपेक्षः शुचिर्दक्ष उदासीनो गतव्यथः।
सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी यो मद्भक्तः स मे प्रियः।।12.16।।
transliteration:
anapekṣhaḥ śhuchir dakṣha udāsīno gata-vyathaḥ
sarvārambha-parityāgī yo mad-bhaktaḥ sa me priyaḥ
word_meanings: anapekṣhaḥ—neutral/independent; śhuchiḥ—pure; dakṣhaḥ—expert; udāsīnaḥ—free from care/impartial; gata-vyathaḥ—freed from all distress; sarva-ārambha—of all endeavors; parityāgī—renouncer; yaḥ—anyone who; mat-bhaktaḥ—My devotee; saḥ—he; me—to Me; priyaḥ—very dear
translation_english: My devotee who is not dependent on the ordinary course of activities, who is pure, expert, without cares, free from all pains, and not striving for some result, is very dear to Me.
deep_dive_text: The Effortless Expert. Spirituality isn't about being incompetent or passive in the world. Krishna says a devotee is 'Daksha' (expert/skillful). They do their job beautifully, but they remain 'Anapekshah' (independent of the outcome) and 'Udasino' (impartial). They act as a 'Sarvarambha-parityagi'—meaning they don't start massive ego-driven projects just to make a name for themselves. They flow with the Divine will, free from the exhaustion of personal ambition.

original_script:
यो न हृष्यति न द्वेष्टि न शोचति न काङ्क्षति।
शुभाशुभपरित्यागी भक्तिमान्यः स मे प्रियः।।12.17।।
transliteration:
yo na hṛiṣhyati na dveṣhṭi na śhochati na kāṅkṣhati
śhubhāśhubha-parityāgī bhaktimān yaḥ sa me priyaḥ
word_meanings: yaḥ—one who; na—never; hṛiṣhyati—takes pleasure; na—never; dveṣhṭi—grieves; na—never; śhochati—laments; na—never; kāṅkṣhati—desires; śhubha—of the auspicious; aśhubha—and the inauspicious; parityāgī—renouncer; bhakti-mān—devotee; yaḥ—one who; saḥ—he; me—to Me; priyaḥ—dear
translation_english: One who neither rejoices nor grieves, who neither laments nor desires, and who renounces both auspicious and inauspicious things—such a devotee is very dear to Me.
deep_dive_text: Beyond Good and Bad Luck. Human life is a pendulum swinging between rejoicing when things go our way and lamenting when they don't. The advanced yogi stops the pendulum. 'Shubha-ashubha-parityagi' is a radical concept: it means renouncing not just the 'bad' things, but also the attachment to 'good' (auspicious) things. When you realize that both good and bad luck are temporary illusions, you step off the emotional rollercoaster entirely.

original_script:
समः शत्रौ च मित्रे च तथा मानापमानयोः।
शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु समः सङ्गविवर्जितः।।12.18।।
तुल्यनिन्दास्तुतिर्मौनी सन्तुष्टो येन केनचित्।
अनिकेतः स्थिरमतिर्भक्तिमान्मे प्रियो नरः।।12.19।।
transliteration:
samaḥ śhatrau cha mitre cha tathā mānāpamānayoḥ
śhītoṣhṇa-sukha-duḥkheṣhu samaḥ saṅga-vivarjitaḥ
tulya-nindā-stutir maunī santuṣhṭo yena kenachit
aniketaḥ sthira-matir bhaktimān me priyo naraḥ
word_meanings: samaḥ—equal; śhatrau—to an enemy; cha—also; mitre—to a friend; cha—also; tathā—so; māna—in honor; apamānayoḥ—and dishonor; śhīta—in cold; uṣhṇa—heat; sukha—happiness; duḥkheṣhu—and distress; samaḥ—equipoised; saṅga-vivarjitaḥ—free from all association; tulya—equal; nindā—in defamation; stutiḥ—and repute; maunī—silent; santuṣhṭaḥ—satisfied; yena kenachit—with anything; aniketaḥ—having no residence; sthira—fixed; matiḥ—determination; bhakti-mān—engaged in devotion; me—to Me; priyaḥ—dear; naraḥ—a man
translation_english: One who is equal to friends and enemies, who is equipoised in honor and dishonor, heat and cold, happiness and distress, fame and infamy, who is always free from contaminating association, always silent and satisfied with anything, who doesn't care for any residence, who is fixed in knowledge and who is engaged in devotional service—such a person is very dear to Me.
deep_dive_text: The Unbreakable Mind. (Grouped to show the ultimate dualities). Krishna provides the final checklist of a liberated mind. Can you treat your enemy with the same internal peace as your best friend? Can you handle an insult exactly the way you handle a compliment ('Tulya ninda stutir')? A person who is 'Mauni' (inwardly silent) and 'Aniketa' (unattached to a specific house/status) cannot be broken or manipulated by the world. Their peace is entirely self-generated through their divine connection.

original_script:
ये तु धर्म्यामृतमिदं यथोक्तं पर्युपासते।
श्रद्दधाना मत्परमा भक्तास्तेऽतीव मे प्रियाः।।12.20।।
transliteration:
ye tu dharmyāmṛitam idaṁ yathoktaṁ paryupāsate
śhraddhadhānāh mat-paramā bhaktās te ’tīva me priyāḥ
word_meanings: ye—those who; tu—but; dharmya-amṛitam—this nectar of dharma; idam—this; yathā-uktam—as declared; paryupāsate—completely engage in; śhraddhadhānāḥ—with full faith; mat-paramāḥ—taking Me as the supreme goal; bhaktāḥ—devotees; te—they; atīva—exceedingly; me—to Me; priyāḥ—dear
translation_english: Those who honor this immortal nectar of dharma as declared here, full of faith, holding Me as their supreme goal—such devotees are exceedingly dear to Me.
deep_dive_text: The Climax of Bhakti. Krishna concludes Chapter 12 by summarizing everything He just taught as 'Dharmyamritam'—the immortal nectar of righteousness. The ultimate requirement isn't flawless execution, but 'Shraddhadhana' (deep faith) and keeping the Divine as the 'Mat-parama' (ultimate goal). Such people are not just accepted; they are 'Ativa me priyah' (exceedingly beloved).

original_script: भक्तियोगः
transliteration: Bhakti Yogaḥ
translation_english: Chapter 12: The Yoga of Devotion
deep_dive_text: Chapter 12 establishes that personal devotion (Bhakti) is superior and more practical for embodied beings than the path of the formless absolute. Krishna provides a compassionate, descending ladder of practices: from unbroken meditation, to regulated practice, to working for the Divine, and finally to simply renouncing the fruits of work. He concludes by detailing the 39 qualities of a true devotee—qualities rooted entirely in emotional intelligence, equanimity, and unconditional love.

original_script: अथ त्रयोदशोऽध्यायः - क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञविभागयोगः
transliteration: Kṣhetra-Kṣhetrajña Vibhāga Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Kṣhetra—the Field; Kṣhetrajña—the Knower of the Field; Vibhāga—Division/Discrimination; Yogaḥ—the path of synthesis.
translation_english: Chapter 13 Summary: The Yoga of Discriminating the Field and the Knower
deep_dive_text: Chapter 13 marks the beginning of the final third of the Gita, focusing on 'Jnana' (Knowledge). It utilizes classical Samkhya philosophy to categorize reality into the 'Kshetra' (the body/mind/material nature) and the 'Kshetrajna' (the observing soul). Historically, this chapter is crucial for defining the 'Twenty Attributes of Knowledge' (Verses 7-11), which are predominantly ethical and psychological rather than academic.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
प्रकृतिं पुरुषं चैव क्षेत्रं क्षेत्रज्ञमेव च।
एतद्वेदितुमिच्छामि ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं च केशव।।13.1।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
prakṛitiṁ puruṣhaṁ chaiva kṣhetraṁ kṣhetrajñam eva cha
etad veditum ichchhāmi jñānaṁ jñeyaṁ cha keśhava
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjuna said; prakṛitim—nature; puruṣham—the enjoyer/soul; cha—also; eva—indeed; kṣhetram—the field; kṣhetrajñam—the knower of the field; eva—certainly; cha—and; etat—all this; veditum—to understand; ichchhāmi—I wish; jñānam—knowledge; jñeyam—the object of knowledge; cha—also; keśhava—O Krishna
translation_english: Arjuna said: O Krishna, I wish to know about prakriti (nature), purusha (the enjoyer), and the field and the knower of the field, and of knowledge and the object of knowledge.
deep_dive_text: This chapter formalizes the 'Kshetra-Kshetrajna' (Field and Knower) distinction. It is the technical culmination of the text's epistemology, separating objective reality from subjective observation.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
इदं शरीरं कौन्तेय क्षेत्रमित्यभिधीयते।
एतद्यो वेत्ति तं प्राहुः क्षेत्रज्ञ इति तद्विदः।।13.2।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
idaṁ śharīraṁ kaunteya kṣhetram ity abhidhīyate
etad yo vetti taṁ prāhuḥ kṣhetrajña iti tad-vidaḥ
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; idam—this; śharīram—body; kaunteya—O son of Kunti; kṣhetram—the field; iti—thus; abhidhīyate—is called; etat—this; yaḥ—who; vetti—knows; tam—him; prāhuḥ—is called; kṣhetrajñaḥ—the knower of the field; iti—thus; tat-vidaḥ—by those who understand this
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: This body, O son of Kunti, is called the field, and one who knows this body is called the knower of the field.
deep_dive_text: The Farmer and the Field. Krishna introduces a brilliant metaphor. Your body (and mind) is a 'Kshetra'—a field. Just as a farmer plants seeds in a field and reaps a harvest, you plant karmic seeds in your body/mind and reap the experiences. But crucially, the farmer is *not* the field. You (the Kshetrajna) are the conscious observer who 'owns' the field but remains distinctly separate from it.

original_script:
क्षेत्रज्ञं चापि मां विद्धि सर्वक्षेत्रेषु भारत।
क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञयोर्ज्ञानं यत्तज्ज्ञानं मतं मम।।13.3।।
transliteration:
kṣhetrajñaṁ chāpi māṁ viddhi sarva-kṣhetreṣhu bhārata
kṣhetra-kṣhetrajñayor jñānaṁ yat taj jñānaṁ mataṁ mama
word_meanings: kṣhetra-jñam—the knower of the field; cha—also; api—indeed; mām—Me; viddhi—know; sarva-kṣhetreṣhu—in all fields; bhārata—O scion of Bharata; kṣhetra—the field of activities; kṣhetrajñayoḥ—and the knower of the field; jñānam—knowledge of; yat—which; tat—that; jñānam—knowledge; matam—opinion; mama—My
translation_english: O scion of Bharata, you should understand that I am also the knower in all bodies, and to understand this body and its knower is called knowledge. That is My opinion.
deep_dive_text: The Universal Observer. While you are the individual 'knower' of your specific body, Krishna reveals that He is the Supreme 'Knower' residing simultaneously in *all* bodies. True 'Knowledge' (Jnanam), according to Krishna, isn't astrophysics or economics; it is simply the ability to perfectly distinguish the unconscious machinery of the body from the conscious light of the soul.

original_script:
तत्क्षेत्रं यच्च यादृक्च यद्विकारि यतश्च यत्।
स च यो यत्प्रभावश्च तत्समासेन मे शृणु।।13.4।।
transliteration:
tat kṣhetraṁ yach cha yādṛik cha yad-vikāri yataśh cha yat
sa cha yo yat-prabhāvaśh cha tat samāsena me śhṛiṇu
word_meanings: tat—that; kṣhetram—field of activities; yat—what; cha—also; yādṛik—what it is like; cha—and; yat-vikāri—what its changes are; yataḥ—from where; cha—and; yat—what; saḥ—he; cha—also; yaḥ—who; yat-prabhāvaḥ—what his influence is; cha—and; tat—that; samāsena—in brief; me—from Me; śhṛiṇu—understand
translation_english: Now please hear in brief from Me what this field of activity is, what its nature is, what its changes are, whence it is produced, who that knower of the field of activities is, and what his influences are.
deep_dive_text: The Syllabus of the Chapter. Krishna lays out the table of contents for His upcoming explanation. He promises to demystify the exact ingredients of the human experience, showing how material nature interacts and who the silent boss behind the machinery really is.

original_script:
ऋषिभिर्बहुधा गीतं छन्दोभिर्विविधैः पृथक्।
ब्रह्मसूत्रपदैश्चैव हेतुमद्भिर्विनिश्चितैः।।13.5।।
transliteration:
ṛiṣhibhir bahudhā gītaṁ chhandobhir vividhaiḥ pṛithak
brahma-sūtra-padaiśh chaiva hetumadbhir viniśhchitaiḥ
word_meanings: ṛiṣhibhiḥ—by the wise sages; bahudhā—in many ways; gītam—described; chhandobhiḥ—by Vedic hymns; vividhaiḥ—various; pṛithak—variously; brahma-sūtra—of the Vedanta; padaiḥ—by the aphorisms; cha—also; eva—certainly; hetu-madbhiḥ—with cause and effect; viniśhchitaiḥ—with conclusive logic
translation_english: That knowledge of the field of activities and of the knower of activities is described by various sages in various Vedic writings. It is especially presented in Vedanta-sutra with all logic as to cause and effect.
deep_dive_text: The Validation of Logic. Krishna points out that what He is teaching isn't an arbitrary dogma; it is perfectly aligned with the deepest analytical texts of ancient India, like the Brahma Sutras. It is 'Hetumadbhir'—backed by solid reasoning, logic, and the undeniable laws of cause and effect.

original_script:
महाभूतान्यहङ्कारो बुद्धिरव्यक्तमेव च।
इन्द्रियाणि दशैकं च पञ्च चेन्द्रियगोचराः।।13.6।।
इच्छा द्वेषः सुखं दुःखं सङ्घातश्चेतना धृतिः।
एतत्क्षेत्रं समासेन सविकारमुदाहृतम्।।13.7।।
transliteration:
mahā-bhūtāny ahaṅkāro buddhir avyaktam eva cha
indriyāṇi daśhaikaṁ cha pañcha chendriya-gocharāḥ
ichchhā dveṣhaḥ sukhaṁ duḥkhaṁ saṅghātaśh chetanā dhṛitiḥ
etat kṣhetraṁ samāsena sa-vikāram udāhṛitam
word_meanings: mahā-bhūtāni—the great elements; ahaṅkāraḥ—false ego; buddhiḥ—intelligence; avyaktam—the unmanifested; eva—certainly; cha—also; indriyāṇi—senses; daśha ekam—eleven; cha—and; pañcha—five; cha—and; indriya-gocharāḥ—objects of the senses; ichchhā—desire; dveṣhaḥ—hatred; sukham—happiness; duḥkham—distress; saṅghātaḥ—the aggregate; chetanā—life symptoms; dhṛitiḥ—conviction; etat—all this; kṣhetram—the field of activities; samāsena—in summary; sa-vikāram—with interactions; udāhṛitam—exemplified
translation_english: The five great elements, false ego, intelligence, the unmanifested, the ten senses and the mind, the five sense objects, desire, hatred, happiness, distress, the aggregate, the life symptoms, and convictions—all these are considered, in summary, to be the field of activities and its interactions.
deep_dive_text: Deconstructing the Matrix. (Grouped to show the complete picture). Krishna provides a stunningly thorough breakdown of the 'Field' (Kshetra). It includes the 24 elements of Sankhya philosophy (matter, senses, mind). Most importantly, He lists emotions like 'Desire, Hatred, Happiness, and Distress' as part of the FIELD, not the Soul. Your feelings are just chemical weather patterns in the field; the real YOU is the one silently watching the weather.

original_script:
अमानित्वमदम्भित्वमहिंसा क्षान्तिरार्जवम्।
आचार्योपासनं शौचं स्थैर्यमात्मविनिग्रहः।।13.8।।
transliteration:
amānitvam adambhitvam ahinsā kṣhāntir ārjavam
āchāryopāsanaṁ śhauchaṁ sthairyam ātma-vinigrahaḥ
word_meanings: amānitvam—humility; adambhitvam—pridelessness; ahinsā—nonviolence; kṣhāntiḥ—tolerance; ārjavam—simplicity; āchārya-upāsanam—approaching a bona fide spiritual master; śhaucham—cleanliness; sthairyam—steadiness; ātma-vinigrahaḥ—self-control
translation_english: Humility, pridelessness, nonviolence, tolerance, simplicity, approaching a bona fide spiritual master, cleanliness, steadiness, self-control...
deep_dive_text: The Attributes of True Knowledge (Part 1). Having described the 'Field,' Krishna now describes 'Knowledge.' To Krishna, knowledge is not information; it is character. If you read a thousand books but lack 'Amanitvam' (humility) or 'Ahinsa' (nonviolence), you are actually in ignorance. This verse begins the famous 20 qualities that indicate a person is truly enlightened.

original_script:
असक्तिरनभिष्वङ्गः पुत्रदारगृहादिषु।
नित्यं च समचित्तत्वमिष्टानिष्टोपपत्तिषु।।13.9।।
transliteration:
asaktir anabhiṣhvaṅgaḥ putra-dāra-gṛihādiṣhu
nityaṁ cha sama-chittatvam iṣhṭāniṣhṭopapattiṣhu
word_meanings: asaktiḥ—detachment; anabhiṣhvaṅgaḥ—freedom from entanglement; putra—children; dāra—wife; gṛiha-ādiṣhu—home, etc.; nityam—constant; cha—and; sama-chittatvam—equanimity; iṣhṭa—desirable; aniṣhṭa—undesirable; upapattiṣhu—upon obtaining
translation_english: Detachment, freedom from entanglement with children, wife, home and the rest, and constant equanimity amid pleasant and unpleasant events...
deep_dive_text: The Attributes of True Knowledge (Part 2). Krishna continues listing the traits of an enlightened mind. 'Asakti' (detachment) regarding family doesn't mean coldness or abandonment; it means loving without toxic enmeshment or the illusion of ownership. When you stop deriving your entire identity from your family or home, you develop 'Sama-chittatvam' (equanimity)—an even mind that doesn't shatter when life inevitably delivers both the pleasant and the unpleasant.

original_script:
मयि चानन्ययोगेन भक्तिरव्यभिचारिणी।
विविक्तदेशसेवित्वमरतिर्जनसंसदि।।13.10।।
transliteration:
mayi chānanya-yogena bhaktir avyabhichāriṇī
vivikta-deśha-sevitvam aratir jana-sansadi
word_meanings: mayi—unto Me; cha—and; ananya-yogena—by unalloyed devotion; bhaktiḥ—devotion; avyabhichāriṇī—constant; vivikta—solitary; deśha—places; sevitvam—resorting to; aratiḥ—detachment; jana-sansadi—from the masses of people
translation_english: Constant and unalloyed devotion to Me, resorting to solitary places, and detachment from the general masses of people...
deep_dive_text: The Attributes of True Knowledge (Part 3). True knowledge requires the oxygen of 'Avyabhicharini Bhakti'—unwavering, deeply loyal love for the Divine. An enlightened person naturally seeks 'Vivikta-desha' (solitary, quiet places) and feels 'Arati' (distaste or detachment) toward the noisy, superficial gossip of the general masses ('Jana-sansadi'). Solitude is not loneliness for the yogi; it is the laboratory where true connection happens.

original_script:
अध्यात्मज्ञाननित्यत्वं तत्त्वज्ञानार्थदर्शनम्।
एतज्ज्ञानमिति प्रोक्तमज्ञानं यदतोऽन्यथा।।13.11।।
transliteration:
adhyātma-jñāna-nityatvaṁ tattva-jñānārtha-darśhanam
etaj jñānam iti proktam ajñānaṁ yad ato ’nyathā
word_meanings: adhyātma—spiritual; jñāna—knowledge; nityatvam—constancy; tattva-jñāna—knowledge of the truth; artha—for the object; darśhanam—philosophy; etat—all this; jñānam—knowledge; iti—thus; proktam—declared; ajñānam—ignorance; yat—which; ataḥ—from this; anyathā—otherwise
translation_english: Constancy in the knowledge of the Spirit, and insight into the goal of the knowledge of Truth—this is declared to be knowledge, and all that is contrary to it is ignorance.
deep_dive_text: The Final Verdict on Knowledge. Krishna concludes the 20 attributes of true knowledge. It culminates in 'Adhyatma-jnana' (spiritual awareness) and a philosophical pursuit of the ultimate truth. The punchline is sharp: 'Ajnanam yad ato anyatha'—anything other than these 20 traits is pure ignorance. In the Gita's framework, you can hold five PhDs and rule the world, but if you lack self-control, humility, and spiritual focus, you are operating in deep ignorance.

original_script:
ज्ञेयं यत्तत्प्रवक्ष्यामि यज्ज्ञात्वामृतमश्नुते।
अनादिमत्परं ब्रह्म न सत्तन्नासदुच्यते।।13.12।।
transliteration:
jñeyaṁ yat tat pravakṣhyāmi yaj jñātvāmṛitam aśhnute
anādimat paraṁ brahma na sat tan nāsad uchyate
word_meanings: jñeyam—the knowable; yat—which; tat—that; pravakṣhyāmi—I shall now explain; yat—which; jñātvā—knowing; amṛitam—nectar of immortality; aśhnute—tastes; anādi-mat—without beginning; param—supreme; brahma—Brahman; na—not; sat—cause; tat—that; na—nor; asat—effect; uchyate—is said to be
translation_english: I shall now explain the knowable, knowing which you will taste the eternal. Brahman, the spirit, beginningless and subordinate to Me, lies beyond the cause and effect of this material world.
deep_dive_text: Introducing the Object of Knowledge. Having defined the 'Field' (the body) and 'Knowledge' (the process), Krishna introduces the 'Jneyam' (the ultimate object to be known). By understanding this, a person 'Ashnute Amritam' (tastes immortality). This Supreme Reality (Brahman) breaks human logic—it is neither 'Sat' (physical existence/cause) nor 'Asat' (non-existence/effect). It is the absolute reality that sits completely outside the material matrix.

original_script:
सर्वतः पाणिपादं तत्सर्वतोऽक्षिशिरोमुखम्।
सर्वतः श्रुतिमल्लोके सर्वमावृत्य तिष्ठति।।13.13।।
transliteration:
sarvataḥ pāṇi-pādaṁ tat sarvato ’kṣhi-śhiro-mukham
sarvataḥ śhrutimal loke sarvam āvṛitya tiṣhṭhati
word_meanings: sarvataḥ—everywhere; pāṇi—hands; pādam—legs; tat—that; sarvataḥ—everywhere; akṣhi—eyes; śhiraḥ—heads; mukham—faces; sarvataḥ—everywhere; śhruti-mat—having ears; loke—in the world; sarvam—everything; āvṛitya—pervading; tiṣhṭhati—exists
translation_english: Everywhere are His hands and legs, His eyes, heads and faces, and He has ears everywhere. In this way the Supersoul exists, pervading everything.
deep_dive_text: The Omnipresent Observer. Krishna uses poetic imagery to describe the formless 'Jneyam' (Supersoul). Because the Divine consciousness animates every living being, every hand that reaches, every eye that sees, and every ear that listens is fundamentally the Divine interacting with the material world. God is not watching from a distant cloud; He is the underlying observer inside the hardware of every living entity, fully pervading the cosmos.

original_script:
सर्वेन्द्रियगुणाभासं सर्वेन्द्रियविवर्जितम्।
असक्तं सर्वभृच्चैव निर्गुणं गुणभोक्तृ च।।13.14।।
transliteration:
sarvendriya-guṇābhāsaṁ sarvendriya-vivarjitam
asaktaṁ sarva-bhṛich chaiva nirguṇaṁ guṇa-bhoktṛi cha
word_meanings: sarva—all; indriya—senses; guṇa—qualities; ābhāsam—the original source; sarva—all; indriya—senses; vivarjitam—without; asaktam—without attachment; sarva-bhṛit—the maintainer of everyone; cha—and; eva—certainly; nirguṇam—without material qualities; guṇa-bhoktṛi—the master of the gunas; cha—also
translation_english: The Supersoul is the original source of all senses, yet He is without senses. He is unattached, although He is the maintainer of all living beings. He transcends the modes of nature, and at the same time He is the master of all modes of material nature.
deep_dive_text: The Ultimate Paradox. Human logic struggles with God because it relies on binaries. Krishna explains that the Divine is the source of all your senses (sight, touch, taste), yet the Divine does not have physical sense organs ('Indriya-vivarjitam'). He sustains everything, but remains entirely 'Asaktam' (detached). He is 'Nirguna' (free from the mechanical traits of nature), yet He is the ultimate experiencer and master of those very traits.

original_script:
बहिरन्तश्च भूतानामचरं चरमेव च।
सूक्ष्मत्वात्तदविज्ञेयं दूरस्थं चान्तिके च तत्।।13.15।।
transliteration:
bahir antaśh cha bhūtānām acharaṁ charam eva cha
sūkṣhmatvāt tad avijñeyaṁ dūra-sthaṁ chāntike cha tat
word_meanings: bahiḥ—outside; antaḥ—inside; cha—also; bhūtānām—of all living entities; acharam—not moving; charam—moving; eva—also; cha—and; sūkṣhmatvāt—on account of being subtle; tat—that; avijñeyam—unknowable; dūra-stham—far away; cha—and; antike—near; cha—and; tat—that
translation_english: The Supreme Truth exists outside and inside of all living beings, the moving and the nonmoving. Because He is subtle, He is beyond the power of the material senses to see or to know. Although far, far away, He is also near to all.
deep_dive_text: Intimate Yet Inconceivable. How close is God? 'Antike' (extremely near)—He is the very consciousness looking through your eyes. How far is God? 'Dura-stham' (far away)—He stretches beyond the limits of the expanding universe. Because this reality is 'Sukshmatvat' (infinitely subtle), it slips right through the clumsy, blunt instruments of our material senses and scientific tools, remaining entirely 'Avijneyam' (unknowable to the physical mind).

original_script:
अविभक्तं च भूतेषु विभक्तमिव च स्थितम्।
भूतभर्तृ च तज्ज्ञेयं ग्रसिष्णु प्रभविष्णु च।।13.16।।
transliteration:
avibhaktaṁ cha bhūteṣhu vibhaktam iva cha sthitam
bhūta-bhartṛi cha taj jñeyaṁ grasiṣhṇu prabhaviṣhṇu cha
word_meanings: avibhaktam—without division; cha—also; bhūteṣhu—in all living beings; vibhaktam—divided; iva—as if; cha—also; sthitam—situated; bhūta-bhartṛi—the maintainer of all living entities; cha—also; tat—that; jñeyam—to be understood; grasiṣhṇu—devouring; prabhaviṣhṇu—developing; cha—also
translation_english: Although the Supersoul appears to be divided among all beings, He is never divided. He is situated as one. Although He is the maintainer of every living entity, it is to be understood that He devours and develops all.
deep_dive_text: The Illusion of Division. If you fill a thousand jars with water and look at the reflection of the moon in them, it looks like there are a thousand moons. That is 'Vibhaktam iva' (appearing divided). But there is only one moon ('Avibhaktam'). Similarly, the one divine consciousness appears fractured across billions of individual bodies, but the underlying Spirit is perfectly unified. He is the single force that generates (Prabhavishnu), sustains (Bhartri), and eventually dissolves (Grasishnu) every form.

original_script:
ज्योतिषामपि तज्ज्योतिस्तमसः परमुच्यते।
ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं ज्ञानगम्यं हृदि सर्वस्य विष्ठितम्।।13.17।।
transliteration:
jyotiṣhām api taj jyotis tamasaḥ param uchyate
jñānaṁ jñeyaṁ jñāna-gamyaṁ hṛidi sarvasya viṣhṭhitam
word_meanings: jyotiṣhām—in all luminous objects; api—also; tat—that; jyotiḥ—the source of light; tamasaḥ—from the darkness; param—beyond; uchyate—is said; jñānam—knowledge; jñeyam—to be known; jñāna-gamyam—the goal of knowledge; hṛidi—in the heart; sarvasya—of everyone; viṣhṭhitam—situated
translation_english: He is the source of light in all luminous objects. He is beyond the darkness of matter and is unmanifested. He is knowledge, He is the object of knowledge, and He is the goal of knowledge. He is situated in everyone's heart.
deep_dive_text: The Light of All Lights. A profoundly poetic and famous verse. The sun, the stars, and the fire only have the power to illuminate because they borrow their radiance from 'Jyotisham api taj jyotis' (The Light of all lights). This Divine presence is completely untouched by material darkness. Best of all, you don't need to travel to the edge of the universe to find it—it is perfectly seated 'Hridi sarvasya' (in the heart of every single being).

original_script:
इति क्षेत्रं तथा ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं चोक्तं समासतः।
मद्भक्त एतद्विज्ञाय मद्भावायोपपद्यते।।13.18।।
transliteration:
iti kṣhetraṁ tathā jñānaṁ jñeyaṁ choktaṁ samāsataḥ
mad-bhakta etad vijñāya mad-bhāvāyopapadyate
word_meanings: iti—thus; kṣhetram—the field of activities (the body); tathā—also; jñānam—knowledge; jñeyam—the knowable; cha—also; uktam—described; samāsataḥ—in summary; mat-bhaktaḥ—My devotee; etat—all this; vijñāya—after understanding; mat-bhāvāya—My nature; upapadyate—attains
translation_english: Thus the field of activities [the body], knowledge, and the knowable have been summarily described by Me. Only My devotees can understand this thoroughly and thus attain to My nature.
deep_dive_text: The Triad Summarized. Krishna closes this specific philosophical section. He has neatly categorized the entirety of existence into three boxes: The Field (matter/the body), Knowledge (the character and process required to see truth), and the Knowable (the Supreme Spirit). But He adds a crucial caveat: intense philosophical analysis isn't enough. Only a 'Mad-bhakta' (a devotee filled with love) can fully digest this deep philosophy and actually 'attain His nature' (Mad-bhavaya). Intellect must be paired with devotion.

original_script:
प्रकृतिं पुरुषं चैव विद्ध्यनादी उभावपि।
विकारांश्च गुणांश्चैव विद्धि प्रकृतिसम्भवान्।।13.19।।
transliteration:
prakṛitiṁ puruṣhaṁ chaiva viddhy anādī ubhāv api
vikārānśh cha guṇānśh chaiva viddhi prakṛiti-sambhavān
word_meanings: prakṛitim—material nature; puruṣham—the living entity; cha—and; eva—certainly; viddhi—know; anādī—beginningless; ubhau—both; api—also; vikārān—transformations; cha—and; guṇān—the three modes of nature; cha—and; eva—certainly; viddhi—know; prakṛiti—material nature; sambhavān—produced of
translation_english: Material nature and the living entity should be understood to be beginningless. Their transformations and the modes of matter are products of material nature.
deep_dive_text: The Co-Eternal Realities. Krishna introduces a profound truth about time: both 'Prakriti' (material nature) and 'Purusha' (the observing soul) are 'Anadi' (without a beginning). They have always existed as energies of the Supreme. However, all the drama of life—your changing body, aging, the shifting modes of goodness, passion, and ignorance (the Vikaras and Gunas)—are entirely generated by Prakriti. The soul itself never ages or changes; it simply experiences the changing weather of nature.

original_script:
कार्यकारणकर्तृत्वे हेतुः प्रकृतिरुच्यते।
पुरुषः सुखदुःखानां भोक्तृत्वे हेतुरुच्यते।।13.20।।
transliteration:
kārya-kāraṇa-kartṛitve hetuḥ prakṛitir uchyate
puruṣhaḥ sukha-duḥkhānāṁ bhoktṛitve hetur uchyate
word_meanings: kārya—of effect; kāraṇa—and cause; kartṛitve—in the matter of creation; hetuḥ—the instrument; prakṛitiḥ—material nature; uchyate—is said to be; puruṣhaḥ—the living entity; sukha—of happiness; duḥkhānām—and distress; bhoktṛitve—in enjoying; hetuḥ—the instrument; uchyate—is said to be
translation_english: Nature is said to be the cause of all material causes and effects, whereas the living entity is the cause of the various sufferings and enjoyments in this world.
deep_dive_text: The Division of Labor. Krishna perfectly separates the machinery from the passenger. 'Prakriti' (Nature) is responsible for the 'Karya' (the physical effect) and the 'Karana' (the physical cause). It builds the body, beats the heart, and runs the cosmos. But the 'Purusha' (the soul) is responsible for the 'Bhoktritve'—the subjective experiencing of happiness and distress. Nature provides the rollercoaster; the soul is the one screaming or laughing in the seat.

original_script:
पुरुषः प्रकृतिस्थो हि भुङ्क्ते प्रकृतिजान्गुणान्।
कारणं गुणसङ्गोऽस्य सदसद्योनिजन्मसु।।13.21।।
transliteration:
puruṣhaḥ prakṛiti-stho hi bhuṅkte prakṛiti-jān guṇān
kāraṇaṁ guṇa-saṅgo ’sya sad-asad-yoni-janmasu
word_meanings: puruṣhaḥ—the living entity; prakṛiti-sthaḥ—being situated in the material energy; hi—certainly; bhuṅkte—enjoys; prakṛiti-jān—produced by the material nature; guṇān—the modes of nature; kāraṇam—the cause; guṇa-saṅgaḥ—the association with the modes of nature; asya—of the living entity; sat-asat—in good and bad; yoni—species of life; janmasu—in births
translation_english: The living entity in material nature thus follows the ways of life, enjoying the three modes of nature. This is due to his association with that material nature. Thus he meets with good and evil among various species.
deep_dive_text: The Trap of Identification. Why does an eternal, blissful soul suffer? Because it is 'Prakriti-stho' (situated in material nature) and becomes deeply attached ('Guna-sango') to the mechanical modes of that nature. When you strap on a virtual reality headset and forget it's a game, you genuinely feel the terror of falling. Because the soul identifies with the temporary body's desires, it gets pulled into 'Sad-asad-yoni-janmasu'—the endless cycle of birth in good and bad species to fulfill those material desires.

original_script:
उपद्रष्टानुमन्ता च भर्ता भोक्ता महेश्वरः।
परमात्मेति चाप्युक्तो देहेऽस्मिन्पुरुषः परः।।13.22।।
transliteration:
upadraṣhṭānumantā cha bhartā bhoktā maheśhvaraḥ
paramātmeti chāpy ukto dehe ’smin puruṣhaḥ paraḥ
word_meanings: upadraṣhṭā—overseer; anumantā—permitter; cha—also; bhartā—master; bhoktā—supreme enjoyer; mahā-īśhvaraḥ—the Supreme Lord; parama-ātmā—the Supersoul; iti—also; cha—and; api—indeed; uktaḥ—is said; dehe—in the body; asmin—this; puruṣhaḥ—enjoyer; paraḥ—transcendental
translation_english: Yet in this body there is another, a transcendental enjoyer, who is the Lord, the supreme proprietor, who exists as the overseer and permitter, and who is known as the Supersoul.
deep_dive_text: The Silent Passenger. This is a breakthrough revelation. Inside your body, there is not just one soul, but two. There is you (the individual soul, stressed and trying to control everything), and then there is 'Paramatma' (the Supersoul, the expansion of God). What is God doing inside you? He is the 'Upadrashta' (the silent witness) and the 'Anumanta' (the permitter). He doesn't force your choices; He simply observes your desires and sanctions the material energy to fulfill them.

original_script:
य एवं वेत्ति पुरुषं प्रकृतिं च गुणैः सह।
सर्वथा वर्तमानोऽपि न स भूयोऽभिजायते।।13.23।।
transliteration:
ya evaṁ vetti puruṣhaṁ prakṛitiṁ cha guṇaiḥ saha
sarvathā vartamāno ’pi na sa bhūyo ’bhijāyate
word_meanings: yaḥ—anyone who; evam—thus; vetti—understands; puruṣham—the living entity; prakṛitim—material nature; cha—and; guṇaiḥ—the modes of material nature; saha—with; sarvathā—in all ways; vartamānaḥ—being situated; api—in spite of; na—never; saḥ—he; bhūyaḥ—again; abhijāyate—takes his birth
translation_english: One who understands this philosophy concerning material nature, the living entity and the interaction of the modes of nature is sure to attain liberation. He will not take birth here again, regardless of his present position.
deep_dive_text: The Escape Velocity of Knowledge. Krishna provides the ultimate guarantee of this specific knowledge. If you can truly 'Vetti' (understand and realize) the separation between your eternal self (Purusha), the mechanics of nature (Prakriti), and the modes (Gunas) that influence you, you break the cycle. 'Sarvatha vartamano api'—no matter what your current situation, past mistakes, or present occupation is, this realization guarantees you will 'na bhuyo abhijayate' (never be forced to take birth in this material trap again).

original_script:
ध्यानेनात्मनि पश्यन्ति केचिदात्मानमात्मना।
अन्ये साङ्ख्येन योगेन कर्मयोगेन चापरे।।13.24।।
transliteration:
dhyānenātmani paśhyanti kechid ātmānam ātmanā
anye sāṅkhyena yogena karma-yogena chāpare
word_meanings: dhyānena—by meditation; ātmani—within the self; paśhyanti—see; kechit—some; ātmānam—the Supersoul; ātmanā—by the mind; anye—others; sāṅkhyena—of philosophical discussion; yogena—by the yoga system; karma-yogena—by activities without fruitive desire; cha—also; apare—others
translation_english: Some perceive the Supersoul within themselves through meditation, others through the cultivation of knowledge, and still others through working without fruitive desires.
deep_dive_text: The Multiple Gates to the Same Truth. Krishna acknowledges that people have different psychological makeups, so there are multiple valid paths to 'see' the Divine within. Some use 'Dhyana' (deep meditation/Raja Yoga) to quiet the mind and spot the Supersoul. Others use 'Sankhya' (deep philosophical analysis/Jnana Yoga) to logically separate truth from illusion. Still others use 'Karma Yoga' (selfless action) to purify their hearts until the truth naturally reflects in it.

original_script:
अन्ये त्वेवमजानन्तः श्रुत्वान्येभ्य उपासते।
तेऽपि चातितरन्त्येव मृत्युं श्रुतिपरायणाः।।13.25।।
transliteration:
anye tv evam ajānantaḥ śhrutvānyebhya upāsate
te ’pi chātitaranty eva mṛityuṁ śhruti-parāyaṇāḥ
word_meanings: anye—others; tu—but; evam—thus; ajānantaḥ—without spiritual knowledge; śhrutvā—by hearing; anyebhyaḥ—from others; upāsate—begin to worship; te—they; api—also; cha—and; atitaranti—transcend; eva—certainly; mṛityum—the path of death; śhruti-parāyaṇāḥ—inclined to the process of hearing
translation_english: Again there are those who, although not conversant in spiritual knowledge, begin to worship the Supreme Person upon hearing about Him from others. Because of their tendency to hear from authorities, they also transcend the path of birth and death.
deep_dive_text: The Grace for the Uneducated. What if you aren't a great meditator, a brilliant philosopher, or a tireless karma yogi? Krishna offers a path for absolutely everyone: 'Shruti-parayanah' (the yoga of hearing). Even if someone lacks formal spiritual education ('Ajanantah'), if they simply listen to the truth from authentic sources with an open heart and begin to worship, that act of humble listening is powerful enough to carry them across the ocean of death.

original_script:
यावत्सञ्जायते किञ्चित्सत्त्वं स्थावरजङ्गमम्।
क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञसंयोगात्तद्विद्धि भरतर्षभ।।13.26।।
transliteration:
yāvat sañjāyate kiñchit sattvaṁ sthāvara-jaṅgamam
kṣhetra-kṣhetrajña-sanyogāt tad viddhi bharatarṣhabha
word_meanings: yāvat—whatever; sañjāyate—comes into being; kiñchit—anything; sattvam—existence; sthāvara—not moving; jaṅgamam—moving; kṣhetra—of the body; kṣhetrajña—and the knower of the body; sanyogāt—by the union between; tat viddhi—you must know it; bharata-ṛiṣhabha—O chief of the Bharatas
translation_english: O chief of the Bharatas, know that whatever you see in existence, both the moving and the nonmoving, is only a combination of the field of activities and the knower of the field.
deep_dive_text: The Universal Formula of Life. Krishna sums up the entirety of biology, physics, and consciousness in one equation. Wherever you see life—whether it's a 'Sthavara' (a stationary tree or mountain) or a 'Jangamam' (a moving animal or human)—it is exclusively the result of 'Sanyogat' (the union) between the Field (matter) and the Knower (spirit). Matter alone is dead; Spirit alone is formless. Their combination is the grand theater of the universe.

original_script:
समं सर्वेषु भूतेषु तिष्ठन्तं परमेश्वरम्।
विनश्यत्स्वविनश्यन्तं यः पश्यति स पश्यति।।13.27।।
transliteration:
samaṁ sarveṣhu bhūteṣhu tiṣhṭhantaṁ parameśhvaram
vinaśhyatsv avinaśhyantaṁ yaḥ paśhyati sa paśhyati
word_meanings: samam—equally; sarveṣhu—in all; bhūteṣhu—living entities; tiṣhṭhantam—residing; parama-īśhvaram—the Supersoul; vinaśhyatsu—in the destructible; avinaśhyantam—not destroyed; yaḥ—anyone who; paśhyati—sees; saḥ—he; paśhyati—actually sees
translation_english: One who sees the Supersoul accompanying the individual soul in all bodies, and who understands that neither the soul nor the Supersoul within the destructible body is ever destroyed, actually sees.
deep_dive_text: The Definition of True Vision. We all look at the world, but Krishna says very few actually 'see' it. 'Yah pashyati sa pashyati' means 'He who sees this, truly sees.' What is the true vision? It is the ability to look at a fragile, dying, 'destructible' physical body, and clearly perceive the 'indestructible' Supreme Lord (Parameshvaram) residing perfectly equally ('Samam') within it. True sight is seeing the eternal hidden inside the temporary.

original_script:
समं पश्यन्हि सर्वत्र समवस्थितमीश्वरम्।
न हिनस्त्यात्मनात्मानं ततो याति परां गतिम्।।13.28।।
transliteration:
samaṁ paśhyan hi sarvatra samavasthitam īśhvaram
na hinasty ātmanātmānaṁ tato yāti parāṁ gatim
word_meanings: samam—equally; paśhyan—seeing; hi—certainly; sarvatra—everywhere; samavasthitam—equally situated; īśhvaram—the Supersoul; na—does not; hinasti—degrade; ātmanā—by the mind; ātmānam—the soul; tataḥ—then; yāti—reaches; parām—the transcendental; gatim—destination
translation_english: One who sees the Supersoul equally present everywhere, in every living being, does not degrade himself by his mind. Thus he approaches the transcendental destination.
deep_dive_text: The Antidote to Self-Destruction. When you look down on others, cheat, or act out of toxic ego, Krishna says you are committing violence against yourself ('hinasty atmana atmanam' - degrading the soul by the mind). However, when your vision shifts and you see the exact same Divine presence ('Ishvaram') situated equally ('Samam') in the saint, the sinner, the rich, and the poor, it becomes impossible to hate or exploit anyone. This spiritual egalitarianism naturally elevates you to the highest destination.

original_script:
प्रकृत्यैव च कर्माणि क्रियमाणानि सर्वशः।
यः पश्यति तथात्मानमकर्तारं स पश्यति।।13.29।।
transliteration:
prakṛityaiva cha karmāṇi kriyamāṇāni sarvaśhaḥ
yaḥ paśhyati tathātmānam akartāraṁ sa paśhyati
word_meanings: prakṛityā—by material nature; eva—certainly; cha—also; karmāṇi—activities; kriyamāṇāni—are performed; sarvaśhaḥ—in all respects; yaḥ—anyone who; paśhyati—sees; tathā—also; ātmānam—the self; akartāram—the non-doer; saḥ—he; paśhyati—sees perfectly
translation_english: One who can see that all activities are performed by the body, which is created of material nature, and sees that the self does nothing, actually sees.
deep_dive_text: The Ultimate Delegation. Here Krishna reiterates that the true Self (the soul) is a pure observer. All work, movement, and emotional turbulence are products of 'Prakriti' (Nature). To understand that 'I am not doing this, my biological and psychological programming is doing this' is the zenith of spiritual vision. The soul is merely the passenger.

original_script:
यदा भूतपृथग्भावमेकस्थमनुपश्यति।
तत एव च विस्तारं ब्रह्म सम्पद्यते तदा।।13.30।।
transliteration:
yadā bhūta-pṛithag-bhāvam eka-stham anupaśhyati
tata eva cha vistāraṁ brahma sampadyate tadā
word_meanings: yadā—when; bhūta—of living entities; pṛithak-bhāvam—diverse identities; eka-stham—situated in one; anupaśhyati—sees; tataḥ—thereafter; eva—certainly; cha—also; vistāram—the expansion; brahma—the Absolute; sampadyate—attains; tadā—at that time
translation_english: When a sensible man ceases to see different identities due to different material bodies and he sees how beings are expanded everywhere, he attains to the Brahman conception.
deep_dive_text: Seeing the Forest, Not Just the Trees. Instead of seeing millions of separate, competing entities, the wise person sees one single spiritual energy (Eka-stham) that has diversified into countless forms (Vistāram). This realization dissolves all prejudice and separation, elevating the mind to the state of Brahman (universal consciousness).

original_script:
अनादित्वान्निर्गुणत्वात्परमात्मायमव्ययः।
शरीरस्थोऽपि कौन्तेय न करोति न लिप्यते।।13.31।।
transliteration:
anāditvān nirguṇatvāt paramātmāyam avyayaḥ
śharīra-stho ’pi kaunteya na karoti na lipyate
word_meanings: anāditvāt—due to eternity; nirguṇatvāt—due to being transcendental; parama-ātmā—the Supreme Soul; ayam—this; avyayaḥ—imperishable; śharīra-sthaḥ—dwelling in the body; api—though; kaunteya—O son of Kunti; na karoti—never does anything; na lipyate—nor is entangled
translation_english: Those with the vision of eternity can see that the imperishable soul is transcendental, eternal, and beyond the modes of nature. Despite contact with the material body, O Arjuna, the soul neither does anything nor is entangled.
deep_dive_text: The Teflon Soul. How does the soul stay pure in a dirty world? Because it is 'Anadi' (beginningless) and 'Nirguna' (without material qualities). Just as Teflon prevents food from sticking to a pan, the soul's transcendental nature ensures it never actually gets entangled (Na lipyate) by the actions or sins of the body.

original_script:
यथा सर्वगतं सौक्ष्म्यादाकाशं नोपलिप्यते।
सर्वत्रावस्थितो देहे तथात्मा नोपलिप्यते।।13.32।।
transliteration:
yathā sarva-gataṁ saukṣhmyād ākāśhaṁ nopalipyate
sarvatrāvasthito dehe tathātmā nopalipyate
word_meanings: yathā—as; sarva-gatam—all-pervading; saukṣhmyāt—due to being subtle; ākāśham—the sky; na—never; upalipyate—mixes; sarvatra—everywhere; avasthitaḥ—situated; dehe—in the body; tathā—so; ātmā—the self; na—never; upalipyate—mixes
translation_english: The sky, due to its subtle nature, does not mix with anything, although it is all-pervading. Similarly, the soul situated in Brahman vision does not mix with the body, though situated in that body.
deep_dive_text: The Sky Analogy. Space (Akasha) holds everything—clouds, smoke, rain, fire—but it is never stained by any of them. Similarly, the soul holds the entire experience of life within the body, but because it is so subtle (Saukshmyad), it is never contaminated by the body's diseases, desires, or ultimate death.

original_script:
यथा प्रकाशयत्येकः कृत्स्नं लोकमिमं रविः।
क्षेत्रं क्षेत्री तथा कृत्स्नं प्रकाशयति भारत।।13.33।।
transliteration:
yathā prakāśhayaty ekaḥ kṛitsnaṁ lokam imaṁ raviḥ
kṣhetraṁ kṣhetrī tathā kṛitsnaṁ prakāśhayati bhārata
word_meanings: yathā—as; prakāśhayati—illuminates; ekaḥ—one; kṛitsnam—the whole; lokam—universe; imam—this; raviḥ—sun; kṣhetram—this body; kṣhetrī—the soul; tathā—similarly; kṛitsnam—all; prakāśhayati—illuminates; bhārata—O son of Bharat
translation_english: O son of Bharata, as the sun alone illuminates all this universe, so does the living entity, one within the body, illuminate the entire body by consciousness.
deep_dive_text: The Inner Sun. Krishna gives another brilliant analogy. The sun sits in one place but lights up the entire solar system. In the same way, the soul (Kshetri) sits in the heart but illuminates the entire physical body (Kshetra) with the radiant energy we call 'consciousness.' Consciousness is the undeniable proof of the soul's presence.

original_script:
क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञयोरेवमन्तरं ज्ञानचक्षुषा।
भूतप्रकृतिमोक्षं च ये विदुर्यान्ति ते परम्।।13.34।।
transliteration:
kṣhetra-kṣhetrajñayor evam antaraṁ jñāna-chakṣhuṣhā
bhūta-prakṛiti-mokṣhaṁ cha ye vidur yānti te param
word_meanings: kṣhetra—of the body; kṣhetrajñayoḥ—of the proprietor of the body; evam—thus; antaram—the difference; jñāna-chakṣhuṣhā—by the vision of knowledge; bhūta—of living entities; prakṛiti—from material nature; mokṣham—liberation; cha—also; ye—those who; viduḥ—know; yānti—approach; te—they; param—the Supreme
translation_english: Those who see with eyes of knowledge the difference between the body and the knower of the body, and can also understand the process of liberation from bondage in material nature, attain to the supreme goal.
deep_dive_text: The Eyes of Knowledge. Krishna concludes the chapter with a call to develop a new kind of vision. When you look at the world with 'Jnana-chakshusha' (the eyes of knowledge), you clearly see the gap between the machinery of the body and the spark of the soul. Understanding this difference is the final key to Moksha (liberation), allowing the soul to exit the matrix of material nature.

original_script: क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञविभागयोगः
transliteration: Kṣhetra Kṣhetrajña Vibhāga Yogaḥ
translation_english: Chapter 13: The Yoga of the Field and the Knower of the Field
deep_dive_text: Chapter 13 dissects reality into two distinct components: the 'Kshetra' (the Field, which includes the physical body, mind, and emotions) and the 'Kshetrajna' (the Knower of the Field, the conscious, eternal soul). Krishna explains that true knowledge is the ability to perfectly distinguish between the observer and the observed. By realizing that we are the silent witness and not the mechanical fluctuations of nature, we free ourselves from karmic entanglement and recognize the Supreme Soul residing equally in all beings.

original_script: अथ चतुर्दशोऽध्यायः - गुणत्रयविभागयोगः
transliteration: Guṇa Traya Vibhāga Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Guṇa—modes of material nature; Traya—three; Vibhāga—divisions; Yogaḥ—the path of connection.
translation_english: Chapter 14 Summary: The Yoga of the Three Modes of Material Nature
deep_dive_text: Summary: Chapter 14 acts as the psychological operating manual for the human mind. Krishna explains that the entire material universe is driven by three invisible forces or 'Gunas': Sattva (Goodness/Clarity), Rajas (Passion/Action), and Tamas (Ignorance/Inertia). These modes constantly compete for dominance in our minds, dictating our behavior, diet, and destiny. By learning to identify which 'mode' is currently driving the steering wheel, a seeker can consciously shift into Goodness, and eventually transcend all three modes entirely to attain pure spiritual freedom.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
परं भूयः प्रवक्ष्यामि ज्ञानानां ज्ञानमुत्तमम्।
यज्ज्ञात्वा मुनयः सर्वे परां सिद्धिमितो गताः।।14.1।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
paraṁ bhūyaḥ pravakṣhyāmi jñānānāṁ jñānam uttamam
yaj jñātvā munayaḥ sarve parāṁ siddhim ito gatāḥ
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; param—transcendental; bhūyaḥ—again; pravakṣhyāmi—I shall speak; jñānānām—of all knowledge; jñānam—knowledge; uttamam—the supreme; yat—which; jñātvā—knowing; munayaḥ—the sages; sarve—all; parām—transcendental; siddhim—perfection; itaḥ—from this world; gatāḥ—attained
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: Again I shall declare to you this supreme wisdom, the best of all knowledge, knowing which all the sages have attained the supreme perfection.
deep_dive_text: The Ultimate Upgrade. Krishna begins Chapter 14 by promising an even deeper layer of truth. He calls this 'jñānānāṁ jñānam uttamam' (the supreme knowledge among all knowledge). It is the wisdom that takes a seeker from mere intellectual understanding to 'param siddhim' (ultimate liberation), just as the great sages achieved in the past.

original_script:
इदं ज्ञानमुपाश्रित्य मम साधर्म्यमागताः।
सर्गेऽपि नोपजायन्ते प्रलये न व्यथन्ति च।।14.2।।
transliteration:
idaṁ jñānam upāśhritya mama sādharmyam āgatāḥ
sarge ’pi nopajāyante pralaye na vyathanti cha
word_meanings: idam—this; jñānam—knowledge; upāśhritya—taking shelter of; mama—My; sādharmyam—same nature; āgatāḥ—having attained; sarge—at the time of creation; api—even; na—never; upajāyante—are born; pralaye—at the time of annihilation; na—nor; vyathanti—are disturbed; cha—also
translation_english: By becoming fixed in this knowledge, one can attain to the transcendental nature like My own. Thus established, one is not born at the time of creation or disturbed at the time of dissolution.
deep_dive_text: The State of 'Sadharmyam'. The goal of this knowledge isn't just to be 'good', but to attain 'Sadharmyam'—a nature qualitatively equal to the Divine. When you upgrade your consciousness to this level, you become immune to the cosmic cycles of birth (creation) and death (dissolution). You achieve permanent spiritual immunity.

original_script:
मम योनिर्महद्ब्रह्म तस्मिन्गर्भं दधाम्यहम्।
सम्भवः सर्वभूतानां ततो भवति भारत।।14.3।।
transliteration:
mama yonir mahad brahma tasmin garbhaṁ dadhāmy aham
sambhavaḥ sarva-bhūtānāṁ tato bhavati bhārata
word_meanings: mama—My; yoniḥ—womb; mahat—the total material substance; brahma—supreme; tasmin—in that; garbham—pregnancy; dadhāmi—create; aham—I; sambhavaḥ—the possibility; sarva-bhūtānām—of all living entities; tataḥ—thereafter; bhavati—becomes; bhārata—O son of Bharata
translation_english: The total material substance, called Brahman, is the source of birth, and it is that Brahman that I impregnate, making possible the births of all living beings, O son of Bharata.
deep_dive_text: The Cosmic Womb. Krishna uses a powerful biological metaphor for creation. 'Mahad Brahma' (the vast expanse of unmanifested material nature) acts as the mother or the cosmic womb. But matter alone cannot create life. It is the Divine (the Father) who impregnates this material womb with the 'Garbham' (the seed of spiritual consciousness/souls). Every living entity is thus a child of material nature and divine spirit.

original_script:
सर्वयोनिषु कौन्तेय मूर्तयः सम्भवन्ति याः।
तासां ब्रह्म महद्योनिरहं बीजप्रदः पिता।।14.4।।
transliteration:
sarva-yoniṣhu kaunteya mūrtayaḥ sambhavanti yāḥ
tāsāṁ brahma mahad yonir ahaṁ bīja-pradaḥ pitā
word_meanings: sarva-yoniṣhu—in all species of life; kaunteya—O son of Kunti; mūrtayaḥ—forms; sambhavanti—they appear; yāḥ—which; tāsām—of all of them; brahma—the supreme; mahat yoniḥ—source of birth in the material substance; aham—I; bīja-pradaḥ—the seed-giving; pitā—father
translation_english: It should be understood that all species of life, O son of Kunti, are made possible by birth in this material nature, and that I am the seed-giving father.
deep_dive_text: The Universal Fatherhood. This verse shatters anthropocentrism (the idea that God only cares about humans). Krishna explicitly states that across 'Sarva-yonishu' (all species of life—plants, animals, demigods, humans), material nature is the mother that provides the physical bodies ('Murtayah'), and He is the 'Bija-pradah Pita' (the seed-giving father). Therefore, every living creature shares a fundamental spiritual brotherhood.

original_script:
सत्त्वं रजस्तम इति गुणाः प्रकृतिसम्भवाः।
निबध्नन्ति महाबाहो देहे देहिनमव्ययम्।।14.5।।
transliteration:
sattvaṁ rajas tama iti guṇāḥ prakṛiti-sambhavāḥ
nibadhnanti mahā-bāho dehe dehinam avyayam
word_meanings: sattvam—the mode of goodness; rajaḥ—the mode of passion; tamaḥ—the mode of ignorance; iti—thus; guṇāḥ—the qualities; prakṛiti—material nature; sambhavāḥ—produced of; nibadhnanti—do condition; mahā-bāho—O mighty-armed one; dehe—in this body; dehinam—the living entity; avyayam—eternal
translation_english: Material nature consists of three modes—goodness, passion and ignorance. When the eternal living entity comes in contact with nature, O mighty-armed Arjuna, he becomes conditioned by these modes.
deep_dive_text: The Three Ropes of Nature. Here, Krishna introduces the 'Gunas' (modes). The word 'Guna' literally translates to 'rope.' When the eternal, free soul ('Dehinam avyayam') enters the physical body, it gets tied down by these three psychological ropes: Sattva (Goodness/Clarity), Rajas (Passion/Action), and Tamas (Ignorance/Inertia). Our personality at any given moment is just a tug-of-war between these three ropes.

original_script:
तत्र सत्त्वं निर्मलत्वात्प्रकाशकमनामयम्।
सुखसङ्गेन बध्नाति ज्ञानसङ्गेन चानघ।।14.6।।
transliteration:
tatra sattvaṁ nirmalatvāt prakāśhakam anāmayam
sukha-saṅgena badhnāti jñāna-saṅgena chānagha
word_meanings: tatra—there; sattvam—the mode of goodness; nirmalatvāt—being purest in the material world; prakāśhakam—illuminating; anāmayam—without any sinful reaction; sukha—with happiness; saṅgena—by association; badhnāti—conditions; jñāna—with knowledge; saṅgena—by association; cha—and; anagha—O sinless one
translation_english: O sinless one, the mode of goodness, being purer than the others, is illuminating, and it frees one from all sinful reactions. Those situated in that mode become conditioned by a sense of happiness and knowledge.
deep_dive_text: The Golden Handcuffs of Sattva. Sattva (Goodness) is the best of the modes. It is 'Nirmalatvat' (pure) and 'Prakashakam' (illuminating). People in this mode are peaceful, intelligent, and ethical. However, Krishna warns that it is still a trap! It binds the soul through 'Sukha-sangena' (attachment to happiness) and 'Jnana-sangena' (attachment to knowledge). The intellectual might become arrogant about his intellect, or the peaceful man might become addicted to his comfort, keeping them tied to the material world.

original_script:
रजो रागात्मकं विद्धि तृष्णासङ्गसमुद्भवम्।
तन्निबध्नाति कौन्तेय कर्मसङ्गेन देहिनम्।।14.7।।
transliteration:
rajo rāgātmakaṁ viddhi tṛiṣhṇā-saṅga-samudbhavam
tan nibadhnāti kaunteya karma-saṅgena dehinam
word_meanings: rajaḥ—the mode of passion; rāga-ātmakam—born of desire or lust; viddhi—know; tṛiṣhṇā—with hankering; saṅga—association; samudbhavam—produced of; tat—that; nibadhnāti—binds; kaunteya—O son of Kunti; karma-saṅgena—by association with fruitive activity; dehinam—the embodied
translation_english: The mode of passion is born of unlimited desires and longings, O son of Kunti, and because of this the embodied living entity is bound to material fruitive actions.
deep_dive_text: The Hustle Culture of Rajas. Rajas is the mode of intense action. It is 'Ragātmakam' (fueled by attraction) and born from 'Trishna' (unquenchable thirst or hankering). A person in Rajas is always chasing the next promotion, the next purchase, the next achievement. This mode binds the soul through 'Karma-sangena'—the absolute obsession with working hard to enjoy the fruits of one's labor. It is the mode of stress and perpetual dissatisfaction.

original_script:
तमस्त्वज्ञानजं विद्धि मोहनं सर्वदेहिनाम्।
प्रमादालस्यनिद्राभिस्तन्निबध्नाति भारत।।14.8।।
transliteration:
tamas tv ajñāna-jaṁ viddhi mohanaṁ sarva-dehinām
pramādālasya-nidrābhis tan nibadhnāti bhārata
word_meanings: tamaḥ—the mode of ignorance; tu—but; ajñāna-jam—produced of ignorance; viddhi—know; mohanam—the delusion; sarva-dehinām—of all embodied beings; pramāda—with madness; ālasya—indolence; nidrābhiḥ—and sleep; tat—that; nibadhnāti—binds; bhārata—O son of Bharata
translation_english: O son of Bharata, know that the mode of darkness, born of ignorance, is the delusion of all embodied living entities. The results of this mode are madness, indolence and sleep, which bind the conditioned soul.
deep_dive_text: The Heavy Chains of Tamas. Tamas is the lowest mode, the mode of darkness and entropy. It is 'Ajnana-jam' (born of sheer ignorance). It deludes the living entity, making them lose touch with reality and their responsibilities. How does it bind the soul? Through 'Pramada' (carelessness/madness), 'Alasya' (laziness), and 'Nidra' (excessive sleep). It is the state of depression, addiction, and total inertia.

original_script:
सत्त्वं सुखे सञ्जयति रजः कर्मणि भारत।
ज्ञानमावृत्य तु तमः प्रमादे सञ्जयत्युत।।14.9।।
transliteration:
sattvaṁ sukhe sañjayati rajaḥ karmaṇi bhārata
jñānam āvṛitya tu tamaḥ pramāde sañjayaty uta
word_meanings: sattvam—the mode of goodness; sukhe—in happiness; sañjayati—binds; rajaḥ—the mode of passion; karmaṇi—in fruitive activity; bhārata—O son of Bharata; jñānam—knowledge; āvṛitya—covering; tu—but; tamaḥ—the mode of ignorance; pramāde—in madness; sañjayati—binds; uta—it is said
translation_english: O son of Bharata, the mode of goodness conditions one to happiness; passion conditions one to fruitive action; and ignorance, covering one's knowledge, binds one to madness.
deep_dive_text: The Summary of the Traps. Krishna summarizes how each mode functions as a psychological trap. Sattva traps you by making you so comfortable and content ('Sukhe') that you stop striving for ultimate liberation. Rajas traps you in a hamster wheel of endless, exhausting work ('Karmani'). Tamas actively covers up your intelligence ('Jnanam avritya') and locks you into a state of destructive delusion ('Pramade').

original_script:
रजस्तमश्चाभिभूय सत्त्वं भवति भारत।
रजः सत्त्वं तमश्चैव तमः सत्त्वं रजस्तथा।।14.10।।
transliteration:
rajas tamaśh chābhibhūya sattvaṁ bhavati bhārata
rajaḥ sattvaṁ tamaśh chaiva tamaḥ sattvaṁ rajas tathā
word_meanings: rajaḥ—the mode of passion; tamaḥ—the mode of ignorance; cha—also; abhibhūya—surpassing; sattvam—the mode of goodness; bhavati—becomes prominent; bhārata—O son of Bharata; rajaḥ—the mode of passion; sattvam—the mode of goodness; tamaḥ—the mode of ignorance; cha—also; eva—like that; tamaḥ—the mode of ignorance; sattvam—the mode of goodness; rajaḥ—the mode of passion; tathā—thus
translation_english: Sometimes the mode of goodness becomes prominent, defeating the modes of passion and ignorance, O son of Bharata. Sometimes the mode of passion defeats goodness and ignorance, and at other times ignorance defeats goodness and passion. In this way there is always competition for supremacy.
deep_dive_text: The Mental Battlefield. The mind is never static. Krishna explains that the three modes are in a constant, aggressive state of competition ('Abhibhuya' - suppressing or defeating). Some days you wake up feeling clear, peaceful, and ready to meditate (Sattva is winning). Later in the day, you might feel an intense urge to hustle and compete (Rajas takes over). By nightfall, you might feel lazy, lethargic, and want to binge-watch TV (Tamas defeats the others). Understanding this shifting tide is key to self-awareness.

original_script:
सर्वद्वारेषु देहेऽस्मिन्प्रकाश उपजायते।
ज्ञानं यदा तदा विद्याद्विवृद्धं सत्त्वमित्युत।।14.11।।
transliteration:
sarva-dvāreṣhu dehe ’smin prakāśha upajāyate
jñānaṁ yadā tadā vidyād vivṛiddhaṁ sattvam ity uta
word_meanings: sarva-dvāreṣhu—in all the gates; dehe asmin—in this body; prakāśhaḥ—the quality of illumination; upajāyate—develops; jñānam—knowledge; yadā—when; tadā—at that time; vidyāt—know; vivṛiddham—is increased; sattvam—the mode of goodness; iti uta—thus it is said
translation_english: The manifestations of the mode of goodness can be experienced when all the gates of the body are illuminated by knowledge.
deep_dive_text: The Symptoms of Sattva. How do you know when Goodness is winning the battle? Krishna gives a diagnostic test. The body has 'doors' or 'gates' (the eyes, ears, mind, intellect). When Sattva is dominant, there is 'Prakasha' (illumination) at all these gates. You see things clearly without prejudice, you listen with patience, and your intellect processes information with calm wisdom. You feel internally bright and awake.

original_script:
लोभः प्रवृत्तिरारम्भः कर्मणामशमः स्पृहा।
रजस्येतानि जायन्ते विवृद्धे भरतर्षभ।।14.12।।
transliteration:
lobhaḥ pravṛittir ārambhaḥ karmaṇām aśhamaḥ spṛihā
rajasy etāni jāyante vivṛiddhe bharatarṣhabha
word_meanings: lobhaḥ—greed; pravṛittiḥ—activity; ārambhaḥ—endeavor; karmaṇām—in activities; aśhamaḥ—uncontrollable; spṛihā—desire; rajasi—of the mode of passion; etāni—all these; jāyante—develop; vivṛiddhe—when there is an excess; bharata-ṛiṣhabha—O chief of the descendants of Bharata
translation_english: O chief of the Bharatas, when there is an increase in the mode of passion the symptoms of great attachment, fruitive activity, intense endeavor, and uncontrollable desire and hankering develop.
deep_dive_text: The Symptoms of Rajas. How do you know when Passion is dominating? The primary symptom is 'Lobhah' (greed). You cannot sit still; you are driven by 'Ashamah' (restlessness) and 'Spriha' (uncontrollable hankering). You start taking on too many projects ('Arambhah karmanam') purely for personal gain. Your mind feels overheated, constantly planning the future, and unable to enjoy the present moment.

original_script:
अप्रकाशोऽप्रवृत्तिश्च प्रमादो मोह एव च।
तमस्येतानि जायन्ते विवृद्धे कुरुनन्दन।।14.13।।
transliteration:
aprakāśho ’pravṛittiśh cha pramādo moha eva cha
tamasy etāni jāyante vivṛiddhe kuru-nandana
word_meanings: aprakāśhaḥ—darkness; apravṛittiḥ—inactivity; cha—and; pramādaḥ—madness; mohaḥ—illusion; eva—certainly; cha—also; tamasi—the mode of ignorance; etāni—these; jāyante—are manifested; vivṛiddhe—when developed; kuru-nandana—O son of Kuru
translation_english: O son of Kuru, when there is an increase in the mode of ignorance, darkness, inertia, madness and illusion are manifested.
deep_dive_text: The Symptoms of Tamas. When Ignorance takes over the steering wheel, the 'gates' of the body shut down. There is 'Aprakasho' (absence of light/clarity) and 'Apravritti' (inertia/inactivity). You can't think clearly, and you don't want to do anything. This leads to 'Pramado' (carelessness) and 'Moha' (deep illusion). It's the psychological equivalent of a black hole, crushing motivation and awareness.

original_script:
यदा सत्त्वे प्रवृद्धे तु प्रलयं याति देहभृत्।
तदोत्तमविदां लोकानमलान्प्रतिपद्यते।।14.14।।
transliteration:
yadā sattve pravṛiddhe tu pralayaṁ yāti deha-bhṛit
tadottama-vidāṁ lokān amalān pratipadyate
word_meanings: yadā—when; sattve—the mode of goodness; pravṛiddhe—develops; tu—but; pralayam—dissolution; yāti—goes; deha-bhṛit—the embodied; tadā—at that time; uttama-vidām—of the great sages; lokān—the planetary systems; amalān—pure; pratipadyate—attains
translation_english: When one dies in the mode of goodness, he attains to the pure higher planets of the great sages.
deep_dive_text: The Destination of Goodness. The state of your mind at death dictates your next destination. If you die when Sattva (Goodness) is 'Pravriddhe' (predominant), your consciousness is light and pure. Therefore, you naturally float upwards to the 'Amalan' (pure) worlds of the 'Uttama-vidam' (the great sages and highly evolved beings). Goodness guarantees a soft landing in a higher reality.

original_script:
रजसि प्रलयं गत्वा कर्मसङ्गिषु जायते।
तथा प्रलीनस्तमसि मूढयोनिषु जायते।।14.15।।
transliteration:
rajasi pralayaṁ gatvā karma-saṅgiṣhu jāyate
tathā pralīnas tamasi mūḍha-yoniṣhu jāyate
word_meanings: rajasi—in passion; pralayam—dissolution; gatvā—attaining; karma-saṅgiṣhu—in the association of fruitive workers; jāyate—takes birth; tathā—similarly; pralīnaḥ—being dissolved; tamasi—in ignorance; mūḍha-yoniṣhu—in animal species; jāyate—takes birth
translation_english: When one dies in the mode of passion, he takes birth among those engaged in fruitive activities; and when one dies in the mode of ignorance, he takes birth in the animal kingdom.
deep_dive_text: The Destinations of Passion and Ignorance. Karma is perfectly logical. If you die deeply obsessed with work, success, and material accumulation (Rajas), nature grants you another human body 'Karma-sangishu' (among those driven by fruitive work) so you can keep hustling. If you die in deep delusion, addiction, or profound ignorance (Tamas), you are downgraded to 'Mudha-yonishu' (ignorant species, like animals or lower life forms) where intellect is restricted.

original_script:
कर्मणः सुकृतस्याहुः सात्त्विकं निर्मलं फलम्।
रजसस्तु फलं दुःखमज्ञानं तमसः फलम्।।14.16।।
transliteration:
karmaṇaḥ sukṛitasyāhuḥ sāttvikaṁ nirmalaṁ phalam
rajasas tu phalaṁ duḥkham ajñānaṁ tamasaḥ phalam
word_meanings: karmaṇaḥ—of work; su-kṛitasya—pious; āhuḥ—is said; sāttvikam—in the mode of goodness; nirmalam—purified; phalam—the result; rajasaḥ—of the mode of passion; tu—but; phalam—the result; duḥkham—misery; ajñānam—nonsense; tamasaḥ—of the mode of ignorance; phalam—the result
