translation_english: The result of pious action is pure and is said to be in the mode of goodness. But action done in the mode of passion results in misery, and action performed in the mode of ignorance results in foolishness.
deep_dive_text: The Harvest of Actions. Every action bears a specific fruit based on the mode driving it. Good, selfless work (Sukritasya) yields 'Nirmalam phalam' (pure, peaceful results). Work driven by intense ambition and passion inevitably yields 'Duhkham' (stress, anxiety, and misery). Actions born of ignorance, laziness, or malice yield only more 'Ajnanam' (deeper foolishness and suffering).

original_script:
सत्त्वात्सञ्जायते ज्ञानं रजसो लोभ एव च।
प्रमादमोहौ तमसो भवतोऽज्ञानमेव च।।14.17।।
transliteration:
sattvāt sañjāyate jñānaṁ rajaso lobha eva cha
pramāda-mohau tamaso bhavato ’jñānam eva cha
word_meanings: sattvāt—from the mode of goodness; sañjāyate—develops; jñānam—knowledge; rajasaḥ—from the mode of passion; lobhaḥ—greed; eva—certainly; cha—also; pramāda—madness; mohau—and illusion; tamasaḥ—from the mode of ignorance; bhavataḥ—develop; ajñānam—nonsense; eva—certainly; cha—also
translation_english: From the mode of goodness, real knowledge develops; from the mode of passion, greed develops; and from the mode of ignorance develop foolishness, madness and illusion.
deep_dive_text: The Psychological Offspring. The modes act as psychological seeds. If you cultivate Goodness, 'Jnanam' (true wisdom) naturally sprouts. If you cultivate Passion, you will inevitably harvest 'Lobha' (insatiable greed). If you wallow in Ignorance, you breed 'Pramada' (madness/recklessness) and 'Moha' (illusion). You cannot plant a seed of passion and expect the fruit of peace.

original_script:
ऊर्ध्वं गच्छन्ति सत्त्वस्था मध्ये तिष्ठन्ति राजसाः।
जघन्यगुणवृत्तिस्था अधो गच्छन्ति तामसाः।।14.18।।
transliteration:
ūrdhvaṁ gachchhanti sattva-sthā madhye tiṣhṭhanti rājasāḥ
jaghanya-guṇa-vṛitti-sthā adho gachchhanti tāmasāḥ
word_meanings: ūrdhvam—upwards; gachchhanti—go; sattva-sthāḥ—those situated in the mode of goodness; madhye—in the middle; tiṣhṭhanti—dwell; rājasāḥ—those situated in the mode of passion; jaghanya—of abominable; guṇa—quality; vṛitti-sthāḥ—whose occupation; adhaḥ—down; gachchhanti—go; tāmasāḥ—persons in the mode of ignorance
translation_english: Those situated in the mode of goodness gradually go upward to the higher planets; those in the mode of passion live on the earthly planets; and those in the abominable mode of ignorance go down to the hellish worlds.
deep_dive_text: The Cosmic Elevator. The modes dictate your vertical movement in the universe. 'Urdhvam' (upward) go the Sattvic, floating to higher realms of light. 'Madhye' (in the middle) stay the Rajasic, stuck on the earthly plane running on the treadmill of human ambition. 'Adho' (downward) sink the Tamasic, dragged by the heavy, abominable traits of ignorance into lower, darker existences.

original_script:
नान्यं गुणेभ्यः कर्तारं यदा द्रष्टानुपश्यति।
गुणेभ्यश्च परं वेत्ति मद्भावं सोऽधिगच्छति।।14.19।।
transliteration:
nānyaṁ guṇebhyaḥ kartāraṁ yadā draṣhṭānupaśhyati
guṇebhyaśh cha paraṁ vetti mad-bhāvaṁ so ’dhigachchhati
word_meanings: na—no; anyam—other; guṇebhyaḥ—than the qualities; kartāram—performer; yadā—when; draṣhṭā—a seer; anupaśhyati—sees properly; guṇebhyaḥ—to the modes of nature; cha—and; param—transcendental; vetti—knows; mat-bhāvam—My spiritual nature; saḥ—he; adhigachchhati—is promoted
translation_english: When one properly sees that in all activities no other performer is at work than these modes of nature and he knows the Supreme Lord, who is transcendental to all these modes, he attains My spiritual nature.
deep_dive_text: The Shift to the Observer. How do you escape the modes? By realizing you aren't doing the acting! When the 'Drashta' (the observing soul) clearly sees that the three modes (Gunas) are doing all the fighting, desiring, and sleeping, and recognizes the Supreme lying completely beyond them, the illusion snaps. This profound detachment automatically elevates the soul to 'Mad-bhavam' (the divine nature).

original_script:
गुणानेतानतीत्य त्रीन्देही देहसमुद्भवान्।
जन्ममृत्युजरादुःखैर्विमुक्तोऽमृतमश्नुते।।14.20।।
transliteration:
guṇān etān atītya trīn dehī deha-samudbhavān
janma-mṛityu-jarā-duḥkhair vimukto ’mṛitam aśhnute
word_meanings: guṇān—qualities; etān—all these; atītya—transcending; trīn—three; dehī—the embodied; deha—the body; samudbhavān—produced of; janma—of birth; mṛityu—death; jarā—and old age; duḥkhaiḥ—the distresses; vimuktaḥ—being freed from; amṛitam—nectar; aśhnute—he enjoys
translation_english: When the embodied being is able to transcend these three modes associated with the material body, he can become free from birth, death, old age and their distresses and can enjoy nectar even in this life.
deep_dive_text: Immortality Now. You don't have to die to experience liberation. When the 'Dehi' (embodied soul) transcends these three psychological ropes that are born of the body, it becomes 'Vimukto' (liberated) from the miseries of birth, old age (Jara), and death. The yogi begins to 'Ashnute Amritam' (taste the nectar of immortality) right here, walking the earth as a completely free being.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
कैर्लिङ्गैस्त्रीन्गुणानेतानतीतो भवति प्रभो।
किमाचारः कथं चैतांस्त्रीन्गुणानतिवर्तते।।14.21।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
kair liṅgais trīn guṇān etān atīto bhavati prabho
kim āchāraḥ kathaṁ chaitāns trīn guṇān ativartate
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjuna said; kaiḥ—by which; liṅgaiḥ—symptoms; trīn—three; guṇān—qualities; etān—all these; atītaḥ—having transcended; bhavati—is; prabho—O my Lord; kim—what; āchāraḥ—behavior; katham—how; cha—also; etān—these; trīn—three; guṇān—qualities; ativartate—transcends
translation_english: Arjuna inquired: O my dear Lord, by which symptoms is one known who is transcendental to these three modes? What is his behavior? And how does he transcend the modes of nature?
deep_dive_text: Arjuna's Diagnostic Questions. Arjuna, always the practical student, asks three brilliant questions to identify a liberated master (a Gunatita). 1. 'Kair lingais?' (What are the symptoms/signs?) 2. 'Kim acharah?' (What is his behavior/conduct?) 3. 'Katham ativartate?' (How exactly did he transcend them?). Arjuna wants the operational manual for achieving this state.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
प्रकाशं च प्रवृत्तिं च मोहमेव च पाण्डव।
न द्वेष्टि सम्प्रवृत्तानि न निवृत्तानि काङ्क्षति।।14.22।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
prakāśhaṁ cha pravṛittiṁ cha moham eva cha pāṇḍava
na dveṣhṭi sampravṛittāni na nivṛittāni kāṅkṣhati
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Personality of Godhead said; prakāśham—illumination; cha—and; pravṛittim—attachment; cha—and; moham—illusion; eva cha—also; pāṇḍava—O son of Pandu; na dveṣhṭi—does not hate; sampravṛittāni—although developed; na nivṛittāni—nor disappearing; kāṅkṣhati—desires
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: O son of Pandu, he who does not hate illumination, attachment and delusion when they are present or long for them when they disappear...
deep_dive_text: The Supreme Equanimity. Krishna begins answering the symptoms. 'Prakasha' represents Sattva (illumination), 'Pravritti' represents Rajas (activity), and 'Moha' represents Tamas (delusion). A transcended person observes these modes cycling through their mind like weather. When Tamas makes them feel lethargic, they don't hate it. When Sattva makes them feel peaceful, they don't cling to it. They watch the mental weather without fighting it or craving it, maintaining absolute neutrality.

original_script:
उदासीनवदासीनो गुणैर्यो न विचाल्यते।
गुणा वर्तन्त इत्येव योऽवतिष्ठति नेङ्गते।।14.23।।
transliteration:
udāsīnavad āsīno guṇair yo na vichālyate
guṇā vartanta ity eva yo ’vatiṣhṭhati neṅgate
word_meanings: udāsīna-vat—like one neutral; āsīnaḥ—situated; guṇaiḥ—by the modes; yaḥ—who; na—never; vichālyate—is agitated; guṇāḥ—the modes; vartante—are acting; iti eva—knowing thus; yaḥ—who; avatiṣhṭhati—remains; na—never; iṅgate—flickers
translation_english: He who is situated like a neutral observer, who is not agitated by the modes of material nature, and who remains firm, knowing that only the modes are active...
deep_dive_text: The Silent Witness. Continuing to answer Arjuna, Krishna explains that the liberated person acts 'Udasinavad' (like a neutral observer). When anger, joy, or laziness arises in the mind, they don't panic or identify with the emotion. They simply observe, 'Ah, the modes are acting.' Because they don't identify with the mental weather, they remain unshaken ('na ingate').

original_script:
समदुःखसुखः स्वस्थः समलोष्टाश्मकाञ्चनः।
तुल्यप्रियाप्रियो धीरस्तुल्यनिन्दात्मसंस्तुतिः।।14.24।।
मानापमानयोस्तुल्यस्तुल्यो मित्रारिपक्षयोः।
सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी गुणातीतः स उच्यते।।14.25।।
transliteration:
sama-duḥkha-sukhaḥ svasthaḥ sama-loṣhṭāśhma-kāñchanaḥ
tulya-priyāpriyo dhīras tulya-nindātma-sanstutiḥ
mānāpamānayos tulyas tulyo mitrāri-pakṣhayoḥ
sarvārambha-parityāgī guṇātītaḥ sa uchyate
word_meanings: sama—equal; duḥkha—in distress; sukhaḥ—and happiness; sva-sthaḥ—being situated in himself; sama—equally; loṣhṭa—a lump of earth; aśhma—stone; kāñchanaḥ—and gold; tulya—equally disposed; priya—to the dear; apriyaḥ—and the undesirable; dhīraḥ—steady; tulya—equal; nindā—in defamation; ātma-sanstutiḥ—and praise of himself; māna—in honor; apamānayoḥ—and dishonor; tulyaḥ—equal; tulyaḥ—equal; mitra—of friends; ari—and enemies; pakṣhayoḥ—to the parties; sarva—of all; ārambha—endeavors; parityāgī—renouncer; guṇa-atītaḥ—transcendental to the material modes of nature; saḥ—he; uchyate—is said to be
translation_english: One who is situated in the self and behaves identically in happiness and distress; who looks upon a clod of dirt, a stone, and a piece of gold with an equal eye; who is equal toward the desirable and the undesirable; who is steady, situated equally well in praise and blame, honor and dishonor; who treats friend and enemy alike; and who has renounced all material endeavors—such a person is said to have transcended the modes of nature.
deep_dive_text: The Zenith of Equanimity. These two verses provide the complete behavioral profile of a 'Gunatita' (one who has transcended the modes). The core operating principle is 'Sama' (equal). Whether someone hands them a lump of clay or solid gold, they assign it no ultimate psychological value. Because they are 'Svasthah' (situated entirely within the self), external praise, insults, friends, or enemies cannot alter their internal peace.

original_script:
मां च योऽव्यभिचारेण भक्तियोगेन सेवते।
स गुणान्समतीत्यैतान्ब्रह्मभूयाय कल्पते।।14.26।।
transliteration:
māṁ cha yo ’vyabhichāreṇa bhakti-yogena sevate
sa guṇān samatītyaitān brahma-bhūyāya kalpate
word_meanings: mām—unto Me; cha—also; yaḥ—one who; avyabhichāreṇa—unfailing; bhakti-yogena—by devotional service; sevate—renders service; saḥ—he; guṇān—the modes of material nature; samatītya—transcending; etān—all these; brahma-bhūyāya—to be elevated to the Brahman platform; kalpate—becomes eligible
translation_english: One who engages in full devotional service, unfailing in all circumstances, at once transcends the modes of material nature and thus comes to the level of Brahman.
deep_dive_text: The Shortcut to Transcendence. How do you actually get to that high level of equanimity? Krishna finally answers Arjuna's third question ('How does one transcend?'). The answer is not exhausting mental gymnastics; it is 'Avyabhicharena Bhakti-yogena' (unfailing, unadulterated devotional service). By directing all love and service toward the Supreme, the soul bypasses the heavy gravity of the three modes and instantly elevates to the spiritual platform (Brahma-bhuyaya).

original_script:
ब्रह्मणो हि प्रतिष्ठाहममृतस्याव्ययस्य च।
शाश्वतस्य च धर्मस्य सुखस्यैकान्तिकस्य च।।14.27।।
transliteration:
brahmaṇo hi pratiṣhṭhāham amṛitasyāvyayasya cha
śhāśhvatasya cha dharmasya sukhasyaikāntikasya cha
word_meanings: brahmaṇaḥ—of the impersonal brahmajyoti; hi—certainly; pratiṣhṭhā—the rest; aham—I am; amṛitasya—of the immortal; avyayasya—of the imperishable; cha—also; śhāśhvatasya—of the eternal; cha—and; dharmasya—of the constitutional position; sukhasya—of happiness; aikāntikasya—ultimate; cha—also
translation_english: And I am the basis of the impersonal Brahman, which is immortal, imperishable and eternal and is the constitutional position of ultimate happiness.
deep_dive_text: The Foundation of the Formless. Chapter 14 concludes with a definitive statement on the nature of God. Many philosophers consider the formless 'Brahman' (the cosmic light) to be the ultimate reality. But Krishna clearly states, 'Brahmano hi pratishtha aham' (I am the basis of Brahman). Just as the sun is the source of the sunshine, the personal, loving Supreme Being is the source of the infinite, formless spiritual light. To reach Krishna is to reach the ultimate source of all eternity and joy.

original_script: गुणत्रयविभागयोगः
transliteration: Guṇa Traya Vibhāga Yogaḥ
translation_english: Chapter 14: The Yoga of the Three Modes of Material Nature
deep_dive_text: Chapter 14 provides the operational manual for the material world. It explains that our minds and actions are constantly manipulated by three invisible ropes (Gunas): Goodness (Sattva), Passion (Rajas), and Ignorance (Tamas). While Goodness is the most illuminating, even it binds the soul with attachment to comfort and knowledge. The ultimate goal is to become a 'Gunatita'—one who observes these modes with total neutrality and transcends them entirely through unalloyed devotion (Bhakti), reaching the supreme, eternal reality.

original_script: अथ पञ्चदशोऽध्यायः - पुरुषोत्तमयोगः
transliteration: Atha Pañchadaśho ’dhyāyaḥ - Puruṣhottama Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Puruṣha—person; Uttama—supreme; Yogaḥ—the path of connection.
translation_english: Chapter 15 Summary: The Yoga of the Supreme Person
deep_dive_text: Summary: Chapter 15 is often considered the most condensed and profound chapter in the Gita, containing the absolute essence of Vedic philosophy in just 20 verses. Krishna uses the metaphor of an upside-down Banyan tree to describe the entangled material world, teaching that the only way to escape is to cut down this tree with the 'weapon of detachment.' He then reveals Himself as the 'Purushottama'—the Supreme Person who transcends both the fallible material world and the infallible spiritual souls, sustaining everything from the sun's light to the digestion in our stomachs.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
ऊर्ध्वमूलमधःशाखमश्वत्थं प्राहुरव्ययम्।
छन्दांसि यस्य पर्णानि यस्तं वेद स वेदवित्।।15.1।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
ūrdhva-mūlam adhaḥ-śhākham aśhvatthaṁ prāhur avyayam
chhandānsi yasya parṇāni yas taṁ veda sa veda-vit
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Personality of Godhead said; ūrdhva-mūlam—with roots upward; adhaḥ-śhākham—with branches downward; aśhvattham—a banyan tree; prāhuḥ—is said; avyayam—eternal; chhandānsi—the Vedic hymns; yasya—of which; parṇāni—the leaves; yaḥ—anyone who; tam—that; veda—knows; saḥ—he; veda-vit—the knower of the Vedas
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: It is said that there is an imperishable banyan tree that has its roots upward and its branches down and whose leaves are the Vedic hymns. One who knows this tree is the knower of the Vedas.
deep_dive_text: The Ashvattha Tree. This allegory of the upside-down Banyan tree represents the Vedic view of the cosmos: a manifestation with roots in the transcendent and branches in the material. It describes a complex hierarchical reality that must be severed with the 'sword of detachment' (Asaṅga-śhastreṇa).

original_script:
अधश्चोर्ध्वं प्रसृतास्तस्य शाखा
गुणप्रवृद्धा विषयप्रवालाः।
अधश्च मूलान्यनुसन्ततानि
कर्मानुबन्धीनि मनुष्यलोके।।15.2।।
transliteration:
adhaśh chordhvaṁ prasṛitās tasya śhākhā
guṇa-pravṛiddhā viṣhaya-pravālāḥ
adhaśh cha mūlāny anusantatāni
karmānubandhīni manuṣhya-loke
word_meanings: adhaḥ—downward; cha—and; ūrdhvam—upward; prasṛitāḥ—extended; tasya—its; śhākhāḥ—branches; guṇa—by the modes of material nature; pravṛiddhāḥ—developed; viṣhaya—sense objects; pravālāḥ—twigs; adhaḥ—downward; cha—and; mūlāni—roots; anusantatāni—extended; karma—to work; anubandhīni—bound; manuṣhya-loke—in the world of human society
translation_english: The branches of this tree extend downward and upward, nourished by the three modes of material nature. The twigs are the objects of the senses. This tree also has roots going down, and these are bound to the fruitive actions of human society.
deep_dive_text: The Mechanics of Entanglement. The tree is nourished by the 'Gunas' (the three modes of nature we learned about in Chapter 14). Its twigs are the sense objects (sights, sounds, tastes) that constantly tempt us. While the main root is at the top (in the divine), the secondary roots plunge downward into the human world, binding us tightly through 'Karma' (fruitive actions). Every selfish action we take grows another root, anchoring us deeper into the illusion.

original_script:
न रूपमस्येह तथोपलभ्यते
नान्तो न चादिर्न च सम्प्रतिष्ठा।
अश्वत्थमेनं सुविरूढमूल-
मसङ्गशस्त्रेण दृढेन छित्त्वा।।15.3।।
ततः पदं तत्परिमार्गितव्यं
यस्मिन्गता न निवर्तन्ति भूयः।
तमेव चाद्यं पुरुषं प्रपद्ये
यतः प्रवृत्तिः प्रसृता पुराणी।।15.4।।
transliteration:
na rūpam asyeha tathopalabhyate
nānto na chādir na cha sampratiṣhṭhā
aśhvattham enaṁ su-virūḍha-mūlam
asaṅga-śhastreṇa dṛiḍhena chhittvā
tataḥ padaṁ tat parimārgitavyaṁ
yasmin gatā na nivartanti bhūyaḥ
tam eva chādyaṁ puruṣhaṁ prapadye
yataḥ pravṛittiḥ prasṛitā purāṇī
word_meanings: na—not; rūpam—the form; asya—of this tree; iha—in this world; tathā—also; upalabhyate—can be perceived; na—never; antaḥ—end; na—never; cha—also; ādiḥ—beginning; na—never; cha—also; sampratiṣhṭhā—the foundation; aśhvattham—banyan tree; enam—this; su-virūḍha—strongly; mūlam—rooted; asaṅga-śhastreṇa—by the weapon of detachment; dṛiḍhena—strong; chhittvā—cutting; tataḥ—thereafter; padam—situation; tat—that; parimārgitavyam—has to be searched out; yasmin—where; gatāḥ—going; na—never; nivartanti—they come back; bhūyaḥ—again; tam—to Him; eva—certainly; cha—also; ādyam—original; puruṣham—the Personality of Godhead; prapadye—surrender; yataḥ—from whom; pravṛittiḥ—the beginning; prasṛitā—extended; purāṇī—very old
translation_english: The real form of this tree cannot be perceived in this world. No one can understand where it ends, where it begins, or where its foundation is. But with determination one must cut down this strongly rooted tree with the weapon of detachment. Thereafter, one must seek that place from which, having gone, one never returns, and there surrender to that Supreme Personality of Godhead from whom everything began and from whom the universal energy has been extending since time immemorial.
deep_dive_text: The Weapon of Detachment. You cannot untangle this tree; it is too complex, and its beginning and end are imperceptible to human logic. Krishna says there is only one solution: 'Asanga-shastrena'—the sharp axe of detachment. You must ruthlessly cut down your material attachments. Once the tree is felled, the soul is free to search for its true origin, surrendering to the Supreme Person and returning to the eternal home from which there is no forced return.

original_script:
निर्मानमोहा जितसङ्गदोषा
अध्यात्मनित्या विनिवृत्तकामाः।
द्वन्द्वैर्विमुक्ताः सुखदुःखसंज्ञै-
र्गच्छन्त्यमूढाः पदमव्ययं तत्।।15.5।।
transliteration:
nirmāna-mohā jita-saṅga-doṣhā
adhyātma-nityā vinivṛitta-kāmāḥ
dvandvair vimuktāḥ sukha-duḥkha-sañjñair
gachchhanty amūḍhāḥ padam avyayaṁ tat
word_meanings: nirmāna—free from false prestige; mohāḥ—and illusion; jita—having conquered; saṅga—of association; doṣhāḥ—the faults; adhyātma—in spiritual knowledge; nityāḥ—in eternity; vinivṛitta—discarded; kāmāḥ—lusts; dvandvaiḥ—from the dualities; vimuktāḥ—liberated; sukha-duḥkha—happiness and distress; sañjñaiḥ—named; gachchhanti—attain; amūḍhāḥ—unbewildered; padam—situation; avyayam—eternal; tat—that
translation_english: Those who are free from false prestige, illusion and false association, who understand the eternal, who are done with material lust, who are freed from the dualities of happiness and distress, and who, unbewildered, know how to surrender unto the Supreme Person attain to that eternal kingdom.
deep_dive_text: The Qualifications for Entry. Who actually gets to return to that eternal home? Krishna provides the checklist. They must be 'Nirmana-moha' (free from the illusion of false ego and pride). They must have conquered 'Sanga-dosha' (the flaw of toxic, worldly association). By abandoning selfish lust (Vinivritta-kamah) and rising above the dualities of joy and sorrow, the 'Amudhah' (the unbewildered/wise) smoothly transition into the eternal, imperishable reality (Padam avyayam).

original_script:
न तद्भासयते सूर्यो न शशाङ्को न पावकः।
यद्गत्वा न निवर्तन्ते तद्धाम परमं मम।।15.6।।
transliteration:
na tad bhāsayate sūryo na śhaśhāṅko na pāvakaḥ
yad gatvā na nivartante tad dhāma paramaṁ mama
word_meanings: na—not; tat—that; bhāsayate—illuminates; sūryaḥ—the sun; na—nor; śhaśha-aṅkaḥ—the moon; na—nor; pāvakaḥ—fire; yat—where; gatvā—going; na—never; nivartante—they come back; tat dhāma—that abode; paramam—supreme; mama—My
translation_english: That supreme abode of Mine is not illumined by the sun or moon, nor by fire or electricity. Those who reach it never return to this material world.
deep_dive_text: The Self-Luminous Realm. Krishna describes the ultimate destination, 'Param Dhama'. It doesn't need external light sources like the sun, moon, or fire because it is self-illuminated by the spiritual effulgence of the Divine. Once a soul reaches this state of absolute consciousness, it 'Na nivartante'—never falls back into the dark, cyclical illusion of material existence.

original_script:
ममैवांशो जीवलोके जीवभूतः सनातनः।
मनःषष्ठानीन्द्रियाणि प्रकृतिस्थानि कर्षति।।15.7।।
transliteration:
mamaivānśho jīva-loke jīva-bhūtaḥ sanātanaḥ
manaḥ-ṣhaṣhṭhānīndriyāṇi prakṛiti-sthāni karṣhati
word_meanings: mama—My; eva—certainly; anśhaḥ—fragmental particle; jīva-loke—in the world of conditional life; jīva-bhūtaḥ—the conditioned living entity; sanātanaḥ—eternal; manaḥ—with the mind; ṣhaṣhṭhāni—the six; indriyāṇi—senses; prakṛiti—in material nature; sthāni—situated; karṣhati—is struggling hard
translation_english: The living entities in this conditioned world are My eternal fragmental parts. Due to conditioned life, they are struggling very hard with the six senses, which include the mind.
deep_dive_text: The Divine Fragment. A deeply comforting verse. You are not a random biological accident; you are 'Mamaivamsho' (My eternal fragmental part). The soul is qualitatively identical to the Divine, just as a drop of ocean water is identical to the ocean in composition. The only reason we suffer is because we are 'Karshati' (struggling) with the six senses and the mind, trying to extract permanent happiness from a temporary, material world.

original_script:
शरीरं यदवाप्नोति यच्चाप्युत्क्रामतीश्वरः।
गृहीत्वैतानि संयाति वायुर्गन्धानिवाशयात्।।15.8।।
transliteration:
śharīraṁ yad avāpnoti yach chāpy utkrāmatīśhvaraḥ
gṛihītvaitāni sanyāti vāyur gandhān ivāśhayāt
word_meanings: śharīram—the body; yat—as; avāpnoti—gets; yat—as; cha api—also; utkrāmati—gives up; īśhvaraḥ—the lord of the body; gṛihītvā—taking; etāni—all these; sanyāti—goes away; vāyuḥ—the air; gandhān—smells; iva—like; āśhayāt—from their source
translation_english: The living entity in the material world carries his different conceptions of life from one body to another as the air carries aromas. Thus he takes one kind of body and again quits it to take another.
deep_dive_text: The Transmigration of the Mind. How does reincarnation work? Krishna uses the analogy of the wind. Just as wind carries the fragrance of a flower without carrying the physical flower itself, the subtle body (mind, intelligence, and ego) carries the 'aroma' of your desires, habits, and consciousness from one physical body to the next. You perfectly inherit the psychological state you cultivated in your previous life.

original_script:
श्रोत्रं चक्षुः स्पर्शनं च रसनं घ्राणमेव च।
अधिष्ठाय मनश्चायं विषयानुपसेवते।।15.9।।
transliteration:
śhrotraṁ chakṣhuḥ sparśhanaṁ cha rasanaṁ ghrāṇam eva cha
adhiṣhṭhāya manaśh chāyaṁ viṣhayān upasevate
word_meanings: śhrotram—ears; chakṣhuḥ—eyes; sparśhanam—touch; cha—and; rasanam—tongue; ghrāṇam—smelling power; eva—also; cha—and; adhiṣhṭhāya—being situated in; manaḥ—mind; cha—also; ayam—he; viṣhayān—sense objects; upasevate—enjoys
translation_english: The living entity, thus taking another gross body, obtains a certain type of ear, eye, tongue, nose and sense of touch, which are grouped about the mind. He thus enjoys a particular set of sense objects.
deep_dive_text: The Custom-Built Experience. Based on the 'aroma' of desires carried over from the past life, material nature provides a highly specific, custom-built biological spacesuit (a new body). The senses—ears, eyes, tongue—are precisely calibrated around the mind's central desires, allowing the soul to interact with and 'enjoy' the material world in the exact way it deeply craved.

original_script:
उत्क्रामन्तं स्थितं वापि भुञ्जानं वा गुणान्वितम्।
विमूढा नानुपश्यन्ति पश्यन्ति ज्ञानचक्षुषः।।15.10।।
transliteration:
utkrāmantaṁ sthitaṁ vāpi bhuñjānaṁ vā guṇānvitam
vimūḍhā nānupaśhyanti paśhyanti jñāna-chakṣhuṣhaḥ
word_meanings: utkrāmantam—quitting the body; sthitam—situated in the body; vā api—either; bhuñjānam—enjoying; vā—or; guṇa-anvitam—under the spell of the modes of material nature; vimūḍhāḥ—foolish persons; na—never; anupaśhyanti—can see; paśhyanti—can see; jñāna-chakṣhuṣhaḥ—those who have the eyes of knowledge
translation_english: The foolish cannot understand how a living entity can quit his body, nor can they understand what sort of body he enjoys under the spell of the modes of nature. But one whose eyes are trained in knowledge can see all this.
deep_dive_text: The Blindness of the Foolish. 'Vimudha' (foolish people) only see the physical body. When someone dies, they think the person is entirely gone. When someone is living, they think the person is just chemicals acting out. But those with 'Jnana-chakshusha' (the eyes of knowledge) can see the invisible passenger—the eternal soul—migrating from body to body, driven helplessly by the modes of material nature.

original_script:
यतन्तो योगिनश्चैनं पश्यन्त्यात्मन्यवस्थितम्।
यतन्तोऽप्यकृतात्मानो नैनं पश्यन्त्यचेतसः।।15.11।।
transliteration:
yatanto yoginaśh chainaṁ paśhyanty ātmany avasthitam
yatanto ’py akṛitātmāno nainaṁ paśhyanty achetasaḥ
word_meanings: yatantaḥ—endeavoring; yoginaḥ—transcendentalists; cha—also; enam—this; paśhyanti—can see; ātmani—in the self; avasthitam—situated; yatantaḥ—endeavoring; api—although; akṛita-ātmānaḥ—those without self-realization; na—does not; enam—this; paśhyanti—see; achetasaḥ—having undeveloped minds
translation_english: The endeavoring transcendentalists, who are situated in self-realization, can see all this clearly. But those whose minds are not developed and who are not situated in self-realization cannot see what is taking place, though they may try to.
deep_dive_text: The Prerequisite of Purity. You cannot see spiritual truth just by reading about it; you have to become the right instrument. 'Yatanto Yoginash' (endeavoring yogis) who have purified their consciousness can see the soul clearly. But those with undeveloped, impure minds ('Akritatmanah') remain blind to the reality of the soul, no matter how hard they try to intellectualize or study it.

original_script:
यदादित्यगतं तेजो जगद्भासयतेऽखिलम्।
यच्चन्द्रमसि यच्चाग्नौ तत्तेजो विद्धि मामकम्।।15.12।।
transliteration:
yad āditya-gataṁ tejo jagad bhāsayate ’khilam
yach chandramasi yach chāgnau tat tejo viddhi māmakam
word_meanings: yat—that which; āditya-gatam—in the sunshine; tejaḥ—splendor; jagat—the whole world; bhāsayate—illuminates; akhilam—entire; yat—that which; chandramasi—in the moon; yat—that which; cha—also; agnau—in fire; tat—that; tejaḥ—splendor; viddhi—understand; māmakam—from Me
translation_english: The splendor of the sun, which dissipates the darkness of this whole world, comes from Me. And the splendor of the moon and the splendor of fire are also from Me.
deep_dive_text: The Source of All Energy. Krishna pulls the lens back to the macrocosmic scale. We rely on the Sun for life, the Moon for crop nourishment, and Fire for warmth and digestion. Krishna plainly states that He is the 'Tejas' (splendor/energy) behind all three. Every photon of light and every joule of energy in the universe is simply a physical conversion of His divine power.

original_script:
गामाविश्य च भूतानि धारयाम्यहमोजसा।
पुष्णामि चौषधीः सर्वाः सोमो भूत्वा रसात्मकः।।15.13।।
transliteration:
gām āviśhya cha bhūtāni dhārayāmy aham ojasā
puṣhṇāmi chauṣhadhīḥ sarvāḥ somo bhūtvā rasātmakaḥ
word_meanings: gām—the planets; āviśhya—entering; cha—also; bhūtāni—the living entities; dhārayāmi—sustain; aham—I; ojasā—by My energy; puṣhṇāmi—I nourish; cha—and; auṣhadhīḥ—vegetables; sarvāḥ—all; somaḥ—the moon; bhūtvā—becoming; rasa-ātmakaḥ—supplying the juice
translation_english: I enter into each planet, and by My energy they stay in orbit. I become the moon and thereby supply the juice of life to all vegetables.
deep_dive_text: The Cosmic Gravity and Nourisher. How do massive planets float perfectly in the void of space? Krishna says, 'Gam avishya'—I enter into them. His energy acts as the ultimate gravitational force holding the cosmos together. Furthermore, He acts through the moonlight to nourish the 'Aushadhis' (plants and vegetables), literally providing the life-giving sap that sustains the earth's entire food chain.

original_script:
अहं वैश्वानरो भूत्वा प्राणिनां देहमाश्रितः।
प्राणापानसमायुक्तः पचाम्यन्नं चतुर्विधम्।।15.14।।
transliteration:
ahaṁ vaiśhvānaro bhūtvā prāṇināṁ deham āśhritaḥ
prāṇāpāna-samāyuktaḥ pachāmy annaṁ chatur-vidham
word_meanings: aham—I; vaiśhvānaraḥ—fire of digestion; bhūtvā—becoming; prāṇinām—of all living entities; deham—in the bodies; āśhritaḥ—situated; prāṇa—the outgoing air; apāna—the downgoing air; samāyuktaḥ—keeping in balance; pachāmi—I digest; annam—foodstuff; chatuḥ-vidham—the four kinds
translation_english: I am the fire of digestion in the bodies of all living entities, and I join with the air of life, outgoing and incoming, to digest the four kinds of foodstuff.
deep_dive_text: The Divine Digestion. God is not just 'out there' in the cosmos; He is operating inside your stomach. Krishna identifies as 'Vaishvanara', the biological fire of digestion. He works in tandem with the 'Prana' and 'Apana' (incoming and outgoing breaths/currents) to process the food you eat. Thus, even the mundane biological act of eating and digesting is a divine, metabolic process.

original_script:
सर्वस्य चाहं हृदि सन्निविष्टो
मत्तः स्मृतिर्ज्ञानमपोहनं च।
वेदैश्च सर्वैरहमेव वेद्यो
वेदान्तकृद्वेदविदेव चाहम्।।15.15।।
transliteration:
sarvasya chāhaṁ hṛidi sanniviṣhṭo
mattaḥ smṛitir jñānam apohanaṁ cha
vedaiśh cha sarvair aham eva vedyo
vedānta-kṛid veda-vid eva chāham
word_meanings: sarvasya—of all living beings; cha—and; aham—I; hṛidi—in the heart; sanniviṣhṭaḥ—situated; mattaḥ—from Me; smṛitiḥ—remembrance; jñānam—knowledge; apohanam—forgetfulness; cha—and; vedaiḥ—by the Vedas; cha—also; sarvaiḥ—all; aham—I am; eva—certainly; vedyaḥ—knowable; vedānta-kṛit—the compiler of the Vedanta; veda-vit—the knower of the Vedas; eva—certainly; cha—and; aham—I
translation_english: I am seated in everyone's heart, and from Me come remembrance, knowledge and forgetfulness. By all the Vedas, I am to be known. Indeed, I am the compiler of Vedanta, and I am the knower of the Vedas.
deep_dive_text: The Inner Director. A monumental verse summarizing the relationship between God and the individual. Krishna is 'Hridi Sannivishto'—seated intimately in the heart of every single being. He acts as the inner search engine, providing 'Smriti' (memory), 'Jnanam' (knowledge), and even 'Apohanam' (forgetfulness) based on what the soul desires to experience. Ultimately, the entire vast library of the Vedas was written purely to help us find Him.

original_script:
द्वाविमौ पुरुषौ लोके क्षरश्चाक्षर एव च।
क्षरः सर्वाणि भूतानि कूटस्थोऽक्षर उच्यते।।15.16।।
transliteration:
dvāv imau puruṣhau loke kṣharaśh chākṣhara eva cha
kṣharaḥ sarvāṇi bhūtāni kūṭa-stho ’kṣhara uchyate
word_meanings: dvau—two; imau—these; puruṣhau—living entities; loke—in the world; kṣharaḥ—fallible; cha—and; akṣharaḥ—infallible; eva—certainly; cha—and; kṣharaḥ—fallible; sarvāṇi—all; bhūtāni—living entities; kūṭa-sthaḥ—in oneness; akṣharaḥ—infallible; uchyate—is said
translation_english: There are two classes of beings, the fallible and the infallible. In the material world every living entity is fallible, and in the spiritual world every living entity is called infallible.
deep_dive_text: The Two Categories of Beings. Krishna categorizes all living entities into two groups. 'Kshara' (fallible) refers to souls currently entangled in the material world, subject to mistakes, physical bodies, and the cycle of birth and death. 'Akshara' (infallible) refers to the liberated souls residing in the eternal spiritual realm, perfectly aligned with the Divine and completely free from material illusion.

original_script:
उत्तमः पुरुषस्त्वन्यः परमात्मेत्युदाहृतः।
यो लोकत्रयमाविश्य बिभर्त्यव्यय ईश्वरः।।15.17।।
transliteration:
uttamaḥ puruṣhas tv anyaḥ paramātmety udāhṛitaḥ
yo loka-trayam āviśhya bibharty avyaya īśhvaraḥ
word_meanings: uttamaḥ—the best; puruṣhaḥ—personality; tu—but; anyaḥ—another; parama-ātmā—the Supreme Soul; iti—thus; udāhṛitaḥ—is said; yaḥ—who; loka-trayam—the three planetary systems; āviśhya—entering; bibharti—maintains; avyayaḥ—inexhaustible; īśhvaraḥ—the Lord
translation_english: Besides these two, there is the greatest living personality, the Supreme Soul, the imperishable Lord Himself, who has entered the three worlds and is maintaining them.
deep_dive_text: The term 'Mamaivānśho' (My eternal fragment) defines the soul as qualitatively identical but quantitatively distinct from the Absolute. This is the Gita's 'middle way' between total monism and dualism.

original_script:
यस्मात्क्षरमतीतोऽहमक्षरादपि चोत्तमः।
अतोऽस्मि लोके वेदे च प्रथितः पुरुषोत्तमः।।15.18।।
transliteration:
yasmāt kṣharam atīto ’ham akṣharād api chottamaḥ
ato ’smi loke vede cha prathitaḥ puruṣhottamaḥ
word_meanings: yasmāt—because; kṣharam—to the fallible; atītaḥ—transcendental; aham—I am; akṣharāt—beyond the infallible; api—also; cha—and; uttamaḥ—the best; ataḥ—therefore; asmi—I am; loke—in the world; vede—in the Vedic literature; cha—and; prathitaḥ—celebrated; puruṣha-uttamaḥ—as the Supreme Personality
translation_english: Because I am transcendental, beyond both the fallible and the infallible, and because I am the greatest, I am celebrated both in the world and in the Vedas as that Supreme Person.
deep_dive_text: The Meaning of Purushottama. This verse explains the title of the chapter. Because Krishna is 'Ksharam atitah' (transcendental to the fallible material world) and 'Aksharad api chottamah' (superior even to the infallible liberated souls), He is celebrated everywhere—in the world and in the sacred Vedas—as 'Purushottama', the Supreme Person.

original_script:
यो मामेवमसम्मूढो जानाति पुरुषोत्तमम्।
स सर्वविद्भजति मां सर्वभावेन भारत।।15.19।।
transliteration:
yo mām evam asammūḍho jānāti puruṣhottamam
sa sarva-vid bhajati māṁ sarva-bhāvena bhārata
word_meanings: yaḥ—anyone who; mām—Me; evam—thus; asammūḍhaḥ—without a doubt; jānāti—knows; puruṣha-uttamam—the Supreme Personality of Godhead; saḥ—he; sarva-vit—the knower of everything; bhajati—renders devotional service; mām—unto Me; sarva-bhāvena—in all respects; bhārata—O son of Bharata
translation_english: Whoever knows Me as the Supreme Personality of Godhead, without doubting, is the knower of everything. He therefore engages himself in full devotional service to Me, O son of Bharata.
deep_dive_text: The Culmination of Knowledge. What is the result of truly understanding the concept of Purushottama? A person who grasps this without a shadow of a doubt ('Asammudho') is considered a 'Sarva-vid' (knower of everything). They don't just stop at theoretical knowledge; they naturally engage in 'Sarva-bhavena'—worshipping the Supreme with their entire being, across all emotions and actions.

original_script:
इति गुह्यतमं शास्त्रमिदमुक्तं मयानघ।
एतद्बुद्ध्वा बुद्धिमान्स्यात्कृतकृत्यश्च भारत।।15.20।।
transliteration:
iti guhyatamaṁ śhāstram idam uktaṁ mayānagha
etad buddhvā buddhimān syāt kṛita-kṛityaśh cha bhārata
word_meanings: iti—thus; guhya-tamam—the most confidential; śhāstram—scripture; idam—this; uktam—disclosed; mayā—by Me; anagha—O sinless one; etat—this; buddhvā—understanding; buddhi-mān—intelligent; syāt—one becomes; kṛita-kṛityaḥ—the most perfect in his endeavors; cha—and; bhārata—O son of Bharata
translation_english: This is the most confidential part of the Vedic scriptures, O sinless one, and it is disclosed now by Me. Whoever understands this will become wise, and his endeavors will know perfection.
deep_dive_text: The Most Confidential Secret. Krishna concludes Chapter 15 by declaring it the 'Guhyatamam Shastram' (the most secret or confidential of all scriptures). It is a highly condensed summary of the entire Vedic philosophy. To truly absorb this chapter is to become genuinely 'Buddhiman' (wise) and 'Krita-krityash' (perfect in all endeavors). The ultimate purpose of human life is fulfilled here.

original_script: पुरुषोत्तमयोगः
transliteration: Puruṣhottama Yogaḥ
translation_english: Chapter 15: The Yoga of the Supreme Person
deep_dive_text: Chapter 15 reveals the path of cutting the upside-down Banyan tree of material entanglement using the sharp weapon of detachment. It transitions from explaining the transmigration of the soul and the Divine presence sustaining the universe, to the ultimate philosophical revelation: the identity of the Purushottama. By understanding the Supreme Person who transcends both the fallible and infallible worlds, the seeker attains the highest perfection of wisdom and devotion.

original_script: अथ षोडशोऽध्यायः - दैवासुरसम्पद्विभागयोगः
transliteration: Daivāsura Sampad Vibhāga Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Daiva—divine; Asura—demoniac; Sampad—qualities/nature; Vibhāga—division; Yogaḥ—the path of connection.
translation_english: Chapter 16 Summary: The Yoga of the Divine and Demoniac Natures
deep_dive_text: Summary: Chapter 16 draws a sharp, unmistakable line between two fundamentally opposite psychological and spiritual trajectories: the Divine (Daivi) nature and the Demoniac (Asuri) nature. Krishna outlines 26 qualities of a god-conscious person destined for liberation, followed by the toxic traits of the arrogant and materialistic, who bind themselves to endless suffering. This chapter is a stark behavioral mirror, urging the seeker to ruthlessly eliminate ego, anger, and lust, which are declared to be the three gateways to hell.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
अभयं सत्त्वसंशुद्धिर्ज्ञानयोगव्यवस्थितिः।
दानं दमश्च यज्ञश्च स्वाध्यायस्तप आर्जवम्।।16.1।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
abhayaṁ sattva-sanśhuddhir jñāna-yoga-vyavasthitiḥ
dānaṁ damaśh cha yajñaśh cha svādhyāyas tapa ārjavam
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Personality of Godhead said; abhayam—fearlessness; sattva-sanśhuddhiḥ—purification of one's existence; jñāna-yoga—in the yoga of knowledge; vyavasthitiḥ—the situation; dānam—charity; damaḥ—controlling the mind; cha—and; yajñaḥ—performance of sacrifice; cha—and; svādhyāyaḥ—study of Vedic literature; tapaḥ—austerity; ārjavam—simplicity
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: Fearlessness; purification of one's existence; cultivation of spiritual knowledge; charity; self-control; performance of sacrifice; study of the Vedas; austerity; simplicity...
deep_dive_text: The Foundation of Divine Traits (Part 1). Krishna begins listing the 26 qualities of the 'Daivi Sampad' (Divine Nature). It opens powerfully with 'Abhayam' (fearlessness)—the absolute bedrock of a spiritual life. A seeker cannot practice truth or nonviolence if they are governed by fear. This is followed by purity of heart, cultivation of spiritual knowledge, charity, self-control, and the performance of sacrifice.

original_script:
अहिंसा सत्यमक्रोधस्त्यागः शान्तिरपैशुनम्।
दया भूतेष्वलोलुप्त्वं मार्दवं ह्रीरचापलम्।।16.2।।
transliteration:
ahinsā satyam akrodhas tyāgaḥ śhāntir apaiśhunam
dayā bhūteṣhv aloluptvaṁ mārdavaṁ hrīr achāpalam
word_meanings: ahinsā—nonviolence; satyam—truthfulness; akrodhaḥ—freedom from anger; tyāgaḥ—renunciation; śhāntiḥ—tranquility; apaiśhunam—aversion to fault-finding; dayā—mercy; bhūteṣhu—towards all living entities; aloluptvam—freedom from greed; mārdavam—gentleness; hrīḥ—modesty; achāpalam—steady determination
translation_english: Nonviolence; truthfulness; freedom from anger; renunciation; tranquility; aversion to faultfinding; compassion for all living entities; freedom from covetousness; gentleness; modesty; steady determination...
deep_dive_text: The Foundation of Divine Traits (Part 2). The list continues with virtues that govern our interaction with the world. 'Ahimsa' (nonviolence) is not just physical, but mental and verbal. 'Satyam' (truthfulness) is paired with 'Akrodha' (freedom from anger). Compassion for all beings, gentleness, modesty, and a lack of greedy desire ('Aloluptvam') form the core of a soft, yet incredibly strong, spiritual character.

original_script:
तेजः क्षमा धृतिः शौचमद्रोहो नातिमानिता।
भवन्ति सम्पदं दैवीमभिजातस्य भारत।।16.3।।
transliteration:
tejaḥ kṣhamā dhṛitiḥ śhaucham adroho nāti-mānitā
bhavanti sampadaṁ daivīm abhijātasya bhārata
word_meanings: tejaḥ—vigor; kṣhamā—forgiveness; dhṛitiḥ—fortitude; śhaucham—cleanliness; adrohaḥ—freedom from envy; na—not; ati-mānitā—expectation of honor; bhavanti—are; sampadam—the qualities; daivīm—the transcendental nature; abhijātasya—of one who is born of; bhārata—O son of Bharata
translation_english: Vigor, forgiveness, fortitude, cleanliness, and freedom from envy and from the passion for honor—these transcendental qualities, O son of Bharata, belong to godly men endowed with divine nature.
deep_dive_text: The Foundation of Divine Traits (Part 3). The final traits of the divine nature complete the list of 26. 'Tejah' (vigor or spiritual glow) and 'Dhriti' (determination) ensure the seeker doesn't give up during tough times. Purity, freedom from envy ('Adroho'), and the absence of a demand for honor ('Na ati-manita') protect the ego from inflating. Anyone cultivating these transcendental qualities is naturally aimed toward liberation.

original_script:
दम्भो दर्पोऽभिमानश्च क्रोधः पारुष्यमेव च।
अज्ञानं चाभिजातस्य पार्थ सम्पदमासुरीम्।।16.4।।
transliteration:
dambho darpo ’bhimānaśh cha krodhaḥ pāruṣhyam eva cha
ajñānaṁ chābhijātasya pārtha sampadam āsurīm
word_meanings: dambhaḥ—pride; darpaḥ—arrogance; abhimānaḥ—conceit; cha—and; krodhaḥ—anger; pāruṣhyam—harshness; eva—certainly; cha—and; ajñānam—ignorance; cha—and; abhijātasya—of one who is born of; pārtha—O son of Pritha; sampadam—the qualities; āsurīm—the demoniac nature
translation_english: Pride, arrogance, conceit, anger, harshness and ignorance—these qualities belong to those of demoniac nature, O son of Pritha.
deep_dive_text: The Demoniac Profile. Krishna flips the mirror and shows the 'Asuri Sampad' (Demoniac Nature). Notice that 'demons' in the Gita are not mythical monsters with horns; they are humans governed by a highly toxic psychology. The foundation of this dark nature is 'Dambha' (hypocrisy/pride), fueled by anger and harshness. Ultimately, all these destructive traits stem from one single root: 'Ajnanam' (spiritual ignorance).

original_script:
दैवी सम्पद्विमोक्षाय निबन्धायासुरी मता।
मा शुचः सम्पदं दैवीमभिजातोऽसि पाण्डव।।16.5।।
transliteration:
daivī sampad vimokṣhāya nibandhāyāsurī matā
mā śhuchaḥ sampadaṁ daivīm abhijāto ’si pāṇḍava
word_meanings: daivī—transcendental; sampat—nature; vimokṣhāya—meant for liberation; nibandhāya—for bondage; āsurī—demoniac qualities; matā—are considered; mā—do not; śhuchaḥ—worry; sampadam—nature; daivīm—transcendental; abhijātaḥ—born of; asi—you are; pāṇḍava—O son of Pandu
translation_english: The transcendental qualities are conducive to liberation, whereas the demoniac qualities make for bondage. Do not worry, O son of Pandu, for you are born with the divine qualities.
deep_dive_text: The Destinations of the Two Natures. Krishna clarifies the stakes. Character is destiny. The Divine traits naturally lead to 'Vimokshaya' (total spiritual liberation), while the Demoniac traits lead to 'Nibandhaya' (heavy, inescapable karmic bondage). Sensing Arjuna's sudden anxiety about his own flaws, Krishna gently reassures him: 'Ma shuchah' (Do not worry). Arjuna's innate desire for justice and truth proves he is born with the Divine nature.

original_script:
द्वौ भूतसर्गौ लोकेऽस्मिन्दैव आसुर एव च।
दैवो विस्तरशः प्रोक्त आसुरं पार्थ मे शृणु।।16.6।।
transliteration:
dvau bhūta-sargau loke ’smin daiva āsura eva cha
daivo vistaraśhaḥ prokta āsuraṁ pārtha me śhṛiṇu
word_meanings: dvau—two; bhūta-sargau—created living beings; loke—in world; asmin—this; daivaḥ—godly; āsuraḥ—demoniac; eva—certainly; cha—and; daivaḥ—the divine; vistaraśhaḥ—at great length; proktaḥ—said; āsuram—the demoniac; pārtha—O son of Pritha; me—from Me; śhṛiṇu—hear
translation_english: O son of Pritha, in this world there are two kinds of created beings. One is called the divine and the other demoniac. I have already explained the divine at length. Now hear from Me of the demoniac.
deep_dive_text: The Great Divide. Krishna simplifies human psychology into a binary framework: you are either moving towards light (Daiva) or towards darkness (Asura). Having extensively detailed the 26 traits of a saintly person, He now prepares to do a deep, unflinching psychological autopsy on the toxic, materialistic mindset.

original_script:
प्रवृत्तिं च निवृत्तिं च जना न विदुरासुराः।
न शौचं नापि चाचारो न सत्यं तेषु विद्यते।।16.7।।
transliteration:
pravṛittiṁ cha nivṛittiṁ cha janā na vidur āsurāḥ
na śhauchaṁ nāpi chāchāro na satyaṁ teṣhu vidyate
word_meanings: pravṛittim—acting properly; cha—also; nivṛittim—not acting improperly; cha—and; janāḥ—persons; na—never; viduḥ—know; āsurāḥ—of demoniac quality; na—never; śhaucham—cleanliness; na—nor; api—also; cha—and; āchāraḥ—behavior; na—never; satyam—truth; teṣhu—in them; vidyate—there is
translation_english: Those who are demoniac do not know what is to be done and what is not to be done. Neither cleanliness nor proper behavior nor truth is found in them.
deep_dive_text: The Lost Moral Compass. The first casualty of a demoniac mindset is the loss of direction. They simply do not know 'Pravritti' (what actions elevate a person) and 'Nivritti' (what actions degrade a person). Because their internal compass is broken, their external lives reflect chaos: there is no 'Shaucham' (internal or external purity), no noble 'Achara' (conduct), and absolutely no 'Satyam' (truthfulness).

original_script:
असत्यमप्रतिष्ठं ते जगदाहुरनीश्वरम्।
अपरस्परसम्भूतं किमन्यत्कामहैतुकम्।।16.8।।
transliteration:
asatyam apratiṣhṭhaṁ te jagad āhur anīśhvaram
aparaspara-sambhūtaṁ kim anyat kāma-haitukam
word_meanings: asatyam—unreal; apratiṣhṭham—without foundation; te—they; jagat—the cosmic manifestation; āhuḥ—say; anīśhvaram—with no controller; aparaspara—without cause; sambhūtam—arisen; kim anyat—there is no other cause; kāma-haitukam—it is due to lust only
translation_english: They say that this world is unreal, with no foundation, no God in control. They say it is produced of sex desire and has no cause other than lust.
deep_dive_text: The Atheistic Worldview. Behavior is driven by belief. Krishna outlines the worldview of the highly materialistic person: They believe the universe is 'Asatyam' (a random illusion) and 'Anishvaram' (godless, without a moral foundation). They believe life is merely a biological accident ('Aparaspara-sambhutam') driven entirely by 'Kama' (lust/desire) and chemical reactions, stripping human existence of any higher, sacred purpose.

original_script:
एतां दृष्टिमवष्टभ्य नष्टात्मानोऽल्पबुद्धयः।
प्रभवन्त्युग्रकर्माणः क्षयाय जगतोऽहिताः।।16.9।।
transliteration:
etāṁ dṛiṣhṭim avaṣhṭabhya naṣhṭātmāno ’lpa-buddhayaḥ
prabhavanty ugra-karmāṇaḥ kṣhayāya jagato ’hitāḥ
word_meanings: etām—this; dṛiṣhṭim—vision; avaṣhṭabhya—accepting; naṣhṭa—having lost; ātmānaḥ—themselves; alpa-buddhayaḥ—the less intelligent; prabhavanti—flourish; ugra-karmāṇaḥ—engaged in painful activities; kṣhayāya—for destruction; jagataḥ—of the world; ahitāḥ—unbeneficial
translation_english: Following such conclusions, the demoniac, who are lost to themselves and who have no intelligence, engage in unbeneficial, horrible works meant to destroy the world.
deep_dive_text: The Danger of Destructive Innovation. If you believe the universe is a godless, purposeless accident, morality becomes a joke. Krishna warns that people with this 'Alpa-buddhaya' (short-sighted intelligence) become 'Nashtatmanah' (lost souls). Armed with brilliant minds but zero empathy, they engage in 'Ugra-karmanah' (cruel, horrible industries or actions) that eventually lead to the 'Kshayaya' (destruction) of the planet and its people.

original_script:
काममाश्रित्य दुष्पूरं दम्भमानमदान्विताः।
मोहाद्गृहीत्वासद्ग्राहान्प्रवर्तन्तेऽशुचिव्रताः।।16.10।।
transliteration:
kāmam āśhritya duṣhpūraṁ dambha-māna-madānvitāḥ
mohād gṛihītvāsad-grāhān pravartante ’śhuchi-vratāḥ
word_meanings: kāmam—lust; āśhritya—taking shelter of; duṣhpūram—insatiable; dambha—of pride; māna—and false prestige; mada-anvitāḥ—absorbed in the conceit; mohāt—by illusion; gṛihītvā—taking; asat—nonpermanent; grāhān—things; pravartante—they flourish; aśhuchi—to the unclean; vratāḥ—avowed
translation_english: Taking shelter of insatiable lust and absorbed in the conceit of pride and false prestige, the demoniac, thus illusioned, are always sworn to unclean work, attracted by the impermanent.
deep_dive_text: The Cult of Insatiable Ego. The defining driver of this toxic mindset is 'Dushpuram Kamam' (lust that can never be filled). Like trying to put out a fire with gasoline, their desires only grow stronger. Blinded by 'Dambha' (vanity) and 'Mada' (narcissistic pride), they adopt 'Ashuchi-vratah' (corrupt or unethical principles) just to secure wealth, fame, or power, chasing things that are inherently 'Asat' (temporary and unreal).

original_script:
चिन्तामपरिमेयां च प्रलयान्तामुपाश्रिताः।
कामोपभोगपरमा एतावदिति निश्चिताः।।16.11।।
transliteration:
chintām aparimeyāṁ cha pralayāntām upāśhritāḥ
kāmopabhoga-paramā etāvad iti niśhchitāḥ
word_meanings: chintām—fears and anxieties; aparimeyām—unmeasurable; cha—and; pralaya-antām—unto the point of death; upāśhritāḥ—having taken shelter of; kāma-upabhoga—sense gratification; paramāḥ—the highest goal of life; etāvat—thus; iti—in this way; niśhchitāḥ—having ascertained
translation_english: They believe that to gratify the senses is the prime necessity of human civilization. Thus until the end of life their anxiety is immeasurable.
deep_dive_text: The Curse of Infinite Anxiety. This is a brilliant psychological insight. If you believe that 'Kama-upabhoga' (sense gratification and accumulating stuff) is the highest purpose of life, you are mathematically guaranteed to be miserable. Why? Because the senses are never fully satisfied. This belief structure traps the mind in 'Chintam aparimeyam'—an unmeasurable, crushing anxiety that haunts them literally until the moment they die ('Pralayantam').

original_script:
आशापाशशतैर्बद्धाः कामक्रोधपरायणाः।
ईहन्ते कामभोगार्थमन्यायेनार्थसञ्चयान्।।16.12।।
transliteration:
āśhā-pāśha-śhatair baddhāḥ kāma-krodha-parāyaṇāḥ
īhante kāma-bhogārtham anyāyenārtha-sañchayān
word_meanings: āśhā-pāśha—entanglements in a network of hope; śhataiḥ—by hundreds; baddhāḥ—being bound; kāma—of lust; krodha—and anger; parāyaṇāḥ—always situated in the mentality; īhante—they desire; kāma—lust; bhoga—sense enjoyment; artham—for the purpose of; anyāyena—illegally; artha—of wealth; sañchayān—accumulation
translation_english: Bound by a network of hundreds of thousands of desires and absorbed in lust and anger, they secure money by illegal means for sense gratification.
deep_dive_text: The Hustle of Greed. Krishna paints a picture of modern corporate and worldly entanglement. These individuals are 'Baddhah' (bound tightly) by 'Asha-pasha' (hundreds of nooses of hope and expectation). Constantly bouncing between lust (when they want something) and anger (when they don't get it), they completely abandon ethics. They 'Ihante' (strive) to hoard massive wealth 'Anyayena' (through unjust, illegal, or unethical means) simply to fund their addictions.

original_script:
इदमद्य मया लब्धमिमं प्राप्स्ये मनोरथम्।
इदमस्तीदमपि मे भविष्यति पुनर्धनम्।।16.13।।
transliteration:
idam adya mayā labdham imaṁ prāpsye manoratham
idam astīdam api me bhaviṣhyati punar dhanam
word_meanings: idam—this; adya—today; mayā—by me; labdham—gained; imam—this; prāpsye—I shall gain; manaḥ-ratham—according to my desires; idam—this; asti—there is; idam—this; api—also; me—mine; bhaviṣhyati—it will increase in the future; punaḥ—again; dhanam—wealth
translation_english: The demoniac person thinks: 'So much wealth do I have today, and I will gain more according to my schemes. So much is mine now, and it will increase in the future, more and more.'
deep_dive_text: The Inner Monologue of Materialism. Krishna acts as a mind reader, revealing the exact internal monologue of a greedy person. Their mind is entirely trapped in a loop of acquisition: 'I got this today. Tomorrow I'll conquer that market. This money is mine, and next year my portfolio will be even bigger.' It is the ultimate delusion of control, completely ignoring the fragile, temporary nature of human life.

original_script:
असौ मया हतः शत्रुर्हनिष्ये चापरानपि।
ईश्वरोऽहमहं भोगी सिद्धोऽहं बलवान्सुखी।।16.14।।
transliteration:
asau mayā hataḥ śhatrur haniṣhye chāparān api
īśhvaro ’ham ahaṁ bhogī siddho ’haṁ balavān sukhī
word_meanings: asau—that; mayā—by me; hataḥ—has been killed; śhatruḥ—enemy; haniṣhye—I shall kill; cha—also; aparān—others; api—certainly; īśhvaraḥ—the lord; aham—I am; aham—I am; bhogī—the enjoyer; siddhaḥ—perfect; aham—I am; balavān—powerful; sukhī—happy
translation_english: The demoniac person thinks: 'He is my enemy, and I have killed him, and my other enemies will also be killed. I am the lord of everything. I am the enjoyer. I am perfect, powerful and happy.'
deep_dive_text: The God Complex. The toxic mindset peaks in pure megalomania. The individual claims the position of 'Ishvara' (the Lord) for themselves. They believe their success is entirely self-made ('Siddho aham') and their power gives them the absolute right to destroy their enemies. They become the judge, jury, and executioner of their own reality, completely blinded to the laws of karma and the temporary nature of their power.

original_script:
आढ्योऽभिजनवानस्मि कोऽन्योऽस्ति सदृशो मया।
यक्ष्ये दास्यामि मोदिष्य इत्यज्ञानविमोहिताः।।16.15।।
transliteration:
āḍhyo ’bhijanavān asmi ko ’nyo ’sti sadṛiśho mayā
yakṣhye dāsyāmi modiṣhya ity ajñāna-vimohitāḥ
word_meanings: āḍhyaḥ—wealthy; abhijanavān—surrounded by aristocratic relatives; asmi—I am; kaḥ—who; anyaḥ—other; asti—there is; sadṛiśhaḥ—similar; mayā—to me; yakṣhye—I shall perform sacrifices; dāsyāmi—I shall give charity; modiṣhye—I shall rejoice; iti—thus; ajñāna—by ignorance; vimohitāḥ—deluded
translation_english: 'I am the richest man, surrounded by aristocratic relatives. There is none so powerful and happy as I am. I shall perform sacrifices, I shall give some charity, and thus I shall rejoice.' In this way, such persons are deluded by ignorance.
deep_dive_text: The Weaponization of Charity. Even their philanthropy is corrupt. They don't give to help others; they perform 'charity' (Dasyami) and host grand events (Yakshye) merely to feed their ego, flaunt their wealth ('Adhyo asmi'), and solidify their aristocratic status. Krishna diagnoses this not as greatness, but as 'Ajnana-vimohitah' (severe delusion caused by ignorance). Their good deeds are just PR stunts for their narcissism.

original_script:
अनेकचित्तविभ्रान्ता मोहजालसमावृताः।
प्रसक्ताः कामभोगेषु पतन्ति नरकेऽशुचौ।।16.16।।
transliteration:
aneka-chitta-vibhrāntā moha-jāla-samāvṛitāḥ
prasaktāḥ kāma-bhogeṣhu patanti narake ’śhuchau
word_meanings: aneka—numerous; chitta—by anxieties; vibhrāntāḥ—perplexed; moha—of illusions; jāla—by a network; samāvṛitāḥ—surrounded; prasaktāḥ—attached; kāma-bhogeṣhu—to sense gratification; patanti—they glide down; narake—into hell; aśhuchau—unclean
translation_english: Thus perplexed by various anxieties and bound by a network of illusions, they become too strongly attached to sense enjoyment and fall down into a foul hell.
deep_dive_text: The Psychological Hell. What is the result of endless ambition and ego? It isn't peace. It is 'Aneka-chitta-vibhranta' (a fragmented, perpetually anxious mind). Caught in a 'Moha-jala' (a dense web of illusions), they chase sense gratification until they exhaust their karma. Krishna states they fall into a 'foul hell.' This hell isn't necessarily an afterlife destination; it often begins right here as a suffocating, deeply unfulfilled, and paranoid psychological state.

original_script:
आत्मसम्भाविताः स्तब्धा धनमानमदान्विताः।
यजन्ते नामयज्ञैस्ते दम्भेनाविधिपूर्वकम्।।16.17।।
transliteration:
ātma-sambhāvitāḥ stabdhā dhana-māna-madānvitāḥ
yajante nāma-yajñais te dambhenāvidhi-pūrvakam
word_meanings: ātma-sambhāvitāḥ—self-complacent; stabdhāḥ—impudent; dhana-māna—of wealth and false prestige; mada-anvitāḥ—absorbed in the conceit; yajante—they perform sacrifice; nāma—in name only; yajñaiḥ—with sacrifices; te—they; dambhena—out of pride; avidhi-pūrvakam—without following any rules and regulations
translation_english: Self-complacent and always impudent, deluded by wealth and false prestige, they sometimes proudly perform sacrifices in name only, without following any rules or regulations.
deep_dive_text: The Hypocrisy of Spirituality. The demoniac nature loves the aesthetic of spirituality without the discipline. They are 'Atma-sambhavitah' (self-obsessed) and 'Stabdhah' (stubbornly impudent). When they engage in religious or spiritual practices, it is a 'Nama-yajna' (a sacrifice in name only). They do it 'Dambhena' (with extreme pride) and completely ignore the actual rules ('Avidhi-purvakam'), customizing spirituality to suit their ego rather than submitting to a higher truth.

original_script:
अहङ्कारं बलं दर्पं कामं क्रोधं च संश्रिताः।
मामात्मपरदेहेषु प्रद्विषन्तोऽभ्यसूयकाः।।16.18।।
transliteration:
ahaṅkāraṁ balaṁ darpaṁ kāmaṁ krodhaṁ cha sanśhritāḥ
mām ātma-para-deheṣhu pradviṣhanto ’bhyasūyakāḥ
word_meanings: ahaṅkāram—false ego; balam—strength; darpam—pride; kāmam—lust; krodham—anger; cha—also; sanśhritāḥ—having taken shelter of; mām—Me; ātma—in their own; para—and in other; deheṣhu—bodies; pradviṣhantaḥ—blaspheming; abhyasūyakāḥ—envious
translation_english: Bewildered by false ego, strength, pride, lust and anger, the demons become envious of the Supreme Personality of Godhead, who is situated in their own bodies and in the bodies of others, and blaspheme against the real religion.
deep_dive_text: The Root of Envy. Taking shelter of their own massive ego, strength, and anger, these individuals develop a deep-seated envy. They don't just hate external authority; they hate the Divine presence ('Mam') situated within 'Atma-para-deheshu' (their own bodies and the bodies of others). By abusing their own bodies through toxic habits and exploiting the bodies of others, they are actively warring against the Supreme Spirit housed within.

original_script:
तानहं द्विषतः क्रूरान्संसारेषु नराधमान्।
क्षिपाम्यजस्रमशुभानासुरीष्वेव योनिषु।।16.19।।
transliteration:
tān ahaṁ dviṣhataḥ krūrān saṁsāreṣhu narādhamān
kṣhipāmy ajasram aśhubhān āsurīṣhv eva yoniṣhu
word_meanings: tān—those; aham—I; dviṣhataḥ—envious; krūrān—mischievous; saṁsāreṣhu—into the ocean of material existence; nara-adhamān—the lowest of mankind; kṣhipāmi—I put; ajasram—forever; aśhubhān—inauspicious; āsurīṣhu—demoniac; eva—certainly; yoniṣhu—into the wombs
translation_english: Those who are envious and mischievous, who are the lowest among men, I perpetually cast into the ocean of material existence, into various demoniac species of life.
deep_dive_text: The Karmic Downgrade. Krishna delivers the stark consequence of a chronically toxic life. People who dedicate their lives to cruelty ('Kruran') and malicious envy ('Dvishatah') degrade their own consciousness to such a degree that they are classified as 'Naradhaman' (the lowest of mankind). The universe obliges their choices: they are perpetually cast ('Kshipami ajasram') into lower, ignorant, and aggressive species ('Asurishu yonishu') where their hostile nature finds an appropriate biological match.

original_script:
आसुरीं योनिमापन्ना मूढा जन्मनि जन्मनि।
मामप्राप्यैव कौन्तेय ततो यान्त्यधमां गतिम्।।16.20।।
transliteration:
āsurīṁ yonim āpannā mūḍhā janmani janmani
mām aprāpyaiva kaunteya tato yānty adhamāṁ gatim
word_meanings: āsurīm—demoniac; yonim—wombs; āpannāḥ—gaining; mūḍhāḥ—the foolish; janmani janmani—in birth after birth; mām—Me; aprāpya—without achieving; eva—certainly; kaunteya—O son of Kunti; tataḥ—thereafter; yānti—go; adhamām—condemned; gatim—destination
translation_english: Attaining repeated birth amongst the species of demoniac life, O son of Kunti, such persons can never approach Me. Gradually they sink down to the most abominable type of existence.
deep_dive_text: The Downward Spiral. Once a soul enters these dark, aggressive species ('Asurim yonim'), it is incredibly difficult to break out. Because their environment and biology are wired for pure survival, lust, and anger, they miss the opportunity for spiritual realization ('Mam aprapya'). Without a conscious intervention of grace, they continue to spiral downward, birth after birth, hitting the absolute rock bottom of existential awareness ('Adhamam gatim').

original_script:
त्रिविधं नरकस्येदं द्वारं नाशनमात्मनः।
कामः क्रोधस्तथा लोभस्तस्मादेतत्त्रयं त्यजेत्।।16.21।।
transliteration:
tri-vidhaṁ narakasyedaṁ dvāraṁ nāśhanam ātmanaḥ
kāmaḥ krodhas tathā lobhas tasmād etat trayaṁ tyajet
word_meanings: tri-vidham—of three kinds; narakasya—of hell; idam—this; dvāram—gate; nāśhanam—destructive; ātmanaḥ—of the self; kāmaḥ—lust; krodhaḥ—anger; tathā—as well as; lobhaḥ—greed; tasmāt—therefore; etat—these; trayam—three; tyajet—one must give up
translation_english: There are three gates leading to this hell—lust, anger and greed. Every sane man should give these up, for they lead to the degradation of the soul.
deep_dive_text: The Three Gates to Hell. This is one of the most practical, universally applicable verses in the Gita. Krishna summarizes the entire complex 'demoniac' profile into three simple, toxic roots: Kama (Lust/Unchecked Desire), Krodha (Anger), and Lobha (Greed). These are the direct entryways to personal and spiritual destruction ('Nashanam atmanah'). Because they feed each other—unfulfilled lust breeds anger, and fulfilled lust breeds greed—they must be aggressively abandoned ('Tyajet').

original_script:
एतैर्विमुक्तः कौन्तेय तमोद्वारैस्त्रिभिर्नरः।
आचरत्यात्मनः श्रेयस्ततो याति परां गतिम्।।16.22।।
transliteration:
etair vimuktaḥ kaunteya tamo-dvārais tribhir naraḥ
ācharaty ātmanaḥ śhreyas tato yāti parāṁ gatim
word_meanings: etaiḥ—from these; vimuktaḥ—being liberated; kaunteya—O son of Kunti; tamaḥ-dvāraiḥ—from the gates of ignorance; tribhiḥ—of three kinds; naraḥ—a person; ācharati—performs; ātmanaḥ—for the self; śhreyaḥ—benediction; tataḥ—thereafter; yāti—he goes; parām—to the supreme; gatim—destination
translation_english: The man who has escaped these three gates of hell, O son of Kunti, performs acts conducive to self-realization and thus gradually attains the supreme destination.
deep_dive_text: The Freedom of Escape. What happens when you shut the three gates? When a person becomes 'Vimuktah' (liberated) from the gravitational pull of lust, anger, and greed, their energy is suddenly freed. They are no longer constantly putting out emotional fires or chasing mirages. This newfound clarity allows them to actually perform actions that are 'Shreyas' (truly beneficial for the soul), naturally elevating them toward the ultimate, supreme destination.

original_script:
यः शास्त्रविधिमुत्सृज्य वर्तते कामकारतः।
न स सिद्धिमवाप्नोति न सुखं न परां गतिम्।।16.23।।
transliteration:
yaḥ śhāstra-vidhim utsṛijya vartate kāma-kārataḥ
na sa siddhim avāpnoti na sukhaṁ na parāṁ gatim
word_meanings: yaḥ—anyone who; śhāstra-vidhim—the regulations of the scriptures; utsṛijya—giving up; vartate—remains; kāma-kārataḥ—acting whimsically in lust; na—never; saḥ—he; siddhim—perfection; avāpnoti—achieves; na—never; sukham—happiness; na—never; parām—the supreme; gatim—perfectional stage
translation_english: He who discards scriptural injunctions and acts according to his own whims attains neither perfection, nor happiness, nor the supreme destination.
deep_dive_text: The Danger of Whimsical Action. How do we know what is 'lust' and what is 'right'? Krishna introduces the guardrails of life: 'Shastra-vidhim' (the rules and regulations of sacred wisdom). A person who throws out the map and acts purely on 'Kama-karatah' (the whims of their own immediate desires) guarantees three failures: they will never achieve 'Siddhim' (success/perfection), they will never find true 'Sukham' (happiness), and they will certainly miss the 'Param gatim' (the supreme destination).

original_script:
तस्माच्छास्त्रं प्रमाणं ते कार्याकार्यव्यवस्थितौ।
ज्ञात्वा शास्त्रविधानोक्तं कर्म कर्तुमिहार्हसि।।16.24।।
transliteration:
tasmāch chhāstraṁ pramāṇaṁ te kāryākārya-vyavasthitau
jñātvā śhāstra-vidhānoktaṁ karma kartum ihārhasi
word_meanings: tasmāt—therefore; śhāstram—the scriptures; pramāṇam—evidence; te—your; kārya—duty; akārya—and forbidden activities; vyavasthitau—in determining; jñātvā—knowing; śhāstra—of scripture; vidhāna—the rules and regulations; uktam—as declared; karma—work; kartum—do; iha—in this world; arhasi—you should
translation_english: Therefore, let the scriptures be your authority in determining what should be done and what should not be done. Understanding the scriptural regulations, you should perform your duty in this world.
deep_dive_text: The Ultimate Authority. Krishna concludes the chapter by establishing the ultimate standard for human behavior. To escape the demoniac nature and the three gates of hell, one must surrender their fragile ego and whims to the 'Shastram' (sacred texts and universal wisdom). The scriptures act as the 'Pramanam' (the reliable guide or ultimate evidence) to navigate the complexities of 'Karya' (what to do) and 'Akarya' (what to avoid). True freedom is found within the guardrails of divine law.

original_script: दैवासुरसम्पद्विभागयोगः
transliteration: Daivāsura Sampad Vibhāga Yogaḥ
translation_english: Chapter 16: The Yoga of the Divine and Demoniac Natures
deep_dive_text: Chapter 16 provides a stark behavioral mirror. Krishna details the 26 qualities of the Divine Nature—rooted in fearlessness, purity, and compassion—which guarantee spiritual liberation. He then ruthlessly exposes the Demoniac Nature—driven by pride, atheism, and the insatiable triad of lust, anger, and greed (the three gates to hell). The chapter concludes that human beings cannot rely on their own whimsical desires for guidance; they must anchor their actions in the timeless wisdom of the scriptures to achieve true perfection.

original_script: अथ सप्तदशोऽध्यायः - श्रद्धात्रयविभागयोगः
transliteration: Atha Saptadaśho ’dhyāyaḥ - Śhraddhā Traya Vibhāga Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Śhraddhā—faith; Traya—threefold; Vibhāga—divisions; Yogaḥ—the path of connection.
translation_english: Chapter 17 Summary: The Yoga of the Threefold Divisions of Faith
deep_dive_text: Summary: Chapter 17 picks up immediately where Chapter 16 left off. Arjuna asks a very practical question: 'What happens to someone who has faith, but doesn't follow the scriptures?' In response, Krishna introduces the psychology of 'Shraddha' (Faith). He explains that every single human being has faith, but that faith is colored by the three modes of nature (Sattva, Rajas, and Tamas). This chapter acts as a diagnostic toolkit, breaking down how our modes dictate the food we eat, the charity we give, the austerities we perform, and the gods we worship.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
ये शास्त्रविधिमुत्सृज्य यजन्ते श्रद्धयान्विताः।
तेषां निष्ठा तु का कृष्ण सत्त्वमाहो रजस्तमः।।17.1।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
ye śhāstra-vidhim utsṛijya yajante śhraddhayānvitāḥ
teṣhāṁ niṣhṭhā tu kā kṛiṣhṇa sattvam āho rajas tamaḥ
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjuna said; ye—those who; śhāstra-vidhim—the regulations of scripture; utsṛijya—giving up; yajante—worship; śhraddhayā—with faith; anvitāḥ—possessed of; teṣhām—of them; niṣhṭhā—the faith; tu—but; kā—what; kṛiṣhṇa—O Krishna; sattvam—in goodness; āho—or; rajaḥ—in passion; tamaḥ—in ignorance
translation_english: Arjuna inquired: O Krishna, what is the situation of those who do not follow the principles of scripture but worship according to their own imagination? Are they in goodness, in passion or in ignorance?
deep_dive_text: The Question of Blind Faith. Arjuna brings up a massive gray area. Chapter 16 said you *must* follow scriptures, and those who don't are demoniac. But what about people who are genuinely sincere, faithful, and worshipful, but simply invent their own rules or follow unauthorized traditions? Are they automatically doomed, or does their 'Shraddhaya' (faith) count for something? Where do they fit into the three modes of nature?

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
त्रिविधा भवति श्रद्धा देहिनां सा स्वभावजा।
सात्त्विकी राजसी चैव तामसी चेति तां शृणु।।17.2।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
tri-vidhā bhavati śhraddhā dehināṁ sā svabhāva-jā
sāttvikī rājasī chaiva tāmasī cheti tāṁ śhṛiṇu
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Personality of Godhead said; tri-vidhā—of three kinds; bhavati—becomes; śhraddhā—the faith; dehinām—of the embodied; sā—that; sva-bhāva-jā—born of their own nature; sāttvikī—in the mode of goodness; rājasī—in the mode of passion; cha—and; eva—certainly; tāmasī—in the mode of ignorance; cha—and; iti—thus; tām—about that; śhṛiṇu—hear from Me
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: According to the modes of nature acquired by the embodied soul, one's faith can be of three kinds—in goodness, in passion or in ignorance. Now hear about this.
deep_dive_text: Faith is Biological. Krishna answers by revealing that faith is not just a choice you make; it is 'Svabhava-ja' (born of your nature). The quality of your faith is directly determined by the karmic baggage and the specific mix of the three modes (Gunas) you have acquired over lifetimes. Therefore, not all faith is equal. You can have pure faith (Sattviki), ambitious/greedy faith (Rajasi), or dark, destructive faith (Tamasi).

original_script:
सत्त्वानुरूपा सर्वस्य श्रद्धा भवति भारत।
श्रद्धामयोऽयं पुरुषो यो यच्छ्रद्धः स एव सः।।17.3।।
transliteration:
sattvānurūpā sarvasya śhraddhā bhavati bhārata
śhraddhāmayo ’yaṁ puruṣho yo yach-chhraddhaḥ sa eva saḥ
word_meanings: sattva-anurūpā—according to the existence; sarvasya—of everyone; śhraddhā—faith; bhavati—is; bhārata—O son of Bharata; śhraddhā-mayaḥ—full of faith; ayam—this; puruṣhaḥ—living entity; yaḥ—who; yat—whatever; śhraddhaḥ—faith; saḥ—he; eva—certainly; saḥ—he
translation_english: O son of Bharata, according to one's existence under the various modes of nature, one evolves a particular kind of faith. The living being is said to be of a particular faith according to the modes he has acquired.
deep_dive_text: You Are Your Faith. This is a profound psychological axiom: 'Yo yach-chraddhah sa eva sah' (You are what your faith is). Humans are fundamentally 'Shraddhamayo' (creatures made of faith). You don't just *have* a belief system; your belief system *is* you. If you place your absolute faith in money, you become a materialistic creature. If you place your faith in the Divine, you become a divine creature.

original_script:
यजन्ते सात्त्विका देवान्यक्षरक्षांसि राजसाः।
प्रेतान्भूतगणांश्चान्ये यजन्ते तामसा जनाः।।17.4।।
transliteration:
yajante sāttvikā devān yakṣha-rakṣhānsi rājasāḥ
pretān bhūta-gaṇānśh chānye yajante tāmasā janāḥ
word_meanings: yajante—worship; sāttvikāḥ—those who are in the mode of goodness; devān—demigods; yakṣha-rakṣhānsi—demons; rājasāḥ—those who are in the mode of passion; pretān—spirits of the dead; bhūta-gaṇān—ghosts; cha—and; anye—others; yajante—worship; tāmasāḥ—in the mode of ignorance; janāḥ—people
translation_english: Men in the mode of goodness worship the demigods; those in the mode of passion worship the demons; and those in the mode of ignorance worship ghosts and spirits.
deep_dive_text: The Targets of Worship. How do you know what mode someone is in? Look at who they idolize. Sattvic (Good) people worship 'Devas' (beings of light, higher principles, and the divine). Rajasic (Passionate) people worship 'Yaksha-rakshansi' (powerful, wealthy, or ruthless figures, celebrities, and tycoons). Tamasic (Ignorant) people worship 'Pretan bhuta-ganans' (ghosts, dark spirits, and destructive, macabre forces). You worship what you secretly want to become.

original_script:
अशास्त्रविहितं घोरं तप्यन्ते ये तपो जनाः।
दम्भाहङ्कारसंयुक्ताः कामरागबलान्विताः।।17.5।।
कर्षयन्तः शरीरस्थं भूतग्राममचेतसः।
मां चैवान्तःशरीरस्थं तान्विद्ध्यासुरनिश्चयान्।।17.6।।
transliteration:
aśhāstra-vihitaṁ ghoraṁ tapyante ye tapo janāḥ
dambhāhaṅkāra-samyuktāḥ kāma-rāga-balānvitāḥ
karṣhayantaḥ śharīra-sthaṁ bhūta-grāmam achetasaḥ
māṁ chaivāntaḥ śharīra-sthaṁ tān viddhy āsura-niśhchayān
word_meanings: aśhāstra—not in the scriptures; vihitam—directed; ghoram—harmful; tapyante—undergo; ye—those who; tapaḥ—austerities; janāḥ—persons; dambha—with pride; ahaṅkāra—and egoism; samyuktāḥ—engaged; kāma—of lust; rāga—and attachment; bala—by the force; anvitāḥ—impelled; karṣhayantaḥ—tormenting; śharīra-stham—situated within the body; bhūta-grāmam—the combination of material elements; achetasaḥ—having a misled mentality; mām—Me; cha—also; eva—certainly; antaḥ—within; śharīra-stham—situated in the body; tān—them; viddhi—understand; āsura-niśhchayān—demons
translation_english: Those who undergo severe austerities and penances not recommended in the scriptures, performing them out of pride and egoism, who are impelled by lust and attachment, who are foolish and who torture the material elements of the body as well as the Supersoul dwelling within, are to be known as demons.
deep_dive_text: The Cult of Extreme Austerity. (Grouped to show the full diagnosis). Krishna warns against 'Ghoram Tapo' (extreme, unauthorized self-torture). People who starve themselves to the bone or inflict pain on their bodies for spiritual clout or superpowers are not holy; they are driven by 'Dambhahankara' (hypocritical pride). By torturing the biology of the body, they are actively torturing the Divine ('Mam') seated within it. Such unauthorized, painful fanaticism is the hallmark of the demoniac mindset.

original_script:
आहारस्त्वपि सर्वस्य त्रिविधो भवति प्रियः।
यज्ञस्तपस्तथा दानं तेषां भेदमिमं शृणु।।17.7।।
transliteration:
āhāras tv api sarvasya tri-vidho bhavati priyaḥ
yajñas tapas tathā dānaṁ teṣhāṁ bhedam imaṁ śhṛiṇu
word_meanings: āhāraḥ—eating; tu—certainly; api—also; sarvasya—of everyone; tri-vidhaḥ—of three kinds; bhavati—is; priyaḥ—dear; yajñaḥ—sacrifice; tapaḥ—austerity; tathā—also; dānam—charity; teṣhām—of them; bhedam—the differences; imam—this; śhṛiṇu—hear
translation_english: Even the food each person prefers is of three kinds, according to the three modes of material nature. The same is true of sacrifices, austerities and charity. Now hear of the distinctions between them.
deep_dive_text: The Diagnostic Toolkit. Having explained how the modes dictate our faith and worship, Krishna introduces four observable, everyday behaviors that act as a perfect psychological assessment tool: 1. Ahara (Diet/Food), 2. Yajna (Sacrifice/Worship), 3. Tapas (Discipline/Austerity), and 4. Danam (Charity). By analyzing *how* you eat and give, you can immediately identify which mode is controlling your mind.

original_script:
आयुःसत्त्वबलारोग्यसुखप्रीतिविवर्धनाः।
रस्याः स्निग्धाः स्थिरा हृद्या आहाराः सात्त्विकप्रियाः।।17.8।।
transliteration:
āyuḥ-sattva-balārogya-sukha-prīti-vivardhanāḥ
rasyāḥ snigdhāḥ sthirā hṛidyā āhārāḥ sāttvika-priyāḥ
word_meanings: āyuḥ—duration of life; sattva—existence; bala—strength; ārogya—health; sukha—happiness; prīti—and satisfaction; vivardhanāḥ—increasing; rasyāḥ—juicy; snigdhāḥ—fatty; sthirāḥ—enduring; hṛidyāḥ—pleasing to the heart; āhārāḥ—food; sāttvika—to one in goodness; priyāḥ—palatable
translation_english: Foods dear to those in the mode of goodness increase the duration of life, purify one's existence and give strength, health, happiness and satisfaction. Such foods are juicy, fatty, wholesome, and pleasing to the heart.
deep_dive_text: The Sattvic Diet. Krishna begins with food. People in the mode of Goodness naturally crave foods that are 'Ayuh' (life-extending), 'Arogya' (health-giving), and 'Priti' (satisfying). These foods are 'Rasya' (juicy/fresh), 'Snigdha' (naturally fatty/nourishing, like ghee or nuts), and 'Sthira' (wholesome). Sattvic food isn't just fuel; it fundamentally purifies the consciousness and brings quiet joy to the heart.

original_script:
कट्वम्ललवणात्युष्णतीक्ष्णरूक्षविदाहिनः।
आहारा राजसस्येष्टा दुःखशोकामयप्रदाः।।17.9।।
transliteration:
kaṭv-amla-lavaṇāty-uṣhṇa- tīkṣhṇa-rūkṣha-vidāhinaḥ
āhārā rājasasyeṣhṭā duḥkha-śhokāmaya-pradāḥ
word_meanings: kaṭu—bitter; amla—sour; lavaṇa—salty; ati-uṣhṇa—very hot; tīkṣhṇa—pungent; rūkṣha—dry; vidāhinaḥ—burning; āhārāḥ—food; rājasasya—to one in the mode of passion; iṣhṭāḥ—dear; duḥkha—distress; śhoka—misery; amaya—disease; pradāḥ—causing
translation_english: Foods that are too bitter, too sour, salty, hot, pungent, dry and burning are dear to those in the mode of passion. Such foods cause distress, misery and disease.
deep_dive_text: The Rajasic Diet. Passion craves intense stimulation. A person dominated by Rajas isn't satisfied with simple, nourishing food; they want extremes—food that burns the tongue, shocks the palate, and overstimulates the nervous system. However, this sensory thrill is a short-term loan with a high interest rate. Krishna warns that these intensely 'Vidahinah' (burning) foods ultimately yield 'Duhkha' (distress) and 'Amaya' (physical disease/inflammation).

original_script:
यातयामं गतरसं पूति पर्युषितं च यत्।
उच्छिष्टमपि चामेध्यं भोजनं तामसप्रियम्।।17.10।।
transliteration:
yāta-yāmaṁ gata-rasaṁ pūti paryuṣhitaṁ cha yat
uchchhiṣhṭam api chāmedhyaṁ bhojanaṁ tāmasa-priyam
word_meanings: yāta-yāmam—food cooked three hours before being eaten; gata-rasam—tasteless; pūti—bad-smelling; paryuṣhitam—decomposed; cha—also; yat—that which; uchchhiṣhṭam—remnants of food eaten by others; api—also; cha—and; amedhyam—untouchable; bhojanam—eating; tāmasa—to one in the mode of darkness; priyam—dear
translation_english: Food prepared more than three hours before being eaten, food that is tasteless, decomposed and putrid, and food consisting of remnants and untouchable things is dear to those in the mode of darkness.
deep_dive_text: The Tamasic Diet. Ignorance craves dead energy. Tamasic people are drawn to 'Yata-yamam' (stale, old food), 'Puti' (foul-smelling/fermented), and 'Uchchishtam' (unclean leftovers).
