Because the mode of ignorance is characterized by inertia and darkness, the body naturally desires food that has lost its life-force ('Gata-rasam'). Eating this way further drags the mind down into depression, lethargy, and delusion.

original_script:
अफलाकाङ्क्षिभिर्यज्ञो विधिदृष्टो य इज्यते।
यष्टव्यमेवेति मनः समाधाय स सात्त्विकः।।17.11।।
transliteration:
aphalākāṅkṣhibhir yajño vidhi-dṛiṣhṭo ya ijyate
yaṣhṭavyam eveti manaḥ samādhāya sa sāttvikaḥ
word_meanings: aphala-ākāṅkṣhibhiḥ—by those devoid of desire for result; yajñaḥ—sacrifice; vidhi-dṛiṣhṭaḥ—according to the direction of scripture; yaḥ—which; ijyate—is performed; yaṣhṭavyam—must be performed; eva—certainly; iti—thus; manaḥ—mind; samādhāya—fixing; saḥ—it; sāttvikaḥ—in the mode of goodness
translation_english: Of sacrifices, the sacrifice performed according to the directions of scripture, as a matter of duty, by those who desire no reward, is of the nature of goodness.
deep_dive_text: Sattvic Worship. How do the pure-hearted pray or perform rituals (Yajna)? Two criteria define it: 1. 'Vidhi-drishto' (It is done properly, according to authentic scriptural traditions, not made up). 2. 'Aphala-akankshibhir' (It is done completely without the desire for a material reward). They don't pray to win the lottery or defeat a rival. They pray 'Yashtavyam eveti'—simply because it is their sacred duty to express gratitude to the Divine. It is pure, unselfish connection.

original_script:
अभिसन्धाय तु फलं दम्भार्थमपि चैव यत्।
इज्यते भरतश्रेष्ठ तं यज्ञं विद्धि राजसम्।।17.12।।
transliteration:
abhisandhāya tu phalaṁ dambhārtham api chaiva yat
ijyate bharata-śhreṣhṭha taṁ yajñaṁ viddhi rājasam
word_meanings: abhisandhāya—desiring; tu—but; phalam—the result; dambha—pride; artham—for the sake of; api—also; cha—and; eva—certainly; yat—that which; ijyate—is performed; bharata-śhreṣhṭha—O chief of the Bharatas; tam—that; yajñam—sacrifice; viddhi—know; rājasam—in the mode of passion
translation_english: But the sacrifice performed for some material benefit, or for the sake of pride, O chief of the Bharatas, you should know to be in the mode of passion.
deep_dive_text: Rajasic Worship. The mode of passion turns spirituality into a transaction. A Rajasic person performs 'Yajna' (sacrifice/worship) strictly to extract a 'Phalam' (a material result like wealth, success, or health). Furthermore, they often do it 'Dambhartham' (for the sake of show or pride). They want the community to see how generous, religious, or pious they are. Their worship is an extension of their ego and ambition.

original_script:
विधिहीनमसृष्टान्नं मन्त्रहीनमदक्षिणम्।
श्रद्धाविरहितं यज्ञं तामसं परिचक्षते।।17.13।।
transliteration:
vidhi-hīnam asṛiṣhṭānnaṁ mantra-hīnam adakṣhiṇam
śhraddhā-virahitaṁ yajñaṁ tāmasaṁ parichakṣhate
word_meanings: vidhi-hīnam—without scriptural direction; asṛiṣhṭa-annam—without distribution of prasadam; mantra-hīnam—with no chanting of the Vedic hymns; adakṣhiṇam—with no remunerations to the priests; śhraddhā—faith; virahitam—without; yajñam—sacrifice; tāmasam—in the mode of ignorance; parichakṣhate—is to be considered
translation_english: Any sacrifice performed without regard for the directions of scripture, without distribution of prasadam [spiritual food], without chanting of Vedic hymns and remunerations to the priests, and without faith is considered to be in the mode of ignorance.
deep_dive_text: Tamasic Worship. Ignorance breeds chaos. A Tamasic sacrifice is 'Vidhi-hinam' (devoid of rules). It is reckless, disrespectful, and often involves dark or forbidden practices. There is no charity (food or remuneration given to others), no authentic mantras chanted, and crucially, it is 'Shraddha-virahitam'—completely devoid of genuine faith. It is a hollow, superstitious, or destructive performance.

original_script:
देवद्विजगुरुप्राज्ञपूजनं शौचमार्जवम्।
ब्रह्मचर्यमहिंसा च शारीरं तप उच्यते।।17.14।।
transliteration:
deva-dvija-guru-prājña- pūjanaṁ śhaucham ārjavam
brahmacharyam ahinsā cha śhārīraṁ tapa uchyate
word_meanings: deva—the Supreme Lord; dvija—the brahmanas; guru—the spiritual master; prājña—and worshipable personalities; pūjanam—worship; śhaucham—cleanliness; ārjavam—simplicity; brahmacharyam—celibacy; ahinsā—nonviolence; cha—also; śhārīram—pertaining to the body; tapaḥ—austerity; uchyate—is said to be
translation_english: Austerity of the body consists in worship of the Supreme Lord, the brahmanas, the spiritual master, and superiors like the father and mother, and in cleanliness, simplicity, celibacy and nonviolence.
deep_dive_text: Tapas (Discipline) of the Body. Krishna redefines 'austerity'. It is not starving yourself or sleeping on a bed of nails. True bodily discipline ('Shariram tapah') is how you carry yourself in the world. It involves showing deep respect ('Pujanam') to the Divine, to teachers (Gurus), and to the wise. It requires maintaining physical cleanliness ('Shaucham'), living with absolute simplicity ('Arjavam'), practicing sexual discipline ('Brahmacharyam'), and ensuring your physical actions cause no harm to others ('Ahimsa').

original_script:
अनुद्वेगकरं वाक्यं सत्यं प्रियहितं च यत्।
स्वाध्यायाभ्यसनं चैव वाङ्मयं तप उच्यते।।17.15।।
transliteration:
anudvega-karaṁ vākyaṁ satyaṁ priya-hitaṁ cha yat
svādhyāyābhyasanaṁ chaiva vāṅ-mayaṁ tapa uchyate
word_meanings: anudvega-karam—not agitating; vākyam—words; satyam—truthful; priya—pleasing; hitam—beneficial; cha—also; yat—which; svādhyāya—of Vedic study; abhyasanam—practice; cha—also; eva—certainly; vāk-mayam—of the voice; tapaḥ—austerity; uchyate—is said to be
translation_english: Austerity of speech consists in speaking words that are truthful, pleasing, beneficial, and not agitating to others, and also in regularly reciting Vedic literature.
deep_dive_text: Tapas (Discipline) of Speech. This verse acts as the ultimate filter for communication. Before speaking, words must pass four strict tests: 1. Is it 'Satyam' (truthful)? 2. Is it 'Priya' (pleasing/kind in delivery)? 3. Is it 'Hitam' (actually beneficial to the listener)? 4. Is it 'Anudvega-karam' (non-agitating, meaning it doesn't cause unnecessary anxiety or rage)? Mastering this—along with the recitation of sacred texts—is the true discipline of the tongue.

original_script:
मनःप्रसादः सौम्यत्वं मौनमात्मविनिग्रहः।
भावसंशुद्धिरित्येतत्तपो मानसमुच्यते।।17.16।।
transliteration:
manaḥ-prasādaḥ saumyatvaṁ maunam ātma-vinigrahaḥ
bhāva-sanśhuddhir ity etat tapo mānasam uchyate
word_meanings: manaḥ-prasādaḥ—satisfaction of the mind; saumyatvam—being without duplicity towards others; maunam—gravity; ātma-vinigrahaḥ—control of the mind; bhāva—of one's nature; sanśhuddhiḥ—purification; iti—thus; etat—this; tapaḥ—austerity; mānasam—of the mind; uchyate—is said to be
translation_english: And satisfaction, simplicity, gravity, self-control and purification of one's existence are the austerities of the mind.
deep_dive_text: Tapas (Discipline) of the Mind. The hardest battleground is the mind. Mental austerity isn't about blocking out thoughts; it is about cultivating 'Manah-prasadah' (a deep, satisfied serenity). It requires 'Saumyatvam' (gentleness of thought), 'Maunam' (internal silence/freedom from constant inner chatter), and 'Atma-vinigrahah' (strict self-control). When you constantly monitor and purify the intentions behind your feelings ('Bhava-samshuddhir'), you have mastered the discipline of the mind.

original_script:
श्रद्धया परया तप्तं तपस्तत्त्रिविधं नरैः।
अफलाकाङ्क्षिभिर्युक्तैः सात्त्विकं परिचक्षते।।17.17।।
transliteration:
śhraddhayā parayā taptaṁ tapas tat tri-vidhaṁ naraiḥ
aphalākāṅkṣhibhir yuktaiḥ sāttvikaṁ parichakṣhate
word_meanings: śhraddhayā—with faith; parayā—transcendental; taptam—executed; tapaḥ—austerity; tat—that; tri-vidham—of three kinds; naraiḥ—by men; aphala-ākāṅkṣhibhiḥ—who are without desires for fruits; yuktaiḥ—engaged; sāttvikam—in the mode of goodness; parichakṣhate—is called
translation_english: This threefold austerity, performed with transcendental faith by men not expecting material benefits but engaged only for the sake of the Supreme, is called austerity in goodness.
deep_dive_text: Sattvic Austerity. Krishna categorizes the discipline. When a person practices all three austerities (body, speech, and mind) with 'Paraya Shraddhaya' (supreme, unwavering faith) and absolutely zero expectation of a material reward ('Aphala-akankshibhir'), that discipline is classified as Sattvic (Goodness). It is pure self-improvement done for the love of the Divine, free from ego.

original_script:
सत्कारमानपूजार्थं तपो दम्भेन चैव यत्।
क्रियते तदिह प्रोक्तं राजसं चलमध्रुवम्।।17.18।।
transliteration:
satkāra-māna-pūjārthaṁ tapo dambhena chaiva yat
kriyate tad iha proktaṁ rājasaṁ chalam adhruvam
word_meanings: sat-kāra—respect; māna—honor; pūjā—and worship; artham—for the sake of; tapaḥ—austerity; dambhena—with pride; cha—and; eva—certainly; yat—which; kriyate—is performed; tat—that; iha—in this world; proktam—is said; rājasam—in the mode of passion; chalam—flickering; adhruvam—temporary
translation_english: Penance performed out of pride and for the sake of gaining respect, honor and worship is said to be in the mode of passion. It is neither stable nor permanent.
deep_dive_text: Rajasic Austerity. What happens when your discipline becomes a performance? When a person fasts, meditates, or speaks kindly purely to extract 'Satkara' (respect), 'Mana' (honor), or 'Puja' (adoration) from others, their austerity is poisoned by 'Dambhena' (pride). Because this discipline relies entirely on the applause of a fickle society, Krishna declares its results to be 'Chalam' (flickering) and 'Adhruvam' (temporary). It is an ego trip, not a spiritual journey.

original_script:
मूढग्राहेणात्मनो यत्पीडया क्रियते तपः।
परस्योत्सादनार्थं वा तत्तामसमुदाहृतम्।।17.19।।
transliteration:
mūḍha-grāheṇātmano yat pīḍayā kriyate tapaḥ
parasyotsādanārthaṁ vā tat tāmasam udāhṛitam
word_meanings: mūḍha-grāheṇa—with foolish obstinacy; ātmanaḥ—of one's own self; yat—which; pīḍayā—by torture; kriyate—is performed; tapaḥ—penance; parasya—of others; utsādana-artham—for the sake of destroying; vā—or; tat—that; tāmasam—in the mode of ignorance; udāhṛitam—is said to be
translation_english: Penance performed out of foolish obstinacy, with self-torture or to destroy or injure others, is said to be in the mode of ignorance.
deep_dive_text: Tamasic Austerity. The darkest form of discipline is 'Mudha-grahena' (foolish, stubborn obsession). It involves self-mutilation ('Pīdaya') or practices meant explicitly for the 'Utsadanartham' (destruction or injury) of others. It is discipline weaponized by ignorance, yielding only suffering.

original_script:
दातव्यमिति यद्दानं दीयतेऽनुपकारिणे।
देशे काले च पात्रे च तद्दानं सात्त्विकं स्मृतम्।।17.20।।
transliteration:
dātavyam iti yad dānaṁ dīyate ’nupakāriṇe
deśhe kāle cha pātre cha tad dānaṁ sāttvikaṁ smṛitam
word_meanings: dātavyam—worth giving; iti—thus; yat—that which; dānam—charity; dīyate—is given; anupakāriṇe—to any person who does nothing in return; deśhe—in a proper place; kāle—at a proper time; cha—and; pātre—to a suitable person; cha—and; tat—that; dānam—charity; sāttvikam—in the mode of goodness; smṛitam—is considered
translation_english: Charity given out of duty, without expectation of return, at the proper time and place, and to a worthy person is considered to be in the mode of goodness.
deep_dive_text: Sattvic Charity. How to give perfectly. It must be 'Datavyam iti' (given simply because it is the right thing to do), completely 'Anupakarine' (expecting absolutely nothing in return). Furthermore, the context matters: it must be given in the right 'Deshe' (place), at the right 'Kale' (time), and to the right 'Patre' (worthy recipient). This is pure, unselfish giving.

original_script:
यत्तु प्रत्युपकारार्थं फलमुद्दिश्य वा पुनः।
दीयते च परिक्लिष्टं तद्दानं राजसं स्मृतम्।।17.21।।
transliteration:
yat tu pratyupakārārthaṁ phalam uddiśhya vā punaḥ
dīyate cha parikliṣhṭaṁ tad dānaṁ rājasaṁ smṛitam
word_meanings: yat—that which; tu—but; prati-upakāra-artham—for the sake of getting some return; phalam—a result; uddiśhya—desiring; vā—or; punaḥ—again; dīyate—is given; cha—and; parikliṣhṭam—grudgingly; tat—that; dānam—charity; rājasam—in the mode of passion; smṛitam—is understood to be
translation_english: But charity performed with the expectation of some return, or with a desire for fruitive results, or in a grudging mood, is said to be charity in the mode of passion.
deep_dive_text: Rajasic Charity. Charity as a transaction. It is given 'Prati-upakarartham' (with strings attached, expecting a favor back) or 'Phalam uddishya' (hoping for a tax break, fame, or a ticket to heaven). Most telling is 'Pariklishtam'—it is given grudgingly. The donor feels a pinch in their heart while giving, regretting the loss of their wealth.

original_script:
अदेशकाले यद्दानमपात्रेभ्यश्च दीयते।
असत्कृतमवज्ञातं तत्तामसमुदाहृतम्।।17.22।।
transliteration:
adeśha-kāle yad dānam apātrebhyaśh cha dīyate
asat-kṛitam avajñātaṁ tat tāmasam udāhṛitam
word_meanings: adeśha—at an unpurified place; kāle—and unpurified time; yat—that which; dānam—charity; apātrebhyaḥ—to unworthy persons; cha—and; dīyate—is given; asat-kṛitam—without respect; avajñātam—with contempt; tat—that; tāmasam—in the mode of ignorance; udāhṛitam—is said to be
translation_english: And charity performed at an impure place, at an improper time, to unworthy persons, or without proper attention and respect is said to be in the mode of ignorance.
deep_dive_text: Tamasic Charity. Toxic giving. It is given 'Adesha-kale' (at the wrong time and place, like giving money to an addict to buy drugs). The recipient is 'Apatrebhyah' (unworthy or destructive). Even worse, it is given 'Avajnatam' (with contempt or disrespect, humiliating the receiver). This kind of charity does not elevate; it degrades both the giver and the receiver.

original_script:
ॐ तत्सदिति निर्देशो ब्रह्मणस्त्रिविधः स्मृतः।
ब्राह्मणास्तेन वेदाश्च यज्ञाश्च विहिताः पुरा।।17.23।।
transliteration:
oṁ tat sad iti nirdeśho brahmaṇas tri-vidhaḥ smṛitaḥ
brāhmaṇās tena vedāśh cha yajñāśh cha vihitāḥ purā
word_meanings: oṁ—indication of the Supreme; tat—that; sat—eternal; iti—thus; nirdeśhaḥ—indication; brahmaṇaḥ—of the Supreme Absolute Truth; tri-vidhaḥ—threefold; smṛitaḥ—is considered; brāhmaṇāḥ—the brahmanas; tena—with that; vedāḥ—the Vedic literature; cha—also; yajñāḥ—sacrifices; cha—also; vihitāḥ—used; purā—formerly
translation_english: From the beginning of creation, the three words om tat sat were used to indicate the Supreme Absolute Truth. These three symbolic representations were used by brahmanas while chanting the hymns of the Vedas and during sacrifices for the satisfaction of the Supreme.
deep_dive_text: The Ultimate Spiritual Mantra. Krishna shifts from diagnostics to the cure. How do we purify our flawed actions? By invoking 'Om Tat Sat'. This is the threefold name of the Supreme Absolute Truth (Brahman). Since the dawn of creation ('Pura'), these syllables have been woven into Vedic chants and sacrifices to ensure that human efforts are connected to the perfection of the Divine.

original_script:
तस्मादोमित्युदाहृत्य यज्ञदानतपःक्रियाः।
प्रवर्तन्ते विधानोक्ताः सततं ब्रह्मवादिनाम्।।17.24।।
transliteration:
tasmād oṁ ity udāhṛitya yajña-dāna-tapaḥ-kriyāḥ
pravartante vidhānoktāḥ satataṁ brahma-vādinām
word_meanings: tasmāt—therefore; oṁ—om; iti—thus; udāhṛitya—indicating; yajña—of sacrifice; dāna—charity; tapaḥ—and penance; kriyāḥ—performances; pravartante—begin; vidhāna-uktāḥ—according to scriptural regulation; satatam—always; brahma-vādinām—of the transcendentalists
translation_english: Therefore, transcendentalists undertaking performances of sacrifice, charity and penance in accordance with scriptural regulations begin always with Om, to attain the Supreme.
deep_dive_text: The Meaning of 'OM'. The syllable 'Om' invokes the presence of the infinite. Whenever a transcendentalist begins a 'Yajna' (sacrifice), 'Dana' (charity), or 'Tapah' (discipline), they first vibrate 'Om'. It acts as a spiritual starter motor. It acknowledges that the energy and capacity to perform the action come from the Supreme, stripping away the ego right at the beginning.

original_script:
तदित्यनभिसन्धाय फलं यज्ञतपःक्रियाः।
दानक्रियाश्च विविधाः क्रियन्ते मोक्षकाङ्क्षिभिः।।17.25।।
transliteration:
tad ity anabhisandhāya phalaṁ yajña-tapaḥ-kriyāḥ
dāna-kriyāśh cha vividhāḥ kriyante mokṣha-kāṅkṣhibhiḥ
word_meanings: tat—that; iti—thus; anabhisandhāya—without desiring; phalam—the fruitive result; yajña—of sacrifice; tapaḥ—and penance; kriyāḥ—activities; dāna—of charity; kriyāḥ—activities; cha—also; vividhāḥ—various; kriyante—are done; mokṣha-kāṅkṣhibhiḥ—by those who actually desire liberation
translation_english: Without desiring fruitive results, one should perform various kinds of sacrifice, penance and charity with the word tat. The purpose of such transcendental activities is to get free from material entanglement.
deep_dive_text: The Meaning of 'TAT'. 'Tat' translates to 'That' (referring to the Supreme). Uttering 'Tat' during an action signifies 'Anabhisandhaya phalam'—doing it without claiming the result. It is a verbal declaration of detachment: 'This charity, this hard work, this sacrifice is not for me; it is for *That*.' It is the mantra of the 'Moksha-kankshibhih' (those genuinely seeking liberation from ego).

original_script:
सद्भावे साधुभावे च सदित्येतत्प्रयुज्यते।
प्रशस्ते कर्मणि तथा सच्छब्दः पार्थ युज्यते।।17.26।।
transliteration:
sad-bhāve sādhu-bhāve cha sad ity etat prayujyate
praśhaste karmaṇi tathā sach-chhabdaḥ pārtha yujyate
word_meanings: sat-bhāve—in the sense of the nature of the Supreme; sādhu-bhāve—in the sense of the nature of the devotee; cha—and; sat—the word sat; iti—thus; etat—this; prayujyate—is used; praśhaste—in bona fide; karmaṇi—activities; tathā—also; sat-śhabdaḥ—the sound sat; pārtha—O son of Pritha; yujyate—is used
translation_english: The Absolute Truth is the objective of devotional sacrifice, and it is indicated by the word sat. The performer of such sacrifice is also called sat, as are all works of sacrifice, penance and charity which, true to the absolute nature, are performed to please the Supreme Person, O son of Pritha.
deep_dive_text: The Meaning of 'SAT' (Part 1). 'Sat' means 'Eternal', 'Good', or 'Truth'. It is used to describe 'Sad-bhave' (the eternal nature of reality) and 'Sadhu-bhave' (the goodness of a saintly person). When you attach the word 'Sat' to an action ('Prashaste karmani'), you are declaring that the action itself is pure, auspicious, and perfectly aligned with the eternal truth.

original_script:
यज्ञे तपसि दाने च स्थितिः सदिति चोच्यते।
कर्म चैव तदर्थीयं सदित्येवाभिधीयते।।17.27।।
transliteration:
yajñe tapasi dāne cha sthitiḥ sad iti chochyate
karma chaiva tad-arthīyaṁ sad ity evābhidhīyate
word_meanings: yajñe—in sacrifice; tapasi—in penance; dāne—in charity; cha—also; sthitiḥ—the situation; sat—the Supreme; iti—thus; cha—and; uchyate—is pronounced; karma—work; cha—and; eva—certainly; tat—for that; arthīyam—meant; sat—the Supreme; iti—thus; eva—certainly; abhidhīyate—is indicated
translation_english: All works of sacrifice, penance and charity, performed to please the Supreme Person, are also called sat. And any action dedicated to the Supreme Lord is also called sat.
deep_dive_text: The Meaning of 'SAT' (Part 2). 'Sat' completes the spiritual circuit. Being 'Sthitih' (steadfastly situated) in sacrifice, discipline, and charity is considered 'Sat'. Even if your actions are biologically flawed or imperfectly executed, dedicating them entirely to the Supreme ('Tad-arthiyam') purifies them. The 'Om Tat Sat' formula acts as a divine autocorrect, transforming ordinary human effort into eternal spiritual perfection.

original_script:
अश्रद्धया हुतं दत्तं तपस्तप्तं कृतं च यत्।
असदित्युच्यते पार्थ न च तत्प्रेत्य नो इह।।17.28।।
transliteration:
aśhraddhayā hutaṁ dattaṁ tapas taptaṁ kṛitaṁ cha yat
asad ity uchyate pārtha na cha tat pretya no iha
word_meanings: aśhraddhayā—without faith; hutam—offered in sacrifice; dattam—given; tapaḥ—penance; taptam—executed; kṛitam—performed; cha—also; yat—that which; asat—false; iti—thus; uchyate—is said to be; pārtha—O son of Pritha; na—never; cha—also; tat—that; pretya—after death; na u—nor; iha—in this life
translation_english: Anything done as sacrifice, charity or penance without faith in the Supreme, O son of Pritha, is impermanent. It is called asat and is useless both in this life and the next.
deep_dive_text: The Tragedy of 'Asat'. Krishna closes the chapter with a profound warning about 'Ashraddhaya' (faithlessness). If you perform massive charities, severe austerities, or complex rituals without genuine spiritual faith or devotion, it is entirely 'Asat' (unreal/useless). Because it lacks the soul of devotion, it produces no lasting spiritual benefit in the afterlife ('Pretya'), nor does it bring genuine peace in this current life ('Iha'). Intention is everything.

original_script: श्रद्धात्रयविभागयोगः
transliteration: Śhraddhā Traya Vibhāga Yogaḥ
translation_english: Chapter 17: The Yoga of the Threefold Divisions of Faith
deep_dive_text: Chapter 17 acts as a diagnostic manual for the soul. It reveals that our innate faith, the food we crave, the charity we give, and the discipline we practice are all dictated by the three modes of nature. Krishna teaches that actions performed in goodness elevate us, those in passion cause stress, and those in ignorance lead to degradation. Ultimately, any action can be purified and made eternal by performing it with selfless devotion and dedicating it to the Supreme with the mantra 'Om Tat Sat'.

original_script: अथ अष्टादशोऽध्यायः - मोक्षसन्न्यासयोगः
transliteration: Atha Aṣhṭādaśho ’dhyāyaḥ - Mokṣha Sannyāsa Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Mokṣha—liberation; Sannyāsa—renunciation; Yogaḥ—the path of connection.
translation_english: Chapter 18 Summary: The Yoga of Liberation Through Renunciation
deep_dive_text: Summary: Chapter 18 is the grand finale and master summary of the entire Bhagavad Gita. Arjuna asks for the definitive difference between formal renunciation (Sannyasa) and internal detachment (Tyaga). Krishna brilliantly explains that true renunciation doesn't mean quitting your job or abandoning your duties; it means performing your natural duties without any selfish attachment to the results. He breaks down how the three modes influence our knowledge, action, and intellect, eventually leading to the ultimate conclusion: abandoning all varieties of religion and simply surrendering to the Supreme with absolute love.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
सन्न्यासस्य महाबाहो तत्त्वमिच्छामि वेदितुम्।
त्यागस्य च हृषीकेश पृथक्केशिनिषूदन।।18.1।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
sannyāsasya mahā-bāho tattvam ichchhāmi veditum
tyāgasya cha hṛiṣhīkeśha pṛithak keśhi-niṣhūdana
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjuna said; sannyāsasya—of renunciation; mahā-bāho—O mighty-armed one; tattvam—the truth; ichchhāmi—I wish; veditum—to understand; tyāgasya—of renunciation; cha—also; hṛiṣhīka-īśha—O master of the senses; pṛithak—differently; keśhi-niṣhūdana—O killer of the Keshi demon
translation_english: Arjuna said: O mighty-armed one, I wish to understand the purpose of renunciation [sannyasa] and of the renounced order of life [tyaga], O killer of the Keshi demon, master of the senses.
deep_dive_text: The Final Clarification. Arjuna is still slightly confused about the practical application of letting go. Throughout the Gita, Krishna has praised both 'Sannyasa' (formal renunciation of the world) and 'Tyaga' (internal detachment from the fruits of work). Arjuna asks Krishna, the master of the senses (Hrishikesha), to clearly define the boundary ('Prithak') between these two concepts once and for all.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
काम्यानां कर्मणां न्यासं सन्न्यासं कवयो विदुः।
सर्वकर्मफलत्यागं प्राहुस्त्यागं विचक्षणाः।।18.2।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
kāmyānāṁ karmaṇāṁ nyāsaṁ sannyāsaṁ kavayo viduḥ
sarva-karma-phala-tyāgaṁ prāhus tyāgaṁ vichakṣhaṇāḥ
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Personality of Godhead said; kāmyānām—with desire; karmaṇām—of activities; nyāsam—renunciation; sannyāsam—the renounced order of life; kavayaḥ—the learned; viduḥ—know; sarva—of all; karma—activities; phala—of results; tyāgam—renunciation; prāhuḥ—call; tyāgam—renunciation; vichakṣhaṇāḥ—the experienced
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: The giving up of activities that are based on material desire is what great learned men call the renounced order of life [sannyasa]. And giving up the results of all activities is what the wise call renunciation [tyaga].
deep_dive_text: Sannyasa vs. Tyaga. Krishna provides the ultimate definitions. 'Sannyasa' is abandoning 'Kamyanam karmanam' (actions driven entirely by selfish desire—like expanding a business just to buy a bigger yacht). 'Tyaga', however, is 'Sarva-karma-phala-tyagam' (abandoning the *results* of all actions). In Tyaga, you still work, you still build, you still fight, but you give the fruits of that labor to God or society.

original_script:
त्याज्यं दोषवदित्येके कर्म प्राहुर्मनीषिणः।
यज्ञदानतपःकर्म न त्याज्यमिति चापरे।।18.3।।
transliteration:
tyājyaṁ doṣha-vad ity eke karma prāhur manīṣhiṇaḥ
yajña-dāna-tapaḥ-karma na tyājyam iti chāpare
word_meanings: tyājyam—must be given up; doṣha-vat—as an evil; iti—thus; eke—one group; karma—work; prāhuḥ—they say; manīṣhiṇaḥ—great thinkers; yajña—of sacrifice; dāna—charity; tapaḥ—and penance; karma—works; na—never; tyājyam—are to be given up; iti—thus; cha—and; apare—others
translation_english: Some learned men declare that all kinds of fruitive activities should be given up as faulty, yet other sages maintain that acts of sacrifice, charity and penance should never be abandoned.
deep_dive_text: The Philosophical Debate. Krishna acknowledges the massive debate among ancient Indian philosophers. One camp ('eke manishinah') argues that *all* action binds you to karma, so you should abandon action entirely ('tyajyam dosha-vat'—seeing all work as faulty). The other camp argues that three specific activities must never be abandoned: Yajna (sacrifice/worship), Dana (charity), and Tapas (discipline).

original_script:
निश्चयं शृणु मे तत्र त्यागे भरतसत्तम।
त्यागो हि पुरुषव्याघ्र त्रिविधः सम्प्रकीर्तितः।।18.4।।
transliteration:
niśhchayaṁ śhṛiṇu me tatra tyāge bharata-sattama
tyāgo hi puruṣha-vyāghra tri-vidhaḥ samprakīrtitaḥ
word_meanings: niśhchayam—certainty; śhṛiṇu—hear; me—from Me; tatra—therein; tyāge—in the matter of renunciation; bharata-sat-tama—O best of the Bharatas; tyāgaḥ—renunciation; hi—certainly; puruṣha-vyāghra—O tiger among human beings; tri-vidhaḥ—of three kinds; samprakīrtitaḥ—is declared
translation_english: O best of the Bharatas, now hear My judgment about renunciation. O tiger among men, renunciation is declared in the scriptures to be of three kinds.
deep_dive_text: Krishna's Verdict. Having presented the debate, Krishna prepares to give His final, authoritative judgment ('Nishchayam shrinu me'). He calls Arjuna 'Purusha-vyaghra' (tiger among men) to awaken his courage, and announces that, just like faith and food, 'Tyaga' (renunciation) is also colored by the three modes of material nature (Sattva, Rajas, Tamas).

original_script:
यज्ञदानतपःकर्म न त्याज्यं कार्यमेव तत्।
यज्ञो दानं तपश्चैव पावनानि मनीषिणाम्।।18.5।।
transliteration:
yajña-dāna-tapaḥ-karma na tyājyaṁ kāryam eva tat
yajño dānaṁ tapaśh chaiva pāvanāni manīṣhiṇām
word_meanings: yajña—of sacrifice; dāna—charity; tapaḥ—and penance; karma—activity; na—never; tyājyam—to be given up; kāryam—must be done; eva—certainly; tat—that; yajñaḥ—sacrifice; dānam—charity; tapaḥ—penance; cha—also; eva—certainly; pāvanāni—purifying; manīṣhiṇām—even for the great souls
translation_english: Acts of sacrifice, charity and penance are not to be given up; they must be performed. Indeed, sacrifice, charity and penance purify even the great souls.
deep_dive_text: The Non-Negotiables. Krishna delivers His verdict. You cannot use 'spirituality' as an excuse to stop helping people or to stop disciplining yourself. 'Karyam eva tat'—they *must* be done! Why? Because Yajna (sacrifice), Dana (charity), and Tapas (discipline) are 'Pavanani' (purifying). Even 'Manishinam' (the greatest, most advanced thinkers) need to perform these actions to keep their hearts clean.

original_script:
एतान्यपि तु कर्माणि सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा फलानि च।
कर्तव्यानीति मे पार्थ निश्चितं मतमुत्तमम्।।18.6।।
transliteration:
etāny api tu karmāṇi saṅgaṁ tyaktvā phalāni cha
kartavyānīti me pārtha niśhchitaṁ matam uttamam
word_meanings: etāni—all these; api—even; tu—but; karmāṇi—activities; saṅgam—attachment; tyaktvā—giving up; phalāni—the results; cha—also; kartavyāni—should be done as duty; iti—thus; me—My; pārtha—O son of Pritha; niśhchitam—definite; matam—opinion; uttamam—supreme
translation_english: All these activities should be performed without attachment or any expectation of result. They should be performed as a matter of duty, O son of Pritha. That is My final opinion.
deep_dive_text: The Ultimate Caveat. Krishna adds the crucial condition. Yes, you must perform charity and discipline, but you must do them 'Sangam tyaktva' (abandoning attachment) and giving up the 'Phalani' (fruits/rewards). You do them simply because they are 'Kartavyani' (your duty). Krishna declares this to be His 'Nishchitam matam uttamam' (His definite and supreme opinion).

original_script:
नियतस्य तु सन्न्यासः कर्मणो नोपपद्यते।
मोहात्तस्य परित्यागस्तामसः परिकीर्तितः।।18.7।।
transliteration:
niyatasya tu sannyāsaḥ karmaṇo nopapadyate
mohāt tasya parityāgas tāmasaḥ parikīrtitaḥ
word_meanings: niyatasya—prescribed; tu—but; sannyāsaḥ—renunciation; karmaṇaḥ—of activities; na—never; upapadyate—is proper; mohāt—by illusion; tasya—of them; parityāgaḥ—renunciation; tāmasaḥ—in the mode of ignorance; parikīrtitaḥ—is declared
translation_english: Prescribed duties should never be renounced. If one gives up his prescribed duties because of illusion, such renunciation is said to be in the mode of ignorance.
deep_dive_text: Tamasic Renunciation. Krishna categorizes the types of false renunciation. If you abandon your 'Niyatasya karmanah' (prescribed, inherent duties—like a parent abandoning a child, or a soldier abandoning a post) out of 'Mohat' (illusion, laziness, or confusion), it is 'Tamasah' (renunciation in the mode of ignorance). It is an irresponsible escape dressed up as spirituality.

original_script:
दुःखमित्येव यत्कर्म कायक्लेशभयात्त्यजेत्।
स कृत्वा राजसं त्यागं नैव त्यागफलं लभेत्।।18.8।।
transliteration:
duḥkham ity eva yat karma kāya-kleśha-bhayāt tyajet
sa kṛitvā rājasaṁ tyāgaṁ naiva tyāga-phalaṁ labhet
word_meanings: duḥkham—unhappy; iti—thus; eva—certainly; yat—which; karma—work; kāya—for the body; kleśha—trouble; bhayāt—out of fear; tyajet—gives up; saḥ—he; kṛitvā—after doing; rājasam—in the mode of passion; tyāgam—renunciation; na—not; eva—certainly; tyāga-phalam—the results of renunciation; labhet—gains
translation_english: Anyone who gives up prescribed duties as troublesome or out of fear of bodily discomfort is said to have renounced in the mode of passion. Such action never leads to the elevation of renunciation.
deep_dive_text: Rajasic Renunciation. The second type of false renunciation is driven by passion. If you quit your duty simply because it is 'Duhkham' (difficult/troublesome) or out of 'Kaya-klesha-bhayat' (fear of physical discomfort and hard work), you are performing 'Rajasam tyagam'. Quitting because it's 'too hard' does not earn you the spiritual merit of true renunciation; it's just cowardice or comfort-seeking.

original_script:
कार्यमित्येव यत्कर्म नियतं क्रियतेऽर्जुन।
सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा फलं चैव स त्यागः सात्त्विको मतः।।18.9।।
transliteration:
kāryam ity eva yat karma niyataṁ kriyate ’rjuna
saṅgaṁ tyaktvā phalaṁ chaiva sa tyāgaḥ sāttviko mataḥ
word_meanings: kāryam—duty; iti—thus; eva—indeed; yat—which; karma—work; niyatam—prescribed; kriyate—is performed; arjuna—O Arjuna; saṅgam—attachment; tyaktvā—giving up; phalam—the result; cha—and; eva—certainly; saḥ—that; tyāgaḥ—renunciation; sāttvikaḥ—in the mode of goodness; mataḥ—is considered
translation_english: O Arjuna, when one performs his prescribed duty only because it ought to be done, and renounces all material association and all attachment to the fruit, his renunciation is said to be in the mode of goodness.
deep_dive_text: Sattvic Renunciation. This is the gold standard of detachment. A person in the mode of goodness works just as hard as a passionate person, but their motivation is entirely different. They do the work 'Karyam ity eva' (simply because it is the right thing to do). They detach themselves from both the 'Sangam' (the toxic association or ego of being the 'doer') and the 'Phalam' (the anxiety over the reward). It is duty performed with a perfectly peaceful, objective mind.

original_script:
न द्वेष्ट्यकुशलं कर्म कुशले नानुषज्जते।
त्यागी सत्त्वसमाविष्टो मेधावी छिन्नसंशयः।।18.10।।
transliteration:
na dveṣhṭy akuśhalaṁ karma kuśhale nānuṣhajjate
tyāgī sattva-samāviṣhṭo medhāvī chhinna-sanśhayaḥ
word_meanings: na—never; dveṣhṭi—hates; akuśhalam—inauspicious; karma—work; kuśhale—in auspicious work; na—nor; anuṣhajjate—becomes attached; tyāgī—the renouncer; sattva—in goodness; samāviṣhṭaḥ—absorbed; medhāvī—intelligent; chhinna—cut; sanśhayaḥ—all doubts
translation_english: The intelligent renouncer situated in the mode of goodness, neither hateful of inauspicious work nor attached to auspicious work, has no doubts about work.
deep_dive_text: The Unbothered Worker. What does a Sattvic renouncer look like in practice? They are 'Sattva-samavishto' (steeped in goodness). If they are assigned a glamorous, high-status task ('Kushale karma'), they don't get arrogant or overly attached to it. If they are assigned a menial, difficult, or unglamorous task ('Akushalam karma'), they don't hate it or complain. Because they have 'Chhinna-sanshayah' (slashed all doubts about their identity), they remain steady regardless of the job.

original_script:
न हि देहभृता शक्यं त्यक्तुं कर्माण्यशेषतः।
यस्तु कर्मफलत्यागी स त्यागीत्यभिधीयते।।18.11।।
transliteration:
na hi deha-bhṛitā śhakyaṁ tyaktuṁ karmāṇy aśheṣhataḥ
yas tu karma-phala-tyāgī sa tyāgīty abhidhīyate
word_meanings: na—never; hi—certainly; deha-bhṛitā—by the embodied; śhakyam—is possible; tyaktum—to renounce; karmāṇi—activities; aśheṣhataḥ—entirely; yaḥ—anyone who; tu—but; karma-phala—of the result of work; tyāgī—the renouncer; saḥ—he; tyāgī—a renouncer; iti—thus; abhidhīyate—is said
translation_english: It is indeed impossible for an embodied being to give up all activities. But he who renounces the fruits of action is called one who has truly renounced.
deep_dive_text: The Biological Reality of Action. Krishna ends the philosophical debate with pure pragmatism. As long as you are 'Deha-bhrita' (an embodied being), it is biologically and physically impossible to stop acting ('Asheshatah' - entirely). You must breathe, eat, sleep, and maintain your body. Therefore, the monk who thinks he has quit 'working' is fooling himself. The only true renouncer ('Tyagi') is the one who acts in the world but gives up the 'Karma-phala' (the fruit/reward of the action).

original_script:
अनिष्टमिष्टं मिश्रं च त्रिविधं कर्मणः फलम्।
भवत्यत्यागिनां प्रेत्य न तु सन्न्यासिनां क्वचित्।।18.12।।
transliteration:
aniṣhṭam iṣhṭaṁ miśhraṁ cha tri-vidhaṁ karmaṇaḥ phalam
bhavaty atyāgināṁ pretya na tu sannyāsināṁ kvachit
word_meanings: aniṣhṭam—undesirable; iṣhṭam—desirable; miśhram—mixed; cha—and; tri-vidham—of three kinds; karmaṇaḥ—of work; phalam—the result; bhavati—comes; atyāginām—for those who are not renounced; pretya—after death; na—not; tu—but; sannyāsinām—for the renounced order; kvachit—at any time
translation_english: For one who is not renounced, the threefold fruits of action—desirable, undesirable and mixed—accrue after death. But those who are in the renounced order of life have no such result to suffer or enjoy.
deep_dive_text: The Karmic Receipt. Every action you perform with a selfish motive prints a karmic receipt that must be paid. These fruits are 'Ishtam' (good/desirable), 'Anishtam' (bad/undesirable), or 'Mishram' (a mix of both). People who are 'Atyaginam' (attached/non-renouncers) are forced to take birth again ('Pretya' - after death) to consume these fruits. But the true Sannyasi (the detached worker) burns the receipt before it prints, ensuring they never have to return to the material world.

original_script:
पञ्चैतानि महाबाहो कारणानि निबोध मे।
साङ्ख्ये कृतान्ते प्रोक्तानि सिद्धये सर्वकर्मणाम्।।18.13।।
transliteration:
pañchaitāni mahā-bāho kāraṇāni nibodha me
sāṅkhye kṛitānte proktāni siddhaye sarva-karmaṇām
word_meanings: pañcha—five; etāni—these; mahā-bāho—O mighty-armed one; kāraṇāni—causes; nibodha—just understand; me—from Me; sāṅkhye—in the Vedanta; kṛita-ante—in the conclusion; proktāni—said; siddhaye—for the perfection; sarva-karmaṇām—of all activities
translation_english: O mighty-armed Arjuna, according to the Vedanta there are five causes for the accomplishment of all action. Now learn of these from Me.
deep_dive_text: The Anatomy of an Action. To help Arjuna truly detach from his actions, Krishna breaks down exactly how an action is formed. He refers to the 'Sankhya' philosophy (the deep analytical study of reality). Krishna says that humans mistakenly think, 'I am doing this all by myself.' But in reality, every single action—whether it is baking a cake or fighting a war—requires exactly five interacting components to succeed.

original_script:
अधिष्ठानं तथा कर्ता करणं च पृथग्विधम्।
विविधाश्च पृथक्चेष्टा दैवं चैवात्र पञ्चमम्।।18.14।।
transliteration:
adhiṣhṭhānaṁ tathā kartā karaṇaṁ cha pṛithag-vidham
vividhāśh cha pṛithak cheṣhṭā daivaṁ chaivātra pañchamam
word_meanings: adhiṣhṭhānam—the place (the body); tathā—also; kartā—the doer (the soul); karaṇam—the senses; cha—and; pṛithak-vidham—of different kinds; vividhāḥ—various; cha—and; pṛithak—separate; cheṣhṭāḥ—endeavors; daivam—the Supreme Lord (destiny); cha—and; eva—certainly; atra—here; pañchamam—the fifth
translation_english: The place of action [the body], the performer, the various senses, the many different kinds of endeavor, and ultimately the Supersoul—these are the five factors of action.
deep_dive_text: The Five Factors of Success. Here is the breakdown: 1. 'Adhishthanam' (The venue: your physical body). 2. 'Karta' (The doer: your ego/soul initiating the thought). 3. 'Karanam' (The instruments: your senses, eyes, hands, etc.). 4. 'Cheshta' (The effort: your actual physical exertion). 5. 'Daivam' (The Divine/Destiny: the cosmic sanction or luck required for it to actually work). You only control the effort; the fifth factor is entirely up to the Divine.

original_script:
शरीरवाङ्मनोभिर्यत्कर्म प्रारभते नरः।
न्याय्यं वा विपरीतं वा पञ्चैते तस्य हेतवः।।18.15।।
transliteration:
śharīra-vāṅ-manobhir yat karma prārabhate naraḥ
nyāyyaṁ vā viparītaṁ vā pañchaite tasya hetavaḥ
word_meanings: śharīra—by the body; vāk—speech; manobhiḥ—and mind; yat—whatever; karma—work; prārabhate—begins; naraḥ—a person; nyāyyam—right; vā—or; viparītam—the opposite; vā—or; pañcha—five; ete—all these; tasya—its; hetavaḥ—causes
translation_english: Whatever right or wrong action a man performs by body, mind or speech is caused by these five factors.
deep_dive_text: No Exceptions to the Rule. Whether an action is 'Nyayyam' (righteous and lawful) or 'Viparitam' (wrong, sinful, or opposite to duty), and whether it is performed physically ('Sharira'), verbally ('Vak'), or purely mentally ('Manobhih'), it absolutely relies on those five factors. Even a silent, malicious thought requires the body as a venue, the mind as an instrument, and the Divine sanction to occur.

original_script:
तत्रैवं सति कर्तारमात्मानं केवलं तु यः।
पश्यत्यकृतबुद्धित्वान्न स पश्यति दुर्मतिः।।18.16।।
transliteration:
tatraivaṁ sati kartāram ātmānaṁ kevalaṁ tu yaḥ
paśhyaty akṛita-buddhitvān na sa paśhyati durmatiḥ
word_meanings: tatra—there; evam—thus; sati—being; kartāram—the doer; ātmānam—himself; kevalam—only; tu—but; yaḥ—anyone who; paśhyati—sees; akṛita-buddhitvāt—due to unintelligence; na—never; saḥ—he; paśhyati—sees; durmatiḥ—foolish
translation_english: Therefore one who thinks himself the only doer, not considering the five factors, is certainly not very intelligent and cannot see things as they are.
deep_dive_text: The Illusion of the Solo Doer. Because 5 distinct elements are required to bake a cake, the person who says 'I baked this entirely by myself, with my own power' is delusional. Krishna calls such a person 'Akrita-buddhitvat' (having untrained intelligence) and 'Durmatih' (foolish/wrong-minded). To think your ego ('Atmanam kevalam') is the sole architect of your success or failure is the ultimate material illusion.

original_script:
यस्य नाहङ्कृतो भावो बुद्धिर्यस्य न लिप्यते।
हत्वापि स इमाँल्लोकान्न हन्ति न निबध्यते।।18.17।।
transliteration:
yasya nāhaṅkṛito bhāvo buddhir yasya na lipyate
hatvāpi sa imānl lokān na hanti na nibadhyate
word_meanings: yasya—one whose; na—never; ahaṅkṛitaḥ—of false ego; bhāvaḥ—nature; buddhiḥ—intelligence; yasya—of whom; na—never; lipyate—is attached; hatvā—killing; api—even; saḥ—he; imān—this; lokān—world; na—never; hanti—kills; na—never; nibadhyate—becomes entangled
translation_english: One who is not motivated by false ego, whose intelligence is not entangled, though he kills men in this world, does not kill. Nor is he bound by his actions.
deep_dive_text: The Ultimate Paradox of Action. This is a crucial verse specifically addressing Arjuna's fear of fighting. If a soldier fights a war without 'Ahankrito' (the false ego of 'I am the destroyer') and their intellect is 'Na lipyate' (untainted by personal greed or hatred), they are merely acting as an instrument of the five factors (specifically the fifth factor, Divine Will). Therefore, even if they physically slay enemies ('Hatvapi'), spiritually they kill no one, and they generate zero negative karma ('Na nibadhyate').

original_script:
ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं परिज्ञाता त्रिविधा कर्मचोदना।
करणं कर्म कर्तेति त्रिविधः कर्मसङ्ग्रहः।।18.18।।
transliteration:
jñānaṁ jñeyaṁ parijñātā tri-vidhā karma-chodanā
karaṇaṁ karma karteti tri-vidhaḥ karma-saṅgrahaḥ
word_meanings: jñānam—knowledge; jñeyam—the objective of knowledge; parijñātā—the knower; tri-vidhā—of three kinds; karma-chodanā—the impetus for work; karaṇam—the senses; karma—the work; kartā—the doer; iti—thus; tri-vidhaḥ—of three kinds; karma-saṅgrahaḥ—the accumulation of work
translation_english: Knowledge, the object of knowledge, and the knower are the three factors that motivate action; the senses, the work and the doer are the three constituents of action.
deep_dive_text: The Spark Before the Fire. Before you do something, you have to *want* to do it. Krishna explains the psychology of motivation ('Karma-chodana'). It requires three things: 1. 'Parijnata' (You, the observer), 2. 'Jneyam' (The object you are looking at, like a piece of chocolate), and 3. 'Jnanam' (The knowledge/thought: 'That chocolate will make me happy'). Once the motivation is set, the actual physical execution of the action ('Karma-sangrahah') requires the Doer, the Work, and the Senses to carry it out.

original_script:
ज्ञानं कर्म च कर्ता च त्रिधैव गुणभेदतः।
प्रोच्यते गुणसङ्ख्याने यथावच्छृणु तान्यपि।।18.19।।
transliteration:
jñānaṁ karma cha kartā cha tridhaiva guṇa-bhedataḥ
prochyate guṇa-saṅkhyāne yathā-vach chhṛiṇu tāny api
word_meanings: jñānam—knowledge; karma—action; cha—and; kartā—the worker; cha—and; tridhā—of three kinds; eva—certainly; guṇa-bhedataḥ—in terms of different modes of material nature; prochyate—is said; guṇa-saṅkhyāne—in the analytical study of the modes; yathā-vat—as they are; śhṛiṇu—hear; tāni—all of them; api—also
translation_english: According to the three different modes of material nature, there are three kinds of knowledge, action and performer of action. Now hear of them from Me.
deep_dive_text: The Tripartite Division. Krishna is about to apply the 'Guna test' (the three modes of nature) to the three elements of action He just defined: Knowledge (Jnanam), the Work itself (Karma), and the Worker (Karta). This psychological breakdown will show exactly how Goodness, Passion, and Ignorance alter how we think, what we do, and who we become in the process.

original_script:
सर्वभूतेषु येनैकं भावमव्ययमीक्षते।
अविभक्तं विभक्तेषु तज्ज्ञानं विद्धि सात्त्विकम्।।18.20।।
transliteration:
sarva-bhūteṣhu yenaikaṁ bhāvam avyayam īkṣhate
avibhaktaṁ vibhakteṣhu taj jñānaṁ viddhi sāttvikam
word_meanings: sarva-bhūteṣhu—in all living entities; yena—by which; ekam—one; bhāvam—spiritual nature; avyayam—imperishable; īkṣhate—one sees; avibhaktam—undivided; vibhakteṣhu—in the numberless divided; tat—that; jñānam—knowledge; viddhi—know; sāttvikam—in the mode of goodness
translation_english: That knowledge by which one undivided spiritual nature is seen in all living entities, though they are divided into innumerable forms, you should understand to be in the mode of goodness.
deep_dive_text: Sattvic Knowledge. True intelligence is the ability to see unity amidst diversity. Sattvic (pure) knowledge looks past the superficial differences of race, gender, species, or status ('Vibhakteshu') and perceives the exact same imperishable spiritual spark ('Bhāvam avyayam') in every single living being. It is the vision of absolute spiritual equality.

original_script:
पृथक्त्वेन तु यज्ज्ञानं नानाभावान्पृथग्विधान्।
वेत्ति सर्वेषु भूतेषु तज्ज्ञानं विद्धि राजसम्।।18.21।।
transliteration:
pṛithaktvena tu yaj jñānaṁ nānā-bhāvān pṛithag-vidhān
vetti sarveṣhu bhūteṣhu taj jñānaṁ viddhi rājasam
word_meanings: pṛithaktvena—by division; tu—but; yat—which; jñānam—knowledge; nānā-bhāvān—various natures; pṛithak-vidhān—different kinds; vetti—knows; sarveṣhu—in all; bhūteṣhu—living entities; tat—that; jñānam—knowledge; viddhi—know; rājasam—in the mode of passion
translation_english: That knowledge by which one sees that in every different body there is a different type of living entity you should understand to be in the mode of passion.
deep_dive_text: Rajasic Knowledge. Passion fragments the world. Rajasic knowledge looks at the universe and only sees 'us vs. them'. It focuses entirely on 'Prithaktvena' (separateness/differences). A person with this mindset believes a rich person is fundamentally superior to a poor person, or a human is entirely disconnected from an animal. It is a materialistic, deeply divided, and highly tribal way of perceiving reality.

original_script:
यत्तु कृत्स्नवदेकस्मिन्कार्ये सक्तमहैतुकम्।
अतत्त्वार्थवदल्पं च तत्तामसमुदाहृतम्।।18.22।।
transliteration:
yat tu kṛitsna-vad ekasmin kārye saktam ahaitukam
atattvārtha-vad alpaṁ cha tat tāmasam udāhṛitam
word_meanings: yat—that which; tu—but; kṛitsna-vat—as all in all; ekasmin—in one; kārye—work; saktam—attached; ahaitukam—without cause; atattva-artha-vat—without knowledge of reality; alpam—very meager; cha—and; tat—that; tāmasam—in the mode of darkness; udāhṛitam—is said to be
translation_english: And that knowledge by which one is attached to one kind of work as the all in all, without knowledge of the truth, and which is very meager, is said to be in the mode of darkness.
deep_dive_text: Tamasic Knowledge. Ignorance is tunnel vision. Tamasic knowledge becomes violently attached to one tiny, specific thing—like hoarding money, a dogmatic belief, or an addiction—and treats it 'Kritsna-vad' (as if it is the absolute totality of existence). Because it lacks broader reasoning ('Ahaitukam') and is entirely disconnected from the truth ('Atattvartha-vat'), it traps the person in a narrow, meager, and destructive reality.

original_script:
नियतं सङ्गरहितमरागद्वेषतः कृतम्।
अफलप्रेप्सुना कर्म यत्तत्सात्त्विकमुच्यते।।18.23।।
transliteration:
niyataṁ saṅga-rahitam arāga-dveṣhataḥ kṛitam
aphala-prepsunā karma yat tat sāttvikam uchyate
word_meanings: niyatam—regulated; saṅga-rahitam—without attachment; arāga-dveṣhataḥ—without love or hatred; kṛitam—done; aphala-prepsunā—by one without desire for fruitive result; karma—action; yat—which; tat—that; sāttvikam—in the mode of goodness; uchyate—is called
translation_english: That action which is regulated and which is performed without attachment, without love or hatred, and without desire for fruitive results is said to be in the mode of goodness.
deep_dive_text: Sattvic Action. Now Krishna grades the action itself. Good work isn't defined by success; it is defined by psychological balance. Sattvic action is 'Niyatam' (regulated/lawful duty). It is performed 'Araga-dveshatah' (without extreme emotional highs of love, or bitter lows of hatred toward the task) and 'Aphala-prepsuna' (without hungering for the applause or the paycheck). It is smooth, focused, and steady.

original_script:
यत्तु कामेप्सुना कर्म साहङ्कारेण वा पुनः।
क्रियते बहुलायासं तद्राजसमुदाहृतम्।।18.24।।
transliteration:
yat tu kāmepsunā karma sāhaṅkāreṇa vā punaḥ
kriyate bahulāyāsaṁ tad rājasam udāhṛitam
word_meanings: yat—that which; tu—but; kāma-īpsunā—by one with desires for fruitive results; karma—work; sa-ahaṅkāreṇa—with false ego; vā—or; punaḥ—again; kriyate—is performed; bahula-āyāsam—with great labor; tat—that; rājasam—in the mode of passion; udāhṛitam—is said to be
translation_english: But action performed with great effort by one seeking to gratify his desires, and enacted from a sense of false ego, is called action in the mode of passion.
deep_dive_text: Rajasic Action. Passionate action is loud, stressful, and exhausting. It is driven purely by 'Kamepsuna' (a burning desire for rewards). The key symptom here is 'Bahulayasam' (requiring massive, strenuous, stress-inducing effort) performed 'Sahankarena' (with a swollen ego). Think of a CEO working 100-hour weeks just to prove they are better than their competitors; the work is highly productive, but internally toxic.

original_script:
अनुबन्धं क्षयं हिंसामनपेक्ष्य च पौरुषम्।
मोहादारभ्यते कर्म यत्तत्तामसमुच्यते।।18.25।।
transliteration:
anubandhaṁ kṣhayaṁ hinsām anapekṣhya cha pauruṣham
mohād ārabhyate karma yat tat tāmasam uchyate
word_meanings: anubandham—of future bondage; kṣhayam—destruction; hinsām—and distress to others; anapekṣhya—without considering the consequences; cha—and; pauruṣham—self-sanctioned ability; mohāt—by illusion; ārabhyate—is begun; karma—work; yat—which; tat—that; tāmasam—in the mode of ignorance; uchyate—is said to be
translation_english: That action performed in illusion, in disregard of scriptural injunctions, and without concern for future bondage or for violence or distress caused to others is said to be in the mode of ignorance.
deep_dive_text: Tamasic Action. Ignorant action is reckless and destructive. It is initiated entirely out of 'Mohat' (delusion or blind impulse). A Tamasic worker completely disregards 'Anubandham' (long-term consequences) and 'Kshayam' (the destruction or loss of resources). Most dangerously, they act without considering the 'Hinsam' (violence/pain) they inflict on others. It is the action of a vandal or a reckless addict.

original_script:
मुक्तसङ्गोऽनहंवादी धृत्युत्साहसमन्वितः।
सिद्ध्यसिद्ध्योर्निर्विकारः कर्ता सात्त्विक उच्यते।।18.26।।
transliteration:
mukta-saṅgo ’nahaṁ-vādī dhṛity-utsāha-samanvitaḥ
siddhy-asiddhyor nirvikāraḥ kartā sāttvika uchyate
word_meanings: mukta-saṅgaḥ—liberated from all material association; anaham-vādī—without false ego; dhṛiti—with determination; utsāha—and great enthusiasm; samanvitaḥ—qualified; siddhi—in perfection; asiddhyoḥ—and failure; nirvikāraḥ—without change; kartā—the worker; sāttvikaḥ—in the mode of goodness; uchyate—is said to be
translation_english: One who performs his duty without association with the material modes of nature, without false ego, with great determination and enthusiasm, and without wavering in success or failure is said to be a worker in the mode of goodness.
deep_dive_text: The Sattvic Worker. This is the ideal employee, leader, or student. They are 'Mukta-sango' (unattached) and 'Anaham-vadi' (they don't boast or brag). However, detachment does not mean laziness! They work with incredible 'Dhriti' (determination) and 'Utsaha' (joyful enthusiasm). Their superpower is 'Nirvikarah' (unwavering stability)—they bring the exact same positive energy to the job whether they just succeeded brilliantly or failed miserably.

original_script:
रागी कर्मफलप्रेप्सुर्लुब्धो हिंसात्मकोऽशुचिः।
हर्षशोकान्वितः कर्ता राजसः परिकीर्तितः।।18.27।।
transliteration:
rāgī karma-phala-prepsur lubdho hinsātmako ’śhuchiḥ
harṣha-śhokānvitaḥ kartā rājasaḥ parikīrtitaḥ
word_meanings: rāgī—very much attached; karma-phala—the fruit of the work; prepsuḥ—desiring; lubdhaḥ—greedy; hinsā-ātmakaḥ—always envious; aśhuchiḥ—unclean; harṣha-śhoka-anvitaḥ—subject to joy and sorrow; kartā—such a worker; rājasaḥ—in the mode of passion; parikīrtitaḥ—is declared
translation_english: The worker who is attached to work and the fruits of work, desiring to enjoy those fruits, and who is greedy, always envious, impure, and moved by joy and sorrow, is said to be in the mode of passion.
deep_dive_text: The Rajasic Worker. The passionate worker is an emotional rollercoaster. Because they are 'Ragi' (deeply attached) and 'Karma-phala-prepsur' (obsessed with the payoff), they are constantly tossed between 'Harsha' (manic joy when they win) and 'Shoka' (crushing sorrow when they lose). Their greed ('Lubdho') makes them cut corners ('Ashuchi' - impure) and harbor toxic envy ('Hinsatmako') towards anyone doing better than them.

original_script:
अयुक्तः प्राकृतः स्तब्धः शठो नैष्कृतिकोऽलसः।
विषादी दीर्घसूत्री च कर्ता तामस उच्यते।।18.28।।
transliteration:
ayuktaḥ prākṛitaḥ stabdhaḥ śhaṭho naiṣhkṛitiko ’lasaḥ
viṣhādī dīrgha-sūtrī cha kartā tāmasa uchyate
word_meanings: ayuktaḥ—not referring to the scriptural injunctions; prākṛitaḥ—materialistic; stabdhaḥ—obstinate; śhaṭhaḥ—deceitful; naiṣhkṛitikaḥ—expert in insulting others; alasaḥ—lazy; viṣhādī—morose; dīrgha-sūtrī—procrastinating; cha—also; kartā—worker; tāmasaḥ—in the mode of ignorance; uchyate—is said to be
translation_english: The worker who is always engaged in work against the injunctions of the scripture, who is materialistic, obstinate, cheating and expert in insulting others, and who is lazy, always morose and procrastinating is said to be a worker in the mode of ignorance.
deep_dive_text: The Tamasic Worker. This is the ultimate toxic coworker. They are 'Ayuktah' (undisciplined/chaotic) and 'Stabdhah' (stubbornly resistant to good advice). Rather than doing the actual work, they are 'Shatho' (deceitful/cheating) and 'Naishkritiko' (expert at tearing others down). Because they are profoundly 'Alasah' (lazy) and 'Dirgha-sutri' (habitual procrastinators), they drag everyone around them down into their 'Vishadi' (morose/depressed) state.

original_script:
बुद्धेर्भेदं धृतेश्चैव गुणतस्त्रिविधं शृणु।
प्रोच्यमानमशेषेण पृथक्त्वेन धनञ्जय।।18.29।।
transliteration:
buddher bhedaṁ dhṛiteśh chaiva guṇatas tri-vidhaṁ śhṛiṇu
prochyamānam aśheṣheṇa pṛithaktvena dhanañjaya
word_meanings: buddheḥ—of intelligence; bhedam—the differences; dhṛiteḥ—of determination; cha—and; eva—certainly; guṇataḥ—by the modes of material nature; tri-vidham—of three kinds; śhṛiṇu—just hear; prochyamānam—as described by Me; aśheṣheṇa—in detail; pṛithaktvena—differently; dhanañjaya—O winner of wealth
translation_english: O winner of wealth, now please hear in detail the different kinds of understanding and determination, according to the three modes of material nature.
deep_dive_text: The Anatomy of the Mind. Krishna continues his psychological breakdown. Having categorized knowledge, action, and the worker, He now turns to the internal operating system of the human being: 'Buddhi' (intellect/understanding) and 'Dhriti' (determination/resolve). He promises to explain 'Asheshena' (exhaustively and without leaving anything out) how the modes completely rewire how we process reality and how we sustain our willpower.

original_script:
प्रवृत्तिं च निवृत्तिं च कार्याकार्ये भयाभये।
बन्धं मोक्षं च या वेत्ति बुद्धिः सा पार्थ सात्त्विकी।।18.30।।
transliteration:
pravṛittiṁ cha nivṛittiṁ cha kāryākārye bhayābhaye
bandhaṁ mokṣhaṁ cha yā vetti buddhiḥ sā pārtha sāttvikī
word_meanings: pravṛittim—deserving action; cha—and; nivṛittim—deserving cessation; cha—and; kārya—what ought to be done; akārye—and what ought not to be done; bhaya—fear; abhaye—and fearlessness; bandham—bondage; mokṣham—liberation; cha—and; yā—that which; vetti—knows; buddhiḥ—understanding; sā—that; pārtha—O son of Pritha; sāttvikī—in the mode of goodness
translation_english: O son of Pritha, that understanding by which one knows what ought to be done and what ought not to be done, what is to be feared and what is not to be feared, what is binding and what is liberating, is in the mode of goodness.
deep_dive_text: Sattvic Intellect. Pure intelligence is the ultimate compass. A Sattvic 'Buddhi' clearly knows the difference between 'Pravritti' (proper action that elevates) and 'Nivritti' (improper action that degrades). It correctly identifies what is actually dangerous to the soul ('Bhaya') versus what is harmless ('Abhaya'). Most importantly, it can perfectly distinguish between choices that lead to karmic 'Bandham' (bondage) and choices that grant 'Moksham' (liberation).

original_script:
यया धर्ममधर्मं च कार्यं चाकार्यमेव च।
अयथावत्प्रजानाति बुद्धिः सा पार्थ राजसी।।18.31।।
transliteration:
yayā dharmam adharmaṁ cha kāryaṁ chākāryam eva cha
ayathāvat prajānāti buddhiḥ sā pārtha rājasī
word_meanings: yayā—by which; dharmam—the principles of religion; adharmam—irreligion; cha—and; kāryam—what ought to be done; cha—and; akāryam—what ought not to be done; eva—certainly; cha—also; ayathā-vat—imperfectly; prajānāti—knows; buddhiḥ—intelligence; sā—that; pārtha—O son of Pritha; rājasī—in the mode of passion
translation_english: O son of Pritha, that understanding which cannot distinguish between religion and irreligion, between action that should be done and action that should not be done, is in the mode of passion.
deep_dive_text: Rajasic Intellect. Passion blurs the compass. A Rajasic intellect 'Ayatha-vat prajanati' (understands things imperfectly or in a distorted way). Because the mind is clouded by personal ambition, greed, or attachment, it struggles to tell the difference between 'Dharma' (righteousness) and 'Adharma' (unrighteousness). It rationalizes unethical behavior as 'just business' or 'necessary for success,' twisting the truth to fit its desires.

original_script:
अधर्मं धर्ममिति या मन्यते तमसावृता।
सर्वार्थान्विपरीतांश्च बुद्धिः सा पार्थ तामसी।।18.32।।
transliteration:
adharmaṁ dharmam iti yā manyate tamasāvṛitā
sarvārthān viparītānśh cha buddhiḥ sā pārtha tāmasī
word_meanings: adharmam—irreligion; dharmam—religion; iti—thus; yā—which; manyate—thinks; tamasā—by illusion; āvṛitā—covered; sarva-arthān—all things; viparītān—in the wrong direction; cha—also; buddhiḥ—intelligence; sā—that; pārtha—O son of Pritha; tāmasī—in the mode of ignorance
translation_english: That understanding which considers irreligion to be religion and religion to be irreligion, under the spell of illusion and darkness, and strives always in the wrong direction, O Partha, is in the mode of ignorance.
deep_dive_text: Tamasic Intellect. Ignorance is the compass pointing completely backward. A Tamasic intellect is completely 'Tamasavrita' (shrouded in darkness). It doesn't just blur the lines; it actively reverses them ('Viparitan'). It believes that exploiting others is smart, that violence is strength, and that compassion is weakness. It considers 'Adharmam dharmam iti' (irreligion to be religion). It is a completely inverted, toxic worldview.

original_script:
धृत्या यया धारयते मनःप्राणेन्द्रियक्रियाः।
योगेनाव्यभिचारिण्या धृतिः सा पार्थ सात्त्विकी।।18.33।।
transliteration:
dhṛityā yayā dhārayate manaḥ-prāṇendriya-kriyāḥ
yogenāvyabhichāriṇyā dhṛitiḥ sā pārtha sāttvikī
word_meanings: dhṛityā—by determination; yayā—by which; dhārayate—one sustains; manaḥ—of the mind; prāṇa—life airs; indriya—and senses; kriyāḥ—the activities; yogena—by yoga practice; avyabhichāriṇyā—without any break; dhṛitiḥ—determination; sā—that; pārtha—O son of Pritha; sāttvikī—in the mode of goodness
translation_english: O son of Pritha, that resolve which is unbreakable, which is sustained with steadfastness by yoga practice, and which thus controls the activities of the mind, life and senses, is determination in the mode of goodness.
deep_dive_text: Sattvic Determination. Willpower is the fuel of the mind. Sattvic 'Dhriti' (determination) is 'Avyabhicharinya' (unfailing, unbreakable, and unwavering). This isn't the stubbornness to win an argument; it is the spiritual grit used 'Yogena' (through practice) to actively control the wandering mind, the vital energies ('Prana'), and the turbulent senses. It is the quiet, iron-willed resolve of a true yogi.

original_script:
यया तु धर्मकामार्थान्धृत्या धारयतेऽर्जुन।
प्रसङ्गेन फलाकाङ्क्षी धृतिः सा पार्थ राजसी।।18.34।।
transliteration:
yayā tu dharma-kāmārthān dhṛityā dhārayate ’rjuna
prasaṅgena phalākāṅkṣhī dhṛitiḥ sā pārtha rājasī
word_meanings: yayā—by which; tu—but; dharma—religiosity; kāma—sense gratification; arthān—and economic development; dhṛityā—by determination; dhārayate—one sustains; arjuna—O Arjuna; prasaṅgena—because of attachment; phala-ākāṅkṣhī—desiring fruitive results; dhṛitiḥ—determination; sā—that; pārtha—O son of Pritha; rājasī—in the mode of passion
translation_english: But that resolve by which one holds fast to fruitive results in religion, economic development and sense gratification is of the nature of passion, O Arjuna.
deep_dive_text: Rajasic Determination. The willpower of passion is strong, but it is transactional. A Rajasic person can work incredibly hard, displaying massive determination, but only if they are getting something out of it. Their 'Dhriti' is aimed entirely at 'Dharma-kamarthan' (religiosity for social standing, fulfilling lust, and accumulating massive wealth). Because they are 'Phalakankshi' (desiring the fruit), their willpower collapses the moment the reward is removed.

original_script:
यया स्वप्नं भयं शोकं विषादं मदमेव च।
न विमुञ्चति दुर्मेधा धृतिः सा पार्थ तामसी।।18.35।।
transliteration:
yayā svapnaṁ bhayaṁ śhokaṁ viṣhādaṁ madam eva cha
na vimuñchati durmedhā dhṛitiḥ sā pārtha tāmasī
word_meanings: yayā—by which; svapnam—dreaming; bhayam—fearfulness; śhokam—lamentation; viṣhādam—moroseness; madam—illusion; eva—certainly; cha—also; na—never; vimuñchati—one gives up; durmedhā—unintelligent; dhṛitiḥ—determination; sā—that; pārtha—O son of Pritha; tāmasī—in the mode of ignorance
translation_english: And that resolve which cannot go beyond dreaming, fearfulness, lamentation, moroseness and illusion—such unintelligent resolve is in the mode of darkness.
deep_dive_text: Tamasic Determination. The darkest form of willpower is the stubborn refusal to heal. A 'Durmedha' (unintelligent/foolish person) uses their 'Dhriti' to fiercely hold on to negative states. They simply refuse ('Na vimunchati') to let go of 'Svapnam' (excessive sleep/daydreaming), 'Bhayam' (paranoia), 'Shokam' (chronic grief), and 'Vishadam' (depression). They cling to their trauma and misery, defending their illusions rather than doing the hard work of waking up.

original_script:
सुखं त्विदानीं त्रिविधं शृणु मे भरतर्षभ।
अभ्यासाद्रमते यत्र दुःखान्तं च निगच्छति।।18.36।।
यत्तदग्रे विषमिव परिणामेऽमृतोपमम्।
तत्सुखं सात्त्विकं प्रोक्तमात्मबुद्धिप्रसादजम्।।18.37।।
transliteration:
sukhaṁ tv idānīṁ tri-vidhaṁ śhṛiṇu me bharatarṣhabha
abhyāsād ramate yatra duḥkhāntaṁ cha nigachchhati
yat tad agre viṣham iva pariṇāme ’mṛitopamam
tat sukhaṁ sāttvikaṁ proktam ātma-buddhi-prasāda-jam
word_meanings: sukham—happiness; tu—but; idānīm—now; tri-vidham—of three kinds; śhṛiṇu—hear; me—from Me; bharata-ṛiṣhabha—O best amongst the Bharatas; abhyāsāt—by practice; ramate—one enjoys; yatra—where; duḥkha—of distress; antam—the end; cha—and; nigachchhati—gains; yat—which; tat—that; agre—in the beginning; viṣham iva—like poison; pariṇāme—at the end; amṛita-upamam—like nectar; tat—that; sukham—happiness; sāttvikam—in the mode of goodness; proktam—is said; ātma—in the self; buddhi—of intelligence; prasāda-jam—born of the satisfaction
translation_english: O best of the Bharatas, now please hear from Me about the three kinds of happiness by which the conditioned soul enjoys, and by which he sometimes comes to the end of all distress. That which in the beginning may be just like poison but at the end is just like nectar and which awakens one to self-realization is said to be happiness in the mode of goodness.
deep_dive_text: Sattvic Happiness. (Grouped to show the full thought). Krishna shifts to the ultimate human pursuit: 'Sukham' (Happiness). The rule of Goodness is delayed gratification. Sattvic happiness feels 'Visham iva' (like poison) in the beginning—waking up early, exercising, meditating, or studying requires initial pain and discipline. But 'Pariname' (in the end), it tastes 'Amritopamam' (like immortal nectar). Because it is born from 'Atma-buddhi-prasadajam' (the deep, pure satisfaction of the soul), it permanently ends distress.

original_script:
विषयेन्द्रियसंयोगाद्यत्तदग्रेऽमृतोपमम्।
परिणामे विषमिव तत्सुखं राजसं स्मृतम्।।18.38।।
transliteration:
viṣhayendriya-sanyogād yat tad agre ’mṛitopamam
pariṇāme viṣham iva tat sukhaṁ rājasaṁ smṛitam
word_meanings: viṣhaya—of the objects of the senses; indriya—and the senses; sanyogāt—from the combination; yat—which; tat—that; agre—in the beginning; amṛita-upamam—just like nectar; pariṇāme—at the end; viṣham iva—like poison; tat—that; sukham—happiness; rājasam—in the mode of passion; smṛitam—is considered
translation_english: That happiness which is derived from contact of the senses with their objects and which appears like nectar at first but poison at the end is said to be of the nature of passion.
deep_dive_text: Rajasic Happiness. The rule of Passion is instant gratification. It is derived purely from 'Vishayendriya-sanyogad' (the friction of the senses rubbing against their objects—like eating junk food, toxic hookups, or binge-watching). In the 'Agre' (beginning), it tastes 'Amritopamam' (like sweet nectar). But in the 'Pariname' (the end), the sugar crash hits, the relationship breaks, or the addiction takes over, and it burns 'Visham iva' (exactly like poison).

original_script:
यदग्रे चानुबन्धे च सुखं मोहनमात्मनः।
निद्रालस्यप्रमादोत्थं तत्तामसमुदाहृतम्।।18.39।।
transliteration:
yad agre chānubandhe cha sukhaṁ mohanam ātmanaḥ
nidrālasya-pramādotthaṁ tat tāmasam udāhṛitam
word_meanings: yat—that which; agre—in the beginning; cha—and; anubandhe—at the end; cha—also; sukham—happiness; mohanam—illusory; ātmanaḥ—of the self; nidrā—sleep; ālasya—laziness; pramāda—and illusion; uttham—produced of; tat—that; tāmasam—in the mode of ignorance; udāhṛitam—is said to be
translation_english: And that happiness which is blind to self-realization, which is delusion from beginning to end and which arises from sleep, laziness and illusion is said to be of the nature of ignorance.
deep_dive_text: Tamasic Happiness. Ignorance is the comfort of the void. Tamasic happiness is 'Mohanam atmanah' (an absolute delusion of the self). Unlike Sattva (hard first, sweet later) or Rajas (sweet first, hard later), Tamas is 'Agre chanubandhe cha'—it is a toxic illusion both in the beginning AND at the end. It arises entirely from 'Nidra' (excessive sleep), 'Alasya' (lethargy), and 'Pramada' (carelessness). It is the hollow 'joy' of completely checking out of reality and avoiding responsibility.

original_script:
न तदस्ति पृथिव्यां वा दिवि देवेषु वा पुनः।
सत्त्वं प्रकृतिजैर्मुक्तं यदेभिः स्यात्त्रिभिर्गुणैः।।18.40।।
transliteration:
na tad asti pṛithivyāṁ vā divi deveṣhu vā punaḥ
sattvaṁ prakṛiti-jair muktaṁ yad ebhiḥ syāt tribhir guṇaiḥ
word_meanings: na—not; tat—that; asti—there is; pṛithivyām—on the earth; vā—or; divi—in the higher planetary systems; deveṣhu—amongst the demigods; vā—or; punaḥ—again; sattvam—existence; prakṛiti-jaiḥ—born of material nature; muktam—liberated; yat—that; ebhiḥ—from the influence of these; syāt—is; tribhiḥ—three; guṇaiḥ—modes of material nature
translation_english: There is no being existing, either here or among the demigods in the higher planetary systems, which is freed from these three modes born of material nature.
deep_dive_text: The Universal Matrix. Krishna concludes the massive section on the three Gunas with a staggering absolute statement. 'Na tad asti' (There is absolutely no one) on Earth, nor even 'Divi deveshu' (among the demigods/angels in heaven), who is 'Muktam' (free) from the influence of these three ropes of material nature. As long as a soul inhabits a physical or subtle material body, it is subject to the gravitational pull of Goodness, Passion, and Ignorance.

original_script:
ब्राह्मणक्षत्रियविशां शूद्राणां च परन्तप।
कर्माणि प्रविभक्तानि स्वभावप्रभवैर्गुणैः।।18.41।।
transliteration:
brāhmaṇa-kṣhatriya-viśhāṁ śhūdrāṇāṁ cha parantapa
karmāṇi pravibhaktāni svabhāva-prabhavair guṇaiḥ
word_meanings: brāhmaṇa—of the brahmanas; kṣhatriya—the kshatriyas; viśhām—and the vaishyas; śhūdrāṇām—of the shudras; cha—and; parantapa—O subduer of the enemies; karmāṇi—the activities; pravibhaktāni—are divided; svabhāva—their own nature; prabhavaiḥ—born of; guṇaiḥ—by the modes of material nature
translation_english: Brahmanas, kshatriyas, vaishyas and shudras are distinguished by the qualities born of their own natures in accordance with the material modes, O chastiser of the enemy.
deep_dive_text: The Basis of Vocation. Having established that everyone is driven by the modes, Krishna introduces the four natural occupational divisions of human society (Varnas). The crucial phrase here is 'Svabhava-prabhavair gunaih' (divided according to the modes born of their own psychological nature). A person's 'class' in the Gita is not determined by birth, genetics, or wealth; it is a psychometric classification based strictly on which Gunas (Goodness, Passion, or Ignorance) naturally dominate their intellect and behavior.

original_script:
शमो दमस्तपः शौचं क्षान्तिरार्जवमेव च।
ज्ञानं विज्ञानमास्तिक्यं ब्रह्मकर्म स्वभावजम्।।18.42।।
transliteration:
śhamo damas tapaḥ śhauchaṁ kṣhāntir ārjavam eva cha
jñānaṁ vijñānam āstikyaṁ brahma-karma svabhāva-jam
word_meanings: śhamaḥ—peacefulness; damaḥ—self-control; tapaḥ—austerity; śhaucham—purity; kṣhāntiḥ—tolerance; ārjavam—honesty; eva—certainly; cha—and; jñānam—knowledge; vijñānam—wisdom; āstikyam—religiousness; brahma—of a brahmana; karma—duty; svabhāva-jam—born of his own nature
translation_english: Peacefulness, self-control, austerity, purity, tolerance, honesty, knowledge, wisdom and religiousness—these are the natural qualities by which the brahmanas work.
deep_dive_text: The Mind of the Intellectual/Teacher (Brahmana). The Brahmana represents the pure mode of Sattva (Goodness). Their defining 'Karma' (work) isn't performing rituals; it is cultivating an elite character. They naturally display 'Shamo' (peaceful mind), 'Damo' (controlled senses), and 'Arjavam' (absolute honesty). Because their job is to guide society, they must possess both 'Jnanam' (theoretical knowledge) and 'Vijnanam' (practical, realized wisdom), grounded in 'Astikyam' (deep faith in the Divine).

original_script:
शौर्यं तेजो धृतिर्दाक्ष्यं युद्धे चाप्यपलायनम्।
दानमीश्वरभावश्च क्षात्रं कर्म स्वभावजम्।।18.43।।
transliteration:
śhauryaṁ tejo dhṛitir dākṣhyaṁ yuddhe chāpy apalāyanam
dānam īśhvara-bhāvaśh cha kṣhātraṁ karma svabhāva-jam
word_meanings: śhauryam—heroism; tejaḥ—power; dhṛitiḥ—determination; dākṣhyam—resourcefulness; yuddhe—in battle; cha—and; api—also; apalāyanam—not fleeing; dānam—generosity; īśhvara—of leadership; bhāvaḥ—the nature; cha—and; kṣhātram—of a kshatriya; karma—duty; svabhāva-jam—born of his own nature
translation_english: Heroism, power, determination, resourcefulness, courage in battle, generosity and leadership are the natural qualities of work for the kshatriyas.
deep_dive_text: The Mind of the Leader/Warrior (Kshatriya). The Kshatriya is driven by a mix of Rajas (Passion) and Sattva (Goodness). They are the protectors and administrators of society. Their natural traits are 'Shauryam' (heroism) and 'Tejah' (commanding presence). In times of crisis ('Yuddhe'), their defining trait is 'Apalayanam' (never running away). Furthermore, a true leader is marked by 'Danam' (immense generosity) and 'Ishvara-bhavash' (the natural, charismatic ability to govern and lead others).

original_script:
कृषिगौरक्ष्यवाणिज्यं वैश्यकर्म स्वभावजम्।
परिचर्यात्मकं कर्म शूद्रस्यापि स्वभावजम्।।18.44।।
transliteration:
kṛiṣhi-go-rakṣhya-vāṇijyaṁ vaiśhya-karma svabhāva-jam
paricharyātmakaṁ karma śhūdrasyāpi svabhāva-jam
word_meanings: kṛiṣhi—plowing; go—of cows; rakṣhya—protection; vāṇijyam—trade; vaiśhya—of a vaishya; karma—duty; svabhāva-jam—born of his own nature; paricharyā—service; ātmakam—consisting of; karma—duty; śhūdrasya—of the shudra; api—also; svabhāva-jam—born of his own nature
translation_english: Farming, cow protection and business are the natural work for the vaishyas, and for the shudras there is labor and service to others.
deep_dive_text: The Minds of the Producer and the Worker. The Vaishya (driven by Rajas and Tamas) thrives on commerce and production. Their natural inclinations ('Svabhava-jam') lie in 'Krishi' (agriculture/wealth generation), 'Go-rakshya' (protecting vital resources like cows), and 'Vanijyam' (trade and economics). The Shudra (driven primarily by Tamas and Rajas) finds their natural rhythm in 'Paricharyatmakam' (supporting, assisting, and laboring for the other three sectors). All four are completely interdependent and essential for a functioning ecosystem.

original_script:
स्वे स्वे कर्मण्यभिरतः संसिद्धिं लभते नरः।
स्वकर्मनिरतः सिद्धिं यथा विन्दति तच्छृणु।।18.45।।
transliteration:
sve sve karmaṇy abhirataḥ sansiddhiṁ labhate naraḥ
sva-karma-nirataḥ siddhiṁ yathā vindati tach chhṛiṇu
word_meanings: sve sve—each his own; karmaṇi—work; abhirataḥ—following; sansiddhim—perfection; labhate—achieves; naraḥ—a man; sva-karma—in his own duty; nirataḥ—engaged; siddhim—perfection; yathā—as; vindati—attains; tat—that; śhṛiṇu—listen
translation_english: By following his qualities of work, every man can become perfect. Now please hear from Me how this can be done.
deep_dive_text: Perfection Through the Daily Grind. This is one of the most liberating verses in the Gita. Krishna declares that you do not need to become a monk or a scholar to achieve enlightenment. 'Sve sve karmany abhiratah' (By being passionately engaged in one's OWN specific, natural work), any person ('Narah') can achieve 'Sansiddhim' (the highest spiritual perfection). The path to God is paved by executing your own unique biological and psychological purpose with excellence.

original_script:
यतः प्रवृत्तिर्भूतानां येन सर्वमिदं ततम्।
स्वकर्मणा तमभ्यर्च्य सिद्धिं विन्दति मानवः।।18.46।।
transliteration:
yataḥ pravṛittir bhūtānāṁ yena sarvam idaṁ tatam
sva-karmaṇā tam abhyarchya siddhiṁ vindati mānavaḥ
word_meanings: yataḥ—from whom; pravṛittiḥ—the emanation; bhūtānām—of all living entities; yena—by whom; sarvam—all; idam—this; tatam—is pervaded; sva-karmaṇā—by his own duties; tam—Him; abhyarchya—by worshiping; siddhim—perfection; vindati—achieves; mānavaḥ—a man
translation_english: By worship of the Lord, who is the source of all beings and who is all-pervading, a man can attain perfection through performing his own work.
deep_dive_text: Work as Worship. How does an accountant, a farmer, or a soldier achieve spiritual perfection? Krishna provides the formula. The Supreme Lord is the source ('Yatah') from which all beings emanate, and He completely pervades ('Tatam') the entire universe. Because God is everywhere, you don't need a temple to worship Him. You can 'Tam abhyarchya' (worship Him) strictly 'Sva-karmana' (through your own daily work). When you dedicate your spreadsheets, your art, or your parenting to the Divine, your desk becomes an altar.

original_script:
श्रेयान्स्वधर्मो विगुणः परधर्मात्स्वनुष्ठितात्।
स्वभावनियतं कर्म कुर्वन्नाप्नोति किल्बिषम्।।18.47।।
transliteration:
śhreyān sva-dharmo viguṇaḥ para-dharmāt sv-anuṣhṭhitāt
svabhāva-niyataṁ karma kurvan nāpnoti kilbiṣham
word_meanings: śhreyān—better; sva-dharmaḥ—one's own occupation; viguṇaḥ—imperfectly performed; para-dharmāt—than another's occupation; su-anuṣhṭhitāt—perfectly done; svabhāva-niyatam—prescribed according to one's nature; karma—work; kurvan—performing; na—never; āpnoti—achieves; kilbiṣham—sinful reactions
translation_english: It is better to engage in one's own occupation, even though one may perform it imperfectly, than to accept another's occupation and perform it perfectly. Duties prescribed according to one's nature are never affected by sinful reactions.
deep_dive_text: The Danger of Copying Others. Krishna repeats a vital lesson from Chapter 3. 'Shreyan' (It is far better) to do your 'Sva-dharma' (your own natural duty) even if you do it 'Vigunah' (clumsily or with flaws), than to perfectly copy the 'Para-dharma' (duty of another person). Why? Because 'Svabhava-niyatam' (work dictated by your own authentic nature) keeps you spiritually aligned. Trying to live someone else's life, no matter how successful it looks on paper, forces you to act against your soul's grain, which incurs 'Kilbisham' (karmic sin/stress).

original_script:
सहजं कर्म कौन्तेय सदोषमपि न त्यजेत्।
सर्वारम्भा हि दोषेण धूमेनाग्निरिवावृताः।।18.48।।
transliteration:
saha-jaṁ karma kaunteya sa-doṣham api na tyajet
sarvārambhā hi doṣheṇa dhūmenāgnir ivāvṛitāḥ
word_meanings: saha-jam—born simultaneously; karma—work; kaunteya—O son of Kunti; sa-doṣham—with fault; api—although; na—never; tyajet—one should give up; sarva-ārambhāḥ—all endeavors; hi—certainly; doṣheṇa—with fault; dhūmena—with smoke; agniḥ—fire; iva—as; āvṛitāḥ—covered
translation_english: Every endeavor is covered by some fault, just as fire is covered by smoke. Therefore one should not give up the work born of his nature, O son of Kunti, even if such work is full of fault.
deep_dive_text: The Fire and the Smoke. Arjuna wanted to quit the war because killing is inherently violent and faulty. Krishna counters with profound realism. Every single 'Saha-jam karma' (natural action or endeavor in this world) is 'Sa-dosham' (covered by some flaw or fault). You cannot find a perfect, pristine job. Just as every roaring fire is accompanied by stinging smoke ('Dhumena'), every great endeavor carries some collateral damage or imperfection. You cannot abandon your purpose just because the smoke stings your eyes.

original_script:
असक्तबुद्धिः सर्वत्र जितात्मा विगतस्पृहः।
नैष्कर्म्यसिद्धिं परमां सन्न्यासेनाधिगच्छति।।18.49।।
transliteration:
asakta-buddhiḥ sarvatra jitātmā vigata-spṛihaḥ
naiṣhkarmya-siddhiṁ paramāṁ sannyāsenādhigachchhati
word_meanings: asakta-buddhiḥ—having unattached intelligence; sarvatra—everywhere; jita-ātmā—having control of the mind; vigata-spṛihaḥ—without material desires; naiṣhkarmya-siddhim—the perfection of nonreaction; paramām—supreme; sannyāsena—by the renounced order of life; adhigachchhati—one attains
translation_english: One who is unattached to the fruits of his work and who lives in the material world without being affected by its modes achieves the supreme perfection of freedom from reaction through renunciation.
deep_dive_text: The Zenith of Action. Krishna describes the state of 'Naishkarmya-siddhim' (the supreme perfection of actionless action). This doesn't mean sitting still; it means working with an 'Asakta-buddhih' (completely unattached intellect).
